learnUni_statistics_data_science_f0_kp0: Descriptive statistics: Measures of central tendency: mean (arithmetic average — sensitive to outliers), median (middle value — robust to outliers), mode (most frequent value — useful for categorical data). Measures of spread: range (max-min), variance (average squared deviation from the mean), standard deviation (SD — square root of variance; same units as the data), interquartile range (IQR = Q3-Q1, robust to outliers). Distribution shape: normal distribution (bell curve — symmetric, characterised by mean and SD); skewed distributions (positive skew: long tail to the right; negative: long tail to the left). Box plots and histograms visualise distributions.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f0_kp1: Probability fundamentals: Probability measures the likelihood of an event: P(A) ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Rules: addition rule P(A or B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A and B); multiplication rule P(A and B) = P(A) x P(B|A). Conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B) — the probability of A given B has occurred. Bayes theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A) x P(A) / P(B) — used for updating beliefs with new evidence. Applications: medical diagnosis (positive test result and actual disease probability), spam filtering, weather forecasting. Common misconceptions: gambler's fallacy (past random events do not affect future ones), base rate neglect.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f0_kp2: Statistical distributions: Normal distribution: symmetric, bell-shaped; defined by mean (mu) and standard deviation (sigma). 68-95-99.7 rule: 68% of values within 1 SD, 95% within 2 SD, 99.7% within 3 SD of the mean. Z-scores: standardise values — Z = (X-mu)/sigma — enables comparison across different scales. Binomial distribution: discrete probability distribution for the number of successes in n independent trials, each with probability p. Poisson distribution: models the number of events in a fixed interval (rare events — e.g., calls per hour, defects per unit). t-distribution: like the normal but with heavier tails — used for small samples when the population SD is unknown.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f0_kp3: Hypothesis testing: Hypothesis testing provides a formal framework for using sample data to make decisions about population parameters. Steps: 1. State H0 (null hypothesis — assumes no effect) and H1 (alternative hypothesis). 2. Choose significance level alpha (typically 0.05 or 0.01). 3. Calculate the test statistic. 4. Calculate the p-value (probability of observing a result as extreme as the sample, assuming H0 is true). 5. Decision: if p < alpha, reject H0. p-value misconceptions: p is not the probability that H0 is true; p < 0.05 does not mean the effect is practically significant. Type I error (false positive): rejecting a true H0 (rate = alpha). Type II error (false negative): failing to reject a false H0 (rate = beta). Power = 1-beta.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f0_kp4: Confidence intervals and effect sizes: Confidence interval (CI): a range of values likely to contain the true population parameter. A 95% CI does NOT mean there is a 95% probability the true value is in this specific interval — it means that 95% of intervals constructed this way will contain the true value. Interpretation: a 95% CI for mean weight loss of (2.1 to 5.8 kg) suggests a practically meaningful effect; a CI of (-0.2 to 0.4 kg) overlapping zero suggests no meaningful effect. Effect sizes: standardised measures of the magnitude of an effect. Cohen's d: difference between means in SD units (d=0.2 small, d=0.5 medium, d=0.8 large). Used alongside p-values to assess practical significance.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f0_task: A clinical trial of a new blood pressure drug reports: mean reduction of 4.2 mmHg (SD=8.1) in the treatment group (n=50) vs 1.1 mmHg (SD=7.9) in the control group (n=50). (a) Calculate the pooled SE and the t-statistic for this difference. (b) The reported p-value is 0.03. State the conclusion at alpha=0.05. (c) Calculate Cohen's d and interpret the effect size. (d) Explain why p<0.05 does not guarantee the drug is clinically useful.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f1_desc: Real-world data is messy, incomplete, and full of surprises. Data wrangling — the process of cleaning, transforming, and preparing raw data for analysis — typically consumes 60-80% of a data scientist's time. Exploratory data analysis (EDA) uses visual and statistical techniques to understand data before formal modelling, revealing patterns, outliers, and relationships that guide model selection.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f1_kp0: Data types and structures: Quantitative (numerical): continuous (can take any value — height, temperature) vs discrete (countable integer values — number of children). Qualitative (categorical): nominal (no order — eye colour, country) vs ordinal (ordered categories — satisfaction scale: poor/fair/good/excellent). Data structures: tidy data principle (Wickham, 2014): each variable = one column; each observation = one row; each observational unit = one table. Common data formats: CSV, JSON, SQL databases, Excel. Big data: characterised by Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity (4 Vs) — too large for traditional tools.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f1_kp1: Data cleaning and preprocessing: Missing data: types and handling. MCAR (Missing Completely At Random): safe to delete or impute. MAR (Missing At Random): depends on observed data — imputation appropriate. MNAR (Missing Not At Random): problematic — may require specialist modelling. Handling: listwise deletion (remove rows with missing values — loses information), mean/median imputation (simple but distorts variance), multiple imputation (gold standard for research). Outlier detection: Z-scores >3 or <-3; IQR method (values beyond 1.5xIQR from quartiles); visual inspection via box plots and scatter plots. Data normalisation: min-max scaling (rescale to 0-1); standardisation (Z-score — zero mean, unit SD).
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f1_kp2: Exploratory data analysis (EDA): EDA (popularised by John Tukey, 1977) uses graphical and quantitative tools to summarise data without prior assumptions. Key visualisations: histograms (distribution of single variable); box plots (distribution, quartiles, outliers); scatter plots (relationship between two continuous variables); correlation heatmaps (pairwise correlations in a dataset); bar charts (categorical comparisons). Correlation: Pearson r measures linear relationship (-1 to +1). r=0.8 = strong positive correlation. Spearman rho: rank-based, non-parametric — used when data is not normally distributed or when the relationship is monotonic but not linear. Key caveat: correlation does not imply causation.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f1_kp3: Feature engineering: Feature engineering is the process of creating informative input variables (features) from raw data to improve model performance. Techniques: creating ratio features (BMI = weight/height2); extracting date components (day of week, month, quarter from timestamps); encoding categorical variables (one-hot encoding: one binary column per category; label encoding: numeric codes); binning (converting continuous variable to ordered categories); text features (TF-IDF scores from text data); lag features in time series (previous day's value as a predictor). The quality of features matters more than the choice of model in most practical data science applications.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f1_kp4: SQL and data querying: SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for relational database management. Essential SQL commands: SELECT (specify columns), FROM (specify table), WHERE (filter rows), GROUP BY (aggregate), ORDER BY (sort), JOIN (combine tables on a common key). Key JOIN types: INNER JOIN (matching rows in both tables only), LEFT JOIN (all rows from left table + matching from right), FULL OUTER JOIN (all rows from both tables). Aggregation functions: COUNT(), SUM(), AVG(), MIN(), MAX(). Subqueries: a SELECT within another SELECT. SQL skills are among the most requested in data science job postings globally.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f1_task: You are given a dataset of 1,200 customer records with 15 variables. 12% of values are missing in the income column; 3% in the age column; 28% in a satisfaction survey column. (a) For each missing variable, identify the likely missing data mechanism and the most appropriate handling strategy with justification. (b) Two variables (income and purchase_value) have a Pearson r=0.72 and a Spearman rho=0.69. What does this tell you about their relationship, and what would cause the two correlation measures to differ substantially?
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f1_tp1: Data cleaning: missing values and outliers
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f2_desc: Machine learning (ML) enables computers to learn from data without being explicitly programmed. From fraud detection to medical diagnosis to recommendation systems, ML underpins much of modern technology. This module covers the foundational supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms, model evaluation, and the ethical considerations of deploying ML systems.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f2_kp0: Supervised learning: Supervised learning trains models on labelled examples (input-output pairs) to predict outputs for new inputs. Regression: predicts a continuous output variable. Linear regression: Y = b0 + b1X + error. Multiple regression: multiple predictors. Evaluation: RMSE, MAE, R-squared. Classification: predicts a categorical output. Logistic regression: models the probability of a binary outcome using the sigmoid function. Decision trees: hierarchical binary splits on feature values. Random forests: ensemble of decision trees — reduces overfitting. Evaluation: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f2_kp1: Unsupervised learning and clustering: Unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabelled data. K-means clustering: partition data into K clusters by minimising within-cluster variance. Choose K using the elbow method (plot inertia vs K). Hierarchical clustering: builds a dendrogram — no need to specify K in advance. Principal Component Analysis (PCA): dimensionality reduction — transforms correlated variables into uncorrelated principal components; useful for visualisation and as preprocessing for other algorithms. Association rule mining (Apriori algorithm): discovers co-occurring patterns in transactional data (e.g., market basket analysis — customers who buy bread also buy butter).
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f2_kp2: Model evaluation and the bias-variance tradeoff: Overfitting: model learns the training data including noise — performs well on training data but poorly on new data (high variance). Underfitting: model is too simple — performs poorly on both training and test data (high bias). Bias-variance tradeoff: increasing model complexity reduces bias but increases variance — an optimal middle ground is sought. Cross-validation: split data into k folds; train on k-1 folds, test on the remaining fold; rotate k times — gives more robust performance estimate than a single train-test split. Regularisation: L1 (Lasso — shrinks some coefficients to zero, performs feature selection); L2 (Ridge — shrinks all coefficients towards zero).
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f2_kp3: Neural networks and deep learning basics: Artificial neural networks (ANNs): inspired by biological neurons. Structure: input layer, one or more hidden layers, output layer. Each neuron: receives weighted inputs, applies an activation function (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh), passes output to the next layer. Training: backpropagation computes gradients; stochastic gradient descent (SGD) updates weights to minimise the loss function. Deep learning: ANNs with many hidden layers. Applications: image recognition (CNNs), natural language processing (transformers — GPT, BERT), speech recognition. Requires large datasets and substantial compute — GPU acceleration is standard.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f2_kp4: Ethics and fairness in machine learning: Algorithmic bias: ML models trained on biased historical data reproduce and amplify existing biases. Example: COMPAS recidivism prediction tool (USA) was found to incorrectly flag Black defendants as higher risk at nearly twice the rate of white defendants (ProPublica, 2016). Fairness metrics: equitable false positive rates across demographic groups; disparate impact analysis. Data privacy: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requires lawful basis for data processing; rights to explanation of automated decisions (Article 22). Model transparency: black-box models (neural networks) vs interpretable models (decision trees, logistic regression). SHAP values and LIME: techniques for explaining individual predictions.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f2_task: A bank uses a logistic regression model to predict loan default. On the test set: 95% of actual non-defaults are correctly classified; but only 40% of actual defaults are correctly classified. (a) Calculate and interpret the precision and recall for the default class (assume 5% base rate of default). (b) The model produces an ROC-AUC of 0.82 — what does this mean? (c) The bank notices the model denies loans to a specific ethnic group at twice the rate of others. Suggest two ways to investigate and address this fairness concern.
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f2_tp3: Overfitting, regularisation, and the bias-variance tradeoff
learnUni_statistics_data_science_f2_tp5: Ethics, fairness, and privacy in ML
learnUni_cybersecurity_f0_desc: Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting digital systems, networks, and data from unauthorised access, damage, and attack. As digital infrastructure underpins economies, health systems, and national security, cybersecurity has become one of the most critical fields of the 21st century. This module covers the fundamental principles, threat landscape, and core security concepts that all cybersecurity professionals must understand.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f0_kp0: The CIA triad: The three fundamental properties of secure information systems. Confidentiality: information is accessible only to those authorised to access it (encryption, access control, authentication). Integrity: data is accurate, complete, and has not been modified unauthorised (hashing, digital signatures, checksums). Availability: systems and data are accessible when needed by authorised users (redundancy, backups, DDoS protection). Additional properties sometimes added: Authentication (verifying identity — passwords, MFA, biometrics), Non-repudiation (cannot deny having performed an action — digital signatures, audit logs). Security controls are evaluated against all three CIA properties.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f0_kp1: The threat landscape: Types of threats: Malware: malicious software including viruses (self-replicating, attach to legitimate files), worms (self-propagating across networks without user action), Trojans (disguised as legitimate software), ransomware (encrypts files and demands payment — WannaCry 2017 affected 200,000 systems in 150 countries), spyware (collects data covertly), rootkits (deep-level system control). Threat actors: nation-state actors (APTs — Advanced Persistent Threats; sophisticated, targeted, long-term campaigns — e.g., Lazarus Group/North Korea, Cozy Bear/Russia), cybercriminal organisations, hacktivists, insiders. Attack vectors: phishing (fake communications to steal credentials or install malware), social engineering, unpatched vulnerabilities, supply chain attacks.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f0_kp2: Cryptography fundamentals: Symmetric encryption: the same key encrypts and decrypts. Fast, efficient for large data. AES (Advanced Encryption Standard): 128/256-bit keys; the global standard for symmetric encryption. Problem: key distribution (how to securely share the key). Asymmetric (public-key) encryption: a key pair — public key (share freely) + private key (secret). RSA: security based on difficulty of factoring large primes. Used for: secure key exchange, digital signatures. TLS (Transport Layer Security): combines asymmetric encryption (for key exchange) and symmetric encryption (for data transfer) — underpins HTTPS. Hash functions: one-way transformation of data to a fixed-length digest (SHA-256 produces 64 hex characters). Used for password storage, data integrity verification, digital signatures.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f0_kp3: Authentication and access control: Authentication: verifying that a user is who they claim to be. Three factors: knowledge (password, PIN), possession (smart card, OTP token, phone), inherence (fingerprint, face, iris biometric). Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA): combines two or more factors — dramatically reduces account compromise risk. Passwords: should be long (12+ characters), unique per service, stored as salted hashes (never plaintext). Password managers enable strong unique passwords. Access control models: Mandatory Access Control (MAC): access based on security labels (government/military); Discretionary Access Control (DAC): owner controls access (most personal computing); Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): access determined by job role (most enterprise systems). Principle of least privilege: grant only the minimum access required for the task.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f0_kp4: Network security fundamentals: OSI model (7 layers): Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application — security controls exist at each layer. Firewalls: control network traffic based on rules — packet filtering (Layer 3-4), stateful inspection (tracks connection state), application layer (Layer 7 — can inspect application-level traffic). IDS/IPS: Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems — monitor traffic for attack signatures or anomalies. VPNs: create encrypted tunnels for secure remote access. DMZ (Demilitarised Zone): network segment housing public-facing servers, isolated from internal networks. Common network attacks: Man-in-the-Middle (MitM), DNS poisoning, ARP spoofing, port scanning (reconnaissance), DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service).
learnUni_cybersecurity_f0_task: A small hospital network is evaluating its cybersecurity posture. It stores patient records, is connected to the internet for email and web browsing, and has 50 staff using shared passwords. (a) Identify four specific vulnerabilities or weaknesses in this setup. (b) Propose a prioritised security improvement plan addressing each vulnerability, referencing the CIA triad. (c) Explain why ransomware is a particularly severe threat to healthcare organisations.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f0_tp0: The CIA triad: confidentiality, integrity, availability
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_desc: Ethical hacking (also called penetration testing or "pen testing") is the practice of testing systems for security vulnerabilities with the owner's permission, to identify and remediate weaknesses before malicious actors exploit them. Penetration testers follow a structured methodology, use many of the same tools as attackers, and report findings with remediation advice. This module covers the methodology, tools, and legal framework of ethical hacking.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_kp0: Penetration testing methodology: Standard methodology (PTES — Penetration Testing Execution Standard): 1. Pre-engagement: define scope, rules of engagement, and legal authorisation (written permission is essential). 2. Reconnaissance (information gathering): passive (OSINT — Open Source Intelligence: LinkedIn, Whois, Shodan) and active (scanning, enumeration). 3. Scanning and vulnerability analysis: identify open ports (Nmap), services, OS versions, and known vulnerabilities (Nessus, OpenVAS). 4. Exploitation: attempt to exploit discovered vulnerabilities (Metasploit). 5. Post-exploitation: determine impact (data access, lateral movement). 6. Reporting: document all findings with severity ratings and remediation advice. Legal basis: written authorisation is non-negotiable — without it, penetration testing = criminal hacking.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_kp1: Reconnaissance and OSINT: Passive reconnaissance: gathering information without direct interaction with the target — leaves no trace. OSINT sources: LinkedIn (employee names, roles, technologies); company website (email format, job postings, technologies); Whois (domain registration details); DNS records (MX, A, CNAME records reveal infrastructure); search engine dorking (advanced search operators to find exposed files); Shodan (search engine for internet-connected devices — finds exposed cameras, ICS, default passwords). Active reconnaissance: direct interaction — port scanning (Nmap), service enumeration. Google dorks: site:example.com filetype:pdf confidential.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_kp2: Common vulnerabilities and exploitation: OWASP Top 10 (Open Web Application Security Project) — the most critical web application security risks. Key entries: 1. Broken Access Control (unauthorised access to functions or data). 2. Cryptographic failures (weak encryption, plaintext storage of sensitive data). 3. Injection (SQL injection, command injection — inserting malicious code into queries). 4. Insecure Design (architectural flaws). 5. Security Misconfiguration (default credentials, unnecessary services, verbose error messages). SQL injection example: entering username= admin'-- bypasses authentication by commenting out the password check. Prevention: parameterised queries/prepared statements.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_kp3: Social engineering and phishing: Technical controls alone cannot protect against human manipulation. Social engineering: manipulating people into revealing confidential information or performing actions that compromise security. Techniques: Phishing (mass email campaigns with malicious links or attachments); Spear phishing (targeted phishing — personalised using OSINT); Vishing (voice phishing — phone calls impersonating IT support, banks); Smishing (SMS phishing); Pretexting (fabricating a scenario to extract information — e.g., impersonating a new employee or auditor); Baiting (leaving infected USB drives in parking lots). Defences: security awareness training; simulated phishing campaigns; verification procedures for sensitive requests.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_kp4: Post-exploitation and pivoting: After initial access, attackers typically aim to: establish persistence (create backdoors, add user accounts — survive reboots and password changes); escalate privileges (gain administrator/root access using local exploits); perform lateral movement (use compromised host to attack other systems within the network — pass-the-hash, credential dumping with Mimikatz); exfiltrate data (extract sensitive data — often staged, compressed, encrypted to evade detection). Defenders monitor for: unusual account creation, unexpected outbound connections, large data transfers, credential access events. In pen testing, document every step taken with timestamps and screenshots — this constitutes the evidentiary record.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_task: You are conducting an authorised penetration test against a web application. During reconnaissance, you discover the login page accepts user input directly in a URL parameter. (a) Describe a SQL injection test you would perform and what a successful payload might look like. (b) If successful, what is the maximum impact this vulnerability could have on the target? (c) Write a remediation recommendation for the development team, referencing OWASP guidance.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_tp0: Penetration testing methodology and legal basis
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_tp1: Reconnaissance: passive OSINT and active scanning
learnUni_cybersecurity_f1_tp2: OWASP Top 10 and web application vulnerabilities
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_desc: Security incidents — breaches, ransomware attacks, data theft, insider threats — are inevitable in modern organisations. Incident response (IR) is the structured process for detecting, containing, and recovering from security incidents while minimising damage. Digital forensics is the application of scientific methods to collect, preserve, and analyse digital evidence for legal proceedings or internal investigations.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_kp0: Incident response lifecycle: NIST SP 800-61 defines four phases. Preparation: develop IR plan, train the team, establish tools and communication channels, conduct tabletop exercises. Detection and analysis: identify that an incident has occurred (SIEM alerts, IDS/IPS, user reports, threat intelligence feeds); triage to determine severity; preserve evidence; document everything. Containment, eradication, and recovery: short-term containment (isolate affected systems); long-term containment (patch vulnerabilities, implement additional controls); eradication (remove malware, close access); recovery (restore from clean backups, monitor for recurrence). Post-incident activity: lessons-learned review; update IR plan; report to regulators if required (GDPR: report within 72 hours).
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_kp1: Digital forensics principles: The principles of digital forensics ensure that evidence is legally admissible. Chain of custody: document every person who handles evidence, with dates and purposes — any gap can make evidence inadmissible. Write blockers: hardware or software devices that prevent any modification of the original evidence drive during imaging. Forensic image: bit-for-bit copy of storage media — work only from the copy, never the original. Hashing for integrity: hash the original and image with SHA-256 before and after — matching hashes prove the copy is identical and unmodified. Volatility order: evidence from volatile sources (RAM, network connections) must be collected before shutdown — volatile data is lost when power is removed.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_kp2: Digital forensics techniques: Memory forensics: capturing and analysing RAM contents — reveals running processes, network connections, encryption keys, and malware that leaves no disk trace. Tools: Volatility (open source). Disk forensics: analyse file system artefacts — deleted files (often recoverable until overwritten), timestamps (accessed, modified, created), registry hives (Windows), log files, browser history. Network forensics: analyse packet captures (Wireshark) and NetFlow data — reconstruct attacker activity, identify data exfiltration. Mobile forensics: extract data from smartphones — often locked; specialised tools (Cellebrite, Oxygen Forensics). Log analysis: Windows Event Logs, Syslog, web server access logs, authentication logs.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_kp3: SIEM and threat intelligence: SIEM (Security Information and Event Management): centralised platform that collects, correlates, and analyses log data from across the organisation to detect security incidents. Key functions: log aggregation, normalisation, real-time alerting on correlated events, dashboards, compliance reporting. Major SIEM platforms: Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel. Threat intelligence: structured information about current and emerging threats. Threat intelligence feeds (VirusTotal, MISP, STIX/TAXII) provide indicators of compromise (IoCs — IPs, domains, file hashes associated with known malware). MITRE ATT&CK framework: knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) — used for threat modelling, detection engineering, and purple teaming.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_kp4: Legal and regulatory framework: Key cybersecurity regulations and laws. GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, EU 2018): personal data protection; breach notification within 72 hours; right to erasure; fines up to 4% of global annual turnover. UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018: post-Brexit UK equivalent. Computer Misuse Act 1990 (UK): unauthorised access to computer systems is a criminal offence — the legal basis for prosecuting hacking. NIS Directive (EU)/NIS Regulations (UK): cybersecurity requirements for operators of essential services (energy, water, transport, healthcare) and digital service providers. Cyber Essentials (UK): government-backed certification scheme for basic cyber hygiene. PCI-DSS: security standards for organisations handling payment card data.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_task: A hospital suffers a ransomware attack on a Monday morning. Clinical systems are offline; patient records are encrypted. (a) Describe the first four actions the incident response team should take in the first hour. (b) The attackers have threatened to publish patient data — what additional incident response consideration does this create beyond the ransomware encryption itself? (c) What GDPR obligations does the hospital have, and within what timeframe? (d) The hospital has clean backups from the previous Friday. Describe the recovery process steps.
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_tp1: Digital forensics: chain of custody and imaging
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_tp2: Memory, disk, and network forensics techniques
learnUni_cybersecurity_f2_tp4: Legal framework: GDPR, Computer Misuse Act, NIS
learnUni_social_work_f0_desc: Social work is a practice-based profession and academic discipline that promotes social change, development, and the empowerment of people and communities. It operates at the intersection of people and their social environments, addressing the structural causes of disadvantage alongside individual need. Understanding the theoretical foundations and ethical values of social work is essential for effective, rights-based practice.
learnUni_social_work_f0_kp0: Defining social work: The International Federation of Social Workers (IFSW) definition (2014): Social work is a practice-based profession and an academic discipline that promotes social change and development, social cohesion, and the empowerment and liberation of people. Principles of social justice, human rights, collective responsibility, and respect for diversities are central to social work. Social workers operate in: child protection and family support services; mental health; substance misuse; services for older adults; criminal justice; housing and homelessness; schools and healthcare settings; refugee and asylum support. Social work occupies a dual mandate: care and control — both supporting individuals and sometimes exercising statutory authority (e.g., child protection removal).
learnUni_social_work_f0_kp1: Core social work theories: Systems theory (Bronfenbrenner): individuals are embedded in multiple overlapping systems (microsystem: family and immediate environment; mesosystem: relationships between microsystems; exosystem: broader social structures; macrosystem: cultural context). Social workers must assess all these levels. Psychosocial theory (Erikson): eight stages of psychosocial development — each stage presents a crisis to be resolved (e.g., trust vs mistrust in infancy; identity vs role confusion in adolescence). Social learning theory (Bandura): behaviour is learned through observation and modelling — applied in parenting programmes and offending behaviour work.
learnUni_social_work_f0_kp2: Values and ethics in social work: BASW (British Association of Social Workers) Code of Ethics identifies four key values: Human dignity and worth: respect for the inherent value of every person, regardless of circumstances. Social justice: challenge discrimination; promote fair access to resources; work towards structural change. Service: the primary obligation is to people who use services, not to the employing organisation. Integrity: honesty, reliability, and transparency in all professional relationships. Key ethical tensions in practice: confidentiality vs public safety; service user autonomy vs professional duty of care; organisational pressure vs professional independence. The concept of anti-oppressive practice (AOP): challenging power imbalances at individual, institutional, and structural levels.
learnUni_social_work_f0_kp3: Person-centred and strengths-based approaches: Person-centred practice: developed from Carl Rogers's humanistic psychology. Core conditions: unconditional positive regard (accepting the person without judgement), empathy (understanding the world from the person's perspective), and congruence (being authentic). Strengths-based practice: focuses on individuals' capacities, resources, and resilience rather than deficits and problems. Asset-based community development (ABCD): maps and mobilises community strengths rather than cataloguing needs. Solution-focused brief therapy (SFBT): asks "What is working?" and "What would a slightly better future look like?" rather than dwelling on problems. Evidence: strengths-based approaches are associated with improved engagement and outcomes particularly in mental health and substance misuse.
learnUni_social_work_f0_kp4: Structural social work and anti-oppressive practice: Structural social work (Mullaly): locates the causes of personal troubles in social structures — poverty, racism, sexism, ableism — rather than individual pathology. Practice implication: address structural causes, not just individual symptoms. Intersectionality (Crenshaw): multiple social identities (race, class, gender, disability, sexuality) intersect to create overlapping systems of discrimination and privilege. Anti-oppressive practice: practitioners must examine their own privilege and structural position; challenge discriminatory organisational practices; advocate for structural change; amplify marginalised voices. Critical reflection: ongoing questioning of one's own assumptions, values, and power — essential to anti-oppressive practice.
learnUni_social_work_f0_task: A single parent with two children under 10 is referred to your service following a school report of the children arriving hungry and poorly clothed. The parent is a recent refugee with limited English, working night shifts, and caring for an elderly parent who has recently moved in. Using systems theory and a strengths-based approach, (a) map the key systems affecting this family; (b) identify at least three strengths and protective factors; (c) outline the first assessment steps you would take, referencing anti-oppressive practice principles.
learnUni_social_work_f0_tp0: Defining social work: mandate and settings
learnUni_social_work_f0_tp2: Values and ethics: BASW Code of Ethics
learnUni_social_work_f0_tp5: Social work research and evidence-based practice
learnUni_social_work_f1_desc: Child protection is one of the most demanding and consequential areas of social work practice. Social workers must balance supporting families to care for their children safely with protecting children from significant harm — and sometimes exercising statutory powers to remove children from dangerous situations. This module examines the legal framework, assessment tools, and practice approaches for working with children and families.
learnUni_social_work_f1_kp0: The legal framework for child protection (England): Children Act 1989: the cornerstone of child care law. Key principles: the welfare of the child is the paramount consideration; children are best looked after within their families with support if necessary; courts should not make orders unless they will actively benefit the child. Section 17: duty to assess children in need. Section 47: duty to investigate when a child is suspected of suffering or being likely to suffer significant harm. Children Act 2004: created the Every Child Matters framework; established Local Safeguarding Children Boards (now Multi-Agency Safeguarding Hubs — MASHs). Working Together to Safeguard Children (statutory guidance): sets out how agencies must cooperate.
learnUni_social_work_f1_kp1: Definitions and categories of abuse: Child abuse categories (statutory definitions, Working Together): Physical abuse: deliberately causing physical harm — hitting, shaking, burning, poisoning, suffocation. Sexual abuse: forcing or enticing a child to participate in sexual activities — contact and non-contact (grooming, exposure to sexual imagery). Emotional abuse: persistent emotional maltreatment affecting the child's emotional development — threats, humiliation, witnessing domestic abuse. Neglect: persistent failure to meet the child's basic physical, emotional, medical, or educational needs — the most common category. Risk factors (neither necessary nor sufficient): parental mental illness, domestic violence, substance misuse, learning disability, parental history of being abused, poverty, social isolation.
learnUni_social_work_f1_kp2: Assessment frameworks: Framework for the Assessment of Children in Need and their Families (2000): the "assessment triangle." Three domains: child's developmental needs (health, education, emotional development, identity, family and social relationships, social presentation, self-care skills); parenting capacity (basic care, ensuring safety, emotional warmth, stimulation, guidance, stability); family and environmental factors (family history, housing, employment, social integration). Strengths and limitations: comprehensive; can be over-bureaucratic. Signs of Safety (Turnell and Edwards): safety-focused approach asking: What are we worried about? What is working well? What needs to happen? Widely adopted internationally.
learnUni_social_work_f1_kp3: Child protection processes: Child protection process: Referral (any person can make a referral to children's services) → Initial assessment (within 1 working day response for urgent concerns) → Section 47 investigation if significant harm is suspected → Initial Child Protection Conference (ICPC) → Child Protection Plan (CPP) → Review conferences → Possible care proceedings if child cannot be protected within the family. Thresholds: significant harm (Section 47 triggers); children in need (Section 17). Care proceedings (Public Law Outline): court may make: no order, supervision order, care order (local authority shares parental responsibility), placement order (enables adoption). Key principle: the least interventionist order consistent with child welfare.
learnUni_social_work_f1_kp4: Serious case reviews and learning from failures: Serious Case Reviews (SCRs), now called Child Safeguarding Practice Reviews (CSPRs), examine cases where a child has died or been seriously harmed and abuse is suspected, to identify lessons for the system. Landmark cases: Victoria Climbie (2000): died of abuse despite multiple agency contacts; Laming Inquiry led to Every Child Matters and Children Act 2004. Baby Peter Connelly (2007): died aged 17 months despite being on a child protection plan; highlighted multi-agency communication failures; led to major reforms. Common themes from reviews: failure to see the child (agencies focus on the parent); optimism bias; poor information sharing between agencies; high caseloads; failure of supervision.
learnUni_social_work_f1_task: A neighbour has made a referral to children's services reporting concerns about a 4-year-old child. She has observed the child alone in the garden at night on multiple occasions, the child appears hungry when she speaks to them, and the parents seem to have irregular routines. (a) Using the Framework for the Assessment of Children in Need and their Families, identify what information you would need to gather across each of the three assessment domains. (b) Describe the steps you would take in the first 24 hours following receipt of this referral. (c) Identify three potential indicators of neglect and explain how you would approach the initial visit with the family.
learnUni_social_work_f1_tp1: Definitions and categories of child abuse
learnUni_social_work_f1_tp2: Assessment frameworks: Signs of Safety and the triangle
learnUni_social_work_f1_tp3: Child protection processes and care proceedings
learnUni_social_work_f1_tp5: Working with domestic abuse and its impact on children
learnUni_social_work_f2_name: Mental Health and Adult Social Care
learnUni_social_work_f2_desc: Social work in adult services encompasses work with older adults, people with physical and learning disabilities, individuals with mental health conditions, and those with substance use disorders. It involves needs assessment, care planning, crisis intervention, and advocacy. Understanding the legal frameworks governing adult social care and mental health detention is essential for safe and lawful practice.
learnUni_social_work_f2_kp0: Legal framework for adult social care: Care Act 2014 (England): the principal legislation for adult social care. Key provisions: wellbeing principle (local authorities must promote wellbeing in their decisions); duty to assess anyone who appears to need care and support; eligibility threshold: "significant impact on wellbeing" across at least two areas of daily living; carers have an independent right to assessment and support; duty to prevent, reduce, and delay needs through early intervention; safeguarding adults from abuse and neglect (Section 42 enquiries). Mental Capacity Act 2005: presumption of capacity; two-stage test (impairment of mind or brain + inability to make a specific decision at that time); best interests decision-making; Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS), replaced by Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS).
learnUni_social_work_f2_kp1: Mental Health Act 1983 (amended 2007): The MHA provides powers to detain and treat people with mental disorder without their consent when required for their own health or safety or the protection of others. Key sections: Section 2: assessment detention up to 28 days. Section 3: treatment detention up to 6 months (renewable). Section 4: emergency admission (72 hours; rare). Section 136: police power to remove from public place to place of safety (up to 24 hours). Approved Mental Health Professional (AMHP): usually a social worker; the statutory role in assessing for detention — must determine whether detention is the least restrictive appropriate option. Second Opinion Appointed Doctor (SOAD): independent review of treatment for patients detained long-term.
learnUni_social_work_f2_kp2: Recovery model in mental health: The recovery model represents a shift from the medical model (focus on symptom reduction and cure) to a person-centred model of living a meaningful and satisfying life despite the presence of mental health difficulties. Core principles: hope (recovery is possible); agency (the individual drives their own recovery); self-determination (choice and control); connectedness (relationships and community); identity beyond the patient role. CHIME framework of recovery: Connectedness, Hope and optimism, Identity, Meaning and purpose, Empowerment. Peer support workers: people with lived experience of mental health difficulties employed to support others in their recovery — evidence-based and reducing stigma within services.
learnUni_social_work_f2_kp3: Safeguarding adults: Safeguarding adults encompasses protecting adults who have care and support needs from abuse and neglect. Categories of abuse: physical, sexual, psychological, financial, discriminatory, institutional, neglect and acts of omission, domestic abuse, modern slavery, and self-neglect. Section 42 enquiry (Care Act 2014): triggered when an adult with care and support needs is at risk of abuse or neglect and unable to protect themselves. Safeguarding Adult Boards (SABs): multi-agency bodies (social care, NHS, police) overseeing safeguarding. Safeguarding Adult Reviews (SARs): similar to CSPRs for adults. Key principle: Making Safeguarding Personal — safeguarding interventions should be led by the wishes of the person at risk.
learnUni_social_work_f2_kp4: Working with substance misuse: Substance misuse overlaps significantly with mental health (dual diagnosis), poverty, trauma, and homelessness. Models of addiction: disease model (addiction as a chronic brain disease — requires medical treatment); psychological models (addiction as learned behaviour — cognitive-behavioural approaches); social model (addiction driven by social circumstances — poverty, trauma, social disconnection). Harm reduction philosophy: reducing the harms associated with drug use without requiring abstinence as a precondition of support — needle exchanges, naloxone provision, drug consumption rooms. Motivational Interviewing (MI): evidence-based technique for exploring and resolving ambivalence about change — developed by Miller and Rollnick; avoids confrontation; uses reflective listening, affirmations, open questions, and summarising (OARS).
learnUni_social_work_f2_task: A 78-year-old man living alone has been referred to adult social care by his GP. He has mild dementia, has recently had two falls, and neighbours report he has stopped going out and appears confused when they speak to him. His adult daughter lives locally but says he refuses any help. (a) Outline the initial assessment you would undertake, referencing the Care Act 2014 wellbeing principle. (b) Conduct a two-stage mental capacity assessment for the decision of whether to accept a care package. (c) If he has capacity but continues to refuse support, what are your legal and ethical obligations? (d) Identify two adult safeguarding risks present in this scenario.
learnUni_social_work_f2_tp0: Care Act 2014 and adult social care assessment
learnUni_social_work_f2_tp1: Mental Health Act 1983: detention and AMHPs
learnUni_social_work_f2_tp2: Mental Capacity Act and best interests decisions
learnUni_social_work_f2_tp3: Recovery model and peer support in mental health
learnUni_social_work_f2_tp5: Working with substance misuse and dual diagnosis
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f0_desc: Nutritional science is the study of how food and nutrients affect the body — from cellular metabolism to whole-body health outcomes. Understanding the functions, sources, and recommended intakes of macronutrients and micronutrients is foundational to dietetic practice, public health nutrition, sports nutrition, and clinical nutrition. Evidence-based nutrition is informed by biochemistry, physiology, and epidemiology.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f0_kp0: Macronutrients: Carbohydrates: primary energy source; 4 kcal/g. Types: simple (sugars — glucose, fructose, sucrose) and complex (starches, fibre). Glycaemic index (GI): rate of blood glucose rise after consuming a food — low GI foods (oats, legumes) produce slower, sustained glucose rise. Dietary fibre: insoluble (wheat bran — bulks stools, prevents constipation) and soluble (oats, psyllium — reduces cholesterol, stabilises blood glucose). UK recommendation: 30g fibre/day. Proteins: structural and functional molecules; 4 kcal/g. 20 amino acids, 9 essential (must be obtained from diet). Complete proteins (all essential AAs): animal products, soy, quinoa. UK recommendations: 0.75g protein/kg body weight/day. Fats: 9 kcal/g. Saturated (solid at room temperature — animal fats, coconut oil — raises LDL), unsaturated (mono: olive oil; poly: omega-3 and omega-6 — essential fatty acids). Trans fats: partially hydrogenated vegetable oils — raise LDL, lower HDL — now largely banned in many countries.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f0_kp1: Micronutrients: Vitamins: Vitamin A (retinol): vision, immune function, cell differentiation. Deficiency: night blindness; the leading cause of preventable childhood blindness globally. Fat-soluble (A, D, E, K): stored in fatty tissue; toxicity possible with excess supplementation. Vitamin C (ascorbic acid): antioxidant; collagen synthesis; immune function. Deficiency: scurvy (historical disease of sailors). Water-soluble: excreted in urine; less risk of toxicity; need daily intake. B vitamins: B12 (cobalamin — found only in animal products; deficiency causes megaloblastic anaemia and neurological damage — critical concern for vegans); folate/B9 (DNA synthesis; critical in early pregnancy — prevents neural tube defects). Vitamin D: synthesised in skin on UV-B exposure; regulates calcium absorption; deficiency causes rickets (children) and osteomalacia (adults). Widespread insufficiency in the UK — supplementation recommended Oct-Mar.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f0_kp2: Minerals and water: Key minerals: Calcium: bone and teeth mineralisation; muscle contraction; nerve function. Deficiency: osteoporosis. Sources: dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, tofu. Iron: oxygen transport (haemoglobin); energy metabolism. Types: haem iron (animal products — highly bioavailable) and non-haem iron (plant foods — less bioavailable; enhanced by vitamin C, inhibited by tannins and phytates). Deficiency: iron-deficiency anaemia — the most common nutritional deficiency globally. Zinc: immune function; wound healing; enzyme activity. Iodine: thyroid hormone synthesis; deficiency causes goitre and cretinism. Water: essential for virtually all biological processes; adults need approximately 2-2.5 litres/day from all sources; severe dehydration is life-threatening; even mild dehydration (1-2% body weight) impairs cognitive performance.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f0_kp3: Energy balance and metabolism: Energy intake: from carbohydrate (4 kcal/g), protein (4 kcal/g), fat (9 kcal/g), and alcohol (7 kcal/g). Energy expenditure: Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR): energy required at rest to maintain vital functions — ~60-70% of total energy expenditure. Physical activity: ~15-30% of TEE. Thermic effect of food (TEF): energy cost of digesting food — ~10% of TEE. Mifflin-St Jeor equation for BMR: males: 10W + 6.25H - 5A + 5; females: 10W + 6.25H - 5A - 161 (W=weight in kg, H=height in cm, A=age). Multiply by physical activity level (PAL) for Total Energy Requirement (TER): sedentary (1.2), lightly active (1.375), moderately active (1.55), very active (1.725). Energy balance: weight stability requires energy intake = energy expenditure.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f0_kp4: Dietary reference values: Dietary Reference Values (DRVs) are a set of recommendations for energy and nutrient intake in the UK population (SACN/COMA). Key terms: Reference Nutrient Intake (RNI): the amount sufficient for 97.5% of the population — the figure typically used for planning adequate diets. Estimated Average Requirement (EAR): sufficient for 50% of the population. Lower Reference Nutrient Intake (LRNI): insufficient for all but 2.5% — individuals consuming less are likely to be deficient. Tolerable Upper Limit (UL): the maximum daily intake unlikely to cause adverse health effects. Population Reference Intake (PRI): the EU equivalent of RNI. DRVs vary by age, sex, and physiological state (pregnancy, lactation). Key RNIs: Vitamin C: 40mg/day; Calcium: 700mg/day; Iron: 8.7mg/day (males), 14.8mg/day (females 19-50). Folate: 200mcg/day; 400mcg/day for first 12 weeks of pregnancy.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f0_task: A 25-year-old female vegan athlete (65 kg, 168 cm) asks for dietary advice. She trains for 1.5 hours per day (very active, PAL 1.725). (a) Calculate her Total Energy Requirement using the Mifflin-St Jeor equation. (b) Calculate her recommended protein intake (use 1.4-1.7g/kg for endurance athletes). (c) Identify three micronutrients she is at specific risk of deficiency for (given her vegan diet and female sex), the physiological consequences of deficiency, and one dietary or supplementation strategy for each.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f0_tp4: Dietary reference values: RNI, EAR, UL
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_desc: Diet is a major modifiable risk factor for the majority of the world's leading causes of death — cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, certain cancers, and obesity. This module examines the scientific evidence linking dietary patterns and specific nutrients to disease risk, and the principles of medical nutrition therapy for key clinical conditions.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_kp0: Cardiovascular disease and diet: CVD (heart disease and stroke) is the global leading cause of death. Key dietary risk factors: saturated fat: raises LDL (low-density lipoprotein — carries cholesterol to arteries); trans fats (now largely banned) dramatically increase CVD risk. Protective factors: unsaturated fats (mono and poly — replace SFA to reduce LDL); omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA from oily fish — reduce triglycerides, inflammation, and platelet aggregation); dietary fibre (soluble fibre reduces LDL); fruits and vegetables (antioxidants, potassium); Mediterranean diet pattern: associated with ~30% reduced CVD risk (PREDIMED trial, 2013) — rich in olive oil, vegetables, legumes, nuts, fish, moderate wine. Sodium: excess intake raises blood pressure; UK recommendation: <6g salt/day.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_kp1: Type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome: Type 2 diabetes (T2DM): characterised by insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency. Dietary risk: excess energy intake (particularly refined carbohydrates and saturated fat); obesity (especially abdominal adiposity) is the strongest modifiable risk factor. Dietary management: carbohydrate quality (low GI/GL diet; reduce refined sugars and white starchy carbs); fibre (improves glycaemic control); weight loss (5-10% weight loss significantly improves glycaemic control and may achieve remission in recent-onset T2DM); portion control. Low-carbohydrate diets: strong short-term evidence for glycaemic improvement; long-term sustainability and safety require monitoring in people taking insulin or sulphonylureas (hypoglycaemia risk).
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_kp2: Obesity and weight management: Obesity (BMI >30 kg/m2) affects ~28% of UK adults and is associated with T2DM, CVD, certain cancers (colon, breast, endometrial), sleep apnoea, osteoarthritis, and mental health conditions. Causes: complex — genetic predisposition (~40-70% heritability), obesogenic environment (ultra-processed food availability, reduced physical activity), psychological factors, socioeconomic disadvantage. Weight management approaches: dietary intervention (energy deficit of 500-600 kcal/day produces ~0.5 kg/week weight loss); behavioural change techniques; physical activity (important for weight maintenance; modest impact on weight loss alone); pharmacotherapy (semaglutide/GLP-1 agonists — >15% body weight loss in trials); bariatric surgery (most effective long-term intervention for severe obesity).
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_kp3: Cancer and nutrition: Diet is implicated in approximately 30-40% of cancers. WCRF/AICR Cancer Prevention Recommendations (2018): maintain a healthy body weight (overweight/obesity increases risk of 13 cancer types); eat a diet rich in wholegrains, vegetables, fruit, and legumes; limit consumption of red and processed meat (processed meat: Group 1 carcinogen — IARC; red meat: Group 2A); limit alcohol (increases risk of mouth, pharynx, oesophagus, liver, colon, and breast cancer); avoid sugar-sweetened beverages; do not use supplements for cancer prevention (food rather than supplement sources are recommended). Protective mechanisms of plant foods: dietary fibre, antioxidants (vitamins C, E, carotenoids), phytoestrogens, glucosinolates.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_kp4: Clinical nutrition and nutritional support: Malnutrition (undernutrition) affects ~3 million people in the UK at any one time, mostly due to illness, disability, or poverty. MUST (Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool): five-step validated tool for identifying adults at risk. Nutritional support options: oral nutritional supplements (ONS — high-energy drinks); enteral nutrition (tube feeding — nasogastric or PEG tube); parenteral nutrition (IV delivery of nutrients directly into the bloodstream — used when the GI tract is non-functional). Refeeding syndrome: dangerous electrolyte shifts (particularly hypophosphataemia) occurring when nutrition is reintroduced too rapidly to severely malnourished patients — monitoring of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium is essential in the first 72 hours.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_task: A 55-year-old male (BMI 32.4, waist circumference 103 cm) is referred to dietetics. He has been newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 58 mmol/mol), hypertension, and high LDL cholesterol. He currently eats a diet high in white bread, processed meats, full-fat dairy, and fizzy drinks; rarely eats vegetables; is sedentary; and drinks 4-5 units of alcohol per day. (a) Prioritise and explain three specific dietary changes that would address multiple risk factors simultaneously. (b) Identify whether a low-carbohydrate or Mediterranean-style diet would be most appropriate for this patient and justify your recommendation with evidence. (c) What referrals beyond dietetics would you recommend?
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_tp0: Diet and cardiovascular disease: fats and Mediterranean diet
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_tp1: Type 2 diabetes: dietary management and low-carb approaches
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f1_tp4: Malnutrition: screening, MUST, and nutritional support
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_name: Public Health Nutrition and Dietary Practice
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_desc: Public health nutrition addresses nutrition at the population level — from national dietary guidelines and food policy to health promotion interventions and the commercial determinants of diet. Dietetic practice involves applying nutritional science to individuals and communities, using behaviour change techniques, therapeutic diets, and evidence-based communication. This module covers dietary assessment methods, behaviour change models, and the public health food environment.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_kp0: Dietary assessment methods: Dietary assessment is fundamental to nutrition research and clinical practice, but all methods have significant limitations. 24-hour dietary recall: trained interviewer asks the participant to recall all foods and drinks consumed in the previous 24 hours. Pros: low respondent burden; can be used with low-literacy populations. Cons: single-day recall may not represent habitual intake; relies on memory; underreporting is common (especially for high-calorie foods). Food frequency questionnaire (FFQ): asks how often specific foods are consumed over a defined period. Pros: captures habitual intake; low cost; self-administered. Cons: requires literacy and memory; crude quantification. Weighed food record (food diary): most accurate for quantification but highest respondent burden. Dietary analysis software: NDNS nutrient analysis, Nutritics, Dietplan7 — convert food records to nutrient intakes.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_kp1: Behaviour change models in dietetic practice: Transtheoretical Model (Stages of Change, Prochaska and DiClemente): Precontemplation (not considering change), Contemplation (considering change in the next 6 months), Preparation (planning to change in the next month), Action (actively making change), Maintenance (sustaining change >6 months), Relapse. Dietetic application: match intervention to stage — do not give action-stage advice to someone in precontemplation. Health Belief Model: perceived susceptibility + severity + benefits of action - barriers = likelihood of behaviour change. Social Cognitive Theory (Bandura): self-efficacy (belief in one's ability to perform a behaviour) is the strongest predictor of behaviour change. Building self-efficacy: mastery experiences, vicarious learning, verbal encouragement.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_kp2: UK dietary guidelines and food policy: Eatwell Guide (PHE/OHID): the UK visual dietary guidance tool. Five food groups and proportions: fruit and vegetables (at least 5-a-day, >35% of plate); starchy carbohydrates (choosing wholegrain, ~35% of plate); dairy and alternatives (20%); proteins — beans, fish, eggs, meat (20%); oils and spreads (small amounts of unsaturated). Free sugars: <5% of energy (<30g/day adults). Saturated fat: <10% of energy. Salt: <6g/day. Oily fish: 2 portions per week. Fruit and vegetables: 5 portions (80g each) daily (a portion does NOT include juice beyond 150ml/day, or more than one 30g portion of dried fruit). Government food policies: sugar levy (Soft Drinks Industry Levy 2018 — reduced sugar in soft drinks); calorie labelling; voluntary salt reduction targets.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_kp3: Food insecurity and food environments: Food insecurity: lack of consistent access to enough food for an active, healthy life. Affected 8.4 million adults in the UK (Food Foundation, 2022). Causes: low income, zero-hours contracts, benefit delays, high food costs. Food deserts: areas with poor physical access to affordable healthy food (predominantly in deprived urban areas and rural locations). Food swamps: areas saturated with fast food outlets and convenience stores. Structural drivers of poor diet: ultra-processed food (UPF) is heavily marketed and increasingly affordable relative to fresh produce. NOVA classification (Monteiro): classifies foods by degree of processing — UPF (Group 4) consumption associated with obesity, T2DM, CVD, and all-cause mortality in prospective studies. Policy debate: should UPF be regulated? Marketing restrictions on high fat, sugar, salt (HFSS) foods — UK advertising restrictions post-9pm on TV and on online.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_kp4: Sustainable and plant-based diets: Planetary health: food systems account for ~26% of global greenhouse gas emissions. Animal agriculture is the dominant driver (beef and lamb have ~20-100x the emissions footprint of plant-based proteins). EAT-Lancet Commission (2019): identified a Planetary Health Diet — predominantly plant-based, with small amounts of animal products — as capable of nourishing 10 billion people within planetary boundaries by 2050. Plant-based diets: well-planned vegetarian and vegan diets can be nutritionally adequate at all life stages (British Dietetic Association). Key nutrients to monitor on vegan diet: B12 (must supplement), vitamin D, iodine (not in plant milks unless fortified), calcium, omega-3 (algal oil), zinc, iron. Benefits: lower environmental impact; associated with lower T2DM, CVD, and certain cancer risks.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_task: You are a community dietitian working in a deprived inner-city area with high rates of food insecurity and obesity. You have been asked to design a 6-week group nutrition intervention for 15 adults with T2DM who are managing their diet on a limited budget. (a) Select and justify a dietary assessment method appropriate for this group. (b) Identify one behaviour change model that would best structure your intervention and explain how you would apply it. (c) Design two specific session topics that are evidence-based, budget-sensitive, and appropriate for this group. (d) Identify one structural or policy change that would more effectively address the nutritional challenges this community faces than individual-level interventions.
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_tp0: Dietary assessment methods: recalls, FFQs, diaries
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_tp1: Behaviour change models: stages of change, SCT
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_tp2: UK dietary guidelines and the Eatwell Guide
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_tp3: Food insecurity, food environments, and UPF
learnUni_nutrition_dietetics_f2_tp5: Nutrition across the life course: pregnancy to older age
learnSch_physics_f3_desc: Thermodynamics studies heat, temperature, and the conversion of energy into work. The three main laws govern every heat engine, refrigerator, and chemical reaction on Earth. Understanding thermodynamics explains why engines cannot be 100 % efficient and why ice melts when touched.
learnSch_physics_f3_kp0: Temperature measures the average kinetic energy of particles; it is measured in Kelvin (K) or Celsius (Â°C).
learnSch_physics_f3_kp1: 1st Law of Thermodynamics: energy is conserved â€” heat added to a system equals the change in internal energy plus work done (Î”U = Q âˆ’ W).
learnSch_physics_f3_kp2: 2nd Law: heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold, never the reverse. Entropy (disorder) always increases in an isolated system.
learnSch_physics_f3_kp3: Specific heat capacity (c): Q = mcÎ”T â€” the energy needed to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1 Â°C.
learnSch_physics_f3_kp4: Efficiency of a heat engine: Î· = W/Q_hot = 1 âˆ’ T_cold/T_hot (Carnot efficiency â€” the theoretical maximum).
learnSch_physics_f3_task: A 200 g iron block (c = 0.45 J/gÂ·Â°C) is heated from 20 Â°C to 100 Â°C. How much energy is needed? Then calculate the Carnot efficiency of an engine operating between 500 K and 300 K.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp0_desc: Temperature measures the average kinetic energy of particles in a substance; heat is the transfer of thermal energy between objects at different temperatures. They are related but distinct: a spark has a high temperature but carries little heat because its mass is tiny. Heat flows spontaneously from hotter to cooler objects until thermal equilibrium is reached.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp0_kp0: Temperature scales: Celsius (°C), Fahrenheit (°F), and Kelvin (K); 0 K = absolute zero = −273.15°C (no particle motion).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp0_kp1: Thermal energy transferred: Q = mcΔT (m = mass, c = specific heat capacity, ΔT = temperature change).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp0_kp2: Heat transfer occurs by conduction (solids), convection (fluids), and radiation (electromagnetic waves — requires no medium).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp0_kp3: Thermal equilibrium: when two objects in contact reach the same temperature, net heat transfer ceases (0th Law of Thermodynamics).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp0_kp4: Specific heat capacity c: water has c = 4200 J/kg·K — unusually high, making it excellent for storing thermal energy and moderating climate.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp0_insight: Water's exceptionally high specific heat capacity is why coastal cities have milder climates than inland ones at the same latitude. The ocean acts as a massive heat reservoir, absorbing summer heat and releasing it in winter — moderating temperature swings dramatically.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp1_desc: The four laws of thermodynamics govern all energy processes in the universe. The zeroth law defines temperature; the first law states energy is conserved; the second law says entropy always increases in isolated systems; the third law states absolute zero is unattainable. Together they explain why heat engines have limits and why time appears to flow in one direction.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp1_kp0: 0th Law: if A is in thermal equilibrium with B, and B with C, then A is in equilibrium with C — the basis for thermometers.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp1_kp1: 1st Law: ΔU = Q − W (change in internal energy = heat added − work done by the system); energy is always conserved.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp1_kp2: 2nd Law: entropy (disorder) of an isolated system always increases or stays the same; heat never spontaneously flows from cold to hot.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp1_kp3: 3rd Law: as temperature approaches absolute zero, entropy approaches a minimum; absolute zero itself is unattainable in a finite number of steps.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp1_kp4: The 2nd Law explains the arrow of time: physical processes are irreversible at the macroscale because entropy increases in all spontaneous processes.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp1_insight: The 2nd Law of Thermodynamics is considered by many physicists to be the most profound law in all of science. Richard Feynman said it was the law that would make a time traveller realise they had gone to the past — because you would see entropy decreasing (broken cups reassembling themselves).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp2_desc: A heat engine converts thermal energy into mechanical work by taking heat from a hot reservoir, doing work, and releasing waste heat to a cold reservoir. The Carnot engine sets the theoretical maximum efficiency for any heat engine operating between two temperatures. Real engines are always less efficient due to friction and irreversible processes.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp2_kp0: Efficiency η = useful work output / heat input from hot source = 1 − Q_cold/Q_hot.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp2_kp1: Carnot efficiency η_max = 1 − T_cold/T_hot (temperatures in kelvin); this is the maximum theoretically possible efficiency.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp2_kp2: Steam engines, petrol engines, diesel engines, and jet engines are all heat engines; none can exceed Carnot efficiency.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp2_kp3: A refrigerator is a heat engine run in reverse: it uses work to move heat from cold (inside) to hot (outside the fridge).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp2_kp4: Increasing efficiency requires either raising the hot reservoir temperature or lowering the cold reservoir temperature (or both).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp2_insight: A modern coal power station operates at about 33–40% efficiency — meaning over 60% of the energy in the coal is dumped as waste heat into rivers or cooling towers. Combined-cycle gas turbines can reach 60–63% efficiency by using exhaust gases to run a second generator.
learnSch_physics_f4_desc: Nuclear physics explores the structure of atomic nuclei and the forces that hold them together. Unstable nuclei release energy as radiation to reach a more stable state. Nuclear reactions power the Sun, nuclear plants, and explain the origin of elements in stars.
learnSch_physics_f4_kp0: The nucleus contains protons (positive) and neutrons (neutral), held together by the strong nuclear force â€” far stronger than electrostatic repulsion.
learnSch_physics_f4_kp1: Alpha (Î±) decay emits a helium nucleus (2 protons + 2 neutrons). Beta (Î²) decay emits an electron or positron. Gamma (Î³) decay emits high-energy electromagnetic radiation.
learnSch_physics_f4_kp2: Half-life: the time for half of a radioactive sample to decay. After n half-lives, the remaining fraction is (Â½)â¿.
learnSch_physics_f4_kp3: Massâ€“energy equivalence: E = mcÂ² â€” a tiny mass converts to enormous energy (c = 3Ã—10â¸ m/s).
learnSch_physics_f4_kp4: Fission splits a heavy nucleus (e.g. Â²Â³âµU) releasing energy; fusion joins light nuclei (e.g. Â²H + Â³H â†’ â´He) releasing even more energy per unit mass.
learnSch_physics_f4_task: A radioactive isotope has a half-life of 5 years. If you start with 80 g, how much remains after 15 years? Write each step. Then use E = mcÂ² to find the energy released if 1 g of mass is converted entirely to energy.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp0_desc: Radioactive decay occurs when an unstable atomic nucleus emits radiation to reach a more stable state. There are three main types: alpha (α), beta (β), and gamma (γ). Each has different properties in terms of penetration, ionisation power, and effect on the nucleus. Decay is a random process but follows statistical laws.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp0_kp0: Alpha (α) decay: emits a helium nucleus (²₄He); highly ionising, low penetration (stopped by paper or a few cm of air); nucleus loses 2 protons and 2 neutrons.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp0_kp1: Beta-minus (β⁻) decay: a neutron converts to a proton, emitting an electron and antineutrino; moderately ionising, stopped by a few mm of aluminium.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp0_kp2: Gamma (γ) radiation: high-energy electromagnetic photons emitted after alpha/beta decay; minimally ionising, requires several cm of lead or metres of concrete to attenuate significantly.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp0_kp3: Radioactive decay is random: no individual nucleus can be predicted to decay, but large samples follow exponential decay statistics precisely.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp0_kp4: Background radiation is always present (from cosmic rays, radon gas, rocks, food, and medical procedures) and must be subtracted in experiments.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp0_insight: Marie Curie discovered the elements polonium and radium in 1898 — becoming the first person to win two Nobel Prizes. Tragically, she did not know about radiation's danger to health; her personal notebooks are still so radioactive that they must be stored in lead-lined boxes and researchers must sign a waiver before handling them.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp1_desc: The half-life of a radioactive isotope is the time for half of a given sample to decay. It is constant for a given isotope and can range from microseconds to billions of years. Half-life is used in radiocarbon dating, medical imaging, nuclear waste management, and determining the age of rocks.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp1_kp0: After n half-lives, the remaining amount is N = N_0 × (1/2)^n (e.g. after 3 half-lives: 1/8 of the original remains).
learnSch_physics_f4_tp1_kp1: Exponential decay: N(t) = N_0 e^(−λt), where λ = decay constant = ln(2)/t_{1/2}.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp1_kp2: Activity A = λN (decays per second, measured in becquerels Bq); activity halves every half-life.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp1_kp3: Carbon-14 (t_{1/2} = 5730 years) is used to date organic material up to about 50,000 years old by measuring the remaining ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp1_kp4: Uranium-238 (t_{1/2} = 4.5 billion years) decays to lead-206; by measuring the ratio, geologists can date rocks billions of years old.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp1_insight: Radiocarbon dating was developed by Willard Libby in 1949, who later won the Nobel Prize for it. The technique has revolutionised archaeology — confirming, for example, that the Turin Shroud dates to 1260–1390 AD, not the time of Jesus.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp2_desc: Nuclear fission splits a heavy nucleus into lighter fragments, releasing enormous energy. Nuclear fusion joins light nuclei to form a heavier one, releasing even more energy per unit mass. Both processes release energy because products are more stable (have lower binding energy per nucleon) than reactants. Fission powers nuclear plants; fusion powers the Sun.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp2_kp0: Nuclear fission: ²³⁵U + n → fission fragments + 2-3 neutrons + energy (∼200 MeV per fission event).
learnSch_physics_f4_tp2_kp1: Chain reaction: neutrons from fission trigger more fissions; in a reactor, control rods absorb excess neutrons to maintain a steady controlled chain reaction.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp2_kp2: Nuclear fusion: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; releases 3–4 times more energy per kg than fission.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp2_kp3: Fusion requires temperatures of 100 million°C to overcome electrostatic repulsion (Coulomb barrier) between nuclei — achieved in the Sun by gravitational pressure.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp2_kp4: E = mc²: the mass defect (difference between reactant and product masses) is converted to energy; even tiny mass differences release enormous energy.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp2_insight: Fusion releases about 4 million times more energy per kg than coal and leaves no long-lived radioactive waste. Scientists have been working on controlled fusion since the 1950s — and ITER in France, the world's largest experimental fusion reactor, aims to produce 10 times more power than it consumes by the 2030s.
learnSch_physics_f5_desc: Quantum physics describes the behaviour of matter and energy at the atomic and subatomic scale. At this level, particles behave like waves, energy comes in discrete packets (quanta), and the act of measurement affects what is observed. Quantum physics underlies lasers, semiconductors, and MRI scanners.
learnSch_physics_f5_kp0: Planck's relation: energy of a photon is E = hf, where h = 6.63Ã—10â»Â³â´ JÂ·s (Planck's constant) and f is frequency.
learnSch_physics_f5_kp1: Photoelectric effect: light ejects electrons from metal surfaces â€” proves light behaves as particles (photons). Einstein won the Nobel Prize for this explanation.
learnSch_physics_f5_kp2: Waveâ€“particle duality: electrons (and all particles) behave as both waves and particles; de Broglie wavelength Î» = h/(mv).
learnSch_physics_f5_kp3: Heisenberg uncertainty principle: Î”x Â· Î”p â‰¥ â„/2 â€” you cannot know both position and momentum of a particle precisely at the same time.
learnSch_physics_f5_kp4: Electrons occupy discrete energy levels in atoms; when an electron drops to a lower level, it emits a photon with energy equal to the difference.
learnSch_physics_f5_task: A photon has frequency 6.0Ã—10Â¹â´ Hz. Calculate its energy (h = 6.63Ã—10â»Â³â´ JÂ·s). Then find the wavelength of an electron moving at 2.0Ã—10â¶ m/s (mass = 9.11Ã—10â»Â³Â¹ kg).
learnSch_physics_f5_tp0_desc: The photoelectric effect is the emission of electrons from a metal surface when light above a threshold frequency shines on it. Classical wave theory could not explain why frequency (not intensity) determines whether electrons are emitted. Einstein explained it in 1905 by proposing that light comes in discrete packets called photons, each with energy E = hf.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp0_kp0: Photon energy E = hf = hc/λ, where h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant).
learnSch_physics_f5_tp0_kp1: Photoelectric effect: electrons are emitted only if f ≥ f_threshold; higher intensity just releases more electrons, not more energetic ones.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp0_kp2: Work function ϕ: the minimum energy needed to release an electron from the metal surface; KE_max = hf − ϕ.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp0_kp3: Stopping voltage: the voltage needed to stop the most energetic emitted electrons; eV_s = KE_max = hf − ϕ.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp0_kp4: This effect proves light behaves as particles (photons); for this explanation, Einstein received the Nobel Prize in 1921 (not for relativity).
learnSch_physics_f5_tp0_insight: Einstein won his Nobel Prize for explaining the photoelectric effect, not for relativity — which was considered too speculative by the prize committee at the time. Solar panels and digital cameras both rely on the photoelectric effect to convert light into electricity.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp1_desc: Wave-particle duality is the concept that all matter and electromagnetic radiation exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties depending on how they are observed. Electrons, protons, even atoms, produce interference patterns when passed through double slits — wave behaviour. But they are detected as individual point-like particles. This is one of the deepest mysteries in physics.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp1_kp0: de Broglie wavelength: λ = h/p = h/(mv); every moving particle has an associated wavelength inversely proportional to its momentum.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp1_kp1: Electron diffraction: electrons passing through a crystal lattice produce diffraction patterns — direct evidence of wave-like behaviour.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp1_kp2: Heisenberg uncertainty principle: Δx · Δp ≥ ℏ/2; position and momentum cannot both be precisely known simultaneously.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp1_kp3: The double-slit experiment with single electrons: each electron goes through both slits simultaneously as a wave but is detected as a particle at one point.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp1_kp4: Everyday objects have vanishingly small de Broglie wavelengths (e.g. a 1 kg ball at 1 m/s: λ ≈ 6.6 × 10⁻³⁴ m) — too tiny to observe quantum effects.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp1_insight: In 1999, a group in Vienna demonstrated wave interference using C₆₀ fullerene molecules (each containing 60 carbon atoms and weighing 720 atomic mass units) — the largest objects ever shown to exhibit quantum wave behaviour at the time. Quantum mechanics applies even to fairly large molecules.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp2_desc: Electrons in atoms occupy discrete energy levels, not a continuous range. When an electron transitions between levels, it absorbs or emits a photon whose energy exactly equals the difference between the levels. This explains atomic spectra — the unique set of spectral lines that identifies each element like a fingerprint.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp2_kp0: Electrons occupy quantised energy levels (n = 1, 2, 3...); ground state is the lowest energy level (n = 1) for hydrogen.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp2_kp1: Photon emitted during downward transition: E_photon = E_upper − E_lower = hf; absorbed during upward transition.
