learnSch_physics_f5_tp2_kp2: Emission spectrum: bright lines on a dark background; absorption spectrum: dark lines on a bright background — both are characteristic of the element.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp2_kp3: Hydrogen energy levels: E_n = −13.6 eV/n²; ionisation (n → ∞) requires 13.6 eV from the ground state.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp2_kp4: Lasers work by stimulated emission: an incoming photon triggers an excited atom to emit an identical photon, producing coherent, monochromatic light.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp2_insight: Astronomers can determine the composition, temperature, velocity, and even rotation rate of stars millions of light-years away simply by analysing their spectra. The element helium was discovered in the Sun's spectrum in 1868 — 27 years before it was found on Earth.
learnSch_physics_f6_desc: Einstein's special relativity (1905) revolutionised physics by showing that space and time are not absolute â€” they stretch and compress depending on relative velocity. At speeds close to the speed of light, time slows down, objects shorten, and mass increases. These effects, confirmed by GPS satellites and particle accelerators, change how we understand the universe.
learnSch_physics_f6_kp0: 1st postulate: the laws of physics are identical in all inertial (non-accelerating) reference frames.
learnSch_physics_f6_kp1: 2nd postulate: the speed of light c = 3Ã—10â¸ m/s is constant for all observers, regardless of the motion of the source or observer.
learnSch_physics_f6_kp2: Time dilation: a moving clock runs slower. Î”t' = Î”t / âˆš(1 âˆ’ vÂ²/cÂ²). The faster you move, the slower time passes for you.
learnSch_physics_f6_kp3: Length contraction: a moving object is shorter in the direction of motion. L' = Lâ‚€ âˆš(1 âˆ’ vÂ²/cÂ²).
learnSch_physics_f6_kp4: Massâ€“energy equivalence E = mcÂ²: rest mass is a stored form of energy. This is why nuclear reactions release so much energy.
learnSch_physics_f6_task: A rocket is 100 m long at rest and travels at 0.8c. What is its length as measured by an observer on Earth? Use L' = Lâ‚€âˆš(1 âˆ’ vÂ²/cÂ²). Also, by how many seconds does 1 minute on the rocket differ from 1 minute on Earth?
learnSch_physics_f6_tp0_desc: Time dilation is the slowing of time experienced by a moving object relative to a stationary observer. The faster an object moves, the slower its clocks tick as measured from outside. Time dilation is not an illusion — it has been confirmed by atomic clocks on aircraft and satellites, and is built into GPS system software.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp0_kp0: Time dilation formula: Δt' = Δt / √(1 − v²/c²); Δt' is the dilated (longer) time measured by a stationary observer, Δt is proper time.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp0_kp1: Proper time: the time measured by a clock moving with the object (shortest possible elapsed time between two events).
learnSch_physics_f6_tp0_kp2: The Lorentz factor γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²); as v → c, γ → ∞ and time dilation becomes extreme.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp0_kp3: Twin paradox: one twin travels at near-c and returns younger than the stay-at-home twin — the travelling twin's proper time is genuinely shorter.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp0_kp4: Muon experiment: cosmic-ray muons created 15 km up survive to reach Earth's surface despite a half-life of 2.2 μs — confirmed by time dilation (or length contraction from the muon's frame).
learnSch_physics_f6_tp0_insight: GPS satellites travel at about 14,000 km/h and orbit in weaker gravity. Without corrections for both special relativistic time dilation and general relativistic gravitational time dilation, GPS positioning would drift by about 10 km per day. Einstein's relativity is literally inside every smartphone navigating a route.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp1_desc: Length contraction is the shortening of an object in its direction of motion as measured by a stationary observer. Only the dimension along the direction of motion contracts; dimensions perpendicular to motion are unchanged. Like time dilation, length contraction is a consequence of the constancy of the speed of light and is real and measurable.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp1_kp0: Length contraction formula: L' = L_0 √(1 − v²/c²) = L_0/γ; L_0 is the proper length (measured at rest), L' is the contracted length.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp1_kp1: Proper length: the length of an object as measured in the frame where it is at rest (always the maximum length).
learnSch_physics_f6_tp1_kp2: An object moving at 0.9c has length L' = L_0 × 0.436 (shrinks to 43.6% of rest length in direction of motion).
learnSch_physics_f6_tp1_kp3: Length contraction and time dilation are two sides of the same relativistic coin — both arise from the invariance of spacetime interval s² = c²Δt² − Δx².
learnSch_physics_f6_tp1_kp4: The muon experiment can equivalently be explained by length contraction from the muon's frame: the 15 km atmosphere contracts to ≈ 0.45 km, short enough to traverse in one lifetime.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp1_insight: At the Large Hadron Collider, protons travel at 99.9999991% of c. In the lab frame, the 27 km ring circumference remains 27 km, but in the proton's rest frame the entire accelerator is contracted to just about 0.27 m — a structure the size of a paperback book.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp2_desc: Einstein's most famous equation, E = mc², states that mass and energy are equivalent and interconvertible. The factor c² is enormous, which is why even a tiny mass converts to a huge amount of energy. This principle explains the energy released in nuclear reactions and the source of the Sun's luminosity.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp2_kp0: Rest energy: E_0 = mc² (c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s); 1 kg of mass is equivalent to 9 × 10¹⁶ joules — the energy of 43 megatons of TNT.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp2_kp1: Total relativistic energy: E = γmc²; kinetic energy KE = (γ − 1)mc² (reduces to ½mv² at low speeds).
learnSch_physics_f6_tp2_kp2: In nuclear fission, the mass of products is slightly less than reactants; this mass defect Δm is converted to energy: E = Δmc².
learnSch_physics_f6_tp2_kp3: Pair production: a high-energy photon can spontaneously create a particle-antiparticle pair (e.g. e⁻ + e⁺); pair annihilation reverses this, releasing two gamma photons.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp2_kp4: Relativistic momentum: p = γmv; at v → c, momentum → ∞, which is why massive particles can never reach the speed of light.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp2_insight: The Sun converts about 4 million tonnes of mass to energy every second via nuclear fusion (E = mc²). It has been doing this for 4.6 billion years and has enough hydrogen left for another 5 billion years — even at this extraordinary rate of mass consumption.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_desc: Organic chemistry studies carbon-based compounds. Carbon's unique ability to form four covalent bonds creates an almost infinite variety of molecules â€” the building blocks of life, medicines, fuels, and plastics. Mastering functional groups and reaction types unlocks biochemistry, pharmacology, and materials science.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_kp0: All organic molecules contain carbon, typically bonded to hydrogen (Câ€“H) and often also to O, N, S, or halogens.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_kp1: Hydrocarbons: alkanes (Câ€“C single bonds, saturated), alkenes (C=C double bond, unsaturated), alkynes (Câ‰¡C triple bond).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_kp2: Functional groups determine chemical properties: â€“OH (alcohol), â€“COOH (carboxylic acid), â€“NHâ‚‚ (amine), â€“CHO (aldehyde), â€“C=O (ketone).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_kp3: Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity; stereoisomers have the same connectivity but different spatial arrangement.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_kp4: Polymers are large molecules built from repeating monomer units: addition polymerisation (ethene â†’ polyethene) and condensation polymerisation (amino acids â†’ proteins).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_task: Draw the full structural formula of ethanoic acid (CHâ‚ƒCOOH). Identify the functional group. Write the equation for its reaction with sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and name the products.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp0_desc: Carbon is unique in its ability to form four covalent bonds, including chains, branches, and rings of almost unlimited length. Hydrocarbons contain only carbon and hydrogen. They are the simplest organic compounds and include fuels, solvents, and building blocks for more complex molecules. They are classified as alkanes, alkenes, or alkynes based on bond type.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp0_kp0: Alkanes (C_nH_{2n+2}): single bonds only; saturated; relatively unreactive; undergo combustion and halogenation by free-radical substitution.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp0_kp1: Alkenes (C_nH_{2n}): one C=C double bond; unsaturated; more reactive; undergo addition reactions (hydration, hydrogenation, halogenation, polymerisation).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp0_kp2: Alkynes: one C≡C triple bond (e.g. ethyne C₂H₂); very reactive; used in oxy-acetylene welding torches (burns > 3000°C).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp0_kp3: Arenes (aromatic): benzene ring (C₆H₆) with delocalized π electrons; particularly stable; undergo electrophilic substitution rather than addition.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp0_kp4: Crude oil is a mixture of hydrocarbons separated by fractional distillation (shorter chains = lower boiling point = more volatile).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp0_insight: Carbon is the fourth most abundant element in the universe and the basis of all known life. There are over 10 million known carbon compounds — more than the total compounds of all other elements combined. Silicon is directly below carbon in the periodic table and has similar bonding, but silicon-based 'life' would face enormous difficulties because silicon-oxygen bonds are very stable, making CO₂-equivalent polymers impossibly inert.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp1_desc: Functional groups are specific groups of atoms within organic molecules that determine their chemical reactivity. By learning functional groups, you can predict how a molecule will react in many different conditions. Key reactions include nucleophilic substitution, elimination, addition, and oxidation. The same reaction type applies to all molecules sharing a functional group.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp1_kp0: Alcohol (–OH): reacts with carboxylic acids (esterification), oxidised to aldehyde or ketone; dehydrated to alkene; e.g. ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp1_kp1: Halogenoalkanes (C–X): undergo nucleophilic substitution (Nu attacks, X leaves) and elimination; reactivity order: C–I > C–Br > C–Cl > C–F.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp1_kp2: Carbonyl group (C=O): aldehydes (–CHO at chain end) and ketones (C=O in chain); reduced to alcohols; aldehydes oxidised to carboxylic acids.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp1_kp3: Carboxylic acids (–COOH): react with alcohols (esterification + H₂SO₄ catalyst), bases (neutralisation), and metals (releasing H₂).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp1_kp4: Amines (–NH₂): organic bases; react with acids to form ammonium salts; condensation with carboxylic acids forms amides (found in proteins).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp1_insight: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) was synthesised in 1897 by Felix Hoffmann at Bayer by esterifying salicylic acid with acetic anhydride — a standard organic functional group reaction. It went on to become the world's most widely used drug, with about 40,000 tonnes consumed annually. The synthesis reaction is still used in school chemistry labs today.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp2_desc: Polymers are large molecules made of many repeating monomer units joined by covalent bonds. Addition polymers form from alkenes; condensation polymers form with loss of small molecules (usually water). Biomolecules — carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids — are also polymers, whose monomers carry out all life processes.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp2_kp0: Addition polymerisation: alkene monomers (e.g. ethene → poly(ethene)); no small molecule released; polymer chains grow by breaking C=C double bonds.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp2_kp1: Condensation polymerisation: monomers join with loss of H₂O (or HCl); produces polyesters (–COOH + –OH), polyamides (–COOH + –NH₂), and biological polymers.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp2_kp2: Proteins: condensation polymers of amino acids joined by peptide bonds (–CO–NH–); primary structure = amino acid sequence; folding gives 3D shape and function.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp2_kp3: DNA: condensation polymer of nucleotides (base + sugar + phosphate); complementary base pairing (A–T, C–G) holds double helix together.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp2_kp4: Biodegradable polymers (e.g. polylactic acid, PLA) are broken down by microorganisms; conventional plastic polymers persist for hundreds of years.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp2_insight: Nylon was the first fully synthetic polymer, created by DuPont chemist Wallace Carothers in 1935. When first demonstrated in 1938, women queued around the block to buy nylon stockings — previously only silk, which required importing from Asia, was used. The nylon synthesis (a condensation polymerisation of a diamine and a diacid) is still demonstrated in school labs as the 'nylon rope trick'.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_desc: Electrochemistry explores the relationship between chemical reactions and electrical energy. Batteries convert chemical energy to electricity; electrolysis does the reverse. Redox reactions â€” electron transfers between species â€” are at the heart of every electrochemical process, from rusting iron to charging your phone.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_kp0: Oxidation = loss of electrons (OIL); Reduction = gain of electrons (RIG). Every redox reaction has both processes simultaneously.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_kp1: In a galvanic (voltaic) cell, a spontaneous redox reaction drives an electric current. The anode is negative (oxidation); the cathode is positive (reduction).
learnSch_chemistry_f4_kp2: Electrolysis uses external electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous reaction â€” used to plate metals, produce aluminium, and split water into Hâ‚‚ and Oâ‚‚.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_kp3: Standard electrode potential (EÂ°): higher EÂ° means stronger oxidising agent. Cell voltage = EÂ°cathode âˆ’ EÂ°anode.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_kp4: Faraday's laws: the mass of substance deposited in electrolysis is proportional to the charge passed (Q = It) and to the molar mass.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_task: In the electrolysis of dilute sulfuric acid, write the half-equations at each electrode. Identify the anode and cathode products. If a current of 2 A flows for 500 s, how many moles of electrons are transferred? (F = 96 500 C/mol)
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp0_desc: Redox reactions involve the simultaneous transfer of electrons between chemical species. Oxidation is the loss of electrons and reduction is the gain. Every redox reaction can be split into two half-equations: one for oxidation and one for reduction. Oxidation states (numbers) are a bookkeeping tool to track electron transfer.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp0_kp0: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain. The reducing agent is oxidised (gives electrons); the oxidising agent is reduced (gains electrons).
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp0_kp1: Oxidation state rules: O is usually −2; H is +1 (except in metal hydrides); sum of oxidation states in a neutral compound = 0.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp0_kp2: Disproportionation: one element is simultaneously oxidised and reduced in the same reaction; e.g. Cl₂ + NaOH → NaCl + NaOCl + H₂O.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp0_kp3: To balance redox equations: write half-equations, balance atoms, balance charges with electrons, then combine so electrons cancel.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp0_kp4: Common oxidising agents: KMnO₄ (turns purple → colourless in acid), K₂Cr₂O₇ (orange → green), F₂, O₂, Cl₂, H₂O₂.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp0_insight: Rusting of iron costs the global economy over $2.5 trillion per year — about 3% of global GDP. It is a redox process: iron is oxidised (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻) and oxygen is reduced (O₂ + 4e⁻ → 2O²⁻). Galvanising steel with zinc exploits electrochemical principles: zinc acts as a sacrificial anode because it has a more negative electrode potential than iron.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp1_desc: A galvanic (voltaic) cell converts chemical energy from a spontaneous redox reaction into electrical energy. Two half-cells are connected by a wire and a salt bridge. The anode (negative) undergoes oxidation; the cathode (positive) undergoes reduction. Modern batteries are stacks of galvanic cells engineered for high energy density and rechargeability.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp1_kp0: Standard hydrogen electrode (SHE): defined as 0.00 V; all other electrode potentials measured relative to it. Positive E° = good oxidising agent; negative E° = good reducing agent.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp1_kp1: Cell EMF = E°cathode − E°anode. A positive cell EMF means the reaction is spontaneous (ΔG = −nFE).
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp1_kp2: Salt bridge: allows ion flow to maintain electrical neutrality in each half-cell without mixing the solutions; typically saturated KNO₃.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp1_kp3: Lithium-ion batteries (Li-ion): lithium ions move from anode (graphite) through electrolyte to cathode (LiCoO₂) on discharge; reversed on charging. High energy density, rechargeable.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp1_kp4: Lead-acid battery: Pb anode, PbO₂ cathode, H₂SO₄ electrolyte; each cell = 2 V; six cells in a car battery = 12 V. Cheapest rechargeable battery per kWh.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp1_insight: Alessandro Volta invented the first battery (voltaic pile) in 1800 using alternating zinc and silver discs separated by brine-soaked cardboard. Napoleon was so impressed he awarded Volta a medal and made him a count. The unit of voltage is named after Volta, and his invention allowed scientists like Faraday to discover electrolysis, fundamentally changing chemistry and physics.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp2_desc: Electrolysis uses electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction. It is used industrially to extract aluminium from bauxite, purify copper, plate metals, and produce chlorine and hydrogen from brine. Faraday's laws allow precise calculation of the amount of substance deposited or released.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp2_kp0: At the cathode (negative): cations are reduced (gain electrons); metals are deposited or H₂ gas is produced. At the anode (positive): anions are oxidised; O₂ or Cl₂ is released.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp2_kp1: Selective discharge: when multiple ions are present, the ion with the most positive (or least negative) electrode potential is discharged preferentially at the cathode; halide ions are discharged preferentially at anode over OH⁻.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp2_kp2: Aluminium extraction (Hall-Héroult process): Al₂O₃ dissolved in molten cryolite at ~960°C; Al deposited at cathode, O₂ released at anode (consuming carbon electrode).
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp2_kp3: Faraday's first law: mass deposited ∝ charge (Q = It); second law: mass ∝ molar mass / electrons per ion. m = (M × I × t) / (n × F).
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp2_kp4: Chlor-alkali process: electrolysis of brine (NaCl solution) produces Cl₂ (anode), H₂ (cathode), and NaOH (solution). All three products have major industrial uses.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp2_insight: Before electrolysis was invented, aluminium was more expensive than gold. In 1852, the Emperor Napoleon III served aluminium cutlery to his most honoured guests, while lesser guests used gold and silver. After Charles Hall and Paul Héroult independently invented electrolytic aluminium reduction in 1886, the price dropped by 99.8% within decades. Today, the world produces 65 million tonnes of aluminium per year.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_desc: Chemical equilibrium occurs in a closed system when the forward and reverse reactions proceed at equal rates, so the concentrations of reactants and products remain constant. Understanding equilibrium is essential for industrial chemistry (the Haber process) and biological systems (blood pH, enzyme activity).
learnSch_chemistry_f5_kp0: At equilibrium, the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction â€” concentrations are constant but not necessarily equal.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_kp1: Equilibrium constant K = [products]^n / [reactants]^m. K >> 1: products favoured. K << 1: reactants favoured.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_kp2: Le Chatelier's principle: if a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to counteract the change and restore equilibrium.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_kp3: Increasing concentration of a reactant shifts equilibrium towards products. Removing a product does the same.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_kp4: For gases: increasing pressure shifts equilibrium towards the side with fewer moles of gas. Temperature increase favours the endothermic direction.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_task: Nâ‚‚(g) + 3Hâ‚‚(g) â‡Œ 2NHâ‚ƒ(g)  Î”H = âˆ’92 kJ/mol. Predict the shift in equilibrium when: (a) pressure is increased, (b) temperature is raised, (c) more Nâ‚‚ is added. Explain each using Le Chatelier's principle.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp0_desc: Le Chatelier's principle states that if a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change (in concentration, pressure, or temperature), the system will shift its equilibrium position to partially oppose that change. It is a powerful predictive tool used in industrial chemistry to maximise product yield under practical constraints.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp0_kp0: Concentration change: adding a reactant shifts equilibrium towards products; removing a product shifts equilibrium towards products. Both increase product yield.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp0_kp1: Pressure change (gases): increasing pressure shifts equilibrium towards the side with fewer moles of gas; decreasing pressure shifts towards more moles of gas.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp0_kp2: Temperature change: for exothermic reactions (ΔH < 0), increasing temperature shifts equilibrium left (less product); decreasing temperature shifts right (more product). Opposite for endothermic.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp0_kp3: Catalysts do NOT shift equilibrium; they speed up both forward and reverse reactions equally, reaching equilibrium faster without changing Kc.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp0_kp4: Le Chatelier's principle explains why CO₂ dissolves more in cold seawater (solubility is exothermic), and why warming oceans release CO₂ — a feedback loop in climate change.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp0_insight: Henri Louis Le Chatelier discovered his principle in 1884 while trying to understand why explosions occur during attempted ammonia synthesis at high pressure. He observed that systems always 'fight back' against disturbances. The same principle explains why haemoglobin releases oxygen in tissues (low O₂ concentration shifts binding equilibrium left) and picks it up in the lungs — life-critical chemistry.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp1_desc: The equilibrium constant quantifies the ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium. Kc uses molar concentrations; Kp uses partial pressures of gases. The value of K depends only on temperature — not on how equilibrium was reached, nor on catalysts or concentrations. K tells you how far a reaction proceeds.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp1_kp0: Kc expression for aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD: Kc = [C]ᶜ[D]ᵈ / ([A]ᵃ[B]ᵇ). Square brackets = molar concentration. Pure solids and liquids are omitted.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp1_kp1: Kp uses partial pressures: Kp = p(C)ᶜ × p(D)ᵈ / (p(A)ᵃ × p(B)ᵇ). Relation: Kp = Kc(RT)Δn where Δn = moles of gas products − moles of gas reactants.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp1_kp2: Kc >> 1: equilibrium lies to the right; mostly products. Kc << 1: mostly reactants. Kc = 1: roughly equal amounts of reactants and products.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp1_kp3: Temperature changes Kc: if ΔH < 0 (exothermic), increasing T decreases Kc (less product); if ΔH > 0, increasing T increases Kc.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp1_kp4: Kc for reverse reaction = 1/Kc(forward). Kc for a reaction multiplied by n = (Kc)^n. These rules allow combining equilibria algebraically.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp1_insight: The equilibrium constant was first formulated in 1864 by Cato Guldberg and Peter Waage (the 'law of mass action'). Their idea — that reaction rate depends on the concentrations of reacting substances — was initially dismissed by the chemical community. It took decades to be accepted. Today, Kc is used to model everything from steel furnace chemistry to blood-oxygen binding, making it one of the most important equations in chemistry.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp2_desc: The Haber process synthesises ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen; the Contact process makes sulfuric acid from sulfur. Both are among the most important industrial chemical processes in history, applying equilibrium and kinetics principles to maximise yield and efficiency under practical conditions. Together they underpin global food production and chemical manufacturing.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp2_kp0: Haber process: N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), ΔH = −92 kJ/mol. Conditions: 450°C (compromise between yield and rate), 200 atm, iron catalyst with promoters.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp2_kp1: High pressure (200 atm) shifts equilibrium right (fewer moles of gas on right), increasing yield. High temperature is used for kinetics despite reducing yield — catalyst compensates.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp2_kp2: Contact process (H₂SO₄): S → SO₂ (burning sulfur), then 2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃ (V₂O₅ catalyst, 450°C, 2 atm), then SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → oleum, diluted to H₂SO₄.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp2_kp3: Both processes use moderate temperatures as a compromise: low T favours equilibrium yield but too slow; high T is fast but poor yield. A catalyst achieves a good rate at moderate T.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp2_kp4: Ammonia is the raw material for fertilisers (ammonium nitrate, urea) that feed ~50% of the world's population. Without the Haber process, Earth could support perhaps 3–4 billion people rather than 8 billion.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp2_insight: Fritz Haber won the 1918 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the ammonia synthesis. He is a profoundly controversial figure: the Haber process feeds billions, but Haber also directed Germany's chemical weapons programme in WWI, personally overseeing the first use of chlorine gas at Ypres in 1915. His wife Clara — also a chemist — shot herself in protest. The Haber process remains one of history's most morally complex scientific achievements.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_desc: Environmental chemistry investigates how chemical substances interact with air, water, and soil â€” and how human activity alters these systems. It explains climate change, ozone depletion, water pollution, and the chemistry behind sustainable solutions like green chemistry and biodegradable materials.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_kp0: Greenhouse gases (COâ‚‚, CHâ‚„, Nâ‚‚O, Hâ‚‚O vapour) absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, warming Earth's surface â€” the greenhouse effect.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_kp1: Acid rain: SOâ‚‚ and NOâ‚“ (from fossil fuel combustion) dissolve in rainwater forming Hâ‚‚SOâ‚ƒ and HNOâ‚ƒ, lowering pH and harming ecosystems.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_kp2: Ozone depletion: CFCs release ClÂ· radicals in the stratosphere, which catalytically destroy Oâ‚ƒ: ClÂ· + Oâ‚ƒ â†’ ClOÂ· + Oâ‚‚ (chain reaction).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_kp3: Eutrophication: nitrate and phosphate run-off from fertilisers cause algal blooms that deplete dissolved oxygen and kill aquatic life.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_kp4: Green chemistry principles: design reactions that minimise waste, use renewable feedstocks, avoid hazardous solvents, and maximise atom economy.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_task: Explain the complete chain reaction by which one CFC molecule can destroy thousands of ozone molecules. Write the two key steps. Then calculate the atom economy of the reaction 2Hâ‚‚ + Oâ‚‚ â†’ 2Hâ‚‚O.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp0_desc: The greenhouse effect is a natural process where certain gases in Earth's atmosphere absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, warming the surface. Without it, Earth's average temperature would be −18°C instead of +15°C. Human activities — burning fossil fuels, deforestation, agriculture — are intensifying this effect, driving global warming and climate disruption.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp0_kp0: Greenhouse gases: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O vapour, and ozone. CO₂ from fossil fuels is the dominant human-caused driver; CH₄ is 84× more potent than CO₂ over 20 years.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp0_kp1: Mechanism: Earth absorbs solar radiation and re-emits longer-wavelength infrared; greenhouse gas molecules absorb this IR via vibrational stretching/bending of bonds (only molecules with dipole changes are IR-active).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp0_kp2: Consequences: rising sea levels (thermal expansion + ice melt), more extreme weather events, ocean acidification (CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃), ecosystem disruption.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp0_kp3: Carbon cycle: plants absorb CO₂ (photosynthesis), return it via respiration and decomposition; fossil fuels add ancient carbon that was locked away for millions of years.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp0_kp4: Carbon capture: strategies include BECCS (bioenergy + carbon capture), direct air capture, enhanced weathering of silicate rocks, and reforestation.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp0_insight: The greenhouse effect was first described by Joseph Fourier in 1824 and quantified by Svante Arrhenius in 1896, who correctly predicted that doubling CO₂ would raise global temperature by 5°C. He calculated this by hand using data from thousands of measurements. The scientific understanding of climate change is over 125 years old — it is one of the most thoroughly verified predictions in all of science.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp1_desc: Acid rain forms when sulfur dioxide (SO₂) and nitrogen oxides (NOx) from fossil fuel combustion react with atmospheric water to form sulfuric and nitric acids. These acids damage ecosystems, buildings, and human health. Separately, the ozone layer in the stratosphere protects life from UV radiation, but is destroyed by chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) and other halogenated compounds.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp1_kp0: Acid rain formation: SO₂ + H₂O → H₂SO₃; 2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂; 3NO₂ + H₂O → 2HNO₃ + NO. Rain pH typically 4–5 (natural rain pH ~5.6 due to dissolved CO₂).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp1_kp1: Effects: acidification of lakes (fish die when pH < 5), leaching of aluminium from soils (toxic to roots), erosion of limestone and marble buildings (CaCO₃ + H₂SO₄ → CaSO₄ + H₂O + CO₂).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp1_kp2: Ozone layer: O₃ in stratosphere (15–35 km altitude) absorbs UVB/UVC radiation (200–315 nm), preventing DNA damage, skin cancer, and eye damage on Earth's surface.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp1_kp3: CFC ozone depletion mechanism: UV breaks CFCs (e.g. CCl₂F₂) releasing Cl· radicals; Cl· + O₃ → ClO· + O₂; ClO· + O· → Cl· + O₂. One Cl atom destroys 100,000 ozone molecules (chain reaction).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp1_kp4: Montreal Protocol (1987): banned CFCs; ozone hole has begun recovering; expected to heal fully by 2065. One of the most successful international environmental agreements.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp1_insight: The Antarctic ozone hole was discovered in 1985 by British scientists Farman, Gardiner, and Shanklin using ground-based instruments. NASA had been measuring the same data from satellites but an algorithm was set to discard 'unreasonably low' ozone values — the computer was filtering out the discovery. The satellite data was re-analysed after the ground discovery, confirming a hole larger than North America forming every spring.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp2_desc: Clean water is essential for life, yet industrial and agricultural chemicals contaminate waterways worldwide. Water treatment involves multiple stages of physical and chemical purification. Green chemistry is a philosophy and set of principles designed to make chemical processes inherently safer, less wasteful, and more sustainable from the start — not merely cleaning up after the fact.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp2_kp0: Water contamination types: dissolved heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd), organic pollutants (pesticides, pharmaceuticals), nitrates and phosphates (eutrophication), microplastics, and pathogens.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp2_kp1: Water treatment: screening, sedimentation, flocculation (Al₂(SO₄)₃ coagulates particles), filtration through sand/gravel, chlorination (kills pathogens), sometimes fluoridation and UV treatment.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp2_kp2: Hard water: contains Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions; causes scale (CaCO₃) in pipes and kettles; permanent hardness removed by ion exchange (zeolite or resin replaces Ca²⁺ with Na⁺).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp2_kp3: 12 Principles of Green Chemistry (Anastas & Warner, 1998): prevent waste, atom economy, less hazardous synthesis, design safer chemicals, safer solvents, design for efficiency, use renewable feedstocks, reduce derivatives, catalysis, biodegradable products, real-time analysis, inherently safer chemistry.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp2_kp4: Atom economy = (mass of desired product / total mass of all products) × 100%. Addition reactions have 100% atom economy; many traditional reactions (e.g. Grignard) have low atom economy.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp2_insight: In 1854, physician John Snow stopped a cholera outbreak in London by removing the handle from the Broad Street water pump — 17 years before the germ theory of disease was accepted. He proved the link through careful chemical and spatial analysis of water sources and deaths. This act of evidence-based intervention pioneered epidemiology and water treatment as a public health tool, saving hundreds of millions of lives since.
learnSch_biology_f3_desc: Genetics is the science of heredity â€” how traits are passed from parents to offspring through genes and chromosomes. It underpins medicine (genetic diseases, personalized treatment), agriculture (crop breeding), and forensic science (DNA profiling). Understanding genetics means understanding why individuals differ and how inherited diseases arise.
learnSch_biology_f3_kp0: Genes are segments of DNA that code for proteins. Each gene occupies a specific location (locus) on a chromosome.
learnSch_biology_f3_kp1: Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes (46 total); one set from each parent. Sex chromosomes: XX = female, XY = male.
learnSch_biology_f3_kp2: Dominant allele (B) masks recessive allele (b) in a heterozygote (Bb). Recessive traits only appear when homozygous (bb).
learnSch_biology_f3_kp3: Punnett squares predict the probability of offspring genotypes. A monohybrid cross Bb Ã— Bb gives 3:1 phenotype ratio.
learnSch_biology_f3_kp4: Sex-linked traits (e.g. colour blindness, haemophilia) are carried on the X chromosome; males (XY) are more often affected.
learnSch_biology_f3_task: Two brown-eyed parents (Bb Ã— Bb) have children. Draw the Punnett square. What is the probability of a child having blue eyes (bb)? What are the genotype and phenotype ratios?
learnSch_biology_f3_tp0_desc: Gregor Mendel (1866) discovered the laws of inheritance by experimenting with pea plants. He found that traits are determined by discrete heritable units (genes), that alleles separate during gamete formation (Law of Segregation), and that genes on different chromosomes assort independently (Law of Independent Assortment). His work, ignored for 35 years, became the foundation of modern genetics.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp0_kp0: Alleles: alternative forms of a gene occupying the same locus on homologous chromosomes. Dominant allele (uppercase) masks recessive allele (lowercase) in a heterozygote.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp0_kp1: Genotype vs phenotype: genotype = genetic makeup (e.g. Bb); phenotype = observable trait (e.g. brown eyes). Same phenotype can result from different genotypes (BB or Bb both give brown eyes).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp0_kp2: Law of Segregation: alleles separate during meiosis; each gamete receives one allele from each gene pair. F1 cross: Bb × Bb → 3 dominant : 1 recessive phenotype ratio.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp0_kp3: Law of Independent Assortment: genes on different chromosomes (or far apart on same chromosome) assort independently; gives 9:3:3:1 ratio in dihybrid cross (BbRr × BbRr).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp0_kp4: Incomplete dominance: neither allele fully dominant; heterozygote shows intermediate phenotype (e.g. red × white snapdragon → pink). Codominance: both alleles expressed (e.g. ABO blood group; AB genotype shows both A and B antigens).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp0_insight: Mendel's work on 29,000 pea plants over 8 years was published in 1866 and completely ignored until 1900, when three scientists independently rediscovered his paper. By that time, Mendel had been dead for 16 years. Ironically, Mendel's data has been statistically questioned — his results fit the expected ratios almost too perfectly, leading scientists to suspect his assistant may have unconsciously selected favourable plants. Mendel's genius was real; his data may have been 'improved'.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp1_desc: Punnett squares are diagrams used to predict the probability of offspring genotypes and phenotypes from a genetic cross. Developed by Reginald Crundall Punnett in 1905, they provide a systematic way to apply Mendel's laws. For monohybrid crosses (one gene), a 2×2 square; for dihybrid crosses (two genes), a 4×4 square. Results give ratios — not guarantees.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp1_kp0: Monohybrid cross (Bb × Bb): gametes from each parent in rows/columns; four cells show offspring genotypes BB, Bb, Bb, bb; ratio 1:2:1 genotype, 3:1 phenotype (dominant:recessive).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp1_kp1: Testcross: crossing unknown genotype (dominant phenotype, B?) with recessive homozygote (bb); if all offspring are dominant → BB; if 50:50 dominant:recessive → Bb. Used to reveal hidden recessive alleles.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp1_kp2: Dihybrid cross (BbRr × BbRr): 4 gamete types from each parent (BR, Br, bR, br); 4×4 Punnett square gives 9:3:3:1 phenotype ratio (two dominant : first dominant only : second dominant only : both recessive).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp1_kp3: Probability: each offspring is an independent event. Bb × Bb: P(bb) = 0.25. P(two consecutive bb offspring) = 0.25 × 0.25 = 0.0625 (6.25%).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp1_kp4: Chi-squared test (χ²) compares observed vs expected ratios to assess if deviation is due to chance: χ² = Σ(O−E)²/E. High value (above critical value at p=0.05) means results are not consistent with hypothesis.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp1_insight: Reginald Punnett and William Bateson in 1905 were the first to discover genetic linkage — that two genes on the same chromosome do NOT assort independently, violating Mendel's second law. They noticed pea traits always inherited together. They couldn't explain why. Thomas Hunt Morgan later showed (using fruit flies) that genes on the same chromosome are linked, but crossing over during meiosis can separate them — and the frequency of crossing over maps the distance between genes. Punnett squares remain useful despite this complexity.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp2_desc: Sex-linked traits are controlled by genes located on the sex chromosomes (X or Y). Because males have only one X chromosome (XY), they express all X-linked alleles including recessive ones — making males more susceptible to X-linked recessive diseases. Understanding sex-linked inheritance is essential for genetic counselling, and such diseases include haemophilia, colour blindness, and Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp2_kp0: X-linked recessive: females (XX) need two recessive alleles to be affected; one X with recessive allele makes them a carrier (unaffected). Males (XY) only need one recessive allele (on their single X) to be affected.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp2_kp1: Haemophilia A: Xʰ allele causes inability to produce clotting factor VIII; carrier female (X^H Xʰ) × normal male (X^H Y) gives 50% chance of affected son, 50% chance of carrier daughter.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp2_kp2: Red-green colour blindness: X-linked recessive; ~8% of males affected globally, only ~0.5% of females (requires two recessive alleles). The OPN1LW and OPN1MW genes encode red/green cone photopigments on chromosome X.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp2_kp3: X-inactivation (Lyon hypothesis): in females, one X chromosome in each cell is randomly inactivated (forms Barr body). Tortoiseshell cats show this — orange and black patches arise from different X chromosomes being active in different cells.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp2_kp4: Y-linked traits: only passed from father to all sons (holandric inheritance); very few genes on Y chromosome (SRY gene triggers male development). Mitochondrial DNA (inherited only from mother via egg cytoplasm) traces maternal lineage.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp2_insight: Queen Victoria of Britain was a carrier of haemophilia A — a mutation that arose spontaneously in her genome. She passed it through her daughters to the royal families of Russia, Spain, and Germany. The Russian Tsarevich Alexei (Victoria's great-grandson) was severely affected; his haemophilia contributed to Rasputin's influence over the Romanovs, affecting the politics that led to the Russian Revolution. A single point mutation in one woman's DNA reverberated through European history.
learnSch_biology_f4_desc: Evolution is the change in heritable characteristics of populations over generations. Darwin's natural selection is the main mechanism: individuals with advantageous traits survive and reproduce more. Evolution explains the enormous diversity of life on Earth and the shared features among different species.
learnSch_biology_f4_kp0: Natural selection: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and reproduce more, passing advantageous traits to offspring.
learnSch_biology_f4_kp1: Evidence for evolution: fossil record, comparative anatomy (homologous structures), DNA similarity, biogeography, observed speciation.
learnSch_biology_f4_kp2: Mutations in DNA are the ultimate source of new genetic variation. Most are neutral; some are harmful; a few provide an advantage.
learnSch_biology_f4_kp3: Speciation: when populations are isolated (geographic, reproductive) long enough, they evolve into separate species that can no longer interbreed.
learnSch_biology_f4_kp4: Antibiotic resistance is evolution in action: bacteria with resistance genes survive and reproduce, making resistant strains dominant.
learnSch_biology_f4_task: Explain in 4 steps how antibiotic resistance develops in a bacterial population using natural selection. Then describe two different types of evidence for evolution and explain what each shows.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp0_desc: Natural selection is the primary mechanism of evolution, first described by Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace in 1858. It occurs when individuals with heritable traits better suited to their environment survive and reproduce more successfully, passing those traits to offspring. Over many generations, advantageous traits increase in frequency in the population. Natural selection acts on existing variation — it does not create new variation.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp0_kp0: Four requirements for natural selection: variation (individuals differ), heritability (variation is inherited), differential survival/reproduction (not all survive equally), competition (limited resources). These four were identified by Darwin.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp0_kp1: Types of selection: directional (favours one extreme, shifts mean); stabilising (favours average, reduces variation); disruptive (favours two extremes, can lead to two phenotype groups — may drive speciation).
learnSch_biology_f4_tp0_kp2: Sexual selection: individuals with traits preferred by the opposite sex reproduce more; can produce elaborate structures (peacock tail, stag antlers) that may reduce survival but increase reproductive success.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp0_kp3: Genetic drift: random changes in allele frequency due to chance (not selection); more pronounced in small populations; can fix deleterious alleles or eliminate beneficial ones; bottleneck and founder effects are extreme forms.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp0_kp4: Antibiotic resistance: bacteria with random mutations conferring resistance survive antibiotic treatment; sensitive bacteria die; resistant strains reproduce and dominate. MRSA evolved this way. ~700,000 people die annually from antibiotic-resistant infections — projected to be 10 million by 2050.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp0_insight: Darwin and Wallace simultaneously and independently arrived at the theory of natural selection in the 1850s. Wallace, working in the Malay Archipelago with no library, sent Darwin a letter outlining the same theory. Darwin had sat on his own idea for 20 years (fearing controversy). Both men presented a joint paper at the Linnean Society in 1858 — and neither was present. Darwin published ‘On the Origin of Species’ in 1859; the 1,250 copies sold out on publication day. Wallace generously credited Darwin for the rest of his life.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp1_desc: Multiple independent lines of evidence converge to support evolution. Fossils show gradual change over geological time. Comparative anatomy reveals shared ancestry through homologous structures. Molecular biology shows the degree of DNA similarity between species correlates perfectly with evolutionary relatedness. Biogeography explains species distributions. All these strands of evidence independently point to the same conclusion: all life on Earth shares common ancestry.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp1_kp0: Fossil record: shows gradual change over geological time; transitional fossils (Tiktaalik — fish-tetrapod transition; Archaeopteryx — dinosaur-bird; whale ancestors); gaps exist because fossilisation is rare (soft organisms rarely fossilised).
learnSch_biology_f4_tp1_kp1: Comparative anatomy: homologous structures (same bones, different functions — human arm, whale flipper, bat wing, horse leg); derived from same ancestral limb. Vestigial structures (whale pelvis, snake leg bones, human coccyx, wisdom teeth) are remnants of ancestral functions.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp1_kp2: Molecular evidence: DNA comparison; more similar DNA = more recent common ancestor. Human-chimpanzee DNA: ~98.7% identical. Human-yeast cytochrome c: ~60% identical. Endogenous retroviruses (shared viral DNA insertions at same chromosomal location in humans and chimps): definitive evidence of shared ancestry.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp1_kp3: Biogeography: species distribution matches continental drift patterns; marsupials concentrated in Australia (isolated); Darwin's finches diversified on Galápagos after colonising from mainland; endemic island species related to nearest mainland species, not to similar-looking species on other islands.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp1_kp4: Direct observation of evolution: Galápagos finch beak size changes after drought; pepper moth colour change during industrial revolution (industrial melanism); E. coli long-term evolution experiment (Lenski, 1988–present — 75,000+ generations, evolving new metabolism).
learnSch_biology_f4_tp1_insight: The human genome contains ~8% sequences from ancient retroviruses (endogenous retroviruses, ERVs) that infected our ancestors millions of years ago and became permanently integrated into our DNA. Humans and chimpanzees share ERVs at exactly the same chromosomal positions — the probability of two viruses independently inserting at identical positions is essentially zero. These shared ERVs are perhaps the single most definitive molecular proof of common ancestry — more compelling than mere sequence similarity.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp2_desc: Speciation is the evolutionary process by which populations diverge to become distinct species unable to interbreed. It requires reproductive isolation — preventing gene flow between populations — long enough for them to diverge genetically. The classic route is geographic (allopatric) isolation, but speciation can also occur without geographic separation (sympatric, parapatric). Understanding speciation explains the enormous diversity of life.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp2_kp0: Biological species concept: a species is a group of organisms that can potentially interbreed and produce fertile offspring. Limitations: doesn't apply well to asexual organisms, fossils, or many plants that hybridise.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp2_kp1: Allopatric speciation: physical barrier (mountain, river, ocean) separates populations → no gene flow → each population evolves independently (different mutations, drift, selection) → eventual reproductive incompatibility. Most common mode of speciation.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp2_kp2: Sympatric speciation: occurs without geographic isolation; common in plants via polyploidy (chromosome doubling, e.g. allotetraploid). Polyploid plant cannot breed with diploid parents → new species instantly. ~50% of flowering plant species arose this way.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp2_kp3: Reproductive isolating mechanisms: prezygotic (habitat, temporal, behavioural, mechanical, gametic isolation) prevent mating or fertilisation; postzygotic (hybrid inviability, sterility, breakdown) prevent successful hybrid reproduction. Lions and tigers hybridise but ligers are sterile.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp2_kp4: Adaptive radiation: a single ancestral species rapidly diversifies into many species filling different ecological niches; Galápagos finches (13 species from 1 ancestor in <3 million years); Hawaiian honeycreepers (50+ species from 1 finch ancestor).
learnSch_biology_f4_tp2_insight: The Isthmus of Panama closed about 3 million years ago, separating the Pacific Ocean from the Caribbean. This is a natural experiment in allopatric speciation: 15 species of 'twin' shrimp (one Pacific, one Caribbean) exist, all descended from the same ancestors when the ocean was connected. Pacific and Caribbean twins are visually similar but cannot interbreed — 3 million years of geographic isolation has produced reproductive isolation. Biologists can directly calculate speciation rates from the geological record.
learnSch_biology_f5_desc: Plants are the primary producers in almost all ecosystems â€” they capture solar energy and convert it into organic matter through photosynthesis. Understanding plant biology covers structure (roots, stems, leaves, flowers), photosynthesis, reproduction, and the vital role plants play in regulating Earth's atmosphere and feeding life.
learnSch_biology_f5_kp0: Photosynthesis: 6COâ‚‚ + 6Hâ‚‚O + light energy â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚â‚‚Oâ‚† + 6Oâ‚‚. It occurs in chloroplasts containing chlorophyll.
learnSch_biology_f5_kp1: Leaves are adapted for photosynthesis: large surface area, thin, transparent epidermis, stomata for COâ‚‚ entry, and a rich vascular network.
learnSch_biology_f5_kp2: Xylem transports water and minerals from roots to leaves. Phloem transports sugars from leaves to the rest of the plant.
learnSch_biology_f5_kp3: Transpiration: water evaporates through stomata, pulling water up through the xylem (transpiration stream).
learnSch_biology_f5_kp4: Plant reproduction: flowering plants use pollination (pollen transfer) and fertilisation to produce seeds; seeds disperse to new locations.
learnSch_biology_f5_task: Design an experiment to test whether light intensity affects the rate of photosynthesis in pondweed. State the independent, dependent, and controlled variables. Predict and explain the result.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp0_desc: Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose. It occurs in two stages: the light-dependent reactions (in the thylakoid membranes) and the light-independent reactions/Calvin cycle (in the stroma). Plants are the primary producers of virtually all ecosystems, and photosynthesis is responsible for the oxygen in Earth's atmosphere and all the food energy available to consumers.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp0_kp0: Overall equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Takes place in chloroplasts; chlorophyll a and b absorb red and blue light (reflect green).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp0_kp1: Light-dependent reactions (thylakoid): light excites electrons in chlorophyll → photolysis of water (2H₂O → 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ + O₂); electrons passed along ETC; ATP and NADPH produced (used in Calvin cycle).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp0_kp2: Calvin cycle (stroma): CO₂ fixed by RuBisCO enzyme onto ribulose bisphosphate (5C) → two 3C molecules (glycerate-3-phosphate); reduced to G3P using ATP + NADPH; G3P used to make glucose or regenerate RuBP.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp0_kp3: Limiting factors: light intensity, CO₂ concentration, and temperature all limit photosynthesis. At compensation point, photosynthesis = respiration (no net gas exchange). Light saturation point: above this, extra light gives no benefit.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp0_kp4: C4 plants (maize, sugarcane): adaptations to reduce photorespiration in hot climates; CO₂ concentrated around RuBisCO in bundle sheath cells; 30–50% more efficient than C3 plants in bright light and high temperature.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp0_insight: Photosynthesis created the oxygen-rich atmosphere of Earth over 2.4 billion years ago in the 'Great Oxidation Event', when cyanobacteria began releasing O₂. Before this, Earth's atmosphere had no free oxygen. This oxygen revolution killed most anaerobic life (to them, O₂ was a toxic pollutant) but enabled the evolution of complex aerobic life — including all animals. Every breath you take is a product of ancient photosynthesis.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp1_desc: Plants have two transport systems. Xylem transports water and dissolved minerals from roots to leaves (unidirectional, driven by transpiration). Phloem transports sugars (mainly sucrose) from leaves (sources) to all other parts (sinks) such as growing roots, fruits, and storage organs (bidirectional). Both systems run in vascular bundles throughout the plant.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp1_kp0: Root hair cells: large surface area, thin walls; water enters by osmosis (soil water potential > root cell water potential); minerals absorbed by active transport (against concentration gradient, requires ATP).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp1_kp1: Water movement through root: apoplast pathway (cell walls, intercellular spaces); symplast pathway (through cytoplasm via plasmodesmata); Casparian strip in endodermis forces water through symplast for selective mineral uptake.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp1_kp2: Xylem transport (cohesion-tension theory): transpiration from leaves creates tension; water molecules are held together by hydrogen bonds (cohesion) and to xylem walls (adhesion); continuous water column pulled upward; tallest trees transport water >100 m.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp1_kp3: Transpiration factors: temperature (increases), humidity (decreases rate), wind (increases), light (stomata open, increases). Wilting = water loss > uptake; stomata close (guard cells lose turgor).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp1_kp4: Phloem loading: sucrose actively loaded into sieve tube elements at source; high solute concentration → low water potential → water enters by osmosis → high pressure; sucrose unloaded at sink → water leaves → pressure drops. Mass flow model.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp1_insight: The tallest living tree (Hyperion, a coastal redwood in California) is 116 m tall. Cohesion-tension transport must pull water this distance against gravity — generating negative pressure (tension) of about −1.5 MPa in the xylem, effectively placing the water under tension rather than pressure. This is physically equivalent to supporting a column of water 150 m tall. That trees can do this continuously, every day, using only the energy of evaporating water, is one of the most remarkable feats in biology.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp2_desc: Plants reproduce sexually (flowers, seeds) and asexually (vegetative propagation). Sexual reproduction generates genetic variation; asexual reproduction allows rapid clonal spread. Angiosperms (flowering plants) are the dominant plant group, with over 300,000 species. Their co-evolution with pollinators (insects, birds, bats) produced extraordinary diversity in flower form, colour, scent, and reward.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp2_kp0: Flower structure: sepals (protect bud), petals (attract pollinators), stamens (filament + anther — produces pollen), carpel (stigma + style + ovary containing ovules).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp2_kp1: Pollination: transfer of pollen to stigma; insect-pollinated flowers — colourful, scented, nectar; wind-pollinated — dull, lots of light pollen, feathery stigmas. Pollination ≠ fertilisation.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp2_kp2: Fertilisation: pollen germinates on stigma; pollen tube grows down style to ovule; two male nuclei released — one fertilises egg (embryo), one fertilises polar nuclei (endosperm, food store). Double fertilisation unique to angiosperms.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp2_kp3: Seed and fruit dispersal: wind (maple seeds, dandelion), animal (berries, burrs), water (coconut), explosive mechanisms (squirting cucumber). Dispersal reduces competition with parent plant, colonises new habitats.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp2_kp4: Asexual reproduction: runners (strawberry), bulbs (tulip), rhizomes (ginger), cuttings, tubers (potato — eyes = buds). All produce genetically identical offspring (clones). Used in horticulture to maintain desirable varieties.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp2_insight: The fig-fig wasp mutualism is one of biology's most intimate co-evolutionary relationships — over 750 species of figs, each with its own specific wasp pollinator, evolved together over 60 million years. Female wasps enter the fig through a tiny opening, pollinating the flowers inside, laying eggs, and dying inside the fig. Their offspring mate inside the fig, and females fly out carrying pollen to the next fig. Without fig wasps, no figs; without figs, no fig wasps. They are so interdependent that the extinction of either would eliminate both.
learnSch_biology_f6_desc: Microbiology is the study of microscopic organisms â€” bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists. These tiny entities shape human health, agriculture, food production, and the global carbon cycle. Understanding microbes is essential for medicine (antibiotics, vaccines), biotechnology, and understanding disease.
learnSch_biology_f6_kp0: Bacteria are prokaryotes (no nucleus). Most are harmless or beneficial; some are pathogens causing infections like tuberculosis and cholera.
learnSch_biology_f6_kp1: Viruses are not cells â€” they are strands of DNA/RNA in a protein coat. They replicate only by hijacking a host cell's machinery.
learnSch_biology_f6_kp2: Antibiotics kill or inhibit bacteria but are useless against viruses (the common cold, flu). Overuse drives antibiotic resistance.
learnSch_biology_f6_kp3: The immune system: non-specific defences (skin, phagocytes) and specific defences (lymphocytes producing antibodies). Vaccines train the immune system without causing disease.
learnSch_biology_f6_kp4: Beneficial microbes: gut bacteria aid digestion; fungi produce antibiotics (penicillin from Penicillium); yeast ferments sugars for bread and beer.
learnSch_biology_f6_task: Compare the structure of a bacterium and a virus. Explain why antibiotics work on one but not the other. Then describe two ways the body defends itself against a bacterial infection.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp0_desc: Bacteria and viruses are the two most medically significant classes of microorganisms. Bacteria are single-celled prokaryotes that reproduce independently; viruses are acellular particles that can only replicate inside host cells. Understanding their biology explains how infections spread, why antibiotics cannot treat viral infections, and how we develop effective vaccines and antimicrobials.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp0_kp0: Bacteria structure: cell wall (peptidoglycan), cell membrane, circular chromosome (nucleoid), plasmids, ribosomes (70S), flagella; no nucleus or membrane-bound organelles. Size: 1–5 μm. Shapes: cocci, bacilli, spirilla.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp0_kp1: Virus structure: protein capsid surrounding nucleic acid (DNA or RNA, single or double strand); some have a lipid envelope (HIV, influenza); no ribosomes, no metabolism; 20–300 nm (100× smaller than bacteria).
learnSch_biology_f6_tp0_kp2: Viral replication (lytic cycle): attachment to host receptor → injection of nucleic acid → hijacks host ribosomes and enzymes → new viral particles assembled → host cell lyses releasing virions. Lysogenic cycle: viral DNA integrates into host chromosome (provirus), replicating with the host.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp0_kp3: Antibiotic targets (bacterial, NOT viral): cell wall synthesis (penicillin, cephalosporins block peptidoglycan cross-linking), 70S ribosomes (streptomycin), DNA gyrase (quinolones), cell membrane (polymyxins). Human cells lack these targets, so antibiotics are selective.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp0_kp4: Antibiotic resistance mechanisms: beta-lactamase enzymes (break penicillin ring), modified target sites, efflux pumps (actively remove antibiotic), reduced permeability. Resistance genes spread via conjugation (plasmid transfer) between bacterial species.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp0_insight: MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) kills over 100,000 people per year worldwide and resists nearly all antibiotics. The World Health Organisation estimates that antimicrobial resistance (AMR) could kill 10 million people per year by 2050 — more than cancer. Alexander Fleming, who discovered penicillin in 1928, warned in his Nobel Prize acceptance speech in 1945 about the danger of using too little antibiotic, selecting for resistant mutants. We have been ignoring that warning for 75 years.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp1_desc: The immune system defends the body against pathogens and foreign substances through overlapping lines of defence. The innate immune system provides rapid, non-specific responses; the adaptive immune system provides specific, long-lasting immunity with memory. Vaccines exploit immunological memory to provide protection without the risks of natural infection — one of medicine's greatest achievements.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp1_kp0: First line of defence: physical and chemical barriers — skin (keratinised, acidic pH 4–5), mucus (traps pathogens), cilia (sweep mucus up), stomach acid (pH 2), lysozyme in tears and saliva (breaks peptidoglycan).
learnSch_biology_f6_tp1_kp1: Innate immunity (non-specific): phagocytes (neutrophils, macrophages) engulf pathogens; complement system (proteins that lyse bacteria and mark them for destruction); Natural Killer cells (destroy virally infected cells); inflammation (vasodilation, increased permeability, fever).
learnSch_biology_f6_tp1_kp2: Adaptive immunity: B lymphocytes → plasma cells → antibodies (specific to antigen); T lymphocytes → cytotoxic T cells (kill infected cells), helper T cells (activate B cells and macrophages). Clonal selection: antigen selects specific B/T cells with matching receptors → clonal expansion.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp1_kp3: Immunological memory: some activated B and T cells become memory cells; persist long-term; respond faster and more strongly on second exposure. Basis of vaccination and natural immunity.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp1_kp4: Vaccine types: live attenuated (MMR, oral polio — strong response, can mutate), inactivated (flu shot, hepatitis A), subunit (Hep B, pertussis — purified antigen), mRNA (COVID-19 — encodes antigen in mRNA, no live virus, rapidly designed).
learnSch_biology_f6_tp1_insight: Edward Jenner developed the first vaccine in 1796 using cowpox (Vaccinia) to protect against smallpox — he observed that milkmaids who contracted cowpox never got smallpox. He deliberately inoculated an 8-year-old boy (James Phipps) with cowpox and then exposed him to smallpox — an experiment impossible by modern ethics. Smallpox killed 300–500 million people in the 20th century alone. In 1980, the WHO declared smallpox the only human disease ever eradicated through vaccination — the greatest public health achievement in history.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp2_desc: Not all microorganisms are pathogens — the vast majority are harmless or actively beneficial. The human microbiome (trillions of bacteria in and on our bodies) is essential for digestion, immune development, and protection against pathogens. Industrially, microbes are used to make antibiotics, fermented foods, biofuels, and pharmaceuticals. Antibiotics themselves are microbial products — chemical weapons evolved by fungi and bacteria.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp2_kp0: Human microbiome: 38 trillion bacteria (comparable to human cell number) living in gut, skin, mouth, and other surfaces; gut microbiome critical for digestion, vitamin synthesis (B12, K2), immune system education, and protection against pathogens via competitive exclusion.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp2_kp1: Fermentation: yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) converts sugars to ethanol + CO₂ (alcoholic fermentation for beer, wine, bread); Lactobacillus converts lactose/glucose to lactic acid (yogurt, cheese, sauerkraut, kimchi). Controlled anaerobic respiration.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp2_kp2: Antibiotics: secondary metabolites produced by fungi (Penicillium → penicillin) and bacteria (Streptomyces → streptomycin, erythromycin, vancomycin) as competitive weapons. 80% of antibiotics are derived from Streptomyces species.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp2_kp3: Industrial fermentation (bioreactors): sterile, temperature-controlled vessels; pH regulated; aerated (aerobic) or sealed (anaerobic); used to produce antibiotics, insulin, enzymes (used in detergents and food processing), vaccines, and biofuels.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp2_kp4: Nitrogen fixation: Rhizobium bacteria in root nodules of legumes fix atmospheric N₂ → NH₃ using nitrogenase enzyme; mutualistic symbiosis; legume provides sugars, bacteria provide fixed nitrogen; reduces need for synthetic fertiliser in agriculture.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp2_insight: Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 by noticing that a contaminating mould (Penicillium notatum) had killed the Staphylococcus bacteria on a Petri dish he had left during a holiday. He published the observation but could not purify it. Howard Florey and Ernst Chain purified penicillin in 1940–1941, testing it on a policeman dying of Staphylococcal septicaemia — he began recovering, then died when the supply ran out. All three shared the 1945 Nobel Prize. Penicillin has saved an estimated 200 million lives — arguably the most impactful medical discovery in history.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_desc: Data structures are organised ways to store and access data; algorithms are step-by-step procedures to solve problems. Choosing the right data structure and algorithm determines whether a program runs in milliseconds or hours. This topic is the backbone of efficient software development.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_kp0: Arrays store elements in a fixed sequence; access by index is O(1). Lists allow dynamic size but may have slower access.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_kp1: Stacks (LIFO â€” last in, first out) and queues (FIFO â€” first in, first out) manage ordered collections for specific use cases.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_kp2: Linear search checks every element (O(n)); binary search requires sorted data and halves the search space each step (O(log n)).
learnSch_computer_science_f3_kp3: Bubble sort compares adjacent elements and swaps them (O(nÂ²)). Merge sort splits and merges recursively (O(n log n)) â€” much faster for large data.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_kp4: Big-O notation describes algorithm efficiency: O(1) is constant, O(n) is linear, O(nÂ²) is quadratic â€” the lower the better for large inputs.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_task: Trace bubble sort on the list [5, 3, 8, 1, 4]. Show each pass. Count the total comparisons and swaps. Then write pseudocode for binary search on a sorted list.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp0_desc: Arrays and lists are the most fundamental data structures — ordered collections of elements accessible by index. Arrays have fixed size and O(1) random access; dynamic lists (like Python’s list) can grow and shrink. Most other data structures (stacks, queues, heaps) are built on top of arrays or linked lists.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp0_kp0: Array: fixed-size, same-type elements stored in contiguous memory. Access any element in O(1) by index. Insert/delete in the middle requires shifting elements: O(n).
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp0_kp1: Dynamic array (ArrayList/Python list): grows automatically when capacity is exceeded (typically doubles). Amortised O(1) append; O(n) insert/delete in middle.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp0_kp2: Linked list: nodes containing a value and a pointer to the next node. O(1) insert/delete at head; O(n) access by index (must traverse from head). No wasted memory from pre-allocation.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp0_kp3: Doubly linked list: each node has pointers to both next and previous. Enables O(1) insert/delete anywhere if you already have a pointer to that node.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp0_kp4: Stack (LIFO — Last In First Out): push to top, pop from top. Used for function call stacks, undo operations, expression parsing. Queue (FIFO — First In First Out): enqueue at back, dequeue from front. Used for scheduling, BFS.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp0_insight: Every time you call a function in any programming language, the computer creates a stack frame — a block of memory on the call stack storing the function’s local variables and return address. When the function returns, the frame is popped off. A StackOverflowError (the crash that named the website) occurs when too many nested function calls fill the stack. Infinite recursion without a base case is the most common cause.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp1_desc: Searching algorithms find a target value within a dataset. Linear search checks every element; binary search exploits sorted order to eliminate half the dataset at each step. Choosing the right search algorithm can reduce a task from seconds to microseconds on large data.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp1_kp0: Linear search: check each element in order. O(n) worst case. Works on any list. Simple but slow for large data.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp1_kp1: Binary search: only works on sorted data. Compare target to middle element; if smaller, discard upper half; if larger, discard lower half. Repeat. O(log n) — searching 1 billion items takes at most 30 comparisons.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp1_kp2: Hash table lookup: O(1) average. A hash function maps the key to an array index. Collisions (two keys mapping to the same index) are resolved by chaining (linked list at each slot) or open addressing.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp1_kp3: Binary search tree (BST): each node’s left subtree contains smaller values; right subtree contains larger. Search, insert, delete in O(log n) average if balanced; O(n) worst case if degenerate (sorted insertion).
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp1_kp4: Interpolation search: improvement on binary search for uniformly distributed data — estimates where the target is likely to be instead of always choosing the middle. O(log log n) average.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp1_insight: Google processes over 8 billion searches per day. Each search involves looking up query terms across an index of trillions of web pages in under 200 milliseconds. This is only possible because of inverted indexes (a hash map from each word to all pages containing it) combined with sophisticated ranking algorithms. No human would ever wait for a linear search through the entire web.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp2_desc: Sorting algorithms arrange data in order. Choosing the right algorithm depends on data size, whether it is nearly sorted, and memory constraints. Efficient sorting is fundamental — many other algorithms (binary search, certain graph algorithms) require sorted input.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp2_kp0: Bubble sort: repeatedly swap adjacent out-of-order elements. O(n²) average. Simple to implement but inefficient; mainly used for teaching.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp2_kp1: Insertion sort: build sorted array one element at a time by inserting each into its correct position. O(n²) average but O(n) on nearly-sorted data. Used in practice for small arrays.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp2_kp2: Merge sort: divide array into halves, recursively sort each, merge sorted halves. O(n log n) always. Stable sort. Requires O(n) extra memory.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp2_kp3: Quick sort: choose a pivot, partition array so elements < pivot go left, > pivot go right, recurse. O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst case (bad pivot choice). In-place. Used in most standard library sort functions.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp2_kp4: Comparison-based sorting lower bound: any algorithm that sorts by comparing elements requires at least O(n log n) comparisons in the worst case (proof by decision tree). Radix sort bypasses this by not comparing — O(nk) where k is digit length.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp2_insight: Tim Peters invented Timsort in 2002 for Python, combining merge sort and insertion sort. It detects existing order in the data (natural runs) and exploits it, achieving O(n) on nearly-sorted data. Timsort is now the standard sorting algorithm in Python, Java (Arrays.sort for objects), Android, and Swift. Nearly every time you sort a list on your phone or computer, you are using an algorithm invented by one person just 23 years ago.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp0_desc: Relational databases organise data into tables (relations). Each row is a record; each column is an attribute. Tables are linked by keys, enabling complex data relationships to be stored efficiently without repetition. This model, invented by Edgar Codd in 1970, remains the dominant database paradigm.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp0_kp0: Table (relation): a grid of rows (records/tuples) and columns (attributes/fields). Each cell holds one data value. All values in a column share the same data type.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp0_kp1: Primary key: a column (or combination) that uniquely identifies each row. Must be unique and not null. Often an auto-incrementing integer (id).
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp0_kp2: Foreign key: a column in one table that references the primary key of another, creating a relationship (link) between tables. Enforces referential integrity — you can’t reference a row that doesn’t exist.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp0_kp3: Relationship types: one-to-one (one person, one passport); one-to-many (one customer, many orders); many-to-many (students and courses — requires a junction/bridge table).
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp0_kp4: Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram: a visual model of tables and their relationships. Designed before coding the database to catch structural errors early.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp0_insight: Edgar F. Codd’s 1970 paper 'A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks' is one of the most influential papers in computer science history. IBM initially ignored it because they feared it would make their existing database product (IMS) obsolete. Two young engineers — Larry Ellison and Bob Miner — read the paper and built the first commercial relational database in 1979. That company is now Oracle, worth over £200 billion. IBM eventually built their own relational database (DB2) years later.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp1_desc: SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for interacting with relational databases. It lets you retrieve, insert, update, and delete data with precise, readable commands. SQL is used by virtually every application that stores structured data — from school systems to banking.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp1_kp0: SELECT: SELECT column1, column2 FROM table WHERE condition ORDER BY column LIMIT n. SELECT * returns all columns. WHERE filters rows. ORDER BY sorts. LIMIT restricts how many rows are returned.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp1_kp1: JOIN: combines rows from two tables based on a related column. INNER JOIN returns rows where the join condition matches in both tables. LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table, with NULLs for unmatched right rows.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp1_kp2: Aggregate functions: COUNT(), SUM(), AVG(), MAX(), MIN(). Used with GROUP BY to compute per-group statistics. HAVING filters groups (like WHERE for aggregates).
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp1_kp3: INSERT INTO table (col1, col2) VALUES (v1, v2); UPDATE table SET col = val WHERE condition; DELETE FROM table WHERE condition. Always use WHERE with UPDATE/DELETE or you affect every row.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp1_kp4: SQL injection: if user input is inserted into a SQL query without sanitisation, an attacker can inject malicious SQL. Prevention: use parameterised queries / prepared statements — never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp1_insight: The 2008 Heartland Payment Systems breach — one of the largest in history, exposing 130 million credit card numbers — was carried out entirely via SQL injection. The attacker typed SQL commands into a website login form that Heartland had forgotten to sanitise. SQL injection remains the number one web application vulnerability (OWASP Top 10) despite being trivially preventable with parameterised queries that have been available since 1992.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp2_desc: Normalisation is the process of structuring a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity. It proceeds through 'normal forms' (1NF, 2NF, 3NF), each eliminating a different type of dependency anomaly. A well-normalised database is easier to maintain and avoids update, insertion, and deletion anomalies.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp2_kp0: 1NF (First Normal Form): each column holds atomic (indivisible) values; each row is unique. No repeating groups. Example: split 'phone1, phone2' into separate rows.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp2_kp1: 2NF (Second Normal Form): must be in 1NF + every non-key attribute is fully functionally dependent on the whole primary key (no partial dependencies). Relevant only if primary key is composite.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp2_kp2: 3NF (Third Normal Form): must be in 2NF + no transitive dependencies (non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute). Move transitively dependent data to its own table.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp2_kp3: Anomalies in unnormalised tables: update anomaly (change one value, must update many rows); insertion anomaly (can’t add data without unrelated data); deletion anomaly (deleting one row destroys other information).
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp2_kp4: Denormalisation: sometimes intentionally violating normal forms for performance (fewer JOINs). Used in data warehouses and read-heavy analytics applications where speed matters more than update efficiency.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp2_insight: Edgar Codd also invented normalisation theory (1971–1974). He described it as 'a further step in the direction of making the description of data independent of the way it is stored.' Before normalisation, changing one customer’s address required updating it in dozens of places across multiple files, causing countless inconsistencies. The same data integrity problem reappears today whenever developers skip normalisation to 'save time' — only to spend months later tracking down inconsistencies.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp0_desc: A network connects computing devices to share data and resources. Networks are classified by scale (LAN, WAN) and topology (how devices connect). Protocols are standardised rules ensuring devices from different manufacturers can communicate. Without protocols, the internet could not exist.
