Many Taiwanese and Hong Kong people view Traditional characters as a mark of cultural identity and continuity with Chinese civilisation, while some mainland Chinese view them as unnecessarily complex obstacles to mass literacy. The writer Lu Xun — revered as China's greatest 20th-century writer in both mainland and Taiwan — actually advocated for abolishing characters entirely in favour of a fully alphabetic system (like Pinyin) in the 1930s, arguing that classical Chinese was a 'man-eating culture' that excluded ordinary people from literacy. He died in 1936 before the debate was resolved. The final outcome — Simplified, not alphabetic — was a political compromise between radical reformers and cultural conservatives that satisfied neither fully. The script reform debate is simultaneously a linguistic, political, and cultural argument that remains unresolved today.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp0_desc: Mandarin Chinese grammar is structured quite differently from European languages, but its core rules are surprisingly regular once understood. The absence of grammatical inflection (no verb conjugation, no noun cases, no grammatical gender) means that word order and particles carry the grammatical information that endings carry in European languages. Understanding Chinese sentence structure enables learners to construct an unlimited range of sentences from a relatively small vocabulary.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp0_kp0: Basic sentence order: Mandarin follows Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) word order, like English. Wǒ chī mifàn. (I eat rice.) 我吃米饣. However, the position of time words and location phrases differs from English. In Mandarin, time expressions come BEFORE the verb (unlike English where they can go at the end): Wǒ mióngtiān qù xiéxié. (I tomorrow go school.) 我明天去学校. Location phrases also typically precede the verb: Wǒ zài tuóshūguǎn hé shū. (I at library read book.) 我在图书馆吃汤. General principle: the sentence moves from larger/earlier frame (time, place) to smaller/later action.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp0_kp1: Yes/no questions with mā (吗): add the particle mā to the end of any statement to turn it into a yes/no question. Nǐ shì xuéshēng. (You are a student.) → Nǐ shì xuéshēng mā? (Are you a student?) 你是学生吗？ Answering: repeat the verb for 'yes' (shì or chī or etc.) and add buè + verb for 'no' (bù shì, bù chī). The affirmative-negative question structure: V + buè + V (alternative question, no mā needed): Nǐ shì bù shì xuéshēng? (Are you or are you not a student?) 你是不是学生？ This structure is very natural and common in speech.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp0_kp2: Question words (ç–‘é—®è¯): Mandarin question words stay in the position where the answer would go (unlike English where the question word moves to the front). Shénme (ä»€ä¹ˆ) = what: Nǐ xiǎng chī shénme? (What do you want to eat?) 你想吃什么? NǎƱ / nǎƱr (å“ª/å“ªå„¿) = which/where: Nǐ zài nǎƱr? (Where are you?) 你在哪儿? Shéi (è°) = who: Shéi shì nǐ de lǎo shī? (Who is your teacher?) 谁是你的老师? Shénme shíhòu (ä»€ä¹ˆæ—¶å€™) = when: Nǐ shénme shíhòu huí jiā? (When will you go home?) Jǐge (å‡ ä¸ª) = how many (small numbers): Jǐge rén? (How many people?). Duōshǎo (å¤šå°‘) = how much/many (larger amounts, prices).
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp0_kp3: Negation: use buè (不) before the verb or adjective to negate most statements. Wǒ bù chī ròu. (I don't eat meat.) 我不吃肉. Wǒ bù laméi. (I am not tired.) 我不累. Note: buè changes tone from 4th to 2nd before another 4th-tone syllable (bú + 4th-tone). Méi (没) is used to negate yǒu (有, to have/exist) and for past actions. Méi yǒu (æ²¡æœ‰): I don't have / There isn't. Wǒ méi yǒu qíiàn. (I don't have money.) Wǒ méi chī. (I didn't eat.) Méi = negation of completion/past; bù = negation of will/habit/present state.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp0_kp4: The aspect particles le (了), guò (过), zhè (着): Chinese grammar does not conjugate verbs for tense, but uses particles to indicate aspect (whether an action is completed, ever-done, or ongoing). Le (了): marks a completed action (not strictly past tense). Wǒ chī le. (I have eaten / I ate.) Also marks a change of state: Tā lái le! (He has come! / He came!) Guò (过): marks an action that has been experienced at some point in life. Wǒ qù guò Běījīng. (I have been to Beijing [at some point in my life].) Zhè (着): marks an ongoing state. Tā chuānzhè hóng yīfū. (He is wearing red clothes.) Zhe indicates the continuing state of wearing.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp0_insight: The absence of tense-marking on Chinese verbs — combined with the presence of aspect particles — has generated enormous debate in linguistic philosophy about the relationship between grammar and thought. In European languages, every verb must be marked for tense: you cannot say 'I eat yesterday' in English without the sentence being ungrammatical. In Mandarin, Wǒ zuótiān chī mifàn (我昨天吃米饣) = 'I yesterday eat rice' — the verb chī is unchanged; the time is specified by the adverb (yesterday). This led early Sapir-Whorf hypothesis advocates (1930s–50s) to argue that speakers of tenseless languages might perceive time differently. Modern experimental linguistics has generally not confirmed this strong claim: speakers of Mandarin, Burmese, and other tenseless languages show the same cognitive understanding of time as speakers of tensed languages. The difference is grammatical, not conceptual. However, one consistent finding is that speakers of languages with grammatical tense are slightly faster at identifying temporal sequences in non-verbal tasks — suggesting that compulsory grammatical marking of tense may lightly bias attention toward temporal information without determining thought.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp1_desc: Mastering numbers and shopping vocabulary in Mandarin is one of the most practically rewarding early achievements for Chinese learners. Chinese numbers follow a completely regular decimal system from 1 to 99,999 — unlike English with its irregular 'eleven', 'twelve', 'thirty', 'forty'. Shopping dialogues require knowledge of numbers, prices, measure words, and negotiation phrases, and are deeply embedded in Chinese social culture.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp1_kp0: Cardinal numbers (åŸºæ•°è¯): 0 = líng (零), 1 = yī (一), 2 = èr (二), 3 = sān (三), 4 = sì (四), 5 = wǔ (五), 6 = liù (六), 7 = qī (七), 8 = bā (八), 9 = jiǔ (九), 10 = shí (十), 11 = shí yī, 12 = shí èr... 20 = èr shí, 21 = èr shí yī... 100 = yī bǎi (一百), 1,000 = yī qiān (一千), 10,000 = yī wàn (一万). Note: Chinese has a word for 10,000 (wàn) as a unit, unlike English which counts by thousands. 100,000 = shí wàn (10 ten-thousands). 1,000,000 = yī bǎi wàn. This wàn-unit system requires adjustment for large numbers.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp1_kp1: Prices and money: the Chinese currency is the Renminbi (RMB / 人民币 Rénmín Bì), with the basic unit the Yuán (元 / formally: 元). In speech: kuài (块) = yuan (colloquial); máo (毛) = jiao (0.1 yuan, colloquial); fēn (分) = fen (0.01 yuan). Prices: 5 kuai 8 mao = 5.80 yuan. Duōshǎo qián? (多少錢?) = How much? Tài guì le! (太贵了!) = Too expensive! Piányí yīdiǎn (便宜一点) = A bit cheaper please. Zhège duōshǎo qián? (这个多少錢?) = How much is this one? Wǒ zhǐ yào (我只要) = I only want.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp1_kp2: Measure words (é‡è¯ liàngcí): Chinese requires a specific measure word between a number and a noun, depending on the type of noun. This is unfamiliar to English speakers (English uses 'a cup of water' but 'two waters'; Chinese requires the measure word for every noun + number combination). Gè (个): general measure word for people, things without specific shape. Yī gè rén (one person). Běn (本): for bound items (books, magazines). Yī běn shū (one book). Tiáo (条): for long, flexible things (fish, rivers, trousers). Yī tiáo yú (one fish). Zhāng (张): flat things (tables, paper, maps, faces). Yī zhāng zhī (one sheet of paper). Biā (杠): things with a handle (umbrella, guitar). Yī bǎi sǎn (one umbrella). Liàng (辆): vehicles. Yī liàng chē (one car).
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp1_kp3: Shopping dialogue: Vendor: Nǐn xiǎng mǎi shénme? (What do you want to buy?) / Nǐn chī shénme? (What would you like?) Customer: Wǒ xiǎng mǎi zhège. (I want to buy this.) Zhège duōshǎo qián? (How much is this?) Vendor: Èr shí kuài. (Twenty yuan.) Customer: Tài guì le, piányí yiīdiǎn kěyǐ mā? (Too expensive, can it be a bit cheaper?) Vendor: Hǎo, shí bā kuài. (OK, eighteen yuan.) Customer: Hǎo de, wǒ mǎi le. (OK, I'll take it.) Wǒ yòng weiíxhàn fùkuǎn. (I'll pay by WeChat.) Note: in China, mobile payment (WeChat Pay, Alipay) has largely replaced cash even at market stalls.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp1_kp4: Numbers in daily contexts: dates: yī yuè yī hào = January 1st (literally: 1 month 1 number). Time: xiànzài jǐ diǎn? (What time is it now?) Liǔng diǎn sān shí fēn (2:30). Sān diǎn bàn = 3:30 (bàn = half). Phone numbers: read out digit by digit (each number separately, no grouping). 138-0001-2345: yīo sān bā líng líng líng yīo èr sān sì wǔ. Note: yīo (尺) is used instead of yī (一) for the digit '1' in phone numbers, addresses, and transportation to avoid confusion with qī (七, seven) on the phone.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp1_insight: China's transition to a cashless society has been the fastest and most complete in the world. By 2020, mobile payments (primarily WeChat Pay and Alipay) accounted for over 80% of all payment transactions in China, and many smaller vendors and market stalls no longer accept cash at all. This created an unexpected problem: foreign tourists, who lack Chinese bank accounts and cannot access WeChat Pay or Alipay without a Chinese bank card, found themselves unable to pay for food, transport, or accommodation in many cities. In 2024, the Chinese government announced policy changes requiring vendors to accept cash alongside digital payment to address this exclusion. The cashless revolution also created problems for older Chinese citizens and rural populations without smartphones. The speed of the transition (essentially 2013–2020) reflects both China's state capacity to drive technology adoption and the convenience benefits that drove user uptake — but also illustrates how rapid technology adoption can create unexpected exclusions for those outside the new system. The phrase Wǒ yòng weiíxhàn fùkuǎn ('I pay by WeChat') is now a more common phrase than 'I pay by cash' in most Chinese cities.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp2_desc: Food is central to Chinese culture in a way that goes far beyond nutrition. The phrase 'chī fàn le mǎ?' (Have you eaten?) functions as a greeting; meals are social occasions, business opportunities, and expressions of care. Understanding Chinese food vocabulary, daily life routines, and cultural practices gives learners essential context for language use and reveals the values embedded in the language.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp2_kp0: Essential food vocabulary: mǐfàn (米饣) = cooked rice (the staple); miàntiáo (面条) = noodles; rou (肉) = meat; jīròu (鸡肉) = chicken; zhūròu (猪肉) = pork; niúròu (牛肉) = beef; yū (鱼) = fish; shūcoi (蔬菜) = vegetables; dòufǔ (豆腐) = tofu; jiǎndàn (鸡蛋) = egg; shūguā (蜀瓜) = cucumber; fangié (番茄) = tomato; chānguǐ (香菇) = coriander/cilantro (often divisive among foreigners). Condiments: jiàngyóu (酱油) = soy sauce; làjiāo (辣椒) = chilli; cu (醇) = vinegar; májiang (芻腱) = sesame paste.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp2_kp1: Ordering food and dining culture: Wǒ yào yī wǎn mifàn. (I want a bowl of rice.) Wǒ bu chī rou. (I don't eat meat.) Wǒ du mià guòmiàn (我对麺过敏。) (I am allergic to wheat.) Hǎn hǎo chī! (很吃!) = Delicious! Gān bēi! (干杨!) = Cheers! (Literally: dry cup — traditionally meant drink the whole cup in one go; in modern contexts often a sip.) In Chinese dining culture: shared dishes are placed in the centre of the table for everyone; the host orders and pays (no splitting; refusing to pay can cause serious social embarrassment); toasting the host is expected; leaving food on your plate shows the host provided enough.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp2_kp2: Daily life vocabulary: zhǒoshāng (早上) = morning; zhōngwǔ (中午) = noon; xiàwǔ (下午) = afternoon; wǎnsāng (晚上) = evening. Daily routine phrases: Wǒ qiǐ chuáng. (I get up.) Wǒ xiǎn zǎo. (I wash/shower.) Wǒ chī zǎoshán. (I eat breakfast.) Wǒ qù xéxié. (I go to school.) Wǒ zuò gōngén jiā. (I do homework.) Wǒ shuì jiào. (I go to sleep.) Weather: tiānqì zěme yàng? (How's the weather?) Tiānqì hǎn hǎo. (The weather is good.) Xià yǔ le. (It's raining. / It has started to rain.) Hen lěng. (Very cold.) Hen rè. (Very hot.)
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp2_kp3: Chinese festivals and their cultural significance: Chūnjié (春节) — Chinese New Year / Spring Festival: the most important holiday. Families gather for a reunion dinner on New Year's Eve (niányèfàn). Red envelopes (hóngbāo / 红包) with money given to children and unmarried adults. Fireworks. Lantern Festival (Yuanán Jié) on the 15th day. Qiūxiōng Jié (中秋节) — Mid-Autumn Festival: families eat mooncakes and admire the full moon. Associated with family reunion. Duànwǔ Jié (端午节) — Dragon Boat Festival: dragon boat races; eating zòngzi (sticky rice wrapped in bamboo leaves). Qingmíng Jié (清明节) — Tomb Sweeping Day: families visit ancestors' graves and tidy them.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp2_kp4: Chinese social values and etiquette: Miànzi (面子, face): prestige, reputation, social dignity. Causing someone to 'lose face' (diī miànzi) publicly is a serious social offence. Praising someone in public 'gives face' (gěng miànzi). Guānxi (关系, relationships/connections): the network of relationships that facilitates social and business life. Building guānxi requires time, mutual favour, and social investment. Rénqiíng (人情, human feelings): the social currency of favours and obligations that flow through relationships. Refusing someone's offer of food or help directly may give offence: polite initial refusal, second offer, acceptance is the expected sequence.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_tp2_insight: The Chinese word miànzi (面子, 'face') became familiar in English through anthropological and sociological studies of Chinese culture, but linguists have noted that 'face' in Chinese is actually two distinct concepts that English collapses into one: miànzi (prestige associated with success and social status — something you accumulate and others give you) and liǎn (脸, face as moral character — something you can lose through shameful behaviour but not simply through failure). An anthropologist named Hu Hsien Chin first described this distinction in an influential 1944 paper in the American Anthropologist. The distinction matters: you can have high miànzi (social prestige) while lacking liǎn (moral standing), or vice versa. A corrupt official might have high miànzi (power and status) but low liǎn (moral character). The translation of both as 'face' in English loses this nuance. The concept has entered international business English ('face-saving measures', 'don't lose face') and management theory as a key cross-cultural concept, but the simplified English version misses the two-part Chinese structure.
learnSch_chinese_language_f1_desc: Chinese characters (æ±‰å­—, HÃ nzÃ¬) are one of the world's oldest writing systems, with over 50,000 characters â€” though literacy requires knowing about 2,000â€“3,000 common ones. Characters are not random drawings â€” they are built from meaningful components called radicals, which provide clues to meaning and sometimes pronunciation.
learnSch_chinese_language_f1_kp0: Characters are written on a square grid and composed of strokes in a specific order. Stroke order rules ensure consistency and aid memory: generally top to bottom, left to right, horizontal before vertical.
learnSch_chinese_language_f1_kp1: Radicals: all characters contain a semantic radical that hints at meaning. æ°´ (water radical æ°µ) appears in æµ· (sea), æ²³ (river), æ³³ (swim). Knowing ~200 radicals unlocks patterns in thousands of characters.
learnSch_chinese_language_f1_kp2: Simplified vs. Traditional: Simplified characters (used in mainland China, Singapore) have fewer strokes. Traditional characters (Taiwan, Hong Kong, overseas communities) preserve historical forms. Example: love â€” çˆ± (simplified) vs. æ„› (traditional).
learnSch_chinese_language_f1_kp3: HSK (Hanyu Shuiping Kaoshi): the standard Chinese proficiency test. HSK 1 = 150 words, HSK 2 = 300, up to HSK 6 = 5,000 words. Essential benchmark for Chinese learners.
learnSch_chinese_language_f1_kp4: Common characters to learn first: ä¸€äºŒä¸‰å››äº”å…­ä¸ƒå…«ä¹å (1â€“10), äºº (person), å¤§ (big), å° (small), ä¸­ (middle/China), æ—¥ (sun/day), æœˆ (moon/month), å±± (mountain), æ°´ (water), ç« (fire).
learnSch_chinese_language_f1_task: Learn and write (with correct stroke order) the numbers 1â€“10 in Chinese characters. Then learn five more characters using their radicals as memory aids â€” explain the connection for each. Finally, write your name in Pinyin and choose appropriate Chinese characters for it.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_desc: Learning conversational Mandarin opens doors to 1.4 billion potential conversations and one of the world's most vibrant cultures. Core topics â€” introductions, time, shopping, food, directions â€” provide the practical vocabulary for everyday communication. Chinese grammar's regularity makes dialogue surprisingly accessible once tones and basic vocabulary are mastered.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_kp0: Sentence structure: Subject + Time + Place + Verb + Object. Example: æˆ‘ (I) æ˜Žå¤© (tomorrow) åœ¨å›¾ä¹¦é¦† (in the library) å­¦ä¹  (study) ä¸­æ–‡ (Chinese) = æˆ‘æ˜Žå¤©åœ¨å›¾ä¹¦é¦†å­¦ä¹ ä¸­æ–‡.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_kp1: Asking questions: add å— (ma) to a statement for yes/no questions. ä½ æ˜¯å­¦ç”Ÿ (you are a student) â†’ ä½ æ˜¯å­¦ç”Ÿå—? (Are you a student?). Or use question words: ä»€ä¹ˆ (shÃ©nme, what), å“ªé‡Œ (nÇŽlÇ, where), ä»€ä¹ˆæ—¶å€™ (shÃ©nme shÃ­hÃ²u, when).
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_kp2: Numbers and prices: ä¸€ç™¾ (100), ä¸¤ç™¾äº”å (250). In shops: è¿™ä¸ªå¤šå°‘é’±? (zhÃ¨ge duÅshÇŽo qiÃ¡n?) â€” How much is this? å¤ªè´µäº† (tÃ i guÃ¬ le) â€” Too expensive! ä¾¿å®œä¸€ç‚¹ (piÃ¡nyÃ­ yÄ«diÇŽn) â€” A little cheaper please.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_kp3: Food and ordering: æˆ‘è¦ä¸€ç¢—ç±³é¥­ (WÇ’ yÃ o yÄ« wÇŽn mÇfÃ n) â€” I want a bowl of rice. æˆ‘ä¸åƒè‚‰ (WÇ’ bÃ¹ chÄ« rÃ²u) â€” I don't eat meat. å¥½åƒ! (HÇŽochÄ«!) â€” Delicious!
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_kp4: Cultural context: exchanging business cards with two hands; addressing elders formally; the importance of é¢å­ (miÃ nzi, 'face' â€” reputation, dignity); gift-giving etiquette. Language and culture are inseparable.
learnSch_chinese_language_f2_task: Role-play (write or record) a conversation at a Chinese restaurant. Include: greeting the waiter, asking what is good, ordering two dishes and a drink (using correct measure words), asking the price, and saying goodbye. Aim for at least 10 exchanges. Annotate with Pinyin and English translation.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f0_desc: Climate change is the defining challenge of our era. Human emissions of greenhouse gases are warming Earth at an unprecedented rate, disrupting weather patterns, melting ice caps, and threatening ecosystems and human communities. Understanding the science â€” the carbon cycle, tipping points, and feedback loops â€” is essential for any informed citizen.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f0_kp0: The carbon cycle: COâ‚‚ moves between atmosphere, oceans, land (plants via photosynthesis), and living organisms (via respiration). Burning fossil fuels releases carbon stored for millions of years, disrupting the cycle.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f0_kp1: Greenhouse effect: solar radiation warms Earth; Earth emits infrared radiation; greenhouse gases (COâ‚‚, CHâ‚„, Nâ‚‚O, Hâ‚‚O vapour) absorb it and re-radiate heat, warming the surface. Without it, Earth would be âˆ’18Â°C; with it (natural), 15Â°C; enhanced = dangerous warming.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f0_kp2: Current status (2024): atmospheric COâ‚‚ exceeds 420 ppm (pre-industrial: 280 ppm). Global average temperature has risen ~1.2Â°C above pre-industrial levels. The Paris Agreement aims to limit warming to 1.5Â°C.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f0_kp3: Climate tipping points: thresholds beyond which changes become self-reinforcing. Melting permafrost releases CHâ‚„; melting Arctic ice exposes dark ocean, absorbing more heat; Amazon deforestation risks turning the rainforest into savanna.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f0_kp4: Climate justice: developing nations contribute least to emissions but suffer most from climate impacts (sea-level rise, extreme heat, crop failure). The ethical and political dimensions of climate action are inseparable from the science.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f0_task: Draw and annotate the carbon cycle, showing natural flows and the human disruption. Calculate: if a country emits 500 Mt COâ‚‚ per year and plants trees sequestering 50 Mt/year while switching to renewables saves 100 Mt/year, how many years to reach net zero (assuming no new measures)? Evaluate three additional actions the country could take.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f1_desc: Biodiversity â€” the variety of life on Earth â€” is the foundation of healthy ecosystems that provide us with food, clean water, medicine, and a stable climate. We are currently experiencing the sixth mass extinction, driven by habitat destruction, pollution, invasive species, and climate change. Understanding ecosystems reveals why every lost species matters.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f1_kp0: Ecosystem services: the benefits nature provides free of charge â€” pollination (essential for 75% of food crops), water purification, flood control, carbon storage, soil formation, and coastal protection.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f1_kp1: The sixth mass extinction: species are going extinct 100â€“1,000 times faster than the natural background rate. ~1 million species currently threatened. Primary causes: land-use change (habitat loss), overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, climate change.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f1_kp2: Keystone species: species that play a disproportionately large role in their ecosystem. Removing them causes ecosystem collapse â€” e.g. wolves in Yellowstone controlled deer populations, which allowed vegetation to recover, affecting rivers.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f1_kp3: Biomes: large ecological communities defined by climate and vegetation â€” tropical rainforest (maximum biodiversity), temperate forest, grassland/savanna, desert (extreme conditions), tundra (permafrost), ocean (largest biome).
learnSch_environmental_studies_f1_kp4: Conservation strategies: protected areas (national parks, marine reserves), rewilding (restoring natural species and processes), ex-situ conservation (zoos, seed banks), CITES treaty (controls trade in endangered species).
learnSch_environmental_studies_f1_task: Research one endangered ecosystem (e.g. coral reefs, Amazon rainforest, peat bogs). For your chosen ecosystem: (a) describe its biodiversity, (b) explain three threats, (c) quantify the economic value of its ecosystem services, (d) evaluate three conservation strategies and their effectiveness.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f2_desc: Sustainability means meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs (Brundtland definition, 1987). Transitioning to a sustainable world requires changes in energy, food, transport, and consumption â€” and involves everyone from governments and corporations to individuals.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f2_kp0: Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): 17 goals adopted by all UN member states in 2015, targeting poverty, health, education, clean energy, climate action, and biodiversity by 2030.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f2_kp1: Renewable energy: solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, and tidal power produce electricity with minimal greenhouse gas emissions. Solar and wind costs have fallen 90%+ since 2010 â€” they are now the cheapest energy sources ever built.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f2_kp2: Circular economy: instead of takeâ€“makeâ€“dispose, materials are reused, repaired, and recycled in closed loops. Reduces waste, saves resources, and creates jobs.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f2_kp3: Food systems: agriculture accounts for ~25% of global greenhouse gas emissions. Reducing meat consumption (especially beef), cutting food waste, and shifting to sustainable farming (organic, agroforestry) are major levers.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f2_kp4: Individual action matters: carbon footprints vary enormously. Flying, eating meat, and driving petrol cars are the highest-impact individual choices. But systemic change â€” government policy, corporate practice, infrastructure â€” has far greater total impact.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f2_task: Calculate your monthly carbon footprint using an online calculator. Identify your three highest-impact activities. Research and propose one personal change and one systemic change (government or corporate policy) that would reduce your footprint. Present: current footprint, target footprint, and timeline.
learnSch_environmental_studies_f2_tp2: Food systems and individual vs. systemic action
learnUni_mathematics_f5_desc: Real analysis is the rigorous foundation of calculus, proving results about limits, continuity, differentiation, and integration using precise Îµ-Î´ definitions. It replaces intuitive reasoning with watertight logical arguments â€” a fundamental shift in mathematical maturity required at university level.
learnUni_mathematics_f5_kp0: Îµ-Î´ limit: lim_{xâ†’a} f(x) = L means for every Îµ>0 there exists Î´>0 such that 0<|xâˆ’a|<Î´ implies |f(x)âˆ’L|<Îµ. This eliminates ambiguity from the concept of 'approaching'.
learnUni_mathematics_f5_kp1: Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem: every bounded sequence in â„â¿ has a convergent subsequence. Heine-Borel: a subset of â„â¿ is compact iff it is closed and bounded.
learnUni_mathematics_f5_kp2: Intermediate Value Theorem: if f is continuous on [a,b] and f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, there exists c âˆˆ (a,b) with f(c) = 0. Proved rigorously from completeness of â„.
learnUni_mathematics_f5_kp3: Uniform continuity: a single Î´ works for all x simultaneously â€” stronger than pointwise continuity. Every continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous.
learnUni_mathematics_f5_kp4: Riemann vs. Lebesgue integral: the Lebesgue integral extends Riemann's by measuring sets (measure theory) and handles far more functions, including limits of sequences.
learnUni_mathematics_f5_task: Using the Îµ-Î´ definition, prove that lim_{xâ†’3} (2xâˆ’1) = 5. Then use the IVT to show that xâµ âˆ’ x âˆ’ 1 = 0 has at least one real solution. Prove that f(x) = 1/x is not uniformly continuous on (0,1) but is on [0.5, 2].
learnUni_mathematics_f6_desc: Abstract algebra generalises arithmetic operations to abstract structures: groups (one operation), rings (two operations), and fields (two operations with inverses). This unifies disparate mathematics and underpins modern cryptography (elliptic curves over finite fields) and physics (symmetry groups in particle physics).
learnUni_mathematics_f6_kp0: Group axioms: closure (aâ˜…b âˆˆ G), associativity ((aâ˜…b)â˜…c = aâ˜…(bâ˜…c)), identity (eâ˜…a = a), inverse (aâ˜…aâ»Â¹ = e). Examples: (â„¤,+), (â„š\{0},Ã—), dihedral group Dâ‚™ (symmetries of n-gon).
learnUni_mathematics_f6_kp1: Lagrange's theorem: if H is a subgroup of finite group G, then |H| divides |G|. Corollary: order of any element divides |G|. Normal subgroups allow quotient group construction G/N.
learnUni_mathematics_f6_kp2: Ring theory: (R,+,Ã—) where (R,+) is abelian group, Ã— is associative and distributive. Integral domains (no zero divisors). Ideals generalise subgroups for rings. Principal ideal domains (PIDs): every ideal generated by one element.
learnUni_mathematics_f6_kp3: Fields: every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse. Examples: â„š, â„, â„‚, and finite fields ð”½_p = â„¤/pâ„¤ (p prime). Finite fields underpin AES encryption and error-correcting codes.
learnUni_mathematics_f6_kp4: Galois theory: connects roots of polynomials to group theory. Galois group of a polynomial captures the symmetries of its roots. A polynomial is solvable by radicals iff its Galois group is a solvable group â€” explains the insolvability of degreeâ‰¥5 general equations.
learnUni_mathematics_f6_task: Prove that â„¤â‚† under addition is a group. Find all subgroups and verify Lagrange's theorem. Prove â„¤â‚‚Ã—â„¤â‚ƒ â‰… â„¤â‚† by exhibiting an explicit isomorphism. Then show â„¤â‚‚Ã—â„¤â‚‚ â‰‡ â„¤â‚„ by finding a property that distinguishes them.
learnUni_mathematics_f7_desc: Complex analysis extends calculus to functions of a complex variable. Holomorphic (complex-differentiable) functions are infinitely differentiable and encode global information in local data. Cauchy's integral theorem, residue calculus, and conformal mappings make it powerful across physics, engineering, and number theory.
learnUni_mathematics_f7_kp0: Complex differentiation: f'(z) = lim_{hâ†’0} [f(z+h)âˆ’f(z)]/h (h âˆˆ â„‚). Cauchy-Riemann equations: if f = u+iv is holomorphic then âˆ‚u/âˆ‚x = âˆ‚v/âˆ‚y and âˆ‚u/âˆ‚y = âˆ’âˆ‚v/âˆ‚x.
learnUni_mathematics_f7_kp1: Cauchy's integral theorem: for holomorphic f inside and on simple closed contour C, âˆ®_C f(z) dz = 0. Cauchy's formula: f(zâ‚€) = (1/2Ï€i) âˆ®_C f(z)/(zâˆ’zâ‚€) dz.
learnUni_mathematics_f7_kp2: Taylor and Laurent series: holomorphic functions expand as power series. Laurent series allows negative powers â€” essential near isolated singularities (poles, essential singularities).
learnUni_mathematics_f7_kp3: Residue theorem: âˆ®_C f(z) dz = 2Ï€i Ã— Î£ Res(f, zâ‚–) for all poles zâ‚– inside C. Residue at simple pole zâ‚€: Res = lim_{zâ†’zâ‚€} (zâˆ’zâ‚€)f(z). Used to evaluate real integrals by clever contour choice.
learnUni_mathematics_f7_kp4: Conformal mappings: holomorphic functions with non-zero derivative preserve angles locally. MÃ¶bius transformations map circles/lines to circles/lines. Used in fluid dynamics, electrostatics, and cartography.
learnUni_mathematics_f7_task: Verify f(z)=zÂ² is holomorphic via Cauchy-Riemann equations. Compute âˆ®_{|z|=2} dz/(zÂ²âˆ’1) using the residue theorem: find poles, check which lie inside |z|=2, compute residues, and apply the theorem. Verify by partial fractions.
learnUni_physics_f2_desc: Maxwell's equations unify electricity, magnetism, and optics into one elegant framework. They predict electromagnetic waves travelling at c, explain radiation, and form the basis of all electrical engineering. Understanding them at the vector calculus level is a hallmark of a physics education.
learnUni_physics_f2_kp0: Maxwell's equations: âˆ‡Â·E = Ï/Îµâ‚€, âˆ‡Â·B = 0, âˆ‡Ã—E = âˆ’âˆ‚B/âˆ‚t, âˆ‡Ã—B = Î¼â‚€(J + Îµâ‚€âˆ‚E/âˆ‚t). Together they unify all classical electromagnetic phenomena.
learnUni_physics_f2_kp1: Electromagnetic waves: in free space, E and B satisfy âˆ‡Â²E = Î¼â‚€Îµâ‚€âˆ‚Â²E/âˆ‚tÂ², giving wave speed c = 1/âˆš(Î¼â‚€Îµâ‚€) â‰ˆ 3Ã—10â¸ m/s. Light is an electromagnetic wave.
learnUni_physics_f2_kp2: Poynting vector S = (1/Î¼â‚€) EÃ—B: direction and rate of electromagnetic energy flow. Radiation pressure: EM waves carry momentum p = U/c.
learnUni_physics_f2_kp3: Boundary conditions at material interfaces: tangential E and normal B are continuous. Determines reflection and refraction (Fresnel equations give amplitudes; Snell's law gives angles).
learnUni_physics_f2_kp4: Multipole expansion: far from a charge distribution, fields are dominated by leading terms â€” monopole (1/rÂ²), dipole (1/rÂ³), quadrupole (1/râ´). Oscillating dipoles radiate EM waves â€” basis of antennas.
learnUni_physics_f2_task: Starting from Maxwell's equations in vacuum (Ï=0, J=0), derive the electromagnetic wave equation for E. Show c = 1/âˆš(Î¼â‚€Îµâ‚€). Then write the fields for a plane wave propagating in the z-direction and show that E, B, and k are mutually perpendicular and that |E|/|B| = c.
learnUni_physics_f3_desc: Statistical mechanics derives macroscopic thermodynamics from the statistical behaviour of microscopic constituents. The Boltzmann distribution, partition function, and entropy as log of microstates connect atomic physics to everyday thermal phenomena â€” from black-body radiation to phase transitions.
learnUni_physics_f3_kp0: Boltzmann distribution: probability of microstate with energy E is P âˆ e^{âˆ’E/k_BT}. Higher temperature allows occupation of higher energy states. This single equation underlies chemistry, astrophysics, and biology.
learnUni_physics_f3_kp1: Partition function Z = Î£áµ¢ e^{âˆ’Î²Eáµ¢} (Î²=1/k_BT). Free energy F = âˆ’k_BT ln Z. All thermodynamic quantities follow: âŸ¨EâŸ© = âˆ’âˆ‚ln Z/âˆ‚Î², entropy S = âˆ’âˆ‚F/âˆ‚T, pressure P = âˆ’âˆ‚F/âˆ‚V.
learnUni_physics_f3_kp2: Boltzmann entropy: S = k_B ln Î© (Î© = number of microstates). Second law: Î© increases spontaneously â†’ S always increases in isolated systems. Arrow of time emerges from statistics.
learnUni_physics_f3_kp3: Quantum statistics: bosons obey Bose-Einstein distribution (multiple particles per state â†’ superfluidity, lasers, Bose-Einstein condensate). Fermions obey Fermi-Dirac (one per state â†’ electron structure of metals, white dwarfs, neutron stars).
learnUni_physics_f3_kp4: Phase transitions: at critical temperature Tc, order parameter jumps (1st order) or rises continuously (2nd order). Ising model captures ferromagnetism. Near critical point: universal scaling laws, divergent correlation length, universality classes.
learnUni_physics_f3_task: Derive the partition function for a two-level system (energies 0 and Îµ). Find âŸ¨EâŸ©, C_V = âˆ‚âŸ¨EâŸ©/âˆ‚T, and entropy S. Sketch C_V vs. T (Schottky peak). Interpret the peak physically. Apply the Boltzmann factor to estimate the fraction of molecules in an excited state at T = 300 K if Îµ = 0.025 eV.
learnUni_physics_f4_desc: Quantum mechanics is the most accurately tested physical theory, correct to 12 decimal places in QED predictions. It replaces classical trajectories with wavefunctions evolving under the SchrÃ¶dinger equation and explains atoms, molecules, lasers, semiconductors, and MRI scanners. It is also deeply philosophically puzzling.
learnUni_physics_f4_kp0: Wavefunction Ïˆ(x,t): |Ïˆ|Â² is probability density. Normalisation: âˆ«|Ïˆ|Â² dx = 1. Time evolution: iâ„ âˆ‚Ïˆ/âˆ‚t = Ä¤Ïˆ. Stationary states: Ä¤Ïˆ_n = E_n Ïˆ_n â€” energy eigenstates.
learnUni_physics_f4_kp1: Infinite square well: Ïˆ_n = âˆš(2/L) sin(nÏ€x/L), E_n = nÂ²Ï€Â²â„Â²/(2mLÂ²). Quantised energy levels â€” explains atomic spectra. Zero-point energy Eâ‚ > 0 even at T=0.
learnUni_physics_f4_kp2: Quantum harmonic oscillator: E_n = (n+Â½)â„Ï‰, n=0,1,2,... Raising/lowering operators Ã¢Â±. Ground state has Gaussian wavefunction. Models molecular vibrations and field quanta.
learnUni_physics_f4_kp3: Hydrogen atom: spherically symmetric Coulomb potential. Solutions labelled by quantum numbers n (energy), l (angular momentum), m (magnetic). Orbital shapes (s, p, d, f) determine chemical bonding.
learnUni_physics_f4_kp4: Entanglement and Bell's theorem: two particles can be correlated in ways that cannot be explained by local hidden variables. Bell inequalities violated experimentally â€” proves non-locality of quantum mechanics. Basis for quantum cryptography.
learnUni_physics_f4_task: Solve the 1D infinite square well: write Ïˆ_n and E_n. Calculate Eâ‚ for an electron in L = 1 nm well. A particle is prepared in Ïˆ = (1/âˆš2)(Ïˆâ‚ + Ïˆâ‚‚). (a) Is this an energy eigenstate? (b) Calculate âŸ¨EâŸ©. (c) Describe how âŸ¨xâŸ© evolves in time. (d) Explain what happens when you measure the energy.
learnUni_physics_f5_desc: Modern optics treats light both as an electromagnetic wave (wave optics) and as photons (quantum optics). Wave optics explains interference, diffraction, and polarisation precisely. Photonics â€” the science and technology of photon generation and control â€” underpins lasers, optical fibres, solar cells, and emerging quantum technologies.
learnUni_physics_f5_kp0: Wave optics: interference and diffraction arise from Huygens-Fresnel principle (each wavefront point is a secondary source). Young's double slit gives fringe spacing Î”y = Î»L/d.
learnUni_physics_f5_kp1: Fourier optics: a lens performs a Fourier transform in the focal plane. Spatial filtering â€” blocking certain spatial frequencies â€” is the basis of image processing and microscopy.
learnUni_physics_f5_kp2: Laser physics: population inversion achieved by optical or electrical pumping. Stimulated emission produces coherent, monochromatic, directional light. Laser cavities select modes. Applications: surgery, lidar, optical tweezers, barcode scanning.
learnUni_physics_f5_kp3: Optical fibres: total internal reflection (angle > critical angle Î¸_c = arcsin(nâ‚‚/nâ‚)) traps light in core. Single-mode fibres for long-haul communications; multi-mode for short distances. Attenuation ~0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm.
learnUni_physics_f5_kp4: Quantum optics: photon statistics (coherent vs. thermal vs. squeezed light), single-photon sources, entangled photon pairs (via spontaneous parametric down-conversion). Applications: quantum key distribution (BB84 protocol), quantum teleportation.
learnUni_physics_f5_task: A diffraction grating with 500 lines/mm is illuminated by light of Î»=600 nm. Find the angles for m=1 and m=2 diffraction orders. Calculate the resolving power R = mN for N = 1000 lines. Then design an optical fibre with core nâ‚=1.50 and cladding nâ‚‚=1.45: find the critical angle and the numerical aperture NA = âˆš(nâ‚Â²âˆ’nâ‚‚Â²).
learnUni_chemistry_f0_desc: Physical chemistry applies physics â€” thermodynamics, kinetics, quantum mechanics â€” to chemical systems. It explains why reactions occur (thermodynamics), how fast they proceed (kinetics), and what determines molecular structure (quantum chemistry). It is the theoretical backbone of all chemistry.
learnUni_chemistry_f0_kp0: Gibbs free energy: Î”G = Î”H âˆ’ TÎ”S. Î”G < 0: spontaneous; Î”G > 0: non-spontaneous; Î”G = 0: equilibrium. At equilibrium: Î”GÂ° = âˆ’RT ln K.
learnUni_chemistry_f0_kp1: Chemical kinetics: rate = k[A]áµ[B]â¿. First-order: tâ‚/â‚‚ = ln2/k. Arrhenius equation: k = Ae^{âˆ’Ea/RT}. Transition state theory: rate determined by Î”Gâ€¡ (Gibbs energy of transition state).
learnUni_chemistry_f0_kp2: Molecular orbital theory: atomic orbitals combine (LCAO) to form bonding (lower energy) and antibonding (higher energy) MOs. Bond order = Â½(bonding âˆ’ antibonding electrons).
learnUni_chemistry_f0_kp3: Spectroscopy: UV-Vis (Beer-Lambert: A=Îµcl, electronic transitions), IR (molecular vibrations â€” functional group fingerprints), NMR (nuclear spin environments give connectivity and stereochemistry), mass spec (molecular mass and fragmentation).
learnUni_chemistry_f0_kp4: Colligative properties (depend on number of solute particles, not identity): boiling point elevation (Î”T_b = K_b m), freezing point depression (Î”T_f = K_f m), osmotic pressure (Î  = MRT). Used in cryoscopy and osmometry.
learnUni_chemistry_f0_task: For the reaction Nâ‚‚ + 3Hâ‚‚ â†’ 2NHâ‚ƒ at 298 K, Î”HÂ° = âˆ’92 kJ/mol and Î”SÂ° = âˆ’198 J/molÂ·K. (a) Calculate Î”GÂ° and K. (b) Is the reaction spontaneous at 298 K? At 600 K? (c) Interpret how temperature and pressure affect yield in the Haber process. Relate to Le Chatelier's principle.
learnUni_chemistry_f1_desc: University organic chemistry studies reaction mechanisms at the electron level, stereochemistry, synthesis planning, and functional group transformations. Mastery is essential for pharmacology, materials science, biochemistry, and chemical biology.
learnUni_chemistry_f1_kp0: Curly arrow formalism: full-headed arrows show electron pair movement (nucleophile to electrophile). Half-headed arrows show radical mechanisms. Every arrow must start from an electron source (lone pair or bond).
learnUni_chemistry_f1_kp1: SN2 mechanism: concerted one-step backside attack â†’ inversion of configuration (Walden inversion). Favoured: strong nucleophile, primary substrate, polar aprotic solvent (DMF, DMSO, acetone).
learnUni_chemistry_f1_kp2: SN1 mechanism: two steps via carbocation intermediate. Favoured: tertiary substrate, polar protic solvent (water, ethanol). Rate = k[substrate] only. Gives racemisation.
learnUni_chemistry_f1_kp3: Stereochemistry: chiral centres, enantiomers, diastereomers. Cahn-Ingold-Prelog (CIP) rules assign R/S configuration. Optical activity measured by polarimetry. Thalidomide: R-enantiomer therapeutic, S-enantiomer teratogenic â€” the importance of stereochemical purity.
learnUni_chemistry_f1_kp4: Retrosynthetic analysis (Corey): plan synthesis backwards â€” identify disconnections (breaking key bonds) to expose readily available starting materials. Protecting group strategy, functional group interconversions, and oxidation state tracking are key skills.
learnUni_chemistry_f1_task: Propose a complete mechanism with curly arrows for: (a) SN2 reaction of (R)-2-bromobutane with NaOH â€” show the product configuration, (b) SN1 reaction of (CHâ‚ƒ)â‚ƒCBr with water â€” show the carbocation, racemisation, and both stereoisomers of the product. Explain why the same substrate cannot undergo both mechanisms simultaneously.
learnUni_chemistry_f2_desc: Inorganic chemistry encompasses coordination chemistry, organometallics, solid-state materials, and bioinorganic chemistry. Transition metals with their rich d-orbital chemistry underlie industrial catalysis, functional materials, and essential biological processes.
learnUni_chemistry_f2_kp0: Coordination complexes: central metal ion + ligands (Lewis bases donating electron pairs). Chelate effect: polydentate ligands (EDTA) form more stable complexes than monodentate ones â€” thermodynamic advantage from entropy.
learnUni_chemistry_f2_kp1: Crystal field theory (CFT): d-orbitals split in a ligand field. Octahedral splitting Î”_o separates tâ‚‚g (lower) and e_g (higher) sets. Strong field ligands (CNâ», CO) â†’ large Î”_o â†’ low spin. Weak field (Fâ», Hâ‚‚O) â†’ small Î”_o â†’ high spin.
learnUni_chemistry_f2_kp2: Colour and magnetism from CFT: d-d transitions absorb visible light â†’ complementary colour observed. High-spin complexes are paramagnetic (unpaired electrons); low-spin are diamagnetic.
learnUni_chemistry_f2_kp3: Organometallic chemistry: metal-carbon bonds (metal carbonyls M(CO)â‚™, metallocenes, Wilkinson's catalyst [RhCl(PPhâ‚ƒ)â‚ƒ] for hydrogenation). 18-electron rule predicts stability of organometallic complexes.
learnUni_chemistry_f2_kp4: Bioinorganic chemistry: Fe in haemoglobin (reversible Oâ‚‚ binding), Fe-S clusters (electron transfer), Zn in carbonic anhydrase, Mg in chlorophyll (photosynthesis), Cu in cytochrome c oxidase (respiration), Co in Vitamin Bâ‚â‚‚.
learnUni_chemistry_f2_task: Draw the octahedral splitting diagram for [Fe(CN)â‚†]Â³â» and [Fe(Hâ‚‚O)â‚†]Â³âº. Assign electrons using CFT, determine spin state and number of unpaired electrons for each. Predict which absorbs higher-energy light and why. Calculate the oxidation state of Fe in each complex and identify the ligands as strong or weak field using the spectrochemical series.
learnUni_chemistry_f3_desc: Analytical chemistry provides the tools to identify substances and measure their concentrations in complex matrices. Modern analytical techniques â€” HPLC, GC-MS, NMR, atomic absorption â€” are indispensable across pharmaceuticals, environmental monitoring, food safety, forensics, and clinical diagnostics.
learnUni_chemistry_f3_kp0: Titrimetric analysis: acid-base, complexometric (EDTA), redox, and precipitation titrations. End-point detection: indicators, pH meters, conductimetry, potentiometry. Standard solutions, primary standards, and back-titrations.
learnUni_chemistry_f3_kp1: Chromatography: stationary phase retards components differentially. GC (volatile analytes, thermal detection), HPLC (non-volatile, UV/fluorescence/MS detection), IC (ion exchange), SFC (supercritical fluid). Resolution R = 2(tâ‚‚âˆ’tâ‚)/(wâ‚+wâ‚‚).
learnUni_chemistry_f3_kp2: Mass spectrometry: electron ionisation (EI) produces radical cations that fragment characteristically. High-resolution MS gives exact molecular formula. LC-MS/MS provides selectivity for complex biological matrices.
learnUni_chemistry_f3_kp3: NMR spectroscopy: Â¹H NMR: chemical shift (Î´, ppm) indicates environment, coupling (J, Hz) shows adjacent H connectivity, integration gives ratio of H types. Â¹Â³C NMR and 2D techniques (COSY, HMBC, NOESY) determine full 3D structure.
learnUni_chemistry_f3_kp4: Method validation: limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), linearity (RÂ²), accuracy (% recovery), precision (RSD%), selectivity (no interference), robustness (reproducible across labs). Required for regulatory submission (FDA, EMA).
learnUni_chemistry_f3_task: An unknown organic compound shows: MW = 122 by MS; IR: broad OH stretch 3200â€“3500, carbonyl 1680 cmâ»Â¹; Â¹H NMR: Î´ 7.8 (d, 2H), 7.5 (d, 2H), 3.9 (s, 3H). Propose a structure. Justify each spectroscopic observation. Then describe how you would validate a HPLC method to quantify this compound in a pharmaceutical tablet.
learnUni_chemistry_f4_desc: Biochemistry studies the chemical processes of life â€” enzyme catalysis, metabolic pathways, DNA replication, and cell signalling. It bridges chemistry and medicine: understanding enzyme kinetics enables drug design; understanding metabolism reveals targets for treating diabetes, cancer, and obesity; DNA biochemistry underpins CRISPR gene editing.
learnUni_chemistry_f4_kp0: Enzyme kinetics: Michaelis-Menten equation v = Vmax[S]/(Km+[S]). Km: substrate concentration at half-maximal velocity â€” reflects enzyme-substrate affinity. Vmax: maximum rate at saturating substrate. Linearised by Lineweaver-Burk plot (1/v vs. 1/[S]).
learnUni_chemistry_f4_kp1: Enzyme inhibition: competitive (inhibitor binds active site, increases apparent Km, Vmax unchanged); non-competitive (inhibitor binds elsewhere, decreases Vmax, Km unchanged); irreversible (covalent â€” aspirin acetylates COX permanently).
learnUni_chemistry_f4_kp2: Metabolism: catabolism (glucose â†’ 2 pyruvate via glycolysis, 2 ATP; Krebs cycle 2 ATP; oxidative phosphorylation ~32 ATP via proton gradient across inner mitochondrial membrane). Total â‰ˆ36 ATP per glucose.
learnUni_chemistry_f4_kp3: DNA biochemistry: double helix stabilised by Watson-Crick base pairing (A:T, G:C) and base stacking (Ï€-Ï€ interactions). DNA replication semi-conservative: DNA polymerase adds 5'â†’3', requires primer (RNA). Error rate ~1 in 10â¹ after proofreading.
learnUni_chemistry_f4_kp4: Protein structure and folding: primary (amino acid sequence) â†’ secondary (Î±-helix: H-bonds i to i+4; Î²-sheet: parallel or anti-parallel strands) â†’ tertiary (3D fold, hydrophobic collapse) â†’ quaternary (multiple subunits). Chaperones assist folding; misfolding causes diseases (Alzheimer, Parkinson, prion diseases).
learnUni_chemistry_f4_task: An enzyme has Km = 2 mM and Vmax = 60 Î¼mol/min. (a) Calculate v at [S] = 1, 2, 6 mM. (b) Draw Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk plots for both uninhibited and competitively inhibited states (inhibitor doubles apparent Km). (c) Drug design: propose how knowledge of the enzyme's active site geometry could guide the design of a transition-state analogue inhibitor.
learnUni_biology_f2_desc: University ecology applies quantitative models to population dynamics, species interactions, and ecosystem function. Conservation biology uses ecological theory â€” minimum viable populations, reserve design, corridor ecology â€” to protect biodiversity in a world undergoing rapid environmental change.
learnUni_biology_f2_kp0: Population models: logistic growth dN/dt = rN(1âˆ’N/K) where r is intrinsic rate of increase and K is carrying capacity. Maximum sustainable yield (MSY) harvested at N = K/2 where dN/dt is maximum.
learnUni_biology_f2_kp1: Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model: dN/dt = rNâˆ’aNP (prey); dP/dt = baNPâˆ’mP (predator). Populations oscillate cyclically â€” demonstrated by hare-lynx data. Stability analysis via eigenvalues of Jacobian.
learnUni_biology_f2_kp2: Island biogeography (MacArthur & Wilson): S_eq from balance of immigration (decreasing with S) and extinction (increasing with S) rates. Larger, closer islands have higher species richness â†’ SLOSS debate in reserve design.
learnUni_biology_f2_kp3: Metapopulation theory (Levins): a network of local populations connected by dispersal. Fraction occupied patches p evolves: dp/dt = cp(1âˆ’p) âˆ’ ep. Critical patch occupancy below which global extinction is inevitable.
learnUni_biology_f2_kp4: Conservation genomics: population genetics tools (FST, effective population size Ne, inbreeding coefficient F) identify genetically distinct populations, measure gene flow, and diagnose inbreeding depression in captive breeding programmes.
learnUni_biology_f2_task: A population of 200 wolves (K=1000, r=0.3/yr) is harvested annually. (a) Calculate the population growth rate at N=200 and at MSY. (b) What annual harvest equals the growth rate at MSY? (c) A second population on a neighbouring island has Ne=15 â€” what risks does this face, and what conservation actions are appropriate?
learnUni_biology_f3_desc: Cell biology at university level investigates signal transduction, the cell cycle, epigenetics, and the molecular mechanisms of disease. Modern molecular biology â€” PCR, gene cloning, RNA-seq, CRISPR â€” provides tools to read, write, and edit genomes. These technologies are transforming medicine, agriculture, and basic science.
learnUni_biology_f3_kp0: Cell cycle regulation: cyclins and CDKs (cyclin-dependent kinases) drive cell cycle transitions. CDK inhibitors (p21, p27) act as brakes. Rb tumour suppressor sequesters E2F transcription factor until phosphorylated by Cyclin D-CDK4/6.
learnUni_biology_f3_kp1: Signal transduction pathways: RTK (receptor tyrosine kinase) activation â†’ RAS/MAPK cascade â†’ transcription factor phosphorylation â†’ gene expression changes. PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway: growth and survival. Both are frequently mutated in cancer.
learnUni_biology_f3_kp2: Epigenetics: heritable changes in gene expression without DNA sequence change. Histone modifications (acetylation â€” active; methylation â€” context-dependent), DNA methylation (CpG methylation silences genes), chromatin remodelling. Epigenetic reprogramming in embryogenesis and cancer.
learnUni_biology_f3_kp3: RNA biology: splicing (spliceosomes remove introns), alternative splicing (one gene â†’ multiple proteins â€” ~95% of human genes are alternatively spliced), non-coding RNA (miRNA silences mRNA, lncRNA regulates chromatin), ribozymes.
learnUni_biology_f3_kp4: CRISPR-Cas9: guide RNA directs Cas9 to target sequence (next to PAM sequence). Cas9 cleaves both strands. Repair by NHEJ (error-prone â†’ knockout) or HDR (template â†’ precise edit). Applications: gene therapy, agriculture, basic research, diagnostics.
learnUni_biology_f3_task: A cancer cell has: (1) constitutively active RAS mutation, (2) deleted Rb gene, (3) p53 mutation. Trace the molecular consequences of each mutation through the cell cycle and signalling pathways. Identify: which checkpoint fails for each, what the consequences are for cell proliferation, and two therapeutic strategies targeting these defects.
learnUni_biology_f4_desc: Neuroscience bridges molecular biology, physiology, and psychology to explain how the brain generates behaviour. From ion channels to neural circuits to consciousness, it is one of the most complex and exciting frontiers in biology. Neurological diseases affect 1 in 6 people globally â€” understanding them requires deep knowledge of brain structure and function.
learnUni_biology_f4_kp0: Action potential: threshold depolarisation opens voltage-gated Naâº channels (rapid depolarisation to +40 mV) â†’ inactivation of Naâº channels + opening of Kâº channels (repolarisation) â†’ hyperpolarisation (refractory period). All-or-nothing, propagates without decrement along axon.
learnUni_biology_f4_kp1: Synaptic transmission: CaÂ²âº influx triggers SNARE-mediated vesicle fusion â†’ neurotransmitter release â†’ postsynaptic receptor binding. Ionotropic receptors (AMPA, GABA_A): fast ion flux. Metabotropic receptors (mGluR, Î²-adrenergic): slow G-protein cascades.
learnUni_biology_f4_kp2: Synaptic plasticity: LTP (long-term potentiation) â€” NMDA receptor coincidence detector (requires both glutamate binding AND postsynaptic depolarisation) â†’ AMPA receptor insertion â†’ increased synaptic strength. Molecular basis of learning and memory.
learnUni_biology_f4_kp3: Neural circuits: excitatory (glutamatergic) and inhibitory (GABAergic) neurons form recurrent circuits. E/I balance: disruption causes epilepsy (excess excitation) or anaesthesia (excess inhibition). Oscillations (theta, gamma) encode information and synchronise brain regions.
learnUni_biology_f4_kp4: Neurological disease mechanisms: Alzheimer's (amyloid-Î² plaques disrupt synaptic transmission; tau hyperphosphorylation destabilises microtubules â†’ neuronal death), Parkinson's (dopaminergic neuron loss in substantia nigra; Î±-synuclein Lewy bodies), Huntington's (polyglutamine expansion in huntingtin â†’ toxic protein aggregation, striatal neurodegeneration).
learnUni_biology_f4_task: You record from a hippocampal neuron. High-frequency stimulation of the presynaptic neuron causes LTP. Describe step-by-step the molecular events: NMDA receptor activation, CaÂ²âº signalling, CaMKII activation, AMPA receptor phosphorylation and insertion, and long-term structural changes. Then explain why blocking NMDA receptors prevents LTP formation.
learnUni_biology_f5_desc: Immunology is the study of the body's defence mechanisms. The immune system discriminates self from non-self, destroys pathogens and cancer cells, and creates immunological memory. Dysregulation causes autoimmune diseases, allergy, and immunodeficiency. Modern immunotherapies â€” checkpoint inhibitors, CAR-T cells, mRNA vaccines â€” are transforming medicine.
learnUni_biology_f5_kp0: Innate immunity: pattern recognition receptors (PRRs, e.g. Toll-like receptors) detect pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs). Activate NF-ÎºB â†’ inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-Î±, IL-1Î²). Complement cascade opsonises and lyses pathogens.
learnUni_biology_f5_kp1: Adaptive immunity: antigen-presenting cells (dendritic cells, macrophages) process antigens and present peptides on MHC I (to CD8âº cytotoxic T cells) or MHC II (to CD4âº helper T cells). Clonal selection and expansion of antigen-specific lymphocytes.
learnUni_biology_f5_kp2: B cell activation and antibodies: T-dependent activation requires helper T cells (CD40L-CD40 interaction + cytokines). Germinal centre reaction: somatic hypermutation + affinity maturation + class switching (IgM â†’ IgG, IgA, IgE). Memory B cells persist for decades.
learnUni_biology_f5_kp3: Immune tolerance and autoimmunity: central tolerance â€” autoreactive T cells deleted in thymus (negative selection). Peripheral tolerance â€” regulatory T cells (Tregs) suppress autoreactive cells. Failure: systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes.
learnUni_biology_f5_kp4: Cancer immunotherapy: PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors block tumour immune evasion â†’ T cell reactivation (pembrolizumab, nivolumab â€” Nobel Prize 2018). CAR-T cell therapy: engineer patient T cells with chimeric antigen receptor targeting tumour antigen â€” dramatic responses in B cell leukaemia.
learnUni_biology_f5_task: A patient presents with recurrent bacterial infections and very low immunoglobulin levels. Peripheral blood shows absent B cells but normal T cells. Diagnose the immunodeficiency (X-linked agammaglobulinaemia â€” BTK mutation). Explain the molecular basis: how BTK normally signals during B cell development, what goes wrong, and how intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy works.
learnUni_computer_science_f2_desc: Algorithms and data structures are the theoretical core of computer science. The right data structure makes an algorithm efficient; the wrong one makes it impractical. Big-O analysis, graph algorithms, and dynamic programming are the fundamental tools every software engineer must master.
learnUni_computer_science_f2_kp0: Big-O: O(1) constant, O(log n) binary search, O(n) linear scan, O(n log n) merge sort, O(nÂ²) bubble sort, O(2â¿) exponential. Î© (best case), Î˜ (tight bound). Amortised analysis for dynamic arrays.
learnUni_computer_science_f2_kp1: Core structures: array (O(1) access), linked list (O(1) head insert), hash table (average O(1) all ops), BST (O(log n) balanced), heap (O(log n) insert/extract), trie (string prefix operations).
learnUni_computer_science_f2_kp2: Graph algorithms: BFS (shortest path unweighted, O(V+E)), DFS (cycle detection, topological sort), Dijkstra (shortest path weighted, O((V+E)log V)), Bellman-Ford (negative weights, O(VE)), Floyd-Warshall (all pairs, O(VÂ³)).
learnUni_computer_science_f2_kp3: Dynamic programming: optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems. Memoisation (top-down) vs. tabulation (bottom-up). Classic problems: 0/1 Knapsack, LCS, Edit Distance, Matrix Chain Multiplication, Coin Change.
learnUni_computer_science_f2_kp4: NP-completeness: P (polynomial-time solvable), NP (polynomial-time verifiable). NP-complete problems (SAT, TSP, Clique) â€” no known polynomial algorithm. Cook-Levin theorem: SAT is NP-complete. Reductions prove NP-hardness.
learnUni_computer_science_f2_task: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm (pseudocode with priority queue) on a 6-node graph. Run it step-by-step, showing the distance table and priority queue state at each iteration. Prove time complexity O((V+E)log V). Then explain: if the graph had negative edges, why does Dijkstra fail, and how does Bellman-Ford handle them?
learnUni_computer_science_f3_desc: Operating systems are the software layer between hardware and applications â€” managing processes, memory, storage, and security. Understanding OS internals enables you to write efficient, concurrent code, understand system performance, and design secure distributed systems.
learnUni_computer_science_f3_kp0: Process vs. thread: a process has its own address space and resources; threads share the process address space. Context switching: OS saves/restores CPU state (registers, PC, stack pointer). Scheduling: FCFS, SJF, Round Robin, Priority, Multilevel Queue.
learnUni_computer_science_f3_kp1: Concurrency hazards: race conditions, deadlock (Coffman conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait), starvation, livelock. Synchronisation: mutex, semaphore, monitor (condition variables), read-write locks.
learnUni_computer_science_f3_kp2: Memory management: paging â€” divide virtual address space into fixed-size pages mapped to physical frames via page table. TLB caches recent translations. Page replacement algorithms: FIFO, LRU, Clock (second chance). Thrashing when working set > RAM.
learnUni_computer_science_f3_kp3: File systems: VFS (Virtual File System) layer for portability. Inodes: file metadata + data block pointers. Journaling (ext4, NTFS) for crash consistency. B-trees used in HFS+, NTFS for directory indexing. RAID levels 0,1,5,6 â€” performance and redundancy trade-offs.
learnUni_computer_science_f3_kp4: Security architecture: ring protection (user mode vs. kernel mode, syscalls as controlled boundary), capability-based access control, mandatory access control (SELinux/AppArmor), hypervisors (type 1 bare metal: VMware ESXi, Xen; type 2 hosted: VirtualBox). Containers (Docker) use cgroups and namespaces â€” not true VMs.
learnUni_computer_science_f3_task: Three processes share resources R1 and R2. P1: holds R1, needs R2. P2: holds R2, needs R1. P3: needs both. (a) Draw the resource allocation graph and detect the deadlock. (b) Show a safe state using the Banker's Algorithm: Max=[R1:2,R2:1], [R1:1,R2:2], [R1:2,R2:2]; Available=[R1:1,R2:1]. Find a safe execution sequence. (c) Propose a protocol to prevent this deadlock.
learnUni_computer_science_f4_desc: Software engineering applies systematic methods to building reliable, maintainable, scalable software. Modern practices â€” Agile, DevOps, microservices, CI/CD â€” have transformed how software is delivered. Core engineering disciplines â€” requirements, architecture, testing, and security â€” remain essential regardless of methodology.
learnUni_computer_science_f4_kp0: Agile (Scrum, XP, Kanban): iterative 2-week sprints, daily standups, sprint retrospectives, backlog grooming. DevOps: development and operations integrated; CI/CD pipelines (GitLab CI, Jenkins, GitHub Actions) automate build, test, deploy on every commit.
learnUni_computer_science_f4_kp1: Software architecture patterns: layered (UI â†’ business logic â†’ data access), microservices (independently deployable services with own databases, communicate via REST/gRPC/messaging), event-driven (services react to events â€” Kafka, RabbitMQ), CQRS+Event Sourcing.
learnUni_computer_science_f4_kp2: Design patterns (GoF): Creational (Singleton, Factory, Builder, Prototype), Structural (Adapter, Decorator, Facade, Proxy), Behavioural (Observer, Strategy, Command, Template Method, Iterator). Know when to apply â€” don't over-engineer.
learnUni_computer_science_f4_kp3: Testing strategy: unit (fast, isolated â€” mock dependencies), integration (multiple components), contract (API compatibility), system (end-to-end), acceptance (user story validation). TDD: red-green-refactor. Mutation testing verifies test quality.
learnUni_computer_science_f4_kp4: Security in software engineering (OWASP Top 10): injection (SQL, command), broken authentication, XSS, insecure direct object references, security misconfiguration. Threat modelling (STRIDE), secure code review, dependency vulnerability scanning (Snyk, Dependabot).
learnUni_computer_science_f4_task: Design a ride-sharing application: (a) Write 5 user stories with acceptance criteria. (b) Draw a C4 architecture diagram (context, container, component levels). (c) Identify 3 design patterns used and justify. (d) Write unit tests (pseudocode) for the 'calculate fare' function covering: base fare, surge pricing, minimum fare, and edge cases (zero distance, negative inputs). (e) Identify 3 OWASP security risks and mitigations.
learnUni_computer_science_f5_desc: Machine learning (ML) enables systems to learn from data. Deep learning â€” neural networks with many layers â€” has driven breakthroughs in vision, language, and games. Understanding ML theory (bias-variance, VC dimension, regularisation) and practice (PyTorch, hyperparameter tuning, deployment) is now a core competency in computer science.
learnUni_computer_science_f5_kp0: Supervised learning: minimise loss L(Î¸) over training data via gradient descent Î¸ â† Î¸ âˆ’ Î±âˆ‡L. Stochastic gradient descent (SGD), Adam optimiser. Regularisation: L2 (weight decay, shrinks weights), L1 (sparsity), dropout (randomly zeroes neurons during training).
learnUni_computer_science_f5_kp1: Neural network architecture: fully connected (dense), convolutional (CNN â€” weight sharing, translation invariance â€” ideal for images), recurrent (RNN, LSTM, GRU â€” sequential data), attention mechanism (Transformer â€” self-attention, O(nÂ²) complexity, but parallelisable).
learnUni_computer_science_f5_kp2: Bias-variance trade-off: training error â‰ˆ biasÂ²; test error âˆ’ training error â‰ˆ variance. Under-fitting: high bias (model too simple). Over-fitting: high variance (model memorises training data). Regularisation, more data, and simpler models address over-fitting.
learnUni_computer_science_f5_kp3: Evaluation beyond accuracy: for imbalanced classes â€” precision (TP/TP+FP), recall (TP/TP+FN), F1 = 2PR/(P+R), AUC-ROC (area under receiver operating characteristic curve). k-fold cross-validation gives robust generalisation estimate.
learnUni_computer_science_f5_kp4: Foundation models and responsible AI: large language models (GPT, BERT) pre-trained on vast data, fine-tuned for specific tasks. Hallucinations, bias, and misuse are serious risks. EU AI Act (2024) classifies AI systems by risk and imposes requirements for high-risk applications.
learnUni_computer_science_f5_task: You build a sentiment classifier (positive/negative reviews). Training accuracy: 97%; validation accuracy: 68%. (a) Diagnose the problem. (b) List 4 techniques to fix it. (c) Your dataset is 90% positive â€” calculate accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 if you classify everything as positive. Why is F1 a better metric? (d) Draw precision-recall curves for two models and explain which you would choose for a medical diagnosis task.
learnUni_economics_f1_desc: Microeconomics analyses decision-making by households, firms, and markets. Consumer and firm theory, market structures from perfect competition to monopoly, externalities, and game theory provide the core analytical toolkit for understanding prices, market outcomes, and the welfare effects of policy.
learnUni_economics_f1_kp0: Consumer theory: utility maximisation subject to budget constraint. At optimum: MU_x/P_x = MU_y/P_y (equal marginal utility per pound). Indifference curves + budget line â†’ tangency condition MRS = price ratio.
learnUni_economics_f1_kp1: Firm theory: profit maximisation where MR = MC. Perfect competition: P = MR = MC in long-run equilibrium, zero economic profit. Short run: shutdown if P < min(AVC).
learnUni_economics_f1_kp2: Market structures: monopoly (MR < P, deadweight loss = welfare loss, natural monopoly regulated by price cap or cost-plus); oligopoly (Cournot â€” quantity competition, Bertrand â€” price competition â†’ marginal cost pricing).
learnUni_economics_f1_kp3: Externalities and public goods: negative externality â†’ overproduction (Pigouvian tax = MEC corrects). Positive externality â†’ underproduction (subsidy corrects). Public goods: non-excludable + non-rival â†’ free rider problem â†’ undersupply by market.
learnUni_economics_f1_kp4: Game theory: Nash equilibrium (no unilateral profitable deviation). Prisoner's dilemma: dominant strategy leads to Pareto-inferior outcome. Repeated games: cooperation sustainable via trigger strategies if discount factor high enough. Auction theory: first-price (bid below value), second-price/Vickrey (dominant strategy to bid true value).
learnUni_economics_f1_task: Two airlines compete on a route. Each can set High or Low price. Payoff matrix (profit Â£m): {H,H}=(10,10), {H,L}=(2,15), {L,H}=(15,2), {L,L}=(5,5). (a) Find Nash equilibrium. (b) Is it Pareto optimal? (c) If the game is repeated indefinitely, when can cooperation be sustained (derive condition on discount factor Î´)? (d) How does competition law view airline price coordination?
learnUni_economics_f2_desc: Econometrics provides the statistical tools to test economic theories and estimate causal relationships from observational data. OLS regression, instrumental variables, and panel data methods are the workhorses. The modern 'credibility revolution' emphasises rigorous identification of causal effects using natural experiments.
learnUni_economics_f2_kp0: OLS: minimise Î£Îµáµ¢Â² to estimate Î²Ì‚ = (X'X)â»Â¹X'y. Gauss-Markov: OLS is BLUE under no endogeneity, homoskedasticity, no perfect multicollinearity. Unbiasedness: E[Î²Ì‚] = Î². Consistency: Î²Ì‚ â†’_p Î² as n â†’ âˆž.
learnUni_economics_f2_kp1: Endogeneity: E[Îµ|X] â‰  0 due to omitted variable bias, reverse causality, or measurement error â†’ OLS biased and inconsistent. IV solution: find instrument Z correlated with X (relevance: Cov(Z,X) â‰  0) but not with Îµ (exclusion: Cov(Z,Îµ) = 0).
learnUni_economics_f2_kp2: Panel data: Yáµ¢â‚œ = Î± + Î²Xáµ¢â‚œ + Î¼áµ¢ + Îµáµ¢â‚œ. Fixed effects (within estimator): eliminates time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity Î¼áµ¢. Random effects: more efficient if Î¼áµ¢ uncorrelated with X (test: Hausman). First difference: Î”yáµ¢â‚œ = Î²Î”xáµ¢â‚œ + Î”Îµáµ¢â‚œ.
learnUni_economics_f2_kp3: Difference-in-differences (DiD): compare treatment and control groups before/after a policy change. Î²_DiD = (È²_{treatment,post} âˆ’ È²_{treatment,pre}) âˆ’ (È²_{control,post} âˆ’ È²_{control,pre}). Key assumption: parallel trends (absent treatment, both groups would have evolved identically).
learnUni_economics_f2_kp4: Regression discontinuity (RD): treatment is assigned based on whether a running variable crosses a threshold. Compares outcomes just above and below the cutoff â€” local randomisation. Sharp RD: all cross the threshold. Fuzzy RD: only some â€” use as instrument.
learnUni_economics_f2_task: You want to estimate the causal effect of university education on earnings. OLS gives Î²Ì‚ = 0.08 (8% wage premium per year of education). (a) Why is OLS likely biased? (b) Propose an instrument â€” state why it satisfies relevance and exclusion restrictions. (c) Describe a DiD strategy using a natural experiment (e.g. a tuition fee change) with parallel trends assumption. (d) Interpret a regression discontinuity design around a scholarship eligibility threshold.
learnUni_economics_f3_desc: Behavioural economics incorporates psychological insights into economic analysis. People are not perfectly rational â€” they are loss-averse, present-biased, influenced by framing and social norms. These deviations are predictable and systematic, enabling better policy design through nudges and choice architecture.
learnUni_economics_f3_kp0: Prospect theory (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979): people evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point. Value function: concave for gains (risk aversion), convex for losses (risk seeking), steeper for losses than gains (Î» â‰ˆ 2 â€” loss aversion coefficient).
learnUni_economics_f3_kp1: Probability weighting: people overweight small probabilities (explains lotteries and insurance) and underweight large probabilities. Cumulative prospect theory handles compound lotteries.
learnUni_economics_f3_kp2: Present bias: quasi-hyperbolic discounting U = uâ‚€ + Î²Î£Î´áµ—uâ‚œ with Î² < 1. Generates time inconsistency â€” plans made now won't be followed. Explains over-eating, under-saving, procrastination. Sophisticates know they're biased; naives don't â€” different policy implications.
learnUni_economics_f3_kp3: Nudge theory (Thaler & Sunstein, 2008): choice architecture shapes decisions without restricting freedom. Default rules (pension auto-enrolment), social norm messages ('most households pay on time'), and simplification reduce barriers to desirable behaviour.
learnUni_economics_f3_kp4: Experimental economics and RCTs: lab experiments test theory in controlled settings. Field experiments (RCTs) test interventions at scale. Randomistas (Banerjee, Duflo â€” Nobel 2019) revolutionised development economics by rigorously testing specific interventions.
learnUni_economics_f3_task: Model a present-biased individual (Î²=0.7, Î´=0.95) deciding whether to exercise today (cost c=5 utils) for future benefit (B=10 utils received next week). (a) Does the individual plan on Sunday to exercise on Monday? (b) When Monday arrives, do they actually exercise? (c) Explain time inconsistency. (d) Design a commitment device to help them, and compare it to a financial penalty nudge.
learnUni_economics_f4_desc: Development economics studies the causes of poverty and the policies that promote prosperity. Modern development economics has moved from grand theories toward rigorous empirical evidence on specific interventions â€” using RCTs, natural experiments, and big data. The field engages with growth, institutions, health, education, and inequality.
learnUni_economics_f4_kp0: Solow growth model: Y = AK^Î± L^{1âˆ’Î±}. Steady state: investment = depreciation; savings rate and population growth determine K/L ratio. Diminishing returns â†’ conditional convergence. Residual A (total factor productivity) drives long-run growth â€” but is exogenous in Solow.
learnUni_economics_f4_kp1: Endogenous growth (Romer 1990): knowledge KÌƒ enters production without diminishing returns. R&D sector produces new ideas. Knowledge spillovers (non-rival, partially excludable) â†’ undersupply of innovation without IP protection or R&D subsidies.
learnUni_economics_f4_kp2: Institutions and growth (Acemoglu & Robinson): inclusive institutions (property rights, rule of law, competitive markets) foster growth. Extractive institutions (colonial legacy â€” settler mortality shaped colonial institutions) trap countries in poverty. History matters â€” persistence.
learnUni_economics_f4_kp3: RCTs in development: Duflo & Banerjee (Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab, J-PAL) use RCTs to evaluate specific interventions â€” deworming, microcredit, conditional cash transfers, fertiliser nudges. Results often surprising â€” theory without data misleads.
learnUni_economics_f4_kp4: Poverty traps and CCTs: poverty traps arise from fixed costs of investment (education, health, fertiliser) that low-income households cannot afford, preventing productivity gains. Conditional cash transfers (Mexico's Oportunidades, Brazil's Bolsa FamÃ­lia) provide income conditioned on school attendance and healthcare â€” breaking the trap with measured long-run effects.
learnUni_economics_f4_task: Compare South Korea and Nigeria: both had similar income levels (~$300 GDP/capita) in 1960. In 2023 South Korea has ~$33,000, Nigeria ~$2,100. Using (a) Solow model â€” what savings and human capital differences explain divergence? (b) Romer model â€” how does innovation capacity matter? (c) Acemoglu & Robinson â€” compare colonial history and institutional trajectories. Conclude with one evidence-based policy recommendation for Nigeria, citing a relevant RCT.
learnUni_law_f0_desc: Constitutional law defines the relationship between the state and individuals, and between the branches of government. Public law governs the exercise of state power. Understanding constitutional principles â€” separation of powers, rule of law, fundamental rights â€” is foundational to all legal study and democratic citizenship.
learnUni_law_f0_kp0: The constitution: may be codified (USA, Germany, France â€” a single written document) or uncodified (UK â€” statute law, common law, and conventions). Hierarchy of norms: constitutional norms prevail over ordinary legislation.
learnUni_law_f0_kp1: Separation of powers (Montesquieu): legislative (Parliament/Congress), executive (government/president), judicial (courts). Checks and balances prevent concentration of power. Judicial review: courts can strike down laws incompatible with the constitution.
learnUni_law_f0_kp2: Rule of law (Dicey): no one is above the law; punishment only for breach of clear law; equal access to courts. In practice: independence of the judiciary, prohibition of arbitrary detention (habeas corpus), proportionality of state action.
learnUni_law_f0_kp3: Fundamental rights: European Convention on Human Rights (Articles 2â€“14: right to life, prohibition of torture, fair trial, privacy, freedom of expression, prohibition of discrimination). Horizontal vs. vertical application of rights.
learnUni_law_f0_kp4: EU constitutional order: supremacy of EU law (Costa v. ENEL, 1964), direct effect (Van Gend en Loos, 1963), proportionality principle, subsidiarity. Post-Brexit: UK is no longer subject to EU law.
learnUni_law_f0_task: Analyse the constitutional position of an independent judiciary in a democracy. Using examples from three different legal systems (UK, USA, Germany), compare: how judicial independence is protected, the scope of judicial review of legislation, and one case where the courts struck down or limited executive action. Evaluate whether judicial review is compatible with democratic legitimacy.
learnUni_law_f0_tp1: Rule of law and fundamental rights
learnUni_law_f1_desc: Contract law governs legally binding agreements. It determines when a contract is formed, what the parties are obliged to do, what happens when obligations are not met (breach), and what remedies are available. Contract law underpins commerce, employment, and most legal relationships between private parties.
learnUni_law_f1_kp0: Elements of a valid contract: offer (definite proposal to be bound on acceptance), acceptance (unequivocal, corresponding to the offer â€” the 'mirror image' rule), consideration (something of value exchanged â€” past consideration is no consideration), intention to create legal relations.
learnUni_law_f1_kp1: Terms of a contract: express terms (stated explicitly), implied terms (implied by statute â€” Sale of Goods Act; or by courts â€” business efficacy test). Conditions (breach allows termination + damages), warranties (breach allows damages only), innominate terms (remedy depends on consequences of breach).
learnUni_law_f1_kp2: Vitiating factors: misrepresentation (false statement of fact inducing a contract â†’ rescission + damages), duress (illegitimate pressure â†’ voidable), undue influence (unfair advantage taken â†’ voidable), mistake (mutual/unilateral fundamental mistake â†’ void or voidable).
learnUni_law_f1_kp3: Breach and remedies: anticipatory breach (refusal to perform before performance due â†’ innocent party may accept and sue immediately). Remedies: damages (primary â€” puts innocent party in position as if contract performed), specific performance (equitable â€” compels performance, granted where damages inadequate), injunction, quantum meruit.
learnUni_law_f1_kp4: Exemption clauses: must be incorporated (notice, signature, or course of dealing); interpreted strictly (contra proferentem â€” ambiguity against party relying on clause); subject to UCTA 1977 and CRA 2015 (unreasonable clauses unenforceable in consumer contracts).
learnUni_law_f1_task: A builder quotes Â£50,000 to renovate a house. The homeowner accepts. During work, the builder discovers unforeseen structural problems and demands Â£70,000 more. The homeowner signs a new agreement under pressure. On completion, the homeowner refuses to pay the extra Â£20,000. Advise: (a) is the original contract valid? (b) Is the variation enforceable? (c) What remedies does the builder have? (d) What defence might the homeowner raise?
learnUni_law_f2_desc: Criminal law defines conduct prohibited by the state and provides for punishment of offenders. It protects individuals and society from harm, deters wrongdoing, and expresses collective moral condemnation. Understanding criminal law requires mastery of actus reus, mens rea, defences, and the principles of criminal liability.
learnUni_law_f2_kp0: Elements of a crime: actus reus (the guilty act â€” includes voluntary act, omission where duty exists, result, circumstances) and mens rea (the guilty mind â€” intention, recklessness, or negligence). Coincidence principle: AR and MR must coincide in time.
learnUni_law_f2_kp1: Levels of mens rea: direct intention (purpose is to bring about result), oblique intention (result a virtually certain consequence, foreseen as such â€” R v Woolin), subjective recklessness (Cunningham â€” conscious risk-taking), objective negligence (gross deviation from reasonable person standard).
learnUni_law_f2_kp2: Homicide: murder (unlawful killing + intention to kill or cause GBH â€” mandatory life sentence); voluntary manslaughter (murder reduced by loss of control or diminished responsibility); involuntary manslaughter (unlawful act or gross negligence â€” no murder MR).
