learnSch_mathematics_f6_tp5_kp4: Comparison and ratio tests: if an ≤ bn and Σbn converges, then Σan converges. Ratio test: if |a(n+1)/an| → L < 1, the series converges absolutely.
learnSch_mathematics_f6_tp5_insight: Zeno of Elea (c. 450 BC) argued that Achilles could never overtake a tortoise: every time Achilles reaches where the tortoise was, it has moved further ahead, ad infinitum. The paradox seems to show infinite steps take infinite time. The resolution is that an infinite number of steps can take finite time: the geometric series 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 + … = 1. Zeno’s paradox was not resolved philosophically until the 17th century, when Newton and Leibniz developed the precise concept of a limit.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp3_desc: The chain rule differentiates composite functions: if y = f(g(x)), then dy/dx = f’(g(x)) · g’(x). Implicit differentiation handles curves like circles where y cannot be easily isolated — you differentiate both sides with respect to x and apply the chain rule to terms involving y.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp3_kp0: Chain rule: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f’(g(x)) · g’(x). Think: derivative of outer × derivative of inner. E.g. d/dx[sin(x²)] = cos(x²) · 2x.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp3_kp1: Leibniz notation: dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx) where u = g(x). The differentials 'cancel' symbolically — a useful mnemonic though not rigorous.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp3_kp2: Implicit differentiation: differentiate both sides w.r.t. x. For any term in y, use chain rule: d/dx[y²] = 2y (dy/dx). Then solve for dy/dx.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp3_kp3: Example: x² + y² = r² ⇒ 2x + 2y(dy/dx) = 0 ⇒ dy/dx = −x/y (slope of circle).
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp3_kp4: Logarithmic differentiation: take ln of both sides before differentiating. Useful for y = x^x or products of many functions. d/dx[x^x] = x^x(1 + ln x).
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp3_insight: The chain rule is the single most used rule in calculus. Neural networks (deep learning) are trained by backpropagation, which is just the chain rule applied repeatedly through layers of composed functions. The algorithm that determines what your phone’s autocomplete suggests, what YouTube recommends next, and how ChatGPT generates text is fundamentally millions of applications of the chain rule per second.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp4_desc: The second derivative f’’(x) measures the rate of change of the rate of change — how quickly the slope is itself changing. It tells you whether a curve is concave up (bowl-shaped) or concave down (dome-shaped), and whether a stationary point is a maximum, minimum, or inflection point.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp4_kp0: Second derivative test: if f’(a) = 0 and f’’(a) > 0, then a is a local minimum (concave up). If f’’(a) < 0, it’s a local maximum (concave down). If f’’(a) = 0, the test is inconclusive.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp4_kp1: Concavity: f’’(x) > 0 throughout an interval ⇒ concave up (slope increasing). f’’(x) < 0 ⇒ concave down (slope decreasing).
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp4_kp2: Inflection point: where concavity changes (f’’ changes sign). Necessary condition: f’’(x) = 0 or undefined. But f’’(x) = 0 alone is not sufficient (e.g. y=x⁴ has no inflection at x=0).
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp4_kp3: In physics: position s(t); velocity v(t) = s’(t); acceleration a(t) = s’’(t). If a(t) > 0 velocity is increasing (speeding up in positive direction).
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp4_kp4: Curve sketching summary: use f’ for stationary points, f’’ for nature and concavity, limits for behaviour as x → ±∞ and at asymptotes.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp4_insight: The economic concept of 'diminishing returns' is precisely the second derivative. If f(K) = output as a function of capital K, then f’(K) > 0 (more capital gives more output) and f’’(K) < 0 (each additional unit of capital gives less extra output than the previous one). Almost every production function, utility function, and cost curve in economics is defined by the sign of its second derivative.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp5_desc: L’Hôpital’s rule resolves indeterminate forms (0/0 or ∞/∞) by differentiating numerator and denominator separately. Related rates problems use implicit differentiation with respect to time to find how one changing quantity affects another.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp5_kp0: L’Hôpital’s rule: if lim f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0 or ∞/∞, then the limit equals lim f’(x)/g’(x), provided this limit exists. Can be applied repeatedly.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp5_kp1: Other indeterminate forms (0·∞, ∞−∞, 1^∞, 0⁰, ∞⁰) can be rewritten as 0/0 or ∞/∞ before applying L’Hôpital.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp5_kp2: Classic limits: lim (sin x)/x as x→0 = 1; lim (1+1/n)^n as n→∞ = e; lim (e^x)/x^n as x→∞ = ∞ for any fixed n (exponential grows faster than any polynomial).
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp5_kp3: Related rates: differentiate an equation with respect to time t. Use chain rule for each variable. Example: if a sphere’s radius increases at 2 cm/s, how fast does its volume increase? V = ⁴⁄₃πr³ ⇒ dV/dt = 4πr² (dr/dt).
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp5_kp4: Strategy for related rates: draw a diagram, write an equation relating all quantities, differentiate implicitly w.r.t. t, substitute known values.
learnSch_mathematics_f7_tp5_insight: L’Hôpital’s rule was actually discovered by Johann Bernoulli, who taught it to the Marquis de L’Hôpital in exchange for a salary and the right to use it freely. L’Hôpital published it in 1696 in the first ever calculus textbook. Bernoulli complained bitterly about the credit for the rest of his life. Today the rule carries the name of the wealthy nobleman who paid for it, not the mathematician who proved it.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp3_desc: Integration by substitution (u-substitution) is the integral analogue of the chain rule. By substituting u = g(x), complicated integrals reduce to standard forms. It is the most widely used integration technique.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp3_kp0: Method: choose u = g(x) (usually the 'inside' function); compute du = g’(x)dx; rewrite the integral entirely in terms of u; integrate; substitute back.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp3_kp1: Example: ∫ 2x·cos(x²) dx. Let u = x², du = 2x dx. Becomes ∫ cos(u) du = sin(u) + C = sin(x²) + C.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp3_kp2: Definite integrals with substitution: either convert the limits to u-values and keep everything in u, or substitute back to x before evaluating.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp3_kp3: Trigonometric substitutions: for √(a²−x²), let x = a·sinθ; for √(a²+x²), let x = a·tanθ; for √(x²−a²), let x = a·secθ.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp3_kp4: Checking: always differentiate your answer to verify it gives the original integrand.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp3_insight: The integral ∫ e^(−x²) dx from −∞ to +∞ cannot be evaluated using standard techniques like substitution — it has no elementary antiderivative. Yet it equals exactly √π. This is proved using a beautiful 2D trick: square the integral, convert to polar coordinates, and use substitution. The result √π appears in the normal distribution formula, connecting the bell curve to a circle via an integral that has no closed-form antiderivative.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp4_desc: Integration by parts is the integral version of the product rule: ∫ u dv = uv − ∫ v du. It transforms the integral of a product into (hopefully) a simpler integral. Choosing which factor to call u and which to call dv is the key skill.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp4_kp0: Formula: ∫ u dv = uv − ∫ v du. Derived from the product rule: d(uv)/dx = u dv/dx + v du/dx, then integrate both sides.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp4_kp1: LIATE rule for choosing u: Logarithm, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential. Choose u as the type highest in this list.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp4_kp2: Example: ∫ x·e^x dx. Let u = x (algebraic), dv = e^x dx. Then du = dx, v = e^x. Result: xe^x − ∫ e^x dx = xe^x − e^x + C.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp4_kp3: Sometimes apply twice: ∫ x² e^x dx requires two applications. Sometimes the original integral reappears on the right — move it to the left and solve algebraically (e.g. ∫ e^x sin x dx).
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp4_kp4: Reduction formulas: repeated integration by parts on ∫ sinⁿ x dx or ∫ xⁿ e^x dx generates a recurrence relation in n — an elegant connection between sequences and calculus.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp4_insight: Bernhard Riemann invented the precise definition of the integral in 1854 as a limit of sums — but integration by parts was known 150 years earlier. Today, computer algebra systems like Wolfram Alpha can integrate by parts, substitution, and partial fractions instantly. Yet understanding the technique is still crucial: knowing why a CAS gives a particular form, detecting errors, and applying integration to physics problems all require human understanding of the underlying method.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp5_desc: When a 2D curve is rotated about an axis, it sweeps out a 3D solid. Integration calculates the volume exactly. This technique calculates volumes of wine casks, engine pistons, biological cells, and astronomical bodies. It unifies the geometry of solids with the power of calculus.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp5_kp0: Disc method (rotation about x-axis): V = π∫[f(x)]² dx from x=a to b. Each thin slice is a disc of radius f(x) and thickness dx.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp5_kp1: Washer method (hollow solid, rotation about x-axis): V = π∫([R(x)]² − [r(x)]²) dx, where R is the outer radius and r the inner radius.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp5_kp2: Shell method (rotation about y-axis): V = 2π∫ x·f(x) dx from x=a to b. Uses cylindrical shells instead of discs.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp5_kp3: Rotation about the y-axis (disc method): express x as a function of y, then V = π∫[x(y)]² dy.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp5_kp4: Pappus’ centroid theorem: the volume of a solid of revolution equals 2π × (distance from centroid of shape to axis) × (area of shape). Gives a quick check for many standard shapes.
learnSch_mathematics_f8_tp5_insight: Kepler used an early form of the disc method in 1615 to calculate the optimal barrel shape for Austrian wine casks, wanting to maximise volume for a given amount of wood. He filled barrels with water and measured the volume, then compared it with his calculus-based prediction. His Nova Stereometria Doliorum Vinariorum ('New Solid Geometry of Wine Barrels') is considered one of the precursors to integral calculus — driven entirely by the practical economics of the wine trade.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp3_desc: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv), a conserved vector quantity. In a closed system with no external forces, total momentum before an event equals total momentum after. Impulse equals the change in momentum and equals average force multiplied by time.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp3_kp0: Momentum p = mv (unit: kg·m/s); it is a vector in the same direction as velocity.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp3_kp1: Conservation of momentum: total momentum before = total momentum after in any closed system with no external forces.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp3_kp2: Impulse J = FΔt = Δp; impulse equals the area under a force-time graph.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp3_kp3: Elastic collisions conserve both momentum and kinetic energy; inelastic collisions conserve momentum but KE is partially lost as heat or deformation.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp3_kp4: Car crumple zones increase collision time, reducing peak force (F = Δp/Δt; same Δp but larger Δt gives smaller F).
learnSch_physics_f0_tp3_insight: Newton's actual 2nd law is F = dp/dt (rate of change of momentum), not just F = ma. The F = ma form only works when mass is constant; the momentum form correctly describes rockets that lose mass as they burn fuel.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp4_desc: An object moving at constant speed in a circle is continuously accelerating because its velocity direction changes. This centripetal acceleration always points toward the centre. Centripetal force is provided by gravity (satellites), tension (a ball on a string), friction (a car on a bend), or normal force (a loop-the-loop) — it is not a new type of force.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp4_kp0: Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r = ω²r, always directed toward the centre of the circle.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp4_kp1: Centripetal force: F = mv²/r = mω²r; it is always perpendicular to velocity, so it does no work.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp4_kp2: Angular velocity ω = 2πf = 2π/T (rad/s); period T = 1/f; frequency f = revolutions per second.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp4_kp3: For a satellite in circular orbit, gravity provides centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r, giving orbital speed v = √(GM/r).
learnSch_physics_f0_tp4_kp4: In a car rounding a bend, friction provides centripetal force; if friction is insufficient (wet road, high speed) the car skids outward.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp4_insight: Astronauts aboard the International Space Station are NOT weightless — gravity there is about 90% of Earth's surface gravity. They feel weightless because they and the station are both in continuous free-fall toward Earth at the same rate.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp5_desc: Projectile motion occurs when an object moves under gravity alone after being launched. Horizontal and vertical motions are completely independent. Horizontally the object travels at constant velocity; vertically it accelerates downward at g = 9.81 m/s². These combine to produce a parabolic trajectory.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp5_kp0: Horizontal: x = uₓt (constant velocity; no horizontal acceleration if air resistance is ignored).
learnSch_physics_f0_tp5_kp1: Vertical: y = uᵧt − ½gt² and vᵧ = uᵧ − gt (constant downward acceleration g ≈ 9.81 m/s²).
learnSch_physics_f0_tp5_kp2: At maximum height, the vertical velocity component is zero; horizontal velocity remains constant throughout.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp5_kp3: Range on level ground: R = u² sin(2θ)/g; maximum range is at launch angle θ = 45°.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp5_kp4: A bullet fired horizontally and one dropped from the same height hit the ground at the same time — both fall with identical vertical acceleration g.
learnSch_physics_f0_tp5_insight: The "monkey and hunter" experiment shows projectile motion perfectly: a hunter fires horizontally at a monkey that drops from a branch the instant it sees the muzzle flash. The bullet ALWAYS hits the monkey because both fall with identical gravitational acceleration — regardless of bullet speed or distance.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp3_desc: Ohm's law states that current through a conductor is proportional to voltage across it (V = IR), provided temperature is constant. Resistance depends on a material's resistivity, its length, and cross-sectional area. Resistivity is an intrinsic material property that changes with temperature.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp3_kp0: Ohm's law: V = IR; resistance R is measured in ohms (Ω); a component is ohmic if R is constant over a range of voltages.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp3_kp1: Resistivity ρ: R = ρL/A; longer or thinner conductors have higher resistance; copper has very low ρ, making it ideal for wiring.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp3_kp2: For metals, ρ increases with temperature; for semiconductors and thermistors, ρ decreases with temperature.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp3_kp3: An I-V graph for an ohmic resistor is a straight line through the origin; a filament bulb curves upward as temperature (and resistance) rises.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp3_kp4: Superconductors have zero resistivity below a critical temperature; they can carry current indefinitely with no energy loss.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp3_insight: Superconductivity was discovered accidentally in 1911 by Heike Kamerlingh Onnes when mercury's resistance dropped to exactly zero at 4.2 K. Superconducting magnets now power MRI scanners and particle accelerators like the Large Hadron Collider.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp4_desc: Electromagnetic induction is the creation of a voltage (EMF) by changing magnetic flux through a conductor. Faraday's law states the induced EMF is proportional to the rate of change of flux. Lenz's law states the induced current opposes the change that caused it. This principle underlies generators, transformers, and induction charging.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp4_kp0: Magnetic flux Φ = BA cosθ (webers, Wb); B = field strength, A = area, θ = angle between field and normal to the surface.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp4_kp1: Faraday's law: induced EMF ε = −N(dΦ/dt); the negative sign reflects Lenz's law (opposition to change).
learnSch_physics_f1_tp4_kp2: Lenz's law: the induced current creates a magnetic field that opposes the change in flux (energy is conserved).
learnSch_physics_f1_tp4_kp3: A generator converts kinetic energy to electrical energy by spinning a coil in a magnetic field; a motor does the reverse.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp4_kp4: A transformer changes AC voltage: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p; step-up transformers increase voltage for efficient long-distance power transmission.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp4_insight: Faraday discovered electromagnetic induction in 1831 using nothing more than a coil, a magnet, and a galvanometer. His insight that a changing magnetic field creates an electric field is the basis for nearly all electricity generation on Earth today.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp5_desc: A capacitor stores energy in an electric field between two conducting plates separated by an insulator. In AC circuits, capacitors and inductors introduce frequency-dependent impedance. Capacitors block DC but pass AC; their reactance decreases as frequency increases.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp5_kp0: Capacitance C = Q/V (farads); energy stored: E = ½CV² = Q²/2C.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp5_kp1: Capacitors in series: 1/C_total = 1/C1 + 1/C2; capacitors in parallel: C_total = C1 + C2 + ...
learnSch_physics_f1_tp5_kp2: Charging/discharging follows Q = Q_0 e^(−t/RC); the time constant τ = RC is the time to charge to 63% or discharge to 37%.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp5_kp3: Capacitive reactance: X_C = 1/(2πfC); higher frequency → lower reactance → capacitor passes AC more easily.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp5_kp4: In AC circuits, impedance Z = √(R² + (X_L − X_C)²); resonance occurs when X_L = X_C, giving minimum impedance.
learnSch_physics_f1_tp5_insight: The capacitor in your camera flash stores energy gradually then releases it all in a millisecond — that burst of energy creates the blinding flash. The same principle, scaled up enormously, is used in nuclear fusion experiments to deliver terawatts of power for tiny fractions of a second.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp3_desc: All waves share core properties: frequency, wavelength, amplitude, and wave speed. These are related by the wave equation v = fλ. Frequency determines the pitch of sound or the colour of light; amplitude determines loudness or brightness. Period T = 1/f gives the time for one complete oscillation.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp3_kp0: Wave speed v = fλ; frequency f (Hz) = oscillations per second; wavelength λ (m) = distance between two successive crests.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp3_kp1: Amplitude is the maximum displacement from equilibrium; it determines the energy and intensity of a wave.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp3_kp2: Period T = 1/f; e.g. a 440 Hz note has period 1/440 ≈ 0.00227 s.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp3_kp3: Transverse waves: displacement is perpendicular to travel (light, water surface); longitudinal: displacement is parallel (sound, seismic P-waves).
learnSch_physics_f2_tp3_kp4: Wave intensity I ∝ amplitude²; doubling the amplitude quadruples the energy carried by the wave.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp3_insight: The musical note A above middle C is defined internationally as exactly 440 Hz. This standard was adopted in 1939 — but orchestras throughout history used different pitches, and some Baroque period recordings sound noticeably lower to modern ears because of this.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp4_desc: Diffraction is the spreading of waves around obstacles or through gaps, most pronounced when the gap size is close to the wavelength. Superposition states that when two waves meet, their displacements add algebraically. Constructive interference (waves in phase) increases amplitude; destructive interference (waves out of phase) reduces it.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp4_kp0: Principle of superposition: the resultant displacement at any point = algebraic sum of displacements of individual waves.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp4_kp1: Constructive interference: two waves in phase (path difference = nλ) → larger amplitude; destructive: out of phase (path difference = (n+½)λ) → smaller amplitude.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp4_kp2: Young's double-slit experiment: fringe spacing y = λD/d (D = screen distance, d = slit separation) — evidence for the wave nature of light.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp4_kp3: Diffraction increases when the gap width approaches the wavelength; long radio waves diffract around hills, but visible light barely diffracts around a hair.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp4_kp4: Noise-cancelling headphones generate sound waves with inverted phase (180° out of phase) to destructively interfere with external noise.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp4_insight: Thomas Young's 1801 double-slit experiment was one of the most important in physics: it proved light was a wave, not particles as Newton insisted. Young was so ahead of his time that the scientific establishment ridiculed him for daring to challenge Newton.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp5_desc: The electromagnetic spectrum is a continuous range of electromagnetic radiation ordered by frequency (or wavelength). All EM waves travel at c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum. They include radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays — differing only in frequency and wavelength.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp5_kp0: EM spectrum (low to high frequency): radio → microwave → infrared → visible → UV → X-ray → gamma. Higher frequency = shorter wavelength = more energy per photon.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp5_kp1: Radio waves: communication; microwaves: cooking and radar; infrared: thermal imaging, remote controls, and night vision.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp5_kp2: Ultraviolet: causes sunburn and is blocked by the ozone layer; X-rays: penetrate soft tissue (medical imaging); gamma: emitted by radioactive nuclei (cancer therapy).
learnSch_physics_f2_tp5_kp3: Photon energy E = hf = hc/λ; gamma rays carry enormously more energy per photon than radio waves (same speed, much higher f).
learnSch_physics_f2_tp5_kp4: Human eyes detect only a narrow band (400–700 nm); many animals see UV (bees), and pit vipers detect infrared to hunt warm-blooded prey.
learnSch_physics_f2_tp5_insight: Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GPS, FM radio, TV broadcasts, and mobile phones all use different parts of the radio/microwave spectrum simultaneously. The fact that they don't interfere with each other is due to careful frequency allocation by international regulators — a global coordination feat involving dozens of countries.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp3: The gas laws: Boyle, Charles, Gay-Lussac
learnSch_physics_f3_tp3_desc: The gas laws describe how pressure, volume, and temperature of an ideal gas are related. Boyle's law (constant T): P ∝ 1/V. Charles's law (constant P): V ∝ T. Gay-Lussac's law (constant V): P ∝ T. The ideal gas equation PV = nRT combines all three.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp3_kp0: Boyle's law: P1V1 = P2V2 at constant temperature; pressure and volume are inversely proportional.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp3_kp1: Charles's law: V1/T1 = V2/T2 at constant pressure; volume is proportional to absolute temperature (in kelvin).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp3_kp2: Gay-Lussac's law: P1/T1 = P2/T2 at constant volume; pressure is proportional to absolute temperature.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp3_kp3: Ideal gas equation: PV = nRT (n = moles, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T in kelvin); all three laws are special cases of this.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp3_kp4: Real gases deviate from ideal behaviour at high pressures (molecules take up space) and low temperatures (intermolecular forces become significant).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp3_insight: Robert Boyle discovered his gas law in 1662 using a J-shaped tube and trapped air. He measured pressure using the height of a mercury column — and this was considered the first quantitative law in chemistry, helping launch the modern scientific method.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp4: Specific heat capacity and latent heat
learnSch_physics_f3_tp4_desc: Specific heat capacity c is the energy needed to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1 K (Q = mcΔT). Latent heat L is the energy needed for a phase change (melting, boiling) without temperature change (Q = mL). During a phase change, energy breaks intermolecular bonds rather than increasing kinetic energy.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp4_kp0: Specific heat capacity: Q = mcΔT; water's c = 4200 J/kg·K — one of the highest of any common material.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp4_kp1: Specific latent heat of fusion: energy to melt (or freeze) 1 kg at constant temperature (e.g. water: 334 000 J/kg).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp4_kp2: Specific latent heat of vaporisation: energy to vaporise (or condense) 1 kg at constant temperature (water: 2 260 000 J/kg).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp4_kp3: During a phase change, temperature remains constant because all energy goes into breaking (or forming) intermolecular bonds, not into increasing particle kinetic energy.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp4_kp4: Heating curve: a graph of temperature vs. heat added shows flat regions at melting and boiling points (phase changes) and sloped regions (temperature rising).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp4_insight: Sweating is one of the most thermodynamically efficient cooling mechanisms in nature: the latent heat of vaporisation of water is huge (2.26 MJ/kg), so evaporating even a small amount of sweat removes a large quantity of heat from the body.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp5: Thermal radiation and the Stefan-Boltzmann law
learnSch_physics_f3_tp5_desc: All objects above absolute zero emit electromagnetic radiation (thermal radiation) due to the motion of their charged particles. The power emitted depends on temperature and surface properties. A perfect emitter and absorber is called a blackbody. The Stefan-Boltzmann law and Wien's displacement law quantify these relationships.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp5_kp0: Stefan-Boltzmann law: power radiated per unit area P/A = εσT⁴ (σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴; ε = emissivity, 0 to 1).
learnSch_physics_f3_tp5_kp1: Wien's displacement law: λ_peak × T = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K; hotter objects peak at shorter (bluer) wavelengths.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp5_kp2: A blackbody (ε = 1) absorbs all incident radiation and emits maximum power; a perfect reflector (ε = 0) emits no thermal radiation.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp5_kp3: The Sun (T ≈ 5800 K) peaks in visible light; the human body (T ≈ 310 K) peaks in the infrared — which is why infrared cameras show people glowing in the dark.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp5_kp4: The greenhouse effect: CO₂ and H₂O absorb outgoing infrared radiation from Earth's surface, re-radiating it back — raising surface temperatures above what Stefan-Boltzmann alone would predict.
learnSch_physics_f3_tp5_insight: Max Planck derived his blackbody radiation formula in 1900 by assuming energy was emitted in discrete quanta — an idea he himself found uncomfortable but was mathematically forced to accept. This reluctant assumption launched the quantum revolution and eventually gave us lasers, solar cells, and smartphones.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp3: Mass defect and nuclear binding energy
learnSch_physics_f4_tp3_desc: The mass of an atomic nucleus is always less than the sum of the masses of its individual protons and neutrons. This difference is called the mass defect. By E = mc², the mass defect corresponds to the binding energy — the energy needed to completely separate the nucleus. Higher binding energy per nucleon means a more stable nucleus.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp3_kp0: Mass defect Δm = (sum of constituent masses) − (actual nuclear mass); always positive for stable nuclei.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp3_kp1: Binding energy E_B = Δm × c²; 1 atomic mass unit (u) = 931.5 MeV/c².
learnSch_physics_f4_tp3_kp2: Binding energy per nucleon peaks at iron-56 (≈ 8.8 MeV/nucleon) — iron is the most stable nucleus in existence.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp3_kp3: Fission of heavy nuclei and fusion of light nuclei both release energy by moving products toward the peak of the binding energy curve.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp3_kp4: The strong nuclear force holds nucleons together at very short range (<3 fm) and overcomes electromagnetic repulsion between protons.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp3_insight: Iron is the end product of stellar nucleosynthesis — stars fuse lighter elements for energy until they produce an iron core. After that, fusion consumes energy rather than releasing it, causing massive stars to collapse and explode as supernovae, forging all elements heavier than iron in the universe.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp4_desc: Dosimetry measures the biological effect of ionising radiation on the human body. Different radiation types have different abilities to cause cellular damage. Doses are quantified in grays (energy absorbed) and sieverts (biological effect). Safety is managed through time, distance, and shielding.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp4_kp0: Absorbed dose (gray, Gy): 1 Gy = 1 J/kg of energy absorbed by tissue.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp4_kp1: Equivalent dose (sievert, Sv): absorbed dose × radiation weighting factor; α has Q = 20, β and γ have Q = 1.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp4_kp2: ALARA principle: exposure should be As Low As Reasonably Achievable; use time (reduce duration), distance (I ∝ 1/r²), and shielding.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp4_kp3: Average annual background radiation: ≈2.7 mSv in the UK (mainly radon gas, cosmic rays, food, and natural ground radiation).
learnSch_physics_f4_tp4_kp4: Ionising radiation can break chemical bonds in DNA, causing mutations; the body's repair mechanisms fix most damage, but high doses can cause cancer.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp4_insight: Ramsar in Iran has the world's highest natural background radiation — up to 260 mSv/year from radium-rich spring waters — yet residents show no measurable increase in cancer rates. This challenges simple linear dose-response models and remains debated by radiation biologists.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp5: Medical and industrial applications of nuclear physics
learnSch_physics_f4_tp5_desc: Nuclear physics has transformed medicine and industry. Radioactive tracers and imaging (PET, SPECT) allow doctors to visualise metabolic processes in real time. Radiotherapy uses targeted radiation to destroy cancer cells. In industry, radiation is used for quality control, thickness gauging, and food irradiation.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp5_kp0: PET (Positron Emission Tomography): a radiotracer emitting positrons is injected; annihilation gamma rays are detected to image metabolic activity.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp5_kp1: Gamma knife surgery: focused beams of gamma radiation converge on a tumour, destroying it with minimal damage to surrounding tissue.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp5_kp2: Nuclear power: controlled fission heats water to steam, which drives turbines; provides about 10% of global electricity with very low CO₂ per kWh.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp5_kp3: Industrial radiography: X-rays or gamma rays inspect welds and aircraft components for internal flaws without disassembly.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp5_kp4: Food irradiation: gamma rays kill bacteria and extend shelf life; the food itself does not become radioactive.
learnSch_physics_f4_tp5_insight: Technetium-99m is used in about 30 million medical procedures per year worldwide. Its 6-hour half-life is ideal — long enough for imaging, short enough to minimise dose. It is produced from molybdenum-99 in nuclear reactors and must be shipped fresh daily to hospitals around the world.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp3: The Schrödinger equation and probability clouds
learnSch_physics_f5_tp3_desc: The Schrödinger equation is the fundamental equation of quantum mechanics, describing how the wave function of a quantum system evolves over time. The wave function Ψ does not tell us where a particle is, but gives the probability of finding it in a given location. Squaring the wave function magnitude gives the probability density.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp3_kp0: The Schrödinger equation: iℏ(dΨ/dt) = HΨ; H is the Hamiltonian operator describing the total energy of the system.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp3_kp1: Wave function Ψ(x, t): complex-valued; |Ψ|² gives the probability density of finding the particle at position x at time t.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp3_kp2: Atomic orbitals (s, p, d, f) are solutions to the Schrödinger equation for the hydrogen atom; they are probability clouds, not fixed orbits.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp3_kp3: Quantum numbers (n, l, m_l, m_s) arise naturally from solving the equation and define every possible electron state in an atom.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp3_kp4: The Copenhagen interpretation: the wave function collapses to a definite state upon measurement; before measurement the particle has no definite position.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp3_insight: Erwin Schrödinger devised his famous cat paradox in 1935 as a critique of Copenhagen quantum mechanics — not as an endorsement of it. He argued it was absurd to suggest a cat could be simultaneously alive and dead until observed. Physicists still argue about the true interpretation of quantum mechanics today.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp4: Pauli exclusion principle and electron orbitals
learnSch_physics_f5_tp4_desc: The Pauli exclusion principle states that no two fermions (particles with half-integer spin, like electrons) can occupy the same quantum state simultaneously. This principle is why electrons fill up shells in order, why matter is solid, and why atoms have the chemical properties they do. It underlies the periodic table.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp4_kp0: Pauli exclusion principle: no two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers (n, l, m_l, m_s).
learnSch_physics_f5_tp4_kp1: Each orbital can hold at most 2 electrons (with opposite spins +½ and −½).
learnSch_physics_f5_tp4_kp2: Aufbau principle: electrons fill orbitals from lowest energy upward (1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p...); Hund's rule: electrons spread across degenerate orbitals before pairing.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp4_kp3: Shell capacities: n=1 holds 2, n=2 holds 8, n=3 holds 18 electrons; from 2n².
learnSch_physics_f5_tp4_kp4: The Pauli principle is why matter is solid: electrons in different atoms resist occupying the same space, creating the degeneracy pressure that prevents solids from collapsing.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp4_insight: White dwarf stars are held up against gravitational collapse by electron degeneracy pressure — a direct consequence of the Pauli exclusion principle. When they exceed the Chandrasekhar limit (1.4 solar masses), this pressure fails and they collapse into neutron stars.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp5: Quantum tunnelling and the scanning tunnelling microscope
learnSch_physics_f5_tp5_desc: Quantum tunnelling is the phenomenon where a particle passes through an energy barrier that classical physics says it cannot surmount. The probability of tunnelling decreases exponentially with barrier width and height. Tunnelling is essential in nuclear fusion in stars, radioactive alpha decay, and modern semiconductor devices.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp5_kp0: Tunnelling: because particles have wave-like properties, the wave function extends into (and through) classically forbidden barriers with nonzero probability.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp5_kp1: Tunnelling probability T ∝ e^(−2κd), where κ depends on barrier height and mass; extremely sensitive to barrier thickness d.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp5_kp2: Alpha decay is explained by tunnelling: the alpha particle tunnels through the nuclear potential barrier even though classically it lacks the energy to escape.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp5_kp3: The scanning tunnelling microscope (STM) uses a sharp metal tip a few angstroms from a surface; the tunnelling current (extremely sensitive to tip-surface distance) images individual atoms.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp5_kp4: Tunnel diodes and flash memory (NAND gates) rely on quantum tunnelling of electrons through thin insulating layers for their operation.
learnSch_physics_f5_tp5_insight: Without quantum tunnelling, the Sun would not shine. The core of the Sun is not hot enough (≈ 15 million K) for protons to overcome their mutual electrostatic repulsion classically — but they tunnel through the barrier. Every second, the Sun fuses about 620 million tonnes of hydrogen entirely due to tunnelling.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp3_desc: Spacetime diagrams (Minkowski diagrams) represent events in a coordinate system with time on the vertical axis and space on the horizontal axis. Light cones separate events that can be causally connected from those that cannot. The spacetime interval between events is invariant — the same for all observers regardless of their motion.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp3_kp0: Spacetime diagram: time (ct) on y-axis, space (x) on x-axis; each point (event) is a specific place at a specific time.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp3_kp1: World line: the path of an object through spacetime; a stationary object is a vertical line, a light beam travels at 45°.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp3_kp2: Light cone: defines the boundary between events that can be causally linked (inside the cone) and those that cannot (outside).
learnSch_physics_f6_tp3_kp3: Spacetime interval: s² = c²Δt² − Δx²; this quantity is the same in all inertial frames (Lorentz invariant).
learnSch_physics_f6_tp3_kp4: Simultaneity is relative: two events that are simultaneous in one frame may not be simultaneous in another moving frame.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp3_insight: Hermann Minkowski (Einstein's former mathematics professor) showed in 1907 that space and time are not separate but components of a single 4-dimensional spacetime. Einstein initially dismissed this as 'superfluous erudition' — but later embraced Minkowski's geometric view as essential to general relativity.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp4_desc: Lorentz transformations are the equations that relate the space and time coordinates of events as measured in two different inertial frames moving at constant velocity relative to each other. They replace Galilean transformations and preserve the speed of light. Time dilation, length contraction, and relativistic velocity addition all follow directly from them.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp4_kp0: Lorentz transform (S' moving at v relative to S): x' = γ(x − vt); t' = γ(t − vx/c²); y' = y; z' = z.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp4_kp1: Lorentz factor: γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²); at low v, γ ≈ 1 and Lorentz transforms reduce to Galilean transforms.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp4_kp2: Relativistic velocity addition: u' = (u − v)/(1 − uv/c²); ensures no combination of velocities can exceed c.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp4_kp3: Lorentz transforms leave the spacetime interval s² = c²Δt² − Δx² invariant — they are rotations in Minkowski spacetime.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp4_kp4: The transforms show that simultaneity is frame-dependent: events at the same t but different x in S occur at different t' in S'.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp4_insight: Hendrik Lorentz derived his transformation equations in 1904 to explain the null result of the Michelson-Morley experiment — but he thought they were just a mathematical trick. Einstein independently derived them in 1905 and understood their true physical meaning: space and time themselves change, not material objects contracting.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp5_desc: General relativity (GR) is Einstein's theory of gravity, published in 1915. It describes gravity not as a force but as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy. Objects follow the straightest possible paths (geodesics) through curved spacetime. GR predicted black holes, gravitational time dilation, light bending, and gravitational waves.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp5_kp0: Equivalence principle: a gravitational field is locally indistinguishable from an accelerating reference frame — the foundation of GR.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp5_kp1: Mass curves spacetime; other masses and even light follow curved paths (geodesics) through this warped spacetime — what we perceive as gravitational attraction.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp5_kp2: Gravitational time dilation: clocks run slower in stronger gravitational fields (confirmed by comparing ground and mountain-top atomic clocks).
learnSch_physics_f6_tp5_kp3: Gravitational waves: ripples in spacetime produced by accelerating masses (e.g. merging black holes); first directly detected by LIGO in 2015.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp5_kp4: Predictions of GR: black holes, the expanding universe, gravitational lensing of light, frame dragging, and the orbit of Mercury's perihelion — all confirmed.
learnSch_physics_f6_tp5_insight: LIGO's first detection of gravitational waves in September 2015 (announced February 2016) was the culmination of 40 years of work and cost over $1.1 billion. The waves came from two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away — and the entire signal lasted 0.2 seconds, causing a distortion smaller than one-thousandth of a proton's diameter.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp3_desc: Electron configuration describes how electrons are distributed among atomic orbitals in order of increasing energy. Orbitals come in four types (s, p, d, f), each with a characteristic shape and maximum electron capacity. The aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion principle, and Hund's rule govern how electrons fill these orbitals.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp3_kp0: Orbital types: s (max 2 electrons, spherical), p (max 6, dumbbell), d (max 10), f (max 14); each orbital holds max 2 electrons (opposite spins).
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp3_kp1: Filling order (aufbau): 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p... (energy increases, with 4s below 3d for filling purposes).
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp3_kp2: Hund's rule: electrons fill degenerate (same energy) orbitals singly before pairing; all single electrons have parallel spins.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp3_kp3: Notation example: sodium (Na, Z=11): 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s¹ or [Ne] 3s¹; the 3s¹ valence electron explains Na's reactivity.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp3_kp4: Electron configuration determines chemical properties: elements with the same valence shell configuration are in the same group and have similar reactivity.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp3_insight: Chromium (Z=24) has configuration [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹ rather than the expected [Ar] 3d⁴ 4s² — a half-filled d subshell is extra stable. Copper similarly adopts [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹. These exceptions demonstrate that stability sometimes beats simple aufbau rules.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp4: Ionic and covalent bonding in detail
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp4_desc: Ionic bonding involves the transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal, forming oppositely charged ions held together by strong electrostatic forces. Covalent bonding involves the sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms. The nature and strength of these bonds determine a substance's melting point, solubility, and conductivity.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp4_kp0: Ionic bond: metal loses e⁻ (cation), non-metal gains e⁻ (anion); lattice energy (energy to separate 1 mol of ions) indicates bond strength.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp4_kp1: Covalent bond: shared electron pair; bond order (single/double/triple) increases bond strength and decreases bond length.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp4_kp2: Bond polarity: unequal sharing due to electronegativity difference creates a dipole moment (δ+ and δ−); very unequal sharing → ionic.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp4_kp3: Giant ionic lattice: high melting point; soluble in water; conducts when molten/dissolved but not as a solid.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp4_kp4: Simple covalent molecule (e.g. H₂O): low melting/boiling point (weak intermolecular forces); giant covalent (e.g. diamond, SiO₂): very high melting point.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp4_insight: Water (H₂O) has an unusually high boiling point for such a small molecule (100°C vs. –60°C for H₂S) because oxygen's high electronegativity creates strong hydrogen bonds between molecules. Without this quirk, Earth's oceans would be a gas and life as we know it would not exist.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp5: Intermolecular forces: van der Waals and hydrogen bonding
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp5_desc: Intermolecular forces are the attractive forces between separate molecules (weaker than covalent or ionic bonds). They determine boiling points, viscosity, and solubility. The three main types are London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole interactions, and hydrogen bonds (the strongest type of intermolecular force).
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp5_kp0: London dispersion (van der Waals) forces: temporary dipoles due to electron movement; present in all molecules; strength increases with molecular size and surface area.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp5_kp1: Dipole-dipole interactions: permanent dipoles in polar molecules attract each other; stronger than London forces for similar-sized molecules.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp5_kp2: Hydrogen bonding: N–H, O–H, or F–H groups form strong interactions with lone pairs on N, O, or F; typically 5–30 kJ/mol.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp5_kp3: Ice is less dense than liquid water because hydrogen bonds hold water molecules in an open hexagonal lattice; liquid water has a denser, more disordered structure.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp5_kp4: 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents (water) dissolve ionic and polar covalent substances; non-polar solvents dissolve non-polar substances via London forces.
learnSch_chemistry_f0_tp5_insight: Geckos can walk up glass using only van der Waals forces — no glue or suction. Their feet contain millions of tiny hair-like setae (100–200 nm wide) that create enormous total contact area, generating enough cumulative London dispersion force to support the gecko's weight. Scientists are engineering gecko-inspired dry adhesives based on this principle.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp3_desc: Enthalpy (H) is a measure of the total heat content of a system. Enthalpy change (ΔH) is the heat exchanged at constant pressure. Exothermic reactions release heat (ΔH < 0); endothermic absorb it (ΔH > 0). Hess's law states that the total enthalpy change is the same regardless of the reaction pathway taken.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp3_kp0: Exothermic: ΔH < 0; products have lower enthalpy than reactants; heat is released to surroundings (temperature rises).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp3_kp1: Endothermic: ΔH > 0; products have higher enthalpy than reactants; heat is absorbed from surroundings (temperature drops).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp3_kp2: Hess's law: ΔH_reaction = ΣΔH_f(products) − ΣΔH_f(reactants); allows calculation of unmeasurable reaction enthalpies.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp3_kp3: Standard enthalpy of formation ΔH°_f: energy to form 1 mol of a compound from its elements in standard states (298 K, 1 atm).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp3_kp4: Bond enthalpy: energy to break 1 mol of a given bond in the gas phase; ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) − Σ(bonds formed).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp3_insight: Hess's law allowed 19th-century chemists to determine the enthalpy of the diamond → graphite conversion without directly measuring it — since the reaction is impossibly slow. By measuring combustion enthalpies of both forms and applying Hess's cycle, they found diamond is slightly higher in energy than graphite, making diamond thermodynamically unstable (though kinetically stable for millions of years).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp4_desc: Chemical reactions can be classified into several fundamental types: combination (synthesis), decomposition, single displacement, double displacement, and redox (oxidation-reduction). Recognising the type of reaction allows prediction of products and reaction conditions. Most reactions fall into one or more of these categories.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp4_kp0: Combination: A + B → AB; e.g. 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O (elements or compounds combine to form one product).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp4_kp1: Decomposition: AB → A + B; e.g. 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂ (one substance breaks into simpler ones, often by heat or electricity).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp4_kp2: Single displacement: A + BC → AC + B; a more reactive element displaces a less reactive one (e.g. Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp4_kp3: Double displacement (metathesis): AB + CD → AD + CB; common in precipitation reactions (e.g. AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl↓ + NaNO₃).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp4_kp4: Redox reactions involve electron transfer: OIL RIG (Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain of electrons); both processes always occur simultaneously.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp4_insight: Thermite (Al + Fe₂O₃) is one of the most dramatic single-displacement reactions: aluminium displaces iron because Al is more reactive. It burns at over 2500°C, is virtually impossible to extinguish with water (which just makes it worse), and was historically used to weld railway tracks together in remote locations.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp5_desc: A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed. It does this by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. Catalysts are crucial in industry (reducing energy costs) and in biology (enzymes as biological catalysts). They can be homogeneous (same phase) or heterogeneous (different phase).
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp5_kp0: Activation energy (E_a): the minimum energy needed for reactant molecules to react; catalysts lower E_a but do not change ΔH.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp5_kp1: An Arrhenius plot (ln k vs. 1/T) gives a straight line; the slope = −E_a/R, allowing E_a to be calculated from rate data.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp5_kp2: Heterogeneous catalysts (different phase from reactants): e.g. iron in Haber process, platinum in catalytic converters; reactants adsorb onto surface.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp5_kp3: Homogeneous catalysts (same phase): e.g. H⁺ ions in acid-catalysed esterification; involved in intermediate steps but regenerated.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp5_kp4: Enzymes are biological catalysts (proteins); extremely specific (lock-and-key model), and work optimally at specific pH and temperature; denatured by heat.
learnSch_chemistry_f1_tp5_insight: The catalytic converter in a car reduces toxic CO, NOx, and unburned hydrocarbons into CO₂, N₂, and H₂O using platinum, palladium, and rhodium. At current prices, the precious metals in a typical catalytic converter are worth more than the car's steel body — making them a prime target for theft worldwide.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp3: Strong vs weak acids and Ka
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp3_desc: Strong acids fully dissociate in water, releasing all their H⁺ ions; weak acids only partially dissociate, establishing an equilibrium. The acid dissociation constant Ka measures the extent of dissociation: a larger Ka means a stronger acid. The same concepts apply to bases with Kb and pKb.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp3_kp0: Strong acids (HCl, HBr, HI, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HClO₄) fully dissociate: [H⁺] = initial acid concentration.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp3_kp1: Weak acids partially dissociate: HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻; Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA].
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp3_kp2: pKa = −log Ka; lower pKa = stronger acid. Ethanoic acid pKa ≈ 4.75 (weak); hydrochloric acid pKa ≈ −7 (strong).
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp3_kp3: For weak acids: [H⁺] ≈ √(Ka × C_a); this approximation holds when Ka << C_a (less than 5% dissociation).
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp3_kp4: Relative acid strength: the stronger the acid, the weaker its conjugate base (e.g. HCl is strong → Cl⁻ is a very weak base).
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp3_insight: Formic acid (methanoic acid) — the compound that makes ant stings painful — has a pKa of 3.75, making it stronger than acetic acid (pKa 4.75). Formica is Latin for ant, hence the naming. The same compound is used in industrial textile dyeing and as a preservative in livestock feed.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp4_desc: A buffer solution resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid or alkali are added. It typically contains a weak acid and its conjugate base (or a weak base and its conjugate acid) in comparable amounts. Buffers are essential in biological systems where pH must be tightly controlled.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp4_kp0: Buffer action: adding H⁺ → conjugate base neutralises it; adding OH⁻ → weak acid neutralises it; pH stays approximately constant.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp4_kp1: Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]); used to calculate the pH of a buffer or design one for a target pH.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp4_kp2: Maximum buffering capacity is at pH = pKa (when [A⁻] = [HA]); effective buffering range is approximately pKa ± 1.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp4_kp3: Blood pH 7.35–7.45 is maintained by carbonic acid/bicarbonate buffer: H₂CO₃ ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻; deviations cause acidosis or alkalosis.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp4_kp4: Biological buffers: phosphate buffer (intracellular pH control); haemoglobin buffer (oxygen transport); histidine residues in proteins.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp4_insight: During intense exercise, lactic acid builds up in muscles, dropping blood pH. The body compensates by breathing faster (removing CO₂ to shift the carbonate equilibrium) and using bicarbonate buffers — your lungs and kidneys are constantly fine-tuning a delicate chemical balance to keep you alive.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp5_desc: The solubility product constant Ksp describes the equilibrium between a sparingly soluble ionic compound and its dissolved ions. It is the product of the ion concentrations raised to the power of their stoichiometric coefficients. Ksp can be used to predict whether a precipitate will form when two solutions are mixed.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp5_kp0: For AgCl(s) ⇌ Ag⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq): Ksp = [Ag⁺][Cl⁻] = 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰ at 25°C (very sparingly soluble).
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp5_kp1: Ksp can be used to calculate molar solubility: for AgCl, s = √ Ksp = 1.34 × 10⁻⁵ mol/dm³.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp5_kp2: Reaction quotient Q: if Q < Ksp, solution is unsaturated (more can dissolve); if Q > Ksp, precipitation occurs; if Q = Ksp, equilibrium.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp5_kp3: Common ion effect: adding a common ion (e.g. NaCl to AgCl solution) reduces solubility by shifting equilibrium toward the precipitate.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp5_kp4: Tooth decay: bacteria produce acid that lowers pH; Ca²⁺ ions dissolve from hydroxyapatite (Ksp increases with acidity), causing cavities. Fluoride forms more stable fluorapatite with much lower Ksp.
learnSch_chemistry_f2_tp5_insight: Kidney stones form when Ksp of calcium oxalate (CaC₂O₄) is exceeded in urine. Treatment includes drinking more water (to keep [Ca²⁺] and [C₂O₄²⁻] below Ksp), reducing oxalate-rich foods, and in severe cases, using ultrasound (lithotripsy) to shatter the stones — all rooted in solubility equilibrium chemistry.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp3_desc: Isomers are compounds with the same molecular formula but different arrangements of atoms. Structural isomers differ in how atoms are connected; stereoisomers have the same connectivity but different spatial arrangements. Isomerism has profound effects on physical properties and biological activity — many drugs work as one isomer but are inactive or even harmful as the other.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp3_kp0: Structural isomers: chain isomers (different carbon skeletons), position isomers (functional group at different position), functional group isomers (e.g. alcohol vs. ether).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp3_kp1: Stereoisomers: same connectivity; cis/trans (geometric) isomers occur around a C=C double bond when both carbons carry two different groups.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp3_kp2: Optical (chiral) isomers: a carbon bonded to four different groups is a chiral centre; two non-superimposable mirror images (enantiomers) rotate plane-polarised light in opposite directions.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp3_kp3: Enantiomers have identical physical and chemical properties in achiral environments but behave differently in biological systems (enzymes, receptors).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp3_kp4: Racemic mixture: 50:50 mix of both enantiomers; no net optical rotation; often produced in laboratory synthesis unless a chiral catalyst is used.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp3_insight: Thalidomide (1950s) had two enantiomers: one reduced morning sickness, the other caused severe limb defects in foetuses. The drug was sold as a racemate, causing over 10,000 birth defects worldwide. This tragedy revolutionised pharmaceutical regulation — new drugs must now be tested as individual enantiomers wherever possible.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp4_desc: Alcohols, aldehydes, and ketones are three closely related functional group families. Alcohols (–OH) can be oxidised step by step: primary alcohols give aldehydes then carboxylic acids; secondary alcohols give ketones. The carbonyl group (C=O) in aldehydes and ketones makes them reactive towards nucleophiles and reducing agents.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp4_kp0: Primary alcohol → aldehyde → carboxylic acid (oxidation using acidified K₂Cr₂O₇ or KMnO₄); secondary alcohol → ketone (cannot be oxidised further without breaking C–C).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp4_kp1: Aldehydes are reducing agents; they reduce Tollens' reagent (silver mirror test) and Fehling's solution (brick-red precipitate). Ketones do not.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp4_kp2: Reduction of carbonyl compounds: use NaBH₄ or LiAlH₄; aldehydes → primary alcohols; ketones → secondary alcohols.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp4_kp3: Nucleophilic addition to C=O: cyanide ion (CN⁻) attacks carbonyl carbon, producing a hydroxynitrile; adds a carbon and a new chiral centre.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp4_kp4: Boiling points: alcohols > carbonyl compounds > alkanes (same chain length) because OH–––OH hydrogen bonds are stronger than C=O–––C=O dipole interactions.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp4_insight: Ketones are produced in the body during starvation or uncontrolled diabetes — a process called ketosis. Acetone (propanone), ethyl acetoacetate, and beta-hydroxybutyrate accumulate in blood and are exhaled, giving a distinctive 'pear drop' smell. Doctors once diagnosed diabetes by smelling a patient's breath — a direct application of organic carbonyl chemistry.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp5_desc: Carboxylic acids contain the –COOH group and are weak acids. They react with alcohols to form esters and with amines to form amides — both via condensation reactions (loss of H₂O). Esters give many fruits their characteristic odours; amide bonds form the backbone of proteins and synthetic fibres like nylon and Kevlar.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp5_kp0: Carboxylic acids are weak acids: CH₃COOH ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H⁺; the carboxylate anion (COO⁻) is stabilised by resonance.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp5_kp1: Esterification: alcohol + carboxylic acid ⇌ ester + water; reversible; catalysed by concentrated H₂SO₄; driven right by removing water (Le Chatelier).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp5_kp2: Hydrolysis of esters: acid hydrolysis (H₂O + H⁺ catalyst) → alcohol + acid; base hydrolysis (saponification, NaOH) → alcohol + carboxylate salt (irreversible).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp5_kp3: Amide bond (–CO–NH–): formed between –COOH and –NH₂; found in proteins as the peptide bond; very stable due to resonance (partial double bond character).
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp5_kp4: Fats and oils are triesters of glycerol and fatty acids; saponification with NaOH produces soap (carboxylate salts) and glycerol — mankind's oldest chemical process.
learnSch_chemistry_f3_tp5_insight: Soap has been made for at least 4,000 years: ancient Babylonian records describe boiling animal fat with wood ash (lye = NaOH). The chemistry — base hydrolysis of triglyceride esters — is identical to modern industrial soap production. The soaps work because the carboxylate tail is hydrophilic and the long carbon chain is hydrophobic, forming micelles that encapsulate grease.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp3_desc: Standard electrode potential (E°) measures the tendency of a half-cell to be reduced under standard conditions (1 M, 1 atm, 298 K) relative to the standard hydrogen electrode (0.00 V). Combining two half-cells gives a galvanic cell whose EMF predicts whether a redox reaction is spontaneous and how much energy it can release.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp3_kp0: The electrochemical series lists half-cells from most negative E° (strongest reducing agents) at the top to most positive E° (strongest oxidising agents) at the bottom.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp3_kp1: Cell EMF = E°(cathode) − E°(anode). A positive EMF means ΔG < 0 and the reaction is spontaneous: ΔG° = −nFE°.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp3_kp2: The Nernst equation corrects E° for non-standard concentrations: E = E° − (RT/nF)ln(Q). At equilibrium, E = 0 and Q = K.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp3_kp3: Predicting feasibility: a reaction is spontaneous if the species higher in the electrochemical series (more negative E°) reduces the species lower (more positive E°).
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp3_kp4: Temperature effect: for an exothermic cell reaction, increasing T decreases EMF (Le Chatelier); for endothermic, increasing T increases EMF.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp3_insight: The first measurement of electrode potential was made by Walther Nernst in 1889. His equation — linking cell voltage to concentration — won him the 1920 Nobel Prize. The same equation is used in modern pH meters, oxygen sensors in cars, and glucose biosensors: every time you measure blood sugar with a glucometer, you are applying Nernst's 19th-century electrochemistry.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp4_desc: Electroplating uses electrolysis to deposit a thin layer of metal onto an object, improving appearance, corrosion resistance, or hardness. Electrorefining purifies metals: impure metal is dissolved at the anode and pure metal is deposited at the cathode. Both are essential industrial processes used in jewellery, electronics, and metal production.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp4_kp0: Electroplating setup: object to plate = cathode; plating metal = anode; electrolyte = solution of the plating metal's ions. E.g. copper plating: CuSO₄ solution, Cu anode.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp4_kp1: Cathode reaction (reduction): Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu. Anode reaction (oxidation): Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻. The anode dissolves, keeping [Cu²⁺] constant in solution.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp4_kp2: Electrorefining of copper: blister copper (~98% pure) is the anode; cathode collects 99.99% pure copper. Impurities (gold, silver, platinum) fall as 'anode slime' — worth recovering.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp4_kp3: Thickness of deposit: calculated using Faraday's law; plating time, current, and molar mass determine thickness precisely.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp4_kp4: Applications: chrome plating on car parts (corrosion resistance), gold plating on circuit board connectors (conductivity), silver on cutlery (aesthetics), zinc on steel (galvanising).
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp4_insight: The anode slime from copper electrorefining contains gold, silver, and platinum-group metals. For every tonne of copper refined, up to 30 g of silver and 0.3 g of gold are recovered. This 'by-product' is so valuable that anode slime recovery can account for 20% of a copper refinery's revenue — making electrorefining one of the most profitable industrial electrochemical processes.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp5: Fuel cells and the hydrogen economy
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp5_desc: Fuel cells generate electricity by combining hydrogen and oxygen electrochemically without combustion. They are highly efficient (up to 60% vs 25-30% for internal combustion engines), produce only water as a by-product, and can run continuously as long as fuel is supplied. The hydrogen economy envisions using H₂ as a clean energy carrier.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp5_kp0: Hydrogen fuel cell: H₂ is oxidised at the anode (H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻); O₂ is reduced at the cathode (O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂O); only by-product is water.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp5_kp1: Types: proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cell (operates at ~80°C, used in vehicles), solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC, ~1000°C, used for stationary power), alkaline fuel cells (used in NASA Apollo missions).
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp5_kp2: Efficiency advantage: fuel cells bypass the Carnot limit on heat engines; theoretical efficiency ~83%, practical ~40–60%.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp5_kp3: Challenges: H₂ storage (low volumetric density, requires compression or cryogenic liquefaction), production (currently 95% from fossil fuels via steam methane reforming), and platinum catalyst cost.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp5_kp4: Green hydrogen: produced by electrolysis of water using renewable electricity; zero-carbon if the electricity is renewable; key to decarbonising steel, ammonia, and heavy transport.
learnSch_chemistry_f4_tp5_insight: Fuel cells were invented by Sir William Grove in 1839 — nearly a century before the internal combustion engine became dominant. NASA used alkaline fuel cells to power the Apollo lunar missions, and the only waste product — water — was drunk by the astronauts. Today, the Toyota Mirai and Hyundai Nexo use PEM fuel cells; they refuel in 5 minutes and have a range of ~600 km with zero tailpipe emissions except water vapour.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp3: Reaction quotient Q and equilibrium direction
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp3_desc: The reaction quotient Q has the same mathematical form as the equilibrium constant K, but is calculated at any point in time — not necessarily at equilibrium. Comparing Q to K tells you the direction the reaction must shift to reach equilibrium. It is an essential tool in predicting reaction outcomes when mixing reactants and products together.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp3_kp0: Q < K: too few products; reaction shifts forwards (→) to produce more products and reach equilibrium.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp3_kp1: Q > K: too many products; reaction shifts backwards (←) to consume products and reach equilibrium.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp3_kp2: Q = K: the system is already at equilibrium; no net reaction occurs (though forward and reverse rates are still equal and non-zero).
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp3_kp3: Q is useful when mixing two solutions or when conditions change suddenly (e.g. temperature spike); it predicts which direction equilibrium will be re-established.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp3_kp4: For dissolution equilibria: Q = Ksp means saturated; Q > Ksp means supersaturated (precipitation occurs); Q < Ksp means unsaturated (more dissolves).
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp3_insight: The reaction quotient Q underpins the operation of blood pH buffers. In blood plasma, Q for CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻ is continuously monitored by chemoreceptors in the brainstem. When Q rises (too much CO₂ from respiration), breathing rate increases to expel CO₂, restoring equilibrium — a real-time, real-body application of Q vs K every second of your life.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp4: Solubility equilibria and the common ion effect
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp4_desc: When a sparingly soluble ionic compound dissolves, it reaches a dissolution equilibrium described by the solubility product Ksp. The common ion effect occurs when an ion already present in solution suppresses further dissolution. This is why kidney stones form, why drug solubility matters in medicine, and why water hardness is controlled in industrial processes.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp4_kp0: For AgCl: AgCl(s) ⇌ Ag⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq); Ksp = [Ag⁺][Cl⁻]. Ksp is the product of ion concentrations at saturation (pure solid not included).
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp4_kp1: Common ion effect: adding NaCl to a saturated AgCl solution increases [Cl⁻], making Q > Ksp, causing AgCl to precipitate (equilibrium shifts left). Solubility decreases.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp4_kp2: Selective precipitation: by carefully controlling [ion], you can precipitate one ion while keeping another in solution (used in qualitative analysis and metal recovery).
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp4_kp3: Temperature generally increases Ksp (endothermic dissolution), so hot water dissolves more solid. Exceptions: Ca(OH)₂ becomes less soluble when heated.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp4_kp4: Molar solubility (s): the moles of solute that dissolve per litre. For CaF₂: Ksp = [Ca²⁺][F⁻]² = (s)(2s)² = 4s³, so s = (Ksp/4)^(1/3).
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp4_insight: Kidney stones (calcium oxalate, CaC₂O₄) form when Ksp is exceeded in urine. The main treatments — drinking more water (dilutes ions, Q drops below Ksp), avoiding oxalate-rich foods, taking citrate supplements (citrate complexes Ca²⁺, reducing its concentration) — are all direct applications of solubility equilibrium chemistry.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp5_desc: Weak acids and bases only partially dissociate in water. Their dissociation equilibria are described by the acid dissociation constant Ka (or base dissociation constant Kb). These constants determine pH in buffer solutions, are used in pharmaceutical formulation to predict drug absorption, and explain how biological molecules like amino acids and haemoglobin respond to pH changes.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp5_kp0: Weak acid HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻: Ka = [H⁺][A⁻] / [HA]. pKa = −log(Ka). Smaller pKa = stronger acid. For a 50:50 mixture of HA and A⁻, pH = pKa (Henderson-Hasselbalch).
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp5_kp1: For a weak acid of concentration c: [H⁺] ≈ √(Ka × c) when Ka << c (the dissociation approximation). pH = ½(pKa − log c).
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp5_kp2: Conjugate acid-base pairs: when HA loses H⁺ it becomes A⁻ (conjugate base). Ka × Kb = Kw (10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C); so pKa + pKb = 14.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp5_kp3: Amphoteric species: can act as both acid and base (e.g. water, amino acids, HCO₃⁻). They have both a Ka and a Kb.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp5_kp4: Titration curves: weak acid + strong base gives equivalence point above pH 7 (conjugate base is basic). Weak base + strong acid gives equivalence point below pH 7.
learnSch_chemistry_f5_tp5_insight: Human blood contains multiple overlapping buffer systems: CO₂/HCO₃⁻ (pKa 6.1), H₂PO₄⁻/HPO₄²⁻ (pKa 7.2), and haemoglobin itself (pKa ~7.4). Blood pH must stay within 7.35–7.45; a pH of 6.8 or 7.8 is fatal. Alkalosis (too high pH) causes muscle cramps and tetany; acidosis (too low) causes coma. Ka equilibria keep you alive every moment.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp3_desc: Plastics are synthetic polymers derived from petrochemicals. They are durable, cheap, and versatile — but their chemical stability makes them persist in the environment for hundreds of years. Microplastics (< 5 mm) now permeate oceans, soils, air, and human bodies. Managing plastic pollution requires chemistry solutions: biodegradable materials, chemical recycling, and redesigned products.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp3_kp0: Common plastics: polyethylene (PE, bags), polypropylene (PP, food containers), PVC (pipes), PET (bottles), polystyrene (PS, packaging). All are addition polymers from alkene monomers.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp3_kp1: Degradation: UV photolysis breaks polymer chains into smaller fragments (microplastics/nanoplastics), which persist indefinitely in the environment.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp3_kp2: Microplastics in the marine food chain: ingested by plankton → fish → marine mammals and seabirds. They absorb and concentrate persistent organic pollutants (POPs) like PCBs and DDT.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp3_kp3: Biodegradable polymers: PLA (polylactic acid from corn starch), PHBV (bacterial polyesters); compostable under specific industrial conditions (not all home compostable).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp3_kp4: Chemical recycling: breaks polymers back to monomers via pyrolysis or hydrolysis; produces higher-quality material than mechanical recycling; being scaled up industrially.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp3_insight: Microplastics have been found in every environment on Earth: the Mariana Trench (deepest ocean), the summit of Mount Everest, Antarctic sea ice, and human blood. A 2022 study found microplastics in 77% of blood samples tested, with PET from bottles the most common type. The long-term health effects are unknown — we have created a global-scale experiment on ourselves.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp4: Green chemistry principles and atom economy
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp4_desc: Green chemistry (sustainable chemistry) was formalised by Anastas and Warner in 1998 as 12 principles guiding chemists to design reactions and products that are inherently safer, less wasteful, and more resource-efficient. Atom economy is a key metric: what fraction of the atoms in the reactants end up in the desired product? High atom economy reduces waste and cost.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp4_kp0: Atom economy = (Mr of desired product / sum of Mr of all products) × 100%. Addition reactions: 100%. Substitution reactions: lower (waste products formed).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp4_kp1: Example: making ibuprofen traditionally (6 steps, 40% atom economy) vs. the BHC process (3 steps, 77% atom economy, won the Presidential Green Chemistry Award 1997).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp4_kp2: Solvent choice: traditional solvents (e.g. dichloromethane, benzene) are toxic or carcinogenic; green alternatives include water, supercritical CO₂, ionic liquids, and bio-based ethanol.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp4_kp3: Catalysis improves green credentials: replaces stoichiometric reagents, lowers activation energy (less energy input), and can be reused — directly improving atom economy and waste reduction.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp4_kp4: E-factor (waste per kg product): pharmaceutical industry E-factor = 25–100 kg waste / kg product; bulk chemicals = 0.1–5. Green chemistry aims to minimise E-factor.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp4_insight: The synthesis of paracetamol (acetaminophen) illustrates green chemistry trade-offs. The old process used benzene (carcinogenic) and produced toxic waste. Modern green routes use safer solvents, better catalysts, and achieve > 99% yield. Paracetamol is produced at 160,000 tonnes/year — small improvements in atom economy and waste save thousands of tonnes of hazardous chemicals annually.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp5_desc: Atmospheric chemistry studies chemical reactions occurring in Earth's atmosphere, from the troposphere (weather, smog, pollutants) to the stratosphere (ozone layer). Key processes include photolysis (UV breaking molecular bonds), radical chain reactions, and heterogeneous reactions on aerosol surfaces. These processes determine air quality, climate, and UV protection.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp5_kp0: Troposphere chemistry: VOCs + NOx + sunlight → photochemical smog (ground-level ozone O₃, peroxyacetyl nitrates). Ground-level O₃ is a respiratory hazard, unlike stratospheric O₃ which is protective.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp5_kp1: Chapman cycle (natural ozone balance): O₂ + UV → 2O·; O· + O₂ → O₃; O₃ + UV → O₂ + O·; O· + O₃ → 2O₂. This cycle maintains a steady-state ozone concentration.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp5_kp2: Ozone depletion catalysts: halogens (Cl, Br from CFCs, halons), NOx from supersonic aircraft; each catalyst molecule cycles through the chain thousands of times.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp5_kp3: Polar stratospheric clouds (PSCs) form in Antarctic winter; surface chemistry on ice crystals converts HCl to active Cl₂, which releases Cl· radicals in spring sunlight — explaining why the ozone hole is most severe over Antarctica.
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp5_kp4: Recovery: the Montreal Protocol (1987) phased out CFCs; the ozone hole has shrunk since ~2000. Full recovery expected by 2065 due to the long atmospheric lifetime of CFCs (50–100 years).
learnSch_chemistry_f6_tp5_insight: Paul Crutzen, Mario Molina, and F. Sherwood Rowland won the 1995 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for discovering the mechanisms of stratospheric ozone depletion. Molina and Rowland published their landmark CFC-ozone paper in 1974; industry lobbied aggressively against it for 13 years. The evidence ultimately proved irrefutable, and the Montreal Protocol — often called the most successful environmental treaty in history — was signed. The ozone layer is now measurably recovering.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp3: Cell membrane structure and selective permeability
learnSch_biology_f0_tp3_desc: The cell membrane (plasma membrane) controls what enters and leaves the cell, maintaining the internal environment. The fluid mosaic model describes the membrane as a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins that move laterally. Selective permeability allows small non-polar molecules to pass freely, while ions and large molecules require specific protein channels or carriers.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp3_kp0: Phospholipid bilayer: each phospholipid has a hydrophilic head (phosphate, faces water) and two hydrophobic tails (fatty acids, face inward). This creates a semi-permeable barrier.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp3_kp1: Membrane proteins: integral proteins (span the bilayer, e.g. channels, carriers, pumps), peripheral proteins (surface only, e.g. enzymes, receptors, cell recognition glycoproteins).
learnSch_biology_f0_tp3_kp2: Passive transport: diffusion (high → low concentration, no energy); facilitated diffusion (via protein channels/carriers, still down gradient); osmosis (diffusion of water across a semi-permeable membrane).
learnSch_biology_f0_tp3_kp3: Active transport: moves solutes against concentration gradient; requires ATP and carrier proteins (pumps). Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pumps 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per ATP — maintains resting potential in neurons.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp3_kp4: Endocytosis (phagocytosis, pinocytosis) and exocytosis: bulk transport of materials into or out of the cell using vesicles formed from the membrane.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp3_insight: Singer and Nicolson's fluid mosaic model (1972) replaced the earlier 'sandwich model' by showing that proteins were embedded within and could move laterally in the membrane. This was proven using cell fusion experiments where proteins from two differently labelled cells mixed within 40 minutes. Lipid composition also varies with temperature: cold-water fish have more unsaturated fatty acids (remain fluid at lower temperatures), while warm-water species have more saturated ones — an elegant evolutionary adaptation.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp4: Cellular respiration: glycolysis, Krebs cycle, ETC
learnSch_biology_f0_tp4_desc: Cellular respiration is the process by which cells break down glucose to release energy as ATP. Aerobic respiration occurs in three main stages: glycolysis (cytoplasm), the Krebs cycle (mitochondrial matrix), and the electron transport chain (inner mitochondrial membrane). It produces up to 38 ATP per glucose under ideal conditions. Anaerobic respiration produces much less ATP.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp4_kp0: Glycolysis: 10 enzyme-catalysed steps in cytoplasm; glucose (6C) → 2 pyruvate (3C); net gain 2 ATP and 2 NADH; no oxygen required.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp4_kp1: Link reaction: pyruvate → acetyl-CoA (2C) + CO₂; NADH produced; occurs in mitochondrial matrix; links glycolysis to Krebs cycle.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp4_kp2: Krebs cycle: acetyl-CoA combines with oxaloacetate (4C) → citrate (6C); cycled to regenerate oxaloacetate; per turn produces 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 ATP, 2 CO₂. Two turns per glucose.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp4_kp3: Electron transport chain (ETC): NADH and FADH₂ donate electrons to protein complexes in inner mitochondrial membrane; electrochemical gradient drives ATP synthase (chemiosmosis); O₂ is final electron acceptor → H₂O.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp4_kp4: Anaerobic respiration: in animals → lactic acid fermentation (pyruvate + NADH → lactate); in yeast → alcoholic fermentation (pyruvate → ethanol + CO₂); both regenerate NAD⁺ so glycolysis can continue; net yield = 2 ATP only.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp4_insight: Paul Boyer and John Walker won the 1997 Nobel Prize for discovering how ATP synthase works. It is literally a rotary molecular motor — the F₀ subunit spins at ~100 revolutions per second in the inner mitochondrial membrane, driven by proton flow. Each full rotation produces 3 ATP molecules. A human at rest produces and consumes roughly their own body weight in ATP every 24 hours — continuously recycled by this tiny spinning protein engine.
