learnSch_biology_f0_tp5_desc: Enzymes are biological catalysts — proteins that speed up chemical reactions by lowering the activation energy. They are highly specific (lock-and-key model, or more accurately induced-fit model). Enzyme activity is affected by temperature, pH, substrate concentration, and inhibitors. Understanding enzyme kinetics is fundamental to medicine, pharmacology, and metabolic disease.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp5_kp0: Active site: specific 3D region of the enzyme where substrate binds; complementary to substrate shape and charge. Induced-fit: active site changes shape slightly upon substrate binding to improve complementarity.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp5_kp1: Rate factors: increasing [substrate] increases rate until Vmax (all active sites occupied — enzyme saturated). Km = substrate concentration at half Vmax; low Km = high affinity for substrate.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp5_kp2: Temperature: increasing T speeds up reaction (more collisions) until the enzyme denatures (bonds break, active site distorted). Human enzymes optimised for 37°C; thermophile enzymes stable at > 80°C.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp5_kp3: Competitive inhibition: inhibitor has similar shape to substrate; competes for active site; effect overcome by increasing [substrate]; does not change Vmax but increases apparent Km.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp5_kp4: Non-competitive inhibition: inhibitor binds allosteric site (not active site); changes enzyme shape; reduces Vmax but does NOT change Km. Irreversible inhibitors (e.g. organophosphate nerve agents on acetylcholinesterase): covalently bind and permanently inactivate.
learnSch_biology_f0_tp5_insight: Over 75% of modern drugs work by inhibiting specific enzymes. Statins (cholesterol-lowering drugs) are competitive inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase, the enzyme controlling cholesterol synthesis — reducing heart attack risk by ~35%. HIV protease inhibitors block the viral enzyme that cuts viral proteins, stopping HIV from assembling new virus particles. Understanding Km and enzyme kinetics is literally the foundation of modern drug design.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp3: The nervous system and neural signalling
learnSch_biology_f1_tp3_desc: The nervous system allows rapid communication through the body using electrical impulses. The central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) processes information; the peripheral nervous system connects it to receptors and effectors. Neurons transmit action potentials (electrical signals); synapses transmit signals chemically between neurons. This system governs sensation, thought, and muscle control.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp3_kp0: Neuron structure: cell body (nucleus, organelles), axon (conducts impulse away), dendrites (receive signals). Myelinated neurons conduct faster: myelin sheath (from Schwann cells) insulates and enables saltatory conduction at nodes of Ranvier.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp3_kp1: Action potential: stimulus causes Na⁺ channels to open → depolarisation (+40 mV); Na⁺ channels close, K⁺ open → repolarisation (−70 mV); refractory period ensures one-directional transmission.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp3_kp2: Synapse: action potential triggers Ca²⁺ influx → vesicles release neurotransmitter (e.g. acetylcholine) into synaptic cleft → binds receptors on post-synaptic membrane → new potential generated; neurotransmitter is rapidly removed (reuptake or enzymatic breakdown).
learnSch_biology_f1_tp3_kp3: Reflex arcs: fast, involuntary pathways; receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron (spinal cord) → motor neuron → effector; bypasses the brain for speed (e.g. knee-jerk, withdrawal reflex).
learnSch_biology_f1_tp3_kp4: Divisions: somatic nervous system (voluntary, skeletal muscles); autonomic nervous system (involuntary): sympathetic (fight-or-flight, noradrenaline) and parasympathetic (rest-and-digest, acetylcholine) — antagonistic.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp3_insight: Alan Hodgkin and Andrew Huxley recorded the first action potential using giant squid axons in 1952 — squid axons are 1 mm wide (1000× thicker than human axons), making them experimentally accessible. Their mathematical model of ion channel dynamics accurately predicted action potential shape, speed, and the refractory period — earning them the 1963 Nobel Prize. Modern neuroscience, anaesthesia, and cardiac pacemakers all rest on their equations.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp4_desc: The endocrine system communicates via hormones — chemical messengers secreted by glands directly into the bloodstream. Unlike the nervous system, hormonal communication is slower, longer-lasting, and affects distant target cells with complementary receptors. Together, nervous and endocrine systems coordinate all body functions through the neuroendocrine system, with the hypothalamus-pituitary axis as the master control.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp4_kp0: Major glands: pituitary (master gland, releases FSH, LH, TSH, GH, ADH, oxytocin), thyroid (T3/T4, metabolism), adrenal (cortisol, adrenaline), pancreas (insulin, glucagon), gonads (oestrogen, testosterone).
learnSch_biology_f1_tp4_kp1: Hormones: peptide hormones (e.g. insulin) act on surface receptors → second messengers (cAMP cascade); steroid hormones (e.g. oestrogen) are lipid-soluble, cross the membrane and act on nuclear receptors → directly alter gene expression.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp4_kp2: Feedback control: HPG axis (hypothalamus-pituitary-gonad); GnRH → FSH/LH → sex hormones → negative feedback suppresses further GnRH and FSH/LH. Oral contraceptives exploit this by maintaining high oestrogen.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp4_kp3: Adrenaline (epinephrine): secreted by adrenal medulla in stress; increases heart rate, dilates airways, redirects blood to muscles, raises blood glucose; prepares for fight-or-flight within seconds.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp4_kp4: Growth hormone (GH): stimulates growth of bones and muscle; peaks during sleep and exercise; deficiency causes dwarfism; excess causes acromegaly (enlarged hands, jaw, feet). Recombinant GH produced by GM bacteria since 1985.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp4_insight: Insulin was discovered in 1921 by Frederick Banting and Charles Best in Toronto. Before this, Type 1 diabetes was invariably fatal within months. The first patient treated — a 14-year-old boy in diabetic coma — recovered overnight. Banting won the Nobel Prize in 1923. Today, 9 million people worldwide use insulin; since 1982, it has been produced by genetically engineered E. coli bacteria, making it the first commercial GM pharmaceutical.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp5_desc: Human reproduction is sexual, involving the production of haploid gametes (sperm and egg), fertilisation, and development from embryo to adult. Reproductive biology underpins medicine (fertility treatment, contraception, prenatal care), genetics (inheritance), and evolutionary biology. Hormones control the menstrual cycle, pregnancy, birth, and puberty.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp5_kp0: Gametogenesis: spermatogenesis in testes produces 200–500 million sperm per ejaculation; oogenesis in ovaries produces one egg per menstrual cycle; both use meiosis to produce haploid cells.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp5_kp1: Menstrual cycle (28 days): days 1–13 follicular phase (FSH → follicle growth, oestrogen rises); day 14 ovulation (LH surge triggers egg release); days 15–28 luteal phase (progesterone from corpus luteum maintains uterine lining).
learnSch_biology_f1_tp5_kp2: Fertilisation: sperm penetrates egg in fallopian tube; acrosome reaction releases enzymes; cortical reaction prevents polyspermy; diploid zygote formed; implants in uterine endometrium after 6–9 days.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp5_kp3: Embryonic development: zygote → blastocyst → gastrulation (three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm) → neurulation; organs develop from week 3–8 (embryo); weeks 9–40 (foetus); born at ~38 weeks after conception.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp5_kp4: In vitro fertilisation (IVF): eggs retrieved after hormone stimulation; fertilised in lab; embryos transferred to uterus; success rate ~25–40% per cycle for women under 35; over 8 million IVF babies born since 1978.
learnSch_biology_f1_tp5_insight: Louise Brown, born in 1978, was the world's first IVF baby. Her birth was controversial — protesters feared 'unnatural' reproduction. Robert Edwards, who developed IVF with obstetrician Patrick Steptoe, waited 32 years for the Nobel Prize (awarded 2010; Steptoe had died in 1988). Today, 2.5% of all babies in some countries are born through IVF, and the same hormone stimulation techniques are used to treat polycystic ovary syndrome and preserve fertility before cancer chemotherapy.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp3: Nutrient cycles: carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus
learnSch_biology_f2_tp3_desc: Unlike energy, which flows one-way through ecosystems, nutrients are cycled continuously between living organisms and the abiotic environment. The carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus cycles are essential for life — they describe how atoms move through air, soil, water, and organisms. Human activities have dramatically disrupted all three cycles, with major consequences for climate and ecosystems.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp3_kp0: Carbon cycle: photosynthesis removes CO₂; respiration, decomposition, and combustion return it. Human fossil fuel burning adds ~10 billion tonnes of C per year, outpacing natural absorption. Oceans and forests are major carbon sinks.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp3_kp1: Nitrogen cycle: atmospheric N₂ (78%) is biologically unavailable; nitrogen fixation (Rhizobium bacteria in root nodules, lightning) converts N₂ to NH₃; nitrification converts NH₃ to nitrite then nitrate; denitrification returns N₂ to atmosphere.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp3_kp2: Phosphorus cycle: no gaseous phase (unlike C and N); P released by weathering of rock; absorbed by plants; returned by decomposition; critical for DNA, RNA, and ATP; currently being depleted by intensive agriculture without replacement.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp3_kp3: Eutrophication: excess N and P from fertiliser runoff → algal bloom → algae die and decompose → bacteria use up oxygen → anoxic 'dead zones'; Gulf of Mexico dead zone ~22,000 km² annually.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp3_kp4: Decomposers: bacteria and fungi break down dead organic matter into inorganic nutrients; essential for nutrient recycling; temperature and moisture strongly affect decomposition rate (cold/dry conditions slow decomposition, e.g. Arctic permafrost).
learnSch_biology_f2_tp3_insight: Phosphorus may be the most critical nutrient threat the world has not heard of. Phosphate rock (the only commercial source of phosphorus for fertilisers) is a non-renewable resource. At current rates, economically recoverable reserves may last only 50–100 years. Unlike nitrogen, phosphorus cannot be made from the atmosphere. Without phosphorus, modern agriculture cannot feed 8 billion people. Scientists call this 'peak phosphorus' — and it receives far less attention than peak oil.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp4_desc: Population ecology studies how populations grow, decline, and interact over time. Population size depends on natality (births), mortality (deaths), immigration, and emigration. Environmental resistance limits exponential growth; the carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population size an environment can sustainably support. Understanding population dynamics is essential for wildlife management, conservation, and human demography.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp4_kp0: Exponential growth: unlimited resources → J-shaped curve; dN/dt = rN (r = intrinsic growth rate, N = population size). Seen in bacteria, invasive species, and early human population growth.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp4_kp1: Logistic growth: S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve; dN/dt = rN(K−N)/K; growth slows as N approaches K (carrying capacity) due to competition, disease, predation, food limitation.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp4_kp2: Boom-bust (oscillating) cycles: predator-prey interactions cause cyclical population fluctuations; classic example = Canadian lynx and snowshoe hare (10-year cycles recorded since 1845 by Hudson's Bay Company fur records).
learnSch_biology_f2_tp4_kp3: r-strategists vs K-strategists: r-selected species (mice, insects) — small, many offspring, short life, rapid maturation; K-selected species (elephants, whales) — large, few offspring, long lived, delayed maturation. K-strategists are more vulnerable to extinction.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp4_kp4: Allee effect: at very low population sizes, individuals struggle to find mates, cooperate to defend territory, or resist disease; population growth rate becomes negative — leading to extinction vortex. Critical threshold for many endangered species.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp4_insight: The passenger pigeon was the most numerous bird in North America — flocks of billions darkened the sky for hours. By 1914, the last individual died in Cincinnati Zoo. Hunting and habitat loss drove the collapse, but the Allee effect sealed extinction: once numbers fell below a threshold, birds could no longer form functional breeding flocks. The last wild individual was shot in 1900. The lesson: abundant species can collapse faster than expected when population structure is disrupted.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp5_desc: Biodiversity encompasses genetic diversity (variation within species), species diversity (number of species in an ecosystem), and ecosystem diversity (variety of habitats). High biodiversity increases ecosystem stability, productivity, and resilience. Conservation biology uses ecological science to protect biodiversity from the accelerating extinction crisis driven by habitat loss, climate change, overexploitation, invasive species, and pollution.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp5_kp0: Species richness and evenness together define diversity. The Shannon diversity index (H = −Σ pi ln pi) accounts for both number of species and relative abundance.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp5_kp1: Biodiversity hotspots: regions with exceptionally high species richness AND high levels of endemism (species found nowhere else) under threat. E.g. Madagascar, the Amazon, Indonesian archipelago, Cape Floristic Region. 36 hotspots cover 2.5% of land but harbour >50% of all plant species.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp5_kp2: Protected areas: national parks, biosphere reserves, marine protected areas; cover ~16% of land and ~8% of ocean; 30×30 target (protect 30% by 2030) agreed at COP15 (2022). Effectiveness depends on enforcement and connectivity (wildlife corridors).
learnSch_biology_f2_tp5_kp3: Ex situ conservation: preserving species outside natural habitat — zoos, seed banks (Svalbard Global Seed Vault stores 1.4 million seed samples at −18°C), gene banks. Complements in situ (in-habitat) conservation.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp5_kp4: Ecosystem services: biodiversity underpins pollination (~$235 billion/year), water filtration, climate regulation, flood protection, and medicine (25% of pharmaceuticals derived from plants). Biodiversity loss has direct economic costs.
learnSch_biology_f2_tp5_insight: The Svalbard Global Seed Vault, opened in 2008 in the Arctic permafrost of Norway, stores backup seeds for the world's crop varieties — a 'Noah's ark' for food security. In 2015, the first withdrawal was made: Syrian researchers retrieved seeds of drought-resistant wheat and barley varieties lost during the Syrian civil war. The vault holds over 1.4 million seed samples from nearly every country, insuring against the permanent loss of agricultural biodiversity that feeds 8 billion people.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp3_desc: Mutations are changes in the DNA sequence that can alter the proteins produced and potentially affect phenotype. Most mutations are neutral or occur in non-coding regions. Some cause genetic disorders; a few provide evolutionary advantages. Mutations are the ultimate source of all genetic variation. Understanding them is fundamental to medicine, cancer biology, and evolutionary genetics.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp3_kp0: Point mutations: substitution (one base changed — silent, missense, or nonsense); insertion/deletion (frameshift — shifts reading frame, alters all downstream codons). Frameshift mutations are usually more severe.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp3_kp1: Sickle cell anaemia: single point mutation in HBB gene (A → T, codon 6 of beta-globin); glutamic acid → valine; haemoglobin polymerises under low O₂, distorting RBCs. Autosomal recessive; heterozygotes have malaria resistance.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp3_kp2: Cystic fibrosis: autosomal recessive; most common mutation: 3-base deletion (ΔF508) in CFTR gene → misfolded protein, absent from cell membrane; defective Cl⁻ channel → thick mucus in lungs, digestive tract.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp3_kp3: Chromosomal mutations: deletion, duplication, inversion, translocation of chromosome segments. Aneuploidy: wrong chromosome number (Down syndrome: trisomy 21; Turner: 45,X; Klinefelter: 47,XXY).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp3_kp4: Mutagens: chemical (base analogues, alkylating agents, intercalating agents), physical (UV radiation causes thymine dimers; ionising radiation causes double-strand breaks), biological (certain viruses). Cancer results from accumulated somatic mutations in oncogenes and tumour suppressor genes.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp3_insight: The sickle cell allele provides a striking example of heterozygote advantage. In sub-Saharan Africa, where malaria is endemic, individuals with one sickle cell allele (HbAS) are significantly protected against severe malaria while not suffering from sickle cell disease. This explains why the sickle cell allele has risen to frequencies of 15–40% in malaria-endemic regions despite causing severe disease in homozygotes — one of the clearest demonstrations of balancing selection in human evolution.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp4: DNA profiling, PCR, and gel electrophoresis
learnSch_biology_f3_tp4_desc: DNA profiling (fingerprinting) identifies individuals by analysing unique patterns in their DNA. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplifies tiny DNA samples for analysis. Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments by size. Together, these techniques underpin forensic science, paternity testing, disease diagnosis, and evolutionary studies. PCR was also the basis of COVID-19 testing.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp4_kp0: PCR: three steps repeated 30–40 cycles: 1) Denaturation (94°C, strands separate); 2) Annealing (55–65°C, primers bind); 3) Extension (72°C, Taq polymerase extends new strand). Each cycle doubles DNA; 30 cycles → >1 billion copies from one original.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp4_kp1: Gel electrophoresis: DNA fragments loaded into agarose gel; electric field applied (DNA is negatively charged → moves to positive pole); smaller fragments move further; stained with ethidium bromide or SYBR green; compared to molecular weight ladder.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp4_kp2: DNA profiling (STR analysis): analyses short tandem repeats (STRs) at 13–20 specific loci; STR length varies between individuals; probability of two unrelated people matching at all loci: <1 in 1 trillion. Standard for forensic evidence worldwide.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp4_kp3: Restriction enzymes (endonucleases): cut DNA at specific sequences (restriction sites); EcoRI cuts at GːAATTC; different alleles have different restriction sites → different fragment sizes (RFLP — restriction fragment length polymorphism).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp4_kp4: Genetic screening: prenatal diagnosis (amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling) detects chromosomal disorders; PCR-based tests detect single-gene disorders (cystic fibrosis, sickle cell) from foetal cells or pre-implantation embryos in IVF.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp4_insight: Kary Mullis invented PCR in 1983 during a late-night drive in California, reportedly in a flash of inspiration. He won the Nobel Prize in 1993. PCR is arguably the most important technique in modern biology — used in forensics, archaeology (ancient DNA from mammoths, Neanderthals), diagnosis of every viral infection from HIV to COVID-19, cancer genetics, and paternity testing. An idea conceived in minutes changed biology forever.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp5_desc: Genetic engineering allows targeted modification of an organism's genome. Traditional methods used restriction enzymes and plasmid vectors to insert genes. CRISPR-Cas9 (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats) is a revolutionary precision tool that can cut, edit, or replace any gene with unprecedented accuracy and low cost. It is transforming medicine, agriculture, and biological research.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp5_kp0: Traditional GM: target gene cut with restriction enzymes; inserted into plasmid vector; vector transformed into host (bacteria, plant, animal) via heat shock, electroporation, or Agrobacterium. E.g. human insulin gene in E. coli (GM insulin since 1982).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp5_kp1: CRISPR-Cas9 mechanism: guide RNA (gRNA) directs Cas9 endonuclease to specific DNA sequence complementary to gRNA; Cas9 cuts both strands; cell repairs via NHEJ (random, induces mutations = gene knockout) or HDR (precise, uses template = gene correction).
learnSch_biology_f3_tp5_kp2: Medical applications: CRISPR clinical trials for sickle cell disease and beta-thalassaemia (2023: FDA approved Casgevy — first CRISPR therapy, curative); ongoing trials for cancer, HIV, blindness, muscular dystrophy.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp5_kp3: Agricultural GM: Golden Rice (contains beta-carotene precursor of vitamin A; addresses vitamin A deficiency killing 500,000 children/year); Bt crops (insect-resistant gene from Bacillus thuringiensis); herbicide-resistant soy/corn.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp5_kp4: Ethical concerns: germline editing (heritable changes to embryos) is banned in most countries after He Jiankui edited human embryos in 2018 (created twin girls with CCR5 mutation, intended HIV resistance); off-target effects, equity of access, ecological risks from gene drives.
learnSch_biology_f3_tp5_insight: Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier won the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for developing CRISPR-Cas9 as a genome editing tool — the fastest any discovery has moved from publication (2012) to Nobel Prize. CRISPR therapies approved in 2023 can effectively cure sickle cell disease — a condition that has caused suffering for millennia. The cost is ~$2.2 million per patient, raising profound questions about access: the most transformative genetic technology in history may initially help only the very wealthy.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp3: Darwin, Wallace, and the theory of natural selection
learnSch_biology_f4_tp3_desc: Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace independently developed the theory of evolution by natural selection, published jointly in 1858. Darwin's 'On the Origin of Species' (1859) provided extensive evidence and a coherent mechanism for evolution. Their work unified all of biology under a single explanatory framework and is one of the greatest intellectual achievements in human history.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp3_kp0: Darwin's voyage on HMS Beagle (1831–1836) took him to the Galápagos Islands, where he observed that closely related species on different islands had subtly different features adapted to their local environment — seeds of his later theory.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp3_kp1: Malthus's 'Essay on Population' (1798) inspired both Darwin and Wallace: populations grow geometrically but resources grow arithmetically — creating inevitable competition and struggle for existence.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp3_kp2: Darwin's mechanism: variation exists in populations (he didn't know why — Mendel's genetics was unknown to him); those better adapted survive and reproduce; traits accumulate over generations; species gradually change.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp3_kp3: Wallace's independent discovery: working in the Malay Archipelago in 1858, ill with malaria, Wallace wrote up his identical theory and sent it to Darwin. This prompted Darwin to finally publish. Wallace was a broader evolutionary thinker: he also founded biogeography and the Wallace Line (faunal boundary between Asia and Australia).
learnSch_biology_f4_tp3_kp4: Darwin didn't know about DNA or genes. The 'Modern Synthesis' (1930s–50s) merged Darwinian evolution with Mendelian genetics and population genetics (Fisher, Haldane, Wright), creating neo-Darwinism: the current framework of evolutionary biology.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp3_insight: Darwin delayed publishing his theory for 20 years — partly from scientific caution, partly from knowing the social and religious controversy it would cause. His wife Emma was deeply religious, and he was aware the theory undermined special creation. When he finally published, the Bishop of Oxford ('Soapy Sam' Wilberforce) asked Thomas Henry Huxley at the 1860 Oxford debate whether he was descended from an ape on his grandfather's or grandmother's side. Huxley reportedly replied he would rather be descended from an ape than from a man who misused his gifts.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp4: The fossil record and transitional fossils
learnSch_biology_f4_tp4_desc: The fossil record is the accumulated documentation of past life preserved in rock. Fossils form when organisms are buried in sediment; soft tissues decay but hard structures can mineralise. The fossil record is incomplete but consistently shows gradual change over time, with no major lineage appearing without precursors. Transitional fossils capture intermediate stages between ancestral and derived forms — strong evidence for evolution.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp4_kp0: Fossil formation: rapid burial prevents decomposition; minerals replace organic molecules; sedimentary rock preserves sequence (stratigraphy); lower strata = older. Radiometric dating (e.g. uranium-lead, potassium-argon, carbon-14) gives absolute ages.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp4_kp1: Tiktaalik (375 Ma): transitional fish-tetrapod; had fins AND proto-limbs (wrist bones, neck); discovered in 2004 in Canadian Arctic exactly where the fossil record predicted a transitional form would be found.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp4_kp2: Archaeopteryx (147 Ma): transitional dinosaur-bird; feathers, wishbone, and flight apparatus (bird); teeth, clawed wings, and long bony tail (non-avian dinosaur). Discovered in 1861, two years after Darwin published.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp4_kp3: Whale evolution: terrestrial Pakicetus (52 Ma) → amphibious Ambulocetus (49 Ma) → fully aquatic Rodhocetus (47 Ma) → modern whales; vestigial hind limb bones persist in modern whale skeletons — one of the best-documented evolutionary transitions.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp4_kp4: Cambrian explosion (~541–485 Ma): sudden appearance of most major animal body plans in the fossil record within ~25 million years; not a flaw in evolution — explanations include rise of predation, increased oxygen, ecological complexity, and preservation bias (soft-body animals didn't fossilise before shells evolved).
learnSch_biology_f4_tp4_insight: Mary Anning (1799–1847) was a self-taught fossil collector from Lyme Regis who discovered the first complete plesiosaur and ichthyosaur skeletons, revolutionising understanding of prehistoric life. Yet as a working-class woman, she was excluded from the Geological Society and never received proper credit in her lifetime. Scientists who came to buy her specimens published the discoveries under their own names. The tongue twister 'she sells seashells' may have been written about her.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp5_desc: Humans (Homo sapiens) evolved in Africa over the past 6–7 million years from a common ancestor shared with chimpanzees. Our lineage (hominins) includes many species, with complex patterns of divergence, coexistence, and interbreeding. Phylogenetics uses molecular data to reconstruct evolutionary relationships. Ancient DNA has revolutionised our understanding of human prehistory.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp5_kp0: Hominin timeline: Sahelanthropus (~7 Ma) → Australopithecus (4–2 Ma, bipedal, small brain, tool use) → Homo habilis (2.4 Ma, larger brain) → Homo erectus (1.9 Ma, fire, left Africa) → Homo sapiens (300 ka, Africa).
learnSch_biology_f4_tp5_kp1: Neanderthals (Homo neanderthalensis): lived in Europe/Asia 400–40 ka; larger brains than modern humans; interbred with Homo sapiens (modern non-African people carry ~2% Neanderthal DNA); went extinct ~40 ka, possibly due to competition, climate, or disease.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp5_kp2: Out of Africa: Homo sapiens migrated from Africa ~60–70 ka; colonised all continents except Antarctica; evidence from fossil record, archaeology (tools, art), and genetic diversity (African populations have highest genetic diversity — consistent with longest occupation).
learnSch_biology_f4_tp5_kp3: Phylogenetics: reconstructs evolutionary trees (phylogenies) from molecular data; parsimony principle (most parsimonious tree = fewest evolutionary changes); Bayesian methods used for molecular clock analysis. DNA sequences establish the 'tree of life'.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp5_kp4: Cultural evolution: uniquely human (language, cumulative culture, technology); allows much faster adaptation than genetic evolution; tool use transmitted by learning, not genetics; brain size (1350 mL vs chimps 400 mL) and FOXP2 gene (speech and language) key to cognitive evolution.
learnSch_biology_f4_tp5_insight: In 2022, Svante Pääbo won the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for ancient DNA analysis — specifically for sequencing the complete Neanderthal genome from a 40,000-year-old finger bone and discovering that Neanderthals and Homo sapiens interbred. He also discovered the Denisovans (a new human species) from a single finger bone fragment in Siberia. We now know modern humans carry Denisovan immune genes (HLA alleles) that help fight pathogens — our ancient liaisons gave us an immune system upgrade.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp3: Plant hormones: auxin, gibberellin, abscisic acid
learnSch_biology_f5_tp3_desc: Plant hormones (phytohormones) are chemical signals that coordinate growth, development, and responses to the environment. Unlike animal hormones, they are not produced by dedicated glands but by cells throughout the plant. Key hormones include auxin (growth, tropisms), gibberellins (stem elongation, germination), abscisic acid (stress response, dormancy), cytokinins (cell division), and ethylene (ripening, ageing).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp3_kp0: Auxin (IAA): produced at shoot tip; moves downward; stimulates cell elongation; controls phototropism (light causes auxin redistribution to shaded side → uneven growth → bending towards light) and gravitropism.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp3_kp1: Apical dominance: auxin from apical bud diffuses down, inhibiting lateral bud growth. Remove apical bud → lateral buds grow (bushier plant). Used in horticultural pruning.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp3_kp2: Gibberellins: promote stem elongation by stimulating cell division and elongation; break seed dormancy; stimulate germination; promote fruit development. Commercially: make seedless grapes larger.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp3_kp3: Abscisic acid (ABA): 'stress hormone'; accumulates during drought → causes stomata to close (guard cells lose K⁺ → lose turgor → stoma closes); promotes seed dormancy and bud dormancy in winter.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp3_kp4: Ethylene (gas): promotes fruit ripening, leaf abscission (autumn leaf drop via breaking pectin in middle lamella), response to wounding; used commercially to ripen bananas and tomatoes after transport.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp3_insight: The discovery that a gas could act as a plant hormone was accidental. In 1901, Dimitry Neljubow noticed that pea seedlings grew abnormally in the lab — horizontal rather than vertical. He traced it to trace amounts of ethylene leaking from gas lamps. This was the first identification of ethylene as a plant growth regulator. Today, fruits are shipped unripe (no ethylene) and treated with ethylene gas at destination — a billion-dollar global industry built on a serendipitous gas lamp observation.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp4_desc: Leaves are optimised for photosynthesis and gas exchange. Their structure — thin, flat, with a waxy cuticle, palisade mesophyll, spongy mesophyll, and stomata — maximises light capture, CO₂ absorption, and water conservation. Stomata are controlled by guard cells that regulate gas exchange and transpiration in response to light, CO₂ concentration, and water stress.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp4_kp0: Leaf cross-section (top to bottom): upper epidermis (waxy cuticle, no chloroplasts), palisade mesophyll (many chloroplasts, primary photosynthesis site), spongy mesophyll (air spaces, gas exchange), lower epidermis (stomata and guard cells).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp4_kp1: Stomata: pore flanked by two guard cells; guard cells swell (gain turgor) → stoma opens; lose turgor → stoma closes. Guard cells have unevenly thickened walls — inner wall thicker, causing bowing when turgid.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp4_kp2: Stomata opening: light → guard cells absorb K⁺ (active transport) → solute concentration increases → water enters by osmosis → guard cells swell → stoma opens. ABA reverses this in drought.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp4_kp3: Transpiration rate factors: temperature (increases vapour pressure gradient), humidity (decreases gradient, reducing rate), wind (removes moist air, increasing gradient), light (opens stomata), water availability.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp4_kp4: Xerophytes: desert plants adapted to reduce transpiration; thick waxy cuticle, sunken stomata (trapped humid air), rolled leaves (marram grass), reduced leaf area (cacti — leaves modified to spines), CAM photosynthesis (stomata open at night).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp4_insight: A single large oak tree transpires approximately 150,000 litres of water per year — about the volume of a large swimming pool. Globally, plants transpire approximately 62,000 km³ of water per year, which is more than half of all global evaporation. Forests effectively pump water from soil to atmosphere, driving regional rainfall and cooling the climate. The Amazon rainforest generates its own rainfall: atmospheric rivers of water vapour ('flying rivers') move water from the ocean deep into the continent, irrigating areas thousands of kilometres from the sea.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp5_desc: Flowering marks the transition from vegetative to reproductive growth in angiosperms. Photoperiodism — the response to day length (actually night length) — controls flowering in many species. After pollination and fertilisation, seeds develop and are dispersed. Seed dispersal mechanisms are extraordinarily diverse, shaped by millions of years of co-evolution between plants and animals.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp5_kp0: Photoperiodism: long-night plants (short-day) flower when night exceeds a critical length (chrysanthemum, poinsettia); short-night plants (long-day) flower when night is shorter than critical length (lettuce, spinach). Day-neutral plants (tomato, sunflower) are insensitive to photoperiod.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp5_kp1: Phytochrome: photoreceptor pigment; two forms: Pr (inactive, absorbs red light 660 nm → converts to Pfr) and Pfr (active form, absorbs far-red 730 nm → converts to Pr). Pfr promotes flowering in long-day plants, inhibits in short-day plants.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp5_kp2: Seed dormancy: prevents germination until conditions are favourable; broken by cold stratification (winter), fire (banksias), smoke chemicals (South African fynbos), scarification (passage through animal gut), or light (lettuce seeds). Survival strategy.
learnSch_biology_f5_tp5_kp3: Fruit/seed dispersal: wind (winged samaras — ash, sycamore; plumed achenes — dandelion), animal-external (hooked burrs — burdock inspired Velcro), animal-internal (berries and drupes eaten, seeds pass through gut), water (coconuts float months across oceans), explosive (touch-me-not, squirting cucumber).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp5_kp4: Vernalisation: requirement for extended cold (winter) before flowering; ensures spring flowering; wheat and barley require vernalisation; epigenetically regulated (histone methylation silences floral repressor gene FLC during cold).
learnSch_biology_f5_tp5_insight: The invention of Velcro in 1941 was directly inspired by plant seed dispersal. Swiss engineer Georges de Mestral returned from a walk and noticed burdock burrs (hooked achenes) clinging to his dog's fur and his wool socks. Under a microscope he saw tiny hooks catching on fabric loops. He spent 8 years perfecting the design, patenting it in 1955. Today Velcro is used in everything from space suits to surgical dressings — a billion-dollar biomimicry innovation inspired by a plant trying to get its seeds dispersed.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp3: Pathogens and the chain of infection
learnSch_biology_f6_tp3_desc: Pathogens are microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, prions) that cause disease. The chain of infection describes how infectious diseases spread: pathogen → reservoir → exit route → transmission → entry route → susceptible host. Breaking any link in this chain prevents disease transmission. Epidemiology studies the patterns and causes of disease in populations.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp3_kp0: Chain of infection: pathogen (causative agent) → reservoir (source, e.g. infected human or animal) → portal of exit (droplets, blood, faeces) → mode of transmission (direct contact, airborne, vector, contaminated water) → portal of entry (respiratory tract, mucous membranes, breaks in skin) → susceptible host.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp3_kp1: Koch's postulates (1884): the pathogen must be found in all sick individuals; it must be isolated and grown in pure culture; injecting it into a healthy host must cause disease; it must be re-isolated from the newly sick host.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp3_kp2: Transmission routes: droplet (flu, COVID-19), faecal-oral (cholera, typhoid), vector (malaria — Anopheles mosquito, Lyme disease — tick), direct contact (MRSA), sexually transmitted (HIV, gonorrhoea), vertical (mother to foetus).
learnSch_biology_f6_tp3_kp3: R0 (basic reproduction number): average number of secondary infections from one case. R0 < 1: disease dies out; R0 > 1: epidemic spreads. Measles R0 = 12–18 (most infectious known pathogen); COVID-19 original strain ~2–3; Delta ~5–6; Omicron ~8–15.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp3_kp4: Herd immunity: when enough of the population is immune (vaccinated or previously infected), spread is blocked even for non-immune individuals. Herd immunity threshold = 1 − 1/R0. For measles (R0 = 15), ~93% of population must be immune.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp3_insight: Ignaz Semmelweis discovered in 1847 that handwashing before delivering babies reduced maternal death from puerperal fever from 10–18% to 1–2%. He was ignored and ridiculed because the germ theory of disease hadn't been established. He was eventually committed to an asylum where he died — ironically of the same type of infection he spent his life fighting. Fifteen years after his death, Lister used his ideas to develop antiseptic surgery. Semmelweis is now considered a pioneer of infection control.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp4: Fungal biology and the human microbiome
learnSch_biology_f6_tp4_desc: Fungi are eukaryotic organisms — neither plants nor animals — that play vital roles as decomposers, mutualistic partners (mycorrhizae), and producers of medicines (penicillin). The human microbiome — the community of microorganisms inhabiting our bodies — is increasingly understood to affect immunity, mental health, and metabolic disease. It contains more microbial cells than human cells.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp4_kp0: Fungi: eukaryotic; cell walls contain chitin (not cellulose); heterotrophic (digest externally, absorb nutrients); hyphae network = mycelium; reproduce by spores (sexually and asexually); classification: ascomycetes (yeasts, moulds), basidiomycetes (mushrooms).
learnSch_biology_f6_tp4_kp1: Mycorrhizal networks: symbiotic association between fungal hyphae and plant roots; fungi receive sugars from plant; plant gains water and minerals (P, N) via extended hyphal network; ~90% of land plants use mycorrhizae; critical for forest ecosystems.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp4_kp2: Human microbiome: ~38 trillion microbes (bacteria, fungi, viruses, archaea) in gut, skin, oral cavity. Gut microbiome: 1000+ bacterial species; ferments dietary fibre into short-chain fatty acids (SFCAs); produces vitamins B12 and K; trains immune system; Bacteroidetes and Firmicutes are dominant phyla.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp4_kp3: Microbiome and health: disruption (dysbiosis) linked to obesity, inflammatory bowel disease, type 2 diabetes, depression, autism spectrum disorder, and autoimmune conditions. Antibiotic overuse depletes microbiome diversity — 'antibiotic-associated diarrhoea' (Clostridioides difficile overgrowth).
learnSch_biology_f6_tp4_kp4: Fungal pathogens: Candida (thrush), Aspergillus (lung infections in immunocompromised), Tinea (ringworm, athlete's foot); all eukaryotic — harder to treat because human and fungal cells share features, reducing drug selectivity.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp4_insight: Ophiocordyceps fungi (zombie fungi) infect insects, take over their nervous systems, and force them to climb to a specific height before killing them and releasing spores from their corpses. Each fungus species has a specific host insect and precise height for sporulation. This extraordinary host manipulation evolved independently multiple times. Scientists are studying these fungi's ability to control animal behaviour — their biochemical signals could have pharmaceutical applications.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp5_desc: Biotechnology uses biological systems and organisms to develop products and processes. Industrial fermentation uses microorganisms in controlled bioreactors to produce antibiotics, enzymes, vitamins, biofuels, and pharmaceuticals. Genetic engineering has enabled microbes to produce human proteins like insulin at industrial scale, revolutionising medicine. Modern biotechnology includes CRISPR, recombinant DNA, and synthetic biology.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp5_kp0: Bioreactors (fermenters): stainless steel vessels with temperature, pH, O₂, and nutrient control; inoculated with microorganism culture; produce antibiotics, enzymes, amino acids, biofuels, single-cell protein. Continuous culture (chemostat) vs batch culture.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp5_kp1: Recombinant insulin (Humulin): human insulin gene isolated and inserted into E. coli plasmid using restriction enzymes and ligase; bacteria express human insulin; purified and sterilised; available since 1982. Previously extracted from pig/cow pancreases (less pure, some allergic reactions).
learnSch_biology_f6_tp5_kp2: Recombinant DNA technology steps: 1) isolate target gene (PCR or restriction enzyme digest); 2) cut vector (plasmid) with same restriction enzyme; 3) ligate gene into vector; 4) transform into host; 5) select transformants (antibiotic resistance marker); 6) culture and harvest product.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp5_kp3: Gene therapy: introduce functional gene into patient's cells to treat genetic disease; viral vectors (AAV, lentivirus) deliver gene; ex vivo (modify cells outside body, return patient) or in vivo (inject directly). Approved: Luxturna (blindness), Zolgensma (spinal muscular atrophy, cost $3.5M — world's most expensive drug).
learnSch_biology_f6_tp5_kp4: Synthetic biology: design and build new biological systems from standardised genetic parts (BioBricks); applications: bacteria that produce artemisinin (antimalarial), biosensors, biodegradable plastics, living materials. iGEM (International Genetically Engineered Machine) student competition drives innovation.
learnSch_biology_f6_tp5_insight: The entire human insulin gene (1431 base pairs) was synthesised chemically from scratch in 1977 by Herbert Boyer's lab and expressed in E. coli. When Genentech (the company Boyer co-founded) received FDA approval for recombinant insulin in 1982, it was the first genetically engineered human pharmaceutical ever approved. Genentech's initial stock offering in 1980 raised $35 million in 20 minutes — at the time the largest biotech IPO in history. It launched the entire pharmaceutical biotechnology industry.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp3_desc: Pattern recognition is finding similarities and repeated elements within problems. Generalisation extracts the core pattern into a reusable solution. Together they are the bridge between decomposed sub-problems and an efficient, general-purpose algorithm. Recognising patterns reduces redundant work and is the foundation of machine learning.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp3_kp0: Pattern recognition: identifying similarities, regularities, or recurring features in data or problems (e.g. all sorting problems share the pattern of comparing and rearranging elements).
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp3_kp1: Generalisation: removing problem-specific details to create a template or algorithm that solves an entire class of similar problems, not just one instance.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp3_kp2: Functions/procedures as generalisation: instead of repeating code that draws a square, write drawSquare(size) — one general solution replaces many specific duplicates.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp3_kp3: Pattern recognition in data: detecting trends, anomalies, or structures in datasets. Spam filters recognise patterns in email text; fraud detection recognises unusual transaction patterns.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp3_kp4: Generalisation in data modelling: instead of creating a separate database table for every customer, create one Customer table with columns — generalising all customers into a single structure.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp3_insight: Machine learning is fundamentally computational pattern recognition at scale. A neural network trained to recognise images does not have explicit rules programmed in — it learns statistical patterns from millions of examples. AlphaFold (2020) used pattern recognition across 200 million known protein sequences to predict the 3D structure of virtually every protein known to science, solving a 50-year-old biology grand challenge in months.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp4_desc: Flowcharts and pseudocode are planning tools used before writing code. Flowcharts use shapes and arrows to visualise the flow of a program visually. Pseudocode describes logic in structured English — close to code but language-independent. Both help clarify thinking, spot logic errors, and communicate algorithms to others.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp4_kp0: Flowchart symbols: oval/rounded rectangle = start/end (terminal); rectangle = process/instruction; diamond = decision (yes/no); parallelogram = input/output; arrows show flow direction.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp4_kp1: Flowcharts are excellent for visualising branching logic and loops. A loop in a flowchart shows an arrow flowing back to an earlier step when the loop condition is still true.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp4_kp2: Pseudocode uses keywords like IF, ELSE, WHILE, FOR, REPEAT, INPUT, OUTPUT, RETURN. No specific syntax rules — clarity and logical correctness matter more than exact formatting.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp4_kp3: Pseudocode example for linear search: SET found = false; FOR each item in list: IF item = target THEN SET found = true; END FOR; RETURN found.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp4_kp4: Trace tables: manually execute pseudocode step by step, recording variable values at each step. Used to test logic on paper before coding. Excellent for finding off-by-one errors and wrong loop conditions.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp4_insight: NASA’s Mars Climate Orbiter (1999) was lost because one engineering team used metric units (Newton-seconds) and another used imperial units (pound-force-seconds) — and neither caught the mismatch. The spacecraft entered the Martian atmosphere at the wrong angle and was destroyed. The $327 million loss could have been caught by careful algorithm documentation (pseudocode or specification) at the design stage. Ambiguous informal communication between teams was the root cause.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp5_desc: Top-down design starts with the overall problem and breaks it into sub-problems recursively until each piece is simple enough to code directly. Bottom-up design starts by building small, tested components and combines them into larger systems. Most real software uses both approaches in different phases.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp5_kp0: Top-down (stepwise refinement): start with the high-level problem → decompose into major modules → decompose each module into sub-tasks → continue until tasks are small enough to code. Produces a structure chart (hierarchy diagram).
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp5_kp1: Structure chart: a tree diagram showing how modules relate. Parent modules call child modules. Shows hierarchy, not flow. Each box is one task; lines show calls.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp5_kp2: Bottom-up design: write and test small utility functions first, then combine into larger components. Encouraged by test-driven development (TDD): write a test, write minimal code to pass it, refactor, repeat.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp5_kp3: Advantages of top-down: clear overall structure before coding; easy to assign modules to different team members; progress is visible. Disadvantages: integration problems may only appear late.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp5_kp4: Modular programming: code divided into self-contained modules/functions with defined inputs and outputs. Modules can be written and tested independently, then composed. Reduces complexity and enables reuse.
learnSch_computer_science_f0_tp5_insight: The development of the Apollo Guidance Computer (1965–1969) pioneered modular software engineering. MIT’s team led by Margaret Hamilton split the software into modules with strictly defined interfaces. When, during the Apollo 11 landing, the computer was overloaded with data and threatened to abort, the modular design meant the priority system correctly discarded lower-priority tasks and kept the critical navigation running. Hamilton coined the phrase 'software engineering' to give the discipline the same respect as other branches of engineering.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp3_desc: Conditionals control which code executes based on whether conditions are true or false. Boolean logic (AND, OR, NOT) combines multiple conditions. Together they form the decision-making core of every program — from a simple age check to a complex fraud detection system.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp3_kp0: IF-ELIF-ELSE structure: IF condition: do something ELIF other_condition: do something else ELSE: default action. Only one branch executes per run.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp3_kp1: Boolean operators: AND returns True only if both operands are True. OR returns True if at least one is True. NOT reverses the value. Used to combine multiple conditions into one expression.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp3_kp2: Truth tables: list all possible combinations of inputs and the resulting output. AND has 1 True output out of 4; OR has 3 True outputs out of 4; XOR (exclusive or) has 2 True outputs (different inputs).
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp3_kp3: Short-circuit evaluation: in AND, if the first operand is False, the second is never evaluated (result is already False). In OR, if first is True, second is skipped. Used to avoid errors (e.g. checking if list is non-empty before accessing index).
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp3_kp4: Nested conditionals vs compound conditions: IF x > 0 AND x < 10 is cleaner than nested IF x > 0: IF x < 10. Always prefer compound conditions for readability, and match ELSE branches carefully to avoid logic errors.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp3_insight: George Boole published 'An Investigation of the Laws of Thought' in 1854, developing Boolean algebra — a mathematics of True/False logic. He was a self-taught mathematician who never attended university. Eighty years later, Claude Shannon showed that Boolean algebra perfectly describes electrical circuits with two states (on/off). Every transistor in every computer executes Boolean logic billions of times per second. Boole died of pneumonia in 1864, never knowing that his abstract algebra would become the foundation of the digital age.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp4_desc: Lists, arrays, and dictionaries are the workhorses of everyday programming. Lists/arrays store ordered sequences accessed by index; dictionaries (hash maps) store key-value pairs for fast lookup. Choosing the right collection type makes code cleaner and faster.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp4_kp0: Python list: ordered, mutable, allows duplicates, any data type. Access by index (0-based). Common operations: append(), remove(), len(), sorted(), list slicing list[a:b].
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp4_kp1: List comprehension (Python): [expression for item in iterable if condition]. Concise way to create or transform lists. Example: [x**2 for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0] → squares of even numbers 0–8.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp4_kp2: Dictionary (Python dict): unordered (insertion-ordered since Python 3.7) key-value store. Keys must be unique and hashable. dict[key] = value; dict.get(key, default); dict.keys(); dict.values(); dict.items().
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp4_kp3: Dictionary lookup is O(1) average — ideal for frequency counting, caching results (memoisation), and storing structured data. Much faster than searching a list for a key.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp4_kp4: Tuple: immutable ordered sequence. Faster than lists; can be used as dictionary keys (since they are hashable). Set: unordered, no duplicates, O(1) membership test. Use set for uniqueness checks and fast intersection/union operations.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp4_insight: Guido van Rossum created Python in 1991, naming it after Monty Python’s Flying Circus, not the snake. His design philosophy — 'there should be one obvious way to do it' — and the inclusion of powerful built-in collections like dicts and lists made Python enormously accessible. Today Python is the most popular programming language in the world (TIOBE index), powering most machine learning research, data science, and automation scripts. Its dynamic typing and garbage collection trade raw speed for productivity — a tradeoff most programmers accept.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp5_desc: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) organises code around objects — entities that bundle data (attributes) and behaviour (methods). OOP enables modelling of real-world concepts, code reuse through inheritance, and encapsulation of complexity. Java, Python, C++, C#, and most modern languages support OOP.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp5_kp0: Class: a blueprint/template defining attributes (variables) and methods (functions). Object: an instance of a class. Example: class Dog defines breed and name; my_dog = Dog('Labrador', 'Rex') creates one specific dog object.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp5_kp1: Encapsulation: hiding internal implementation details; only expose what is necessary through public methods. Private attributes (prefixed __ in Python) cannot be accessed directly from outside the class.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp5_kp2: Inheritance: a child class inherits attributes and methods from a parent class and can extend or override them. Promotes code reuse. Example: class Cat(Animal) inherits speak() from Animal but overrides it to return 'Meow'.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp5_kp3: Polymorphism: the same method name behaves differently depending on which object calls it. Example: shape.area() works for Circle, Rectangle, Triangle objects — each computes area differently. Enables writing generic code.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp5_kp4: Constructor (__init__ in Python): a special method called automatically when an object is created. Initialises the object’s attributes. self refers to the current instance — always the first parameter of every method.
learnSch_computer_science_f1_tp5_insight: Alan Kay invented Smalltalk in the 1970s at Xerox PARC, the first fully object-oriented language, and coined the term 'object-oriented programming.' His original vision was inspired by biology: cells communicate by sending messages to each other without knowing each other’s internal chemistry. He later said that the most important thing about OOP is the messaging, not the objects themselves. He also invented the concept of the personal computer (the Dynabook) and once said: 'The best way to predict the future is to invent it.'
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp3_desc: All data in a computer — numbers, text, images, sound — is stored as binary (1s and 0s). Hexadecimal (base-16) is a compact shorthand for binary. Understanding data representation is essential for low-level programming, networking, and understanding how computers actually store information.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp3_kp0: Binary (base-2): only digits 0 and 1. Each bit doubles: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16… Convert decimal 42 to binary: 42=32+8+2 → 101010. Convert binary 1101 to decimal: 8+4+0+1=13.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp3_kp1: Hexadecimal (base-16): digits 0–9 then A–F (A=10, B=11…F=15). One hex digit = exactly 4 bits (nibble). FF hex = 1111 1111 = 255 decimal. Used in memory addresses, colour codes (#FF5733), MAC addresses.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp3_kp2: Two's complement: standard way computers represent negative integers. Flip all bits, add 1. With 8 bits, -1 = 11111111, -128 = 10000000. This makes addition of positive and negative numbers work with the same circuitry.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp3_kp3: Text encoding: ASCII maps 128 characters to 7-bit codes (A=65, a=97, space=32). Unicode (UTF-8) extends this to 1.1 million characters covering all world scripts, emoji, and mathematical symbols.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp3_kp4: Image representation: a bitmap stores each pixel as a binary number. 24-bit colour = 8 bits each for Red, Green, Blue (256 levels each → 16.7 million possible colours). Higher bit depth = more colours but more storage.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp3_insight: The Y2K bug (Year 2000 problem) arose because 1960s programmers stored years as 2 digits to save expensive memory (e.g. '99' for 1999). When 2000 approached, '00' would be interpreted as 1900, potentially causing calculations (loan interest, pension payments, air traffic control) to fail catastrophically. Governments and companies worldwide spent an estimated $300–$600 billion fixing old code. The lesson: data representation decisions made early become embedded in systems for decades.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp4_desc: Logic gates are the physical implementation of Boolean operations in hardware. Every processor contains billions of transistors acting as AND, OR, and NOT gates. Understanding logic gates bridges the gap between software (Boolean conditions) and hardware (electronic circuits).
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp4_kp0: Basic gates: NOT (inverter): output = opposite of input. AND: output = 1 only when both inputs = 1. OR: output = 1 when at least one input = 1. NAND, NOR, XOR, XNOR are derived gates.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp4_kp1: NAND gate is 'functionally complete': any logic circuit can be built from NAND gates alone. Most processors are designed primarily with NAND and NOR gates for this reason.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp4_kp2: Boolean algebra laws: De Morgan's theorems: NOT(A AND B) = (NOT A) OR (NOT B); NOT(A OR B) = (NOT A) AND (NOT B). Used to simplify logic circuits.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp4_kp3: Half adder: combines XOR (sum bit) and AND (carry bit) gates to add two 1-bit numbers. Full adder handles carry-in. Chain 8 full adders → add 8-bit numbers. This is how your CPU's ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) does addition.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp4_kp4: Karnaugh maps (K-maps): visual tool for simplifying Boolean expressions by grouping 1s in a truth table grid. Reduces the number of gates needed, saving chip space and power.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp4_insight: A modern Intel or Apple Silicon chip contains up to 20 billion transistors in an area smaller than a fingernail. Each transistor is about 3–7 nanometres across — smaller than most protein molecules. The entire complexity of an operating system, browser, and AI assistant ultimately executes as billions of AND, OR, and NOT operations per nanosecond. Gordon Moore observed in 1965 that transistor density doubles approximately every two years (Moore’s Law) — this prediction held for 60 years and drove the entire modern technology revolution.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp5: CPU components and the fetch-decode-execute cycle
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp5_desc: The CPU (Central Processing Unit) is the brain of a computer. It fetches instructions from memory, decodes what they mean, and executes them. Understanding the CPU's components and the FDE cycle explains why computer speed depends on clock speed, cache size, and the number of cores.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp5_kp0: CPU components: ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit): performs arithmetic (+, -, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations. CU (Control Unit): directs fetch-decode-execute. Registers: tiny, ultra-fast storage inside the CPU. Cache: fast memory close to CPU (L1, L2, L3). Clock: synchronises all operations.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp5_kp1: Fetch: the Program Counter (PC) holds the address of the next instruction. The CPU fetches that instruction from RAM into the Instruction Register (IR) via the data bus. PC is incremented to point to the next instruction.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp5_kp2: Decode: the Control Unit interprets (decodes) the binary instruction in the IR to determine what operation to perform and which data/registers to use.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp5_kp3: Execute: the ALU or other components carry out the instruction (add two registers, load data from memory, jump to new address). Result is stored in a register or written to RAM.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp5_kp4: CPU performance factors: Clock speed (GHz): cycles per second — more cycles = more instructions. Cores: multiple CPUs on one chip (parallel execution). Cache size: larger cache reduces slow RAM accesses. Pipeline: overlap FDE stages for multiple instructions simultaneously.
learnSch_computer_science_f2_tp5_insight: The ENIAC (1945), the first electronic general-purpose computer, weighed 30 tons, occupied 1800 square feet, and consumed 150 kilowatts of power — enough to dim the lights of an entire city block when switched on. It could perform 5000 additions per second. A modern smartphone processor performs 15 trillion operations per second, is 1000 times more energy-efficient, and fits in your pocket. The entire human genome can be sequenced and analysed on a laptop. This — the most rapid capability improvement of any technology in human history — happened in 80 years.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp3_desc: Stacks, queues, and linked lists are abstract data types with specific access rules. Stacks (LIFO) model call stacks and undo history; queues (FIFO) model scheduling and breadth-first search; linked lists model dynamic sequences. Mastering these structures builds the foundation for understanding all other complex data structures.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp3_kp0: Stack (LIFO — Last In, First Out): operations are push (add to top) and pop (remove from top). Peek/top: view top without removing. Used in: function call stack, expression evaluation, undo features, backtracking algorithms.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp3_kp1: Queue (FIFO — First In, First Out): enqueue adds to the back; dequeue removes from the front. Used in: CPU scheduling, print queues, BFS graph traversal, message queues between services.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp3_kp2: Deque (double-ended queue): supports insert/remove at both ends. Used in: sliding window problems, implementing both stacks and queues, browser history (forward and back navigation).
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp3_kp3: Singly linked list: each node has data + pointer to next. O(1) insert/delete at head. O(n) access by index. No wasted pre-allocated memory. Doubly linked list adds a pointer to previous node.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp3_kp4: Circular linked list: last node’s next pointer points back to the head. Used in round-robin scheduling (CPU time slices) and circular buffers (audio streaming, network buffers).
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp3_insight: The JavaScript call stack is a real stack in your browser. Every time a function is called, a frame is pushed; when it returns, the frame is popped. If you write an infinite recursive function in JavaScript and run it, you get: 'Maximum call stack size exceeded' — you literally overflowed the stack. The website Stack Overflow (founded 2008) is named after this exact error. It has over 23 million questions answered, making it arguably the most valuable programming resource ever created.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp4_desc: Trees are hierarchical data structures — one root node connected to child nodes, which have their own children. Binary Search Trees (BSTs) enforce an ordering property that enables fast search, insert, and delete. Trees model file systems, organisation charts, decision processes, and form the basis of most database indexes.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp4_kp0: Tree terminology: root (top node with no parent); leaf (node with no children); depth (distance from root); height (max depth in tree); subtree (any node + its descendants); parent/child/sibling relationships.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp4_kp1: Binary tree: each node has at most 2 children (left and right). Binary Search Tree (BST): left subtree values < node value < right subtree values. This invariant enables binary search.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp4_kp2: BST operations: search O(log n) average; insert O(log n) average; delete O(log n) average. Worst case O(n) if tree is degenerate (all nodes in a line, like inserting sorted data).
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp4_kp3: Tree traversals: In-order (left→node→right): visits BST nodes in sorted order. Pre-order (node→left→right): used for copying/serialising trees. Post-order (left→right→node): used for deletion and expression evaluation.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp4_kp4: Balanced BSTs (AVL tree, Red-Black tree): automatically rebalance after insert/delete to guarantee O(log n) worst case. Red-Black trees are used in Java’s TreeMap, C++’s std::map, and Linux’s process scheduler.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp4_insight: Database B-trees (B+ trees) are the reason your database can find any row among billions in milliseconds. A B+ tree in a database index is balanced, has hundreds of children per node (to minimise disk reads), and stores all data in its leaves. When you query SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 42, the database doesn’t scan all rows — it traverses the B+ tree index in 3–4 disk reads regardless of table size. PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and Oracle all use B+ trees as their primary index structure.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp5_desc: Recursion is when a function calls itself with a smaller input, solving the base case directly and the recursive case by reducing to a smaller version of the same problem. Divide-and-conquer is a strategy that uses recursion: split the problem, solve sub-problems, combine results. Merge sort, quick sort, and binary search are all divide-and-conquer algorithms.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp5_kp0: Recursion requires: a base case (simplest case, solved directly — no recursive call) and a recursive case (problem reduced to a smaller version, calling itself). Without a base case, recursion runs forever until stack overflow.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp5_kp1: Call stack and recursion: each recursive call adds a new frame to the call stack. Factorial(5) calls Factorial(4) → Factorial(3) →…→ Factorial(0) → returns 1 → unwinds. Maximum recursion depth (Python default: 1000) limits how deep you can recurse.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp5_kp2: Divide and conquer pattern: Divide — split problem into smaller subproblems. Conquer — solve each subproblem recursively. Combine — merge the solutions. Examples: merge sort (split/sort/merge), binary search (split in half/search one half).
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp5_kp3: Memoisation: caching the result of recursive calls to avoid recomputing. Fibonacci without memoisation is O(2^n); with memoisation it becomes O(n). Dynamic programming is bottom-up memoisation — fills a table iteratively.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp5_kp4: Master Theorem: for divide-and-conquer recurrences T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(n^d), the time complexity depends on comparing log_b(a) with d. Used to determine O(n log n) for merge sort without solving the recurrence manually.
learnSch_computer_science_f3_tp5_insight: The Towers of Hanoi puzzle (1883) — move n discs from peg A to peg C using peg B, never placing a larger disc on a smaller — has a perfect recursive solution in 3 lines of code. The minimum number of moves required is 2^n - 1. For n=64 discs, that’s over 18 quintillion moves. At one move per second, it would take 585 billion years — 42 times the age of the universe. This puzzle was invented specifically to illustrate exponential growth, yet its recursive solution is elegantly simple. Recursion captures structure that iterative code cannot easily express.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp3_desc: Entity-Relationship (ER) diagrams are visual blueprints for database design. They show entities (things to store data about), their attributes, and the relationships between them. Designing an ER diagram before coding prevents structural errors that are costly to fix later. Every relational database should be designed ER-first.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp3_kp0: Entity: a real-world object or concept about which data is stored (e.g. Student, Course, Book). Drawn as a rectangle. Weak entity: can only exist in relation to another entity (e.g. an OrderItem depends on an Order).
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp3_kp1: Attribute: a property of an entity (e.g. Student has studentID, name, dateOfBirth). Drawn as an oval. Primary key attribute is underlined. Multi-valued attribute (e.g. phone numbers) shown with double oval.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp3_kp2: Relationship: an association between entities. Drawn as a diamond. Degree: unary (self-referencing), binary (two entities), ternary (three). Cardinality notation: 1:1, 1:N, M:N.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp3_kp3: M:N relationships require a junction table in the relational model. Student-Course (many students take many courses) → Enrollment table with (studentID, courseID) as composite primary key, plus attributes like grade.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp3_kp4: Chen notation vs Crow’s Foot notation: Chen uses rectangles and diamonds with 1/N labels; Crow’s Foot uses connecting lines with symbols (single line = 1, crow’s foot = many). Both convey the same information.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp3_insight: When Facebook was founded in 2004, Mark Zuckerberg and his team had to design a data model for social relationships — people, friendships, posts, comments, likes, events, groups. The resulting graph-like structure was so complex that Facebook eventually invented their own graph database (TAO — The Associations and Objects) to handle billions of friend connections efficiently. The ER diagram for Facebook’s social graph would span entire walls. Getting the data model wrong early at that scale would have cost billions to fix.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp4_desc: A database transaction is a sequence of operations treated as a single logical unit of work. ACID properties guarantee that transactions are processed reliably even if the system crashes mid-transaction. Without ACID, databases used for banking, e-commerce, or medical records would produce incorrect or lost data.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp4_kp0: Atomicity: a transaction is all-or-nothing. Either all operations complete successfully, or none of them do. If a bank transfer fails halfway (debit succeeds, credit fails), the entire transaction is rolled back.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp4_kp1: Consistency: a transaction brings the database from one valid state to another valid state. All rules (constraints, foreign keys, uniqueness) must hold before and after. An invalid transaction is rejected.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp4_kp2: Isolation: concurrent transactions execute as if they were sequential. Changes made by a transaction are not visible to other transactions until committed. Prevents dirty reads, phantom reads, and lost updates.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp4_kp3: Durability: once a transaction is committed, its changes are permanent even if the system crashes immediately after. Achieved via write-ahead logging (WAL) — changes are written to a log before being applied to the database.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp4_kp4: COMMIT vs ROLLBACK: COMMIT makes all changes in the transaction permanent. ROLLBACK undoes all changes since the transaction began. SQL syntax: BEGIN TRANSACTION; …operations…; COMMIT; (or ROLLBACK on error).
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp4_insight: On 22 August 2012, Knight Capital Group, a major US financial firm, deployed a new trading algorithm with a bug that sent millions of erroneous stock orders in 45 minutes. The orders executed as intended by the software — there were no ACID violations — but the algorithm logic was wrong. Knight lost $440 million in 45 minutes and was effectively bankrupt by morning. The lesson: ACID properties guarantee database consistency, but they cannot protect you from logically correct transactions that do the wrong thing.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp5_desc: NoSQL (Not Only SQL) databases sacrifice some ACID guarantees for extreme scalability and flexibility. They excel at handling unstructured data, massive scale, and high-velocity writes. Distributed systems spread data and computation across multiple servers, enabling fault tolerance and horizontal scaling.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp5_kp0: NoSQL types: Document (MongoDB — stores JSON-like documents); Key-Value (Redis — ultra-fast cache); Column-family (Cassandra — wide rows, great for time-series); Graph (Neo4j — nodes and edges for relationship queries).
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp5_kp1: CAP theorem: a distributed system can guarantee at most two of three properties — Consistency (all nodes see the same data), Availability (every request gets a response), Partition tolerance (works despite network failures). NoSQL systems often choose availability over consistency.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp5_kp2: BASE (NoSQL alternative to ACID): Basically Available (system remains available), Soft state (data may temporarily be inconsistent), Eventually consistent (given enough time, all nodes converge to same value). Used in social media feeds, shopping carts.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp5_kp3: Horizontal vs vertical scaling: vertical = bigger server (CPU, RAM). Horizontal = more servers. NoSQL databases are designed for horizontal scaling — adding commodity servers. Relational databases are harder to scale horizontally.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp5_kp4: Sharding: splitting a large database across multiple servers, each holding a subset of the data (a shard). Range sharding (by ID range), hash sharding (hash of key determines server). Reduces load per server but complicates queries that span multiple shards.
learnSch_computer_science_f4_tp5_insight: Amazon DynamoDB handles over 89 million requests per second during peak periods (like Prime Day). It is a key-value/document NoSQL database designed by Amazon engineers Werner Vogels and Avinash Lakshman in 2007 after Amazon’s internal relational database collapsed under Black Friday load in 2004. The paper describing DynamoDB's architecture ('Dynamo: Amazon’s Highly Available Key-value Store') became one of the most cited papers in distributed systems research and inspired Apache Cassandra, used by Netflix, Apple, Instagram, and Twitter.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp3: TCP/IP model and the OSI model
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp3_desc: Network communication is organised into layers, each responsible for a specific aspect of data transmission. The TCP/IP model (4 layers) is the practical standard the internet runs on. The OSI model (7 layers) is a more detailed conceptual framework used for teaching and troubleshooting. Both models explain how data travels from one application to another across networks.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp3_kp0: OSI model (7 layers, top-down): Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical. Mnemonic: All People Seem To Need Data Processing.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp3_kp1: TCP/IP model (4 layers): Application (combines OSI layers 5-7: HTTP, DNS, SMTP, FTP), Transport (TCP, UDP), Internet (IP, ICMP), Network Access (Ethernet, Wi-Fi).
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp3_kp2: Encapsulation: as data travels down the stack, each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) containing control information. The Transport layer adds a TCP/UDP header (port numbers); the Internet layer adds an IP header (source/dest IPs); Data Link adds a frame header (MAC addresses).
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp3_kp3: De-encapsulation: at the receiving end, each layer strips off its header and passes the payload to the layer above, until the application receives the original data.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp3_kp4: Port numbers identify which application should receive a packet: HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, DNS=53, SMTP=25, FTP=21, SSH=22. Source port is random (ephemeral); destination port identifies the service.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp3_insight: Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn designed the TCP/IP protocol suite in 1974 to allow different computer networks to communicate with each other regardless of hardware. They deliberately made it open, free, and non-proprietary. Had they patented it, the internet might have become a commercial toll road. Cerf later reflected: 'We didn’t patent it because we wanted it to be adopted as widely as possible.' Both received the Presidential Medal of Freedom and the Turing Award (computing’s Nobel Prize) for this work.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp4_desc: Firewalls, proxies, and VPNs are core network security tools. Firewalls filter traffic by rules; proxies act as intermediaries for web requests; VPNs encrypt all traffic and hide your real IP. Together they form the defensive layer of modern network security.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp4_kp0: Firewall: a system (hardware or software) that monitors and filters incoming/outgoing network traffic based on rules. Packet filtering: checks IP, port, protocol. Stateful inspection: tracks connection state. Application firewall: inspects payload content.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp4_kp1: DMZ (Demilitarised Zone): a network segment between external firewall and internal firewall. Public-facing servers (web, email) placed here. Limits damage if a public server is compromised — attacker cannot directly reach the internal network.
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp4_kp2: Proxy server: sits between client and internet. Forward proxy: client sends all requests to proxy, which forwards them. Anonymises client, enables caching, allows content filtering. Reverse proxy: sits in front of servers, distributes load, caches responses, hides server architecture (used by Nginx, Cloudflare).
learnSch_computer_science_f5_tp4_kp3: VPN (Virtual Private Network): creates an encrypted tunnel from your device to a VPN server. All traffic appears to originate from the VPN server’s IP. Used to: bypass geographic restrictions, secure connections on public Wi-Fi, allow remote workers to access company networks.
