learnSch_geography_f1_tp3_desc: Rural-urban migration is the movement of people from countryside to cities, driving urbanisation. It is explained by push factors (negative conditions in rural areas) and pull factors (attractions of urban areas). This process is reshaping population distributions worldwide, especially in Africa and Asia, creating both opportunities and massive urban challenges.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp3_kp0: Push-pull model (E.G. Lee, 1966): push factors (negative rural conditions driving people away) and pull factors (positive urban attractions drawing people in). Also includes intervening obstacles (distance, cost, visas, language barriers) that people must overcome.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp3_kp1: Rural push factors: agricultural mechanisation (fewer jobs), land degradation and drought, conflict, lack of healthcare and education, natural disasters, limited economic opportunity.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp3_kp2: Urban pull factors: employment opportunities (factories, services), higher wages, better healthcare and education, cultural facilities, social networks from earlier migrants (chain migration), perceived quality of life.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp3_kp3: Rapid urbanisation consequences: positive — economic growth, agglomeration economies (businesses clustering together), innovation hubs. Negative — informal settlements/slums (Kibera in Nairobi, Dharavi in Mumbai), infrastructure strain, pollution, social inequality.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp3_kp4: Megacities: cities with over 10 million inhabitants. In 1950, only New York and Tokyo qualified. By 2023, there were 35 megacities, mostly in Asia and Africa. Lagos is projected to be the world’s largest city by 2100 with up to 100 million people.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp3_insight: Dharavi in Mumbai is one of the most densely populated places on Earth — approximately 1 million people in 2.1 square kilometres (500,000/km², compared to London’s average of 5,500/km²). It is not simply a slum but a thriving economy generating an estimated $1 billion per year through its leather, pottery, recycling, and textile industries. Dharavi’s residents have repeatedly resisted demolition and replacement with tower blocks, arguing their informal economic ecosystem would be destroyed. It exemplifies the complex reality that 'slums' are not simply problems to be cleared but communities with assets, livelihoods, and agency.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp4_desc: Urban areas contain enormous inequalities in housing quality, access to services, and life expectancy. Urban regeneration programmes aim to improve deprived areas, but risk displacing existing residents (gentrification). Understanding these processes requires examining the roles of government, developers, communities, and market forces.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp4_kp0: Urban inequality: measured by housing quality, income levels, access to healthcare, education, green space, and transport. In most cities, deprived inner-city or peripheral housing estates exist within kilometres of wealthy neighbourhoods. Life expectancy can vary 10+ years across a single city.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp4_kp1: Index of Multiple Deprivation (UK): combines income, employment, health, education, crime, housing, and living environment into a composite score. The most deprived 10% of English areas are concentrated in former industrial cities (Blackpool, Middlesbrough, Stoke-on-Trent).
learnSch_geography_f1_tp4_kp2: Urban regeneration strategies: property-led regeneration (attract investment through flagship projects — e.g. Canary Wharf, London Docklands). Community-led regeneration (involve existing residents in planning). Sports-led regeneration (Olympics, Commonwealth Games). Criticisms: benefits often bypass existing communities.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp4_kp3: Gentrification: process by which wealthier residents move into a deprived neighbourhood, raising property values and displacing lower-income residents. Can improve the physical environment but destroy communities, cultural character, and affordable housing stock. Contentious in cities worldwide.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp4_kp4: Informal settlements (squatter settlements/favelas): built by residents on land they don’t legally own, using available materials. Often lack legal recognition, sewage, clean water, and tenure security. Self-help schemes (working with rather than replacing existing housing) have proved more effective than demolition.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp4_insight: Medellín, Colombia was the world’s most dangerous city in the early 1990s (Pablo Escobar’s Medellín Cartel, 380 murders per 100,000 people). By 2013 it had won the Urban Land Institute’s Most Innovative City award. The transformation was achieved through targeted public investment in the city’s most deprived hillside communities (comunas): urban cable cars and outdoor escalators connecting isolated hillsides to the city centre, new libraries, schools, and public spaces in the most dangerous areas. The lesson: urban regeneration that invests in excluded communities — not just showcase developments for wealthier residents — can transform a city’s trajectory.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp5_desc: Development indicators measure the economic and social wellbeing of countries. No single indicator captures all dimensions of development, so composite measures (HDI) are preferred. Global inequality — the gap between high-income and low-income countries — is vast and persistent, with complex causes including geography, history, institutions, and trade relationships.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp5_kp0: GDP per capita: total economic output divided by population. Measures average income but hides inequality (high average can coexist with extreme poverty for many). Expressed in purchasing power parity (PPP) to adjust for different price levels.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp5_kp1: Human Development Index (HDI): composite of life expectancy (health), mean and expected years of schooling (education), and GNI per capita (standard of living). Developed by Mahbub ul Haq and Amartya Sen for the UN (1990). More comprehensive than GDP alone.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp5_kp2: Other indicators: Gini coefficient (inequality within a country — 0 = perfect equality, 1 = one person owns everything); mortality rates (infant, maternal, under-5); access to clean water and sanitation; literacy rates; gender inequality index.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp5_kp3: Causes of global inequality: historical (colonialism extracted resources and disrupted institutions); geographic (tropical disease burden, landlocked nations, poor soils); institutional (rule of law, property rights, corruption); trade (unequal terms of trade disadvantage commodity-exporting nations).
learnSch_geography_f1_tp5_kp4: Dependency theory vs modernisation theory: Modernisation theory (Rostow’s stages of growth) argues poor countries will develop by following the path of rich ones. Dependency theory (Andre Gunder Frank) argues rich countries actively underdeveloped the Global South through trade and financial systems — development requires structural change, not imitation.
learnSch_geography_f1_tp5_insight: Hans Rosling, Swedish professor of global health, spent his career dismantling misconceptions about global development. His 'Gapminder' data visualisations showed that by 2015, most of the world’s population lived in 'the middle' — neither the extreme poverty of the 1960s nor the wealth of Scandinavia. Child mortality had fallen dramatically in countries most people still assumed were 'developing.' His TED Talks have been watched over 35 million times. His book 'Factfulness' (2018) demonstrates that even global health experts consistently underestimate how much the world has improved. Data literacy, he argued, is the most neglected skill in public discourse.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp3_desc: Remote sensing uses satellites, aircraft, and drones to collect data about Earth’s surface without direct contact. It provides global coverage, time-series comparisons, and access to inaccessible areas. Modern remote sensing underpins weather forecasting, climate monitoring, agricultural planning, deforestation tracking, and disaster response.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp3_kp0: Electromagnetic spectrum: remote sensors detect radiation reflected or emitted by Earth’s surface across different wavelengths. Visible light (RGB), near-infrared (vegetation health), thermal infrared (surface temperature), microwave (penetrates cloud cover, used for radar).
learnSch_geography_f2_tp3_kp1: NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index): uses near-infrared and red wavelengths to measure vegetation density and health. Healthy vegetation strongly reflects NIR and absorbs red. NDVI maps show seasonal changes, droughts, deforestation, and crop health globally.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp3_kp2: Satellite orbit types: low Earth orbit (LEO, 160–2000 km — high resolution, fast coverage, used for detailed mapping); medium Earth orbit (GPS satellites); geostationary orbit (35,786 km, same point above Earth — used for weather satellites and communications, full hemisphere view).
learnSch_geography_f2_tp3_kp3: Applications: deforestation monitoring (Brazil’s INPE monitors Amazon deforestation monthly via Landsat/Sentinel satellites); sea ice extent tracking (Arctic ice loss documented since 1979); disaster mapping (floods, wildfires, earthquakes mapped within hours for emergency response).
learnSch_geography_f2_tp3_kp4: LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging): sends laser pulses, measures return time to calculate distance. Penetrates forest canopy to map ground beneath. Discovered hundreds of previously unknown Maya cities hidden under Guatemalan jungle (2018), revolutionising archaeology.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp3_insight: In 2018, LiDAR surveys revealed over 60,000 previously unknown ancient Maya structures beneath the Guatemalan jungle — including cities, causeways, canals, and fortifications suggesting a population of 10–15 million people during the Maya Classic period (250–900 CE), far larger than previously estimated. The entire fieldwork programme that previously would have taken generations of archaeologists was accomplished by one aircraft in weeks. Remote sensing is not just a geography tool — it is transforming archaeology, environmental science, hydrology, and urban planning simultaneously.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp4_desc: GPS (Global Positioning System) provides precise location data anywhere on Earth using a network of satellites. Geographic coordinate systems (latitude, longitude, projections) allow locations to be described and mapped consistently. These technologies underpin navigation, emergency response, precision agriculture, and scientific measurement.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp4_kp0: Geographic coordinate system: latitude (0° at Equator, ±90° at poles — horizontal lines/parallels); longitude (0° at Prime Meridian/Greenwich, ±180° — vertical lines/meridians). One degree of latitude ≈ 111 km. Longitude degrees vary from 111 km at Equator to 0 at poles.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp4_kp1: GPS operation: a GPS receiver simultaneously receives signals from at least 4 satellites. Each signal contains the satellite’s position and precise time. The receiver calculates distance to each satellite from signal travel time × speed of light, then uses trilateration to determine its 3D position. Accuracy: 3–5 metres (civilian), centimetres (differential GPS).
learnSch_geography_f2_tp4_kp2: Map projections: no flat map can perfectly represent a spherical Earth. All projections distort shape, area, distance, or direction. Mercator projection (used in most web maps) preserves angles but distorts area — Greenland appears as large as Africa, though Africa is 14 times larger.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp4_kp3: British National Grid (Ordnance Survey): a coordinate system specific to Great Britain using a Transverse Mercator projection. Grid references (e.g. TQ 305805) give locations to 100m, 10m, or 1m precision. Used on all UK Ordnance Survey maps.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp4_kp4: GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite Systems): GPS is the US system (24+ satellites). Others: GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (EU), BeiDou (China). Modern smartphones use multiple GNSS systems simultaneously for improved accuracy. Precise timing from GNSS also synchronises global financial transactions, power grids, and internet infrastructure.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp4_insight: The Mercator projection, created by Flemish cartographer Gerardus Mercator in 1569 for maritime navigation, remained the dominant world map for 450 years. Its distortion makes Europe and North America appear disproportionately large while Africa and South America appear smaller. Africa is actually 14 times larger than Greenland but appears similar in size on Mercator. Critics argue this distortion reinforced a Eurocentric worldview. The Peters projection (1974) preserves area instead, making Africa appear its true size. The debate over map projections is not merely technical — it is political. The map you use shapes how you see the world.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp5: GIS: planning and disaster response applications
learnSch_geography_f2_tp5_desc: GIS (Geographic Information System) is software that stores, analyses, and visualises spatial data. By layering multiple data types on the same map, GIS reveals patterns invisible to any single dataset. It is essential in urban planning, public health, emergency response, environmental management, and business location analysis.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp5_kp0: GIS layers: GIS combines multiple datasets as transparent layers — e.g. road network + population density + flood risk zones + hospital locations. The overlay reveals which areas have high flood risk, high population, and poor access to emergency services simultaneously.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp5_kp1: Raster vs vector data: vector data uses points, lines, and polygons to represent discrete features (buildings, roads, boundaries). Raster data uses grids of cells/pixels to represent continuous variables (elevation, temperature, NDVI). GIS handles both.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp5_kp2: Urban planning applications: GIS is used to site new schools (where are children underserved?), plan transport routes (where is congestion worst?), identify brownfield land (where are derelict sites suitable for housing?), analyse crime hotspots, and model flood inundation scenarios.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp5_kp3: Disaster response: in the 2010 Haiti earthquake, OpenStreetMap volunteers mapped the entire country within 48 hours using satellite imagery, providing emergency responders with accurate base maps for navigation and casualty location. 'Crisis mapping' has since become a recognised humanitarian tool.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp5_kp4: Health geography applications: John Snow’s 1854 cholera map (manually plotting cases by location to identify the Broad Street pump) is the origin of spatial epidemiology. Modern GIS maps infectious disease spread, identifies healthcare deserts, models air quality exposure, and guides vaccination campaigns.
learnSch_geography_f2_tp5_insight: In 2010, when the Haiti earthquake struck (7.0 magnitude, estimated 160,000–230,000 deaths), Haiti had almost no usable map data. Within 48 hours, the OpenStreetMap community — volunteers worldwide — had traced 1,500 km of roads and 1,000 buildings from newly released satellite imagery, creating the most detailed map Haiti had ever had. Relief organisations used these maps to navigate debris-blocked streets and locate hospitals. The project demonstrated that crowd-sourced open geographic data, produced by volunteers without payment, could save lives faster than any government mapping agency. Over 650,000 volunteers have contributed to OpenStreetMap since.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp3_desc: Climate change refers to long-term shifts in global temperature and weather patterns. Since the Industrial Revolution, human activities — primarily burning fossil fuels — have accelerated natural climate processes to unprecedented rates. The evidence is unequivocal; the causes are well understood; the consequences are already being observed worldwide.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp3_kp0: Evidence: global average temperature has risen 1.1°C since 1880, with most warming since 1975. Atmospheric CO₂ has risen from 280 ppm (pre-industrial) to over 420 ppm (2023 — highest in 3 million years). Sea level has risen 20 cm since 1900. Arctic sea ice extent is declining. Glaciers worldwide are retreating.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp3_kp1: The greenhouse effect: solar radiation passes through the atmosphere and warms the surface. Earth’s surface emits infrared radiation; greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O, ozone) absorb and re-emit this radiation, keeping Earth warmer than it would otherwise be (natural GHE makes Earth habitable). Enhanced GHE from human emissions adds additional warming.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp3_kp2: Human causes: burning fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) releases CO₂. Deforestation reduces carbon sinks. Agriculture produces methane (livestock) and nitrous oxide (fertilisers). Cement production and industry release additional CO₂. Collectively these have increased atmospheric GHG concentrations by 50% above pre-industrial levels.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp3_kp3: Evidence from ice cores: Antarctic and Greenland ice cores record bubbles of ancient atmosphere. CO₂ concentrations and temperature (from oxygen isotope ratios) are tightly correlated over 800,000 years of ice core records. Current CO₂ level is far outside the natural range.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp3_kp4: IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change): established 1988, assesses scientific literature. AR6 (2021) stated: 'It is unequivocal that human influence has warmed the atmosphere, ocean and land.' Warming of 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels expected in early 2030s. Exceeding 2°C risks severe, widespread irreversible impacts.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp3_insight: Svante Arrhenius, Swedish chemist, calculated in 1896 that doubling atmospheric CO₂ would warm Earth by 5–16°C — remarkably close to modern estimates. He published this 127 years ago, doing the calculations by hand over the winter. At the time, he thought it might take 3,000 years to double CO₂. At current emission rates, doubling will occur within this century. Arrhenius was not alarmed — he thought warmer temperatures might benefit Nordic agriculture. He could not have foreseen that industrialisation would accelerate emissions by a factor of thousands relative to what he imagined.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp4_desc: Urban areas are typically 1–3°C warmer than surrounding rural areas — the urban heat island (UHI) effect. This is caused by the replacement of vegetation with heat-absorbing surfaces, reduced evapotranspiration, waste heat from transport and buildings, and reduced sky view factor. Understanding UHI is critical for urban design, public health, and climate adaptation.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp4_kp0: Causes of UHI: dark surfaces (asphalt, buildings) absorb more solar radiation than vegetation; impermeable surfaces reduce evapotranspiration (cooling); tall buildings trap heat and reduce wind speed; waste heat from vehicles, HVAC systems, and industry; reduced green space means less cooling from transpiration.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp4_kp1: Health impacts: heat-related mortality rises sharply above threshold temperatures. In the European heatwave of August 2003, an estimated 70,000 excess deaths occurred. Urban populations, particularly elderly, ill, and low-income residents without air conditioning, are most vulnerable.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp4_kp2: UHI mitigation strategies: green roofs (insulation, evapotranspiration, stormwater management); urban trees and parks (shade, cooling up to 8°C); reflective/cool roofs (high albedo materials reflect more solar radiation); permeable pavements; urban waterways.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp4_kp3: Microclimate: local variations in climate at small scale — caused by topography, aspect (south-facing slopes are warmer), water bodies (coastal areas have smaller temperature ranges — maritime effect), urban canyons, wind shadows. Relevant to building design, agriculture, and urban planning.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp4_kp4: Sea breeze and land breeze: coastal microclimate phenomenon. During the day, land heats faster than sea → lower pressure over land → cool sea breeze blows inland. At night, land cools faster → lower pressure over sea → land breeze blows offshore. Creates distinct coastal weather patterns.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp4_insight: Singapore, a dense tropical city-state with 5.9 million people in 733 km², has implemented perhaps the world’s most ambitious urban cooling programme. The 'City in a Garden' strategy requires all new buildings to replace greenery lost at ground level with rooftop and vertical gardens. Singapore has planted over 1.4 million trees and 700 km of park connectors. Urban temperatures in heavily greened districts are measurably cooler. Singapore’s approach demonstrates that dense tropical urbanisation and biological abundance are not mutually exclusive — but require deliberate, systematic policy rather than market forces alone.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp5: Extreme weather events and adaptation strategies
learnSch_geography_f3_tp5_desc: Extreme weather events — floods, droughts, hurricanes, heatwaves, wildfires — are increasing in frequency and intensity as the climate warms. Adaptation strategies aim to reduce vulnerability to these events. Both physical and human geography are essential to understanding exposure, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp5_kp0: Types of extreme weather: tropical cyclones/hurricanes/typhoons (rotating tropical storms requiring warm ocean water ≥ 26°C); flash floods (rapid flooding from intense localised rainfall); heatwaves (prolonged abnormally high temperatures); droughts (prolonged precipitation deficit); wildfires (hot, dry, windy conditions igniting and spreading fires).
learnSch_geography_f3_tp5_kp1: Climate change and extreme weather: warming increases evaporation → more atmospheric moisture → heavier rainfall events. Warmer oceans → stronger hurricanes. More frequent and intense heatwaves directly linked to global warming. Changing precipitation patterns increase drought risk in many regions.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp5_kp2: Vulnerability framework: risk = hazard × exposure × vulnerability. Low-income countries and communities have lower adaptive capacity (less money for infrastructure, insurance, evacuation). Bangladesh is highly exposed to flooding and cyclones AND has low adaptive capacity — extremely vulnerable. Netherlands has similar exposure but high adaptive capacity (world-leading flood management).
learnSch_geography_f3_tp5_kp3: Adaptation strategies: engineered defences (flood barriers, seawalls); early warning systems (evacuation before cyclones — Bangladesh reduced cyclone deaths from 300,000 in 1970 to under 200 in 2019 through warning systems and cyclone shelters); drought-resistant crops; water storage; managed retreat from high-risk coastal zones.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp5_kp4: Mitigation vs adaptation: mitigation = reducing greenhouse gas emissions to limit future climate change. Adaptation = adjusting to current and future climate impacts. Both are necessary. Mitigation prevents the worst outcomes; adaptation manages what is already inevitable due to emissions already in the atmosphere.
learnSch_geography_f3_tp5_insight: Super Typhoon Haiyan (Yolanda) struck the Philippines in November 2013 with sustained winds of 315 km/h — among the most powerful storms ever recorded. It killed over 6,300 people, displaced 4 million, and caused $2.2 billion in damage. Hours after the storm, Yeb Saño, the Philippines’ climate negotiator at the UN Climate Conference in Warsaw, began an emotional speech and announced he would fast until the conference reached a meaningful agreement. He wept. The speech went viral worldwide. It illustrated the profound injustice at the heart of climate change: those who have emitted the least — the Philippines accounts for 0.3% of global emissions — suffer the worst consequences of warming caused primarily by rich industrialised nations.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp3: Price elasticity of demand and supply
learnSch_economics_f0_tp3_desc: Elasticity measures how much quantity demanded or supplied changes in response to a price change. Elastic goods are luxuries or have many substitutes; inelastic goods are necessities with few alternatives. Elasticity determines who bears the burden of a tax and is central to business pricing strategy.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp3_kp0: Price Elasticity of Demand (PED) = % change in quantity demanded / % change in price. PED is negative (demand falls as price rises). |PED| > 1 = elastic; |PED| < 1 = inelastic; |PED| = 1 = unit elastic.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp3_kp1: Elastic demand (|PED| > 1): luxury goods, goods with close substitutes, goods where spending is a large share of income (e.g. designer handbags, foreign holidays). A price rise causes a more-than-proportional fall in quantity demanded.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp3_kp2: Inelastic demand (|PED| < 1): necessities, goods with few substitutes, addictive goods (e.g. insulin, petrol, cigarettes). A price rise causes a less-than-proportional fall in quantity demanded. Revenue rises with a price increase.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp3_kp3: Price Elasticity of Supply (PES) = % change in quantity supplied / % change in price. PES > 1 = elastic supply (producers can quickly adjust). PES < 1 = inelastic (e.g. agricultural goods with long growing seasons, oil fields requiring years to develop).
learnSch_economics_f0_tp3_kp4: Tax incidence: if demand is inelastic, consumers bear most of the tax burden (they cannot easily switch away). If supply is inelastic, producers bear more. This explains why governments prefer to tax inelastic goods (cigarettes, fuel, alcohol) — revenue is predictable.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp3_insight: Amazon's pricing algorithms adjust prices millions of times per day based on demand elasticity estimates. Items in highly competitive categories (where shoppers easily compare prices) are kept competitive; items with less competition are priced higher. A 2015 study found Amazon charged higher prices for products less likely to appear in comparison searches. This is dynamic elasticity pricing at machine scale — theory that was purely academic in 1970 is now automated and running in real time on the world's largest retail platform.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp4_desc: Consumer surplus is the benefit buyers gain when they pay less than they were willing to pay. Producer surplus is the extra revenue sellers earn above their minimum acceptable price. Together they define total welfare — the measure economists use to evaluate whether a market outcome is efficient.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp4_kp0: Consumer surplus: the area above the price line and below the demand curve on a supply-demand graph. If you would pay £50 for a concert ticket but only pay £30, your consumer surplus is £20.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp4_kp1: Producer surplus: the area below the price line and above the supply curve. If a farmer is willing to sell at £1 per kg but sells at £1.50, their producer surplus is £0.50 per kg.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp4_kp2: Total welfare (social surplus) = consumer surplus + producer surplus. In a competitive equilibrium (where supply = demand), total welfare is maximised — the 'invisible hand' argument for free markets.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp4_kp3: Deadweight loss: loss of total welfare caused by market inefficiency (taxes, price controls, monopoly). A tax raises government revenue but creates deadweight loss as some transactions that would have been beneficial no longer occur.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp4_kp4: Price discrimination: charging different customers different prices based on their willingness to pay (airlines, student discounts, coupons). Extracts consumer surplus for the seller. Third-degree discrimination (different groups pay different prices) vs first-degree (each buyer pays their exact maximum).
learnSch_economics_f0_tp4_insight: Concert ticket scalping (secondary markets) is essentially a market revealing consumer surplus. When Taylor Swift tickets sell for £75 and scalpers resell them for £500, the surplus — the difference between what fans were willing to pay and the face price — transfers to scalpers rather than the artist. Some artists (Radiohead, Pearl Jam) have experimented with dynamic pricing to capture this surplus directly. The economics of concert pricing illuminates the entire consumer surplus concept: face price is rarely equal to what people would willingly pay.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp5: Government intervention: taxes, subsidies, price controls
learnSch_economics_f0_tp5_desc: Governments intervene in markets to correct failures, redistribute income, protect consumers, or pursue social goals. Taxes, subsidies, price floors, and price ceilings each alter market outcomes with predictable efficiency consequences. Every intervention creates winners, losers, and some deadweight loss.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp5_kp0: Indirect tax (per-unit): shifts supply curve upward by the tax amount. Equilibrium price rises, quantity falls. The incidence (who pays) depends on elasticities — inelastic side bears more. Examples: VAT, excise duty on fuel, tobacco, alcohol.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp5_kp1: Subsidy: government payment to producers, shifting supply curve downward. Reduces market price, increases quantity. Used for merit goods (vaccines, public transport, education) where positive externalities mean the market under-produces.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp5_kp2: Price ceiling (maximum price): set below equilibrium. Creates shortage (quantity demanded > quantity supplied). Examples: rent controls (housing shortages), wartime price controls. Intended to protect consumers but often creates black markets.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp5_kp3: Price floor (minimum price): set above equilibrium. Creates surplus (quantity supplied > quantity demanded). Examples: minimum wage (labour surplus = unemployment?), EU Common Agricultural Policy (food surpluses — 'butter mountains', 'wine lakes').
learnSch_economics_f0_tp5_kp4: Market failure justifications for intervention: externalities (costs/benefits to third parties not reflected in price), public goods (non-excludable, non-rival — free rider problem), information asymmetry (one party knows more — insurance, used cars), merit/demerit goods.
learnSch_economics_f0_tp5_insight: New York City's rent control system, introduced during WWII to protect tenants from wartime profiteering, still covers approximately 1 million apartments in 2024. Economic research consistently finds it reduces housing supply (landlords convert to condos or let buildings deteriorate), misallocates housing (tenants stay in apartments that no longer fit their needs to keep the low rent), and transfers wealth from small landlords to lucky tenants rather than from rich to poor. Yet it is politically impossible to remove because existing beneficiaries vote to protect their surplus. A perfect case study in how interventions create constituencies that perpetuate them beyond their intended life.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp3: Compound interest and the time value of money
learnSch_economics_f1_tp3_desc: Money today is worth more than the same amount in the future because it can earn returns. Compound interest is interest calculated on both principal and accumulated interest. These concepts underpin all of finance — mortgages, savings, investments, and government debt — and are among the most practically important mathematical concepts for everyday life.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp3_kp0: Simple interest: Interest = Principal × Rate × Time. Compound interest: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), where P = principal, r = annual rate, n = compounding periods per year, t = years. The more frequent the compounding, the higher the effective return.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp3_kp1: The rule of 72: divide 72 by the annual interest rate to estimate years to double your money. At 6% interest, money doubles in approximately 12 years. At 9%, about 8 years. Simple mental arithmetic for compound growth.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp3_kp2: Present value (PV): the value today of a future sum. PV = FV / (1 + r)^n. Discounting makes future money worth less today because it could have been earning returns. Used to compare cash flows occurring at different times.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp3_kp3: Net Present Value (NPV): sum of present values of all cash flows (positive and negative) from a project. NPV > 0 means the investment is worth making; NPV < 0 means it destroys value. Central to business investment decisions.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp3_kp4: Inflation erodes purchasing power over time. Real interest rate = nominal rate - inflation rate (Fisher equation approximately). A 3% savings rate with 4% inflation = -1% real return. Savers are losing purchasing power despite earning nominal interest.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp3_insight: Albert Einstein allegedly called compound interest the 'eighth wonder of the world' (historians debate whether he actually said this, but the sentiment is economically sound). A single pound invested at the founding of the Bank of England in 1694 at 3% compound interest would be worth over £100 billion today. This is why long-term pension contributions made in your 20s are so much more valuable than contributions made in your 50s: time is the critical variable in compound growth, not the amount invested.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp4_desc: Insurance transfers financial risk from individuals to a pool of policyholders. It functions because most people prefer a certain small loss (premium) to an uncertain large loss. Risk management more broadly involves identifying, assessing, and mitigating financial risks through diversification, hedging, and reserves.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp4_kp0: Principle of insurance: pool risk across many people. Most pay premiums without claiming; premiums from the many cover the large claims of the few. Viable only when risks are independent (not all claimants suffering simultaneously) and calculable.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp4_kp1: Moral hazard: insured people take greater risks because they are protected from consequences. A person with car insurance may park less carefully. Insurers mitigate this with excess/deductibles (making the insured bear some risk), no-claims bonuses, and monitoring.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp4_kp2: Adverse selection: people most likely to need insurance are most likely to buy it. A health insurer offering coverage at average premiums will attract sicker-than-average customers, driving up costs and premiums until only the sickest remain (adverse selection spiral / insurance death spiral).
learnSch_economics_f1_tp4_kp3: Diversification: spreading investments across uncorrelated assets reduces overall portfolio risk without sacrificing expected return. 'Don’t put all your eggs in one basket.' Index funds diversify across hundreds of stocks, nearly eliminating company-specific risk.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp4_kp4: Expected value: EV = (probability of outcome) × (value of outcome), summed across all outcomes. Insurance is worth buying when the disutility of the loss exceeds the premium (because people are risk-averse). At actuarially fair odds, risk-averse people still rationally buy insurance.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp4_insight: Lloyd’s of London began in Edward Lloyd’s coffee house in the 1680s, where merchants and ship owners gathered to share risk on maritime voyages. Today it insures satellites, oil rigs, celebrity body parts (Heidi Klum’s legs were insured for $2.2 million), and pandemic risk. After 9/11, Lloyd’s paid out $4.8 billion in claims — the largest insurance payout in history at the time. The entire modern global insurance industry — worth $7 trillion annually — traces its origins to men sharing shipping risk over coffee in 17th-century London.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp5_desc: Retirement planning requires understanding how to build a fund large enough to last decades without employment income. Pensions (defined benefit and defined contribution), investments, and state benefits form the three pillars of retirement income. Starting early, compound interest, and diversification are the critical variables.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp5_kp0: Defined benefit (DB) pension: employer promises a specific income in retirement, typically based on salary and years of service. Risk borne by employer. Increasingly rare in private sector. Generous for long-serving employees but not portable.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp5_kp1: Defined contribution (DC) pension: employee and employer contribute to a pot that is invested. Retirement income depends on contributions made and investment returns. Risk borne by employee. Common in private sector (401(k) in USA, workplace pension in UK).
learnSch_economics_f1_tp5_kp2: Auto-enrolment (UK): since 2012, employers must automatically enrol eligible workers in a pension scheme. Workers can opt out but must actively choose to. Enrolment rates rose from 55% to 88% of eligible workers within 5 years — a textbook example of how default choices (choice architecture/nudge theory) determine behaviour.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp5_kp3: The 4% rule: a commonly cited guideline suggesting retirees can withdraw 4% of their portfolio per year and not run out of money over a 30-year retirement (based on historical US market returns). Requires approximately 25x annual expenses saved at retirement.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp5_kp4: State pension (UK): the new State Pension (2016) requires 35 qualifying years of National Insurance contributions for the full amount (around £11,500/year in 2024). State pension alone is insufficient for comfortable retirement; workplace and private pensions supplement it.
learnSch_economics_f1_tp5_insight: Richard Thaler, who won the 2017 Nobel Prize in Economics for behavioural economics, co-designed the Save More Tomorrow (SMarT) programme: rather than asking employees to save more now (which triggers loss aversion), it pre-commits them to increase their pension contribution automatically whenever they receive a pay rise. The future increase does not feel like a loss. Implemented across US companies, it doubled average savings rates. Thaler’s insight — that humans are predictably irrational and defaults matter enormously — has changed pension policy worldwide.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp3: Balance of payments and exchange rates
learnSch_economics_f2_tp3_desc: The balance of payments records all economic transactions between a country and the rest of the world. Exchange rates determine the price of one currency in terms of another, affecting exports, imports, inflation, and investment. Together they connect domestic economic policy to the global economy.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp3_kp0: Balance of payments accounts: Current account (trade in goods and services, income, transfers — the most-watched component); Capital account (transfers of fixed assets); Financial account (investment flows, foreign exchange reserves).
learnSch_economics_f2_tp3_kp1: Current account deficit: a country imports more goods/services than it exports (and/or pays more income abroad than it receives). UK runs a persistent current account deficit. Financed by selling assets or borrowing from abroad (financial account surplus).
learnSch_economics_f2_tp3_kp2: Exchange rate systems: floating (determined by supply and demand — e.g. GBP, USD, EUR); fixed (government pegs currency to another — e.g. Hong Kong dollar pegged to USD); managed float (central bank intervenes to smooth movements).
learnSch_economics_f2_tp3_kp3: Currency depreciation: if GBP falls against EUR, UK exports become cheaper (more competitive) but imports become more expensive (inflationary). Depreciation helps manufacturers but raises cost of living. 'Currency wars' occur when countries competitively devalue to gain export advantage.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp3_kp4: Purchasing Power Parity (PPP): the exchange rate at which a basket of goods costs the same in both countries. The Economist's 'Big Mac Index' uses McDonald’s prices worldwide to identify under/overvalued currencies. PPP rates are used for international income comparisons to account for different price levels.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp3_insight: On 16 September 1992 — Black Wednesday — George Soros bet $10 billion that the British pound was overvalued within the European Exchange Rate Mechanism. The UK had joined at too high a rate; keeping it required the Bank of England to raise interest rates to 15% in one day (devastating for homeowners). By evening, the UK was forced to withdraw from the ERM. Soros made $1 billion profit in one day. The episode demonstrated that even sovereign central banks cannot maintain an artificial exchange rate against market pressure indefinitely — a lesson that has shaped exchange rate policy ever since.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp4_desc: International trade agreements reduce barriers to trade (tariffs, quotas, regulations) between countries. The WTO (World Trade Organisation) is the global body overseeing international trade rules and dispute resolution. Free trade increases global output and consumer choice but creates distributional winners and losers domestically.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp4_kp0: WTO (World Trade Organisation): successor to GATT (1947), founded 1995. 164 member countries covering 98% of world trade. Enforces trade rules, adjudicates disputes, and hosts negotiations. Key principles: non-discrimination (most-favoured-nation), reciprocity, transparency.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp4_kp1: Free trade agreement (FTA): two or more countries agree to reduce/eliminate tariffs, quotas, and other trade barriers. Creates a preferential trade zone. Examples: NAFTA/USMCA (US-Canada-Mexico), CPTPP (Asia-Pacific), UK-Australia FTA.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp4_kp2: Customs union: FTA + common external tariff applied to non-members. Requires coordination of trade policy. EU is the world’s largest customs union. Members cannot independently sign bilateral FTAs (a constraint that shaped Brexit negotiations).
learnSch_economics_f2_tp4_kp3: Comparative advantage (Ricardo): a country should specialise in goods it produces at the lowest opportunity cost, even if another country is absolutely better at producing everything. Trade based on comparative advantage increases total output for all parties.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp4_kp4: Protectionism arguments: infant industry (protect new industries until they become competitive); national security (keep strategic industries domestic); retaliation (threat of tariffs as bargaining chip); employment protection (controversial — usually destroys more jobs than it protects through retaliation and higher input costs).
learnSch_economics_f2_tp4_insight: The Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act (1930) raised US import tariffs to historically high levels to protect American industry during the Great Depression. Within a year, 25 countries had retaliated with their own tariffs. Global trade fell by 66% between 1929 and 1934. Economists widely believe Smoot-Hawley deepened and extended the Great Depression. It is the textbook case study in why retaliatory trade wars are destructive for all parties. The lesson was so thoroughly learned that the post-WWII global trading system was explicitly designed around multilateral trade liberalisation — the direct institutional response to Smoot-Hawley.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp5_desc: Globalisation is the increasing integration of economies, cultures, and societies through trade, technology, and migration. It has lifted hundreds of millions out of extreme poverty while simultaneously contributing to deindustrialisation and wage stagnation in rich countries. Understanding its distributional effects is essential to understanding contemporary politics.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp5_kp0: Global poverty reduction: extreme poverty (under $2.15/day) fell from 36% of global population in 1990 to 9% in 2019 (World Bank). Most of this was in China and East Asia — export-led growth powered by globalisation. The greatest mass poverty reduction in human history.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp5_kp1: The 'Elephant chart' (Milanovic, 2012): shows global income growth 1988–2008 was highest for middle classes in developing countries (the elephant's body) and top 1% globally (the trunk), while lower-middle classes in rich countries (the dip) saw little growth. Visualises why Western workers felt left behind.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp5_kp2: Deindustrialisation in rich countries: manufacturing jobs moved to lower-wage countries (China, Vietnam, Bangladesh). Detroit, Sheffield, Dortmund lost manufacturing bases. These communities experienced decades of unemployment, declining public services, and social breakdown. The economic gains from globalisation were unevenly distributed.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp5_kp3: Global supply chains: a modern iPhone has components from 43 countries. Global supply chains reduce costs dramatically but create fragility (exposed by COVID-19, when semiconductor shortages shut down car factories). 'Just in time' manufacturing became a liability overnight.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp5_kp4: Reshoring and friend-shoring: post-COVID trend of bringing supply chains home or to allied countries. Semiconductor fabs being built in USA, Europe, and Taiwan to reduce dependence on East Asia. Political risk now priced into supply chain decisions alongside economic efficiency.
learnSch_economics_f2_tp5_insight: When China joined the WTO in 2001, economists broadly predicted mutual benefits from trade. What they underestimated was the speed and scale of adjustment costs. Economists David Autor, David Dorn, and Gordon Hanson found ('The China Syndrome', 2013) that US regions most exposed to Chinese import competition experienced persistent unemployment, lower wages, and increased disability claims for decades — adjustment did not happen within a few years as standard trade theory predicted. This research directly influenced both Trump’s and Biden’s trade protectionism. Academic economics changed its understanding of trade's distributional effects partly because of this political shock.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp3_desc: A credit score is a numerical assessment of your creditworthiness based on your borrowing and repayment history. Lenders use it to decide whether to lend and at what interest rate. Good credit enables cheaper borrowing; poor credit excludes people from mainstream financial services. Understanding debt management is essential life literacy.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp3_kp0: Credit score factors (UK — Experian, Equifax, TransUnion): payment history (most important), credit utilisation (how much of available credit you use), length of credit history, types of credit, recent credit applications. Score range: 0-999 (Experian). 'Good' is typically above 700.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp3_kp1: How to build credit: get a credit card (use it, pay it off in full monthly), register to vote (electoral roll), pay bills on time, avoid maxing out cards, don’t make multiple credit applications quickly. Young people with no history have poor scores by default.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp3_kp2: Good debt vs bad debt: 'good' debt (mortgage, student loan, business investment) acquires assets or increases earning potential. 'Bad' debt (payday loans, buy-now-pay-later for consumption, high-APR credit cards) finances depreciating goods at high interest. The APR (Annual Percentage Rate) is the key comparison metric.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp3_kp3: Debt spiral: borrowing at high interest to pay existing debt. Payday lenders charge APRs of 1,000%+. A £300 payday loan can balloon to £1,000+ in months. The UK Financial Conduct Authority capped payday loan interest in 2015, reducing harm but not eliminating predatory lending.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp3_kp4: Debt management options: snowball method (pay smallest debts first for psychological momentum); avalanche method (pay highest-interest debts first — mathematically optimal); debt consolidation (combine multiple debts into one lower-interest loan); debt advice (StepChange, Citizens Advice).
learnSch_economics_f3_tp3_insight: In 1950, the Diners Club issued the first modern credit card. By 2023, global credit card debt exceeded $1 trillion. The average US household carries $7,500 in credit card debt at an average APR of 22%. The credit industry earns the majority of its profits from a minority of customers who carry balances and pay interest monthly. Banks deliberately market credit cards using rewards (cashback, airmiles) that benefit only the disciplined minority who pay in full, funded by the interest paid by the struggling majority. Understanding this business model is essential consumer literacy.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp4: Taxation: income tax, VAT, and national insurance
learnSch_economics_f3_tp4_desc: Taxation funds public services, redistributes income, and shapes economic behaviour. Understanding how income tax, National Insurance, and VAT work is essential financial literacy. The UK tax system combines progressive (income tax) and regressive (VAT) elements with complex reliefs, thresholds, and allowances.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp4_kp0: UK Income Tax (2024/25): Personal Allowance £12,570 (tax-free); Basic rate 20% on £12,571–£50,270; Higher rate 40% on £50,271–£125,140; Additional rate 45% above £125,140. Marginal vs effective rate: marginal = rate on the next £1; effective = total tax / total income.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp4_kp1: National Insurance (NI): contributions that fund the National Health Service, State Pension, and other social benefits. Employees pay 8% on earnings £12,570–£50,270, 2% above. Employers pay 13.8% on wages above £9,100. Self-employed pay differently. NI is effectively a second income tax.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp4_kp2: Value Added Tax (VAT): 20% standard rate on most goods/services; 5% reduced rate (domestic energy, children’s car seats); 0% zero-rated (food, children’s clothing, books). VAT is regressive — poorer households spend a higher proportion of income on consumption, so pay proportionally more in VAT.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp4_kp3: Progressive vs regressive taxation: progressive tax takes a higher percentage from higher incomes (income tax). Regressive tax takes a higher percentage from lower incomes (VAT, council tax, fuel duty). Most modern tax systems combine both, aiming for overall progressivity.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp4_kp4: Tax avoidance vs evasion: tax avoidance = legal use of tax rules to minimise liability (ISAs, pension contributions, legitimate business expenses). Tax evasion = illegal — hiding income, false returns, offshore concealment. The distinction is legal but ethically contested.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp4_insight: The 'Laffer Curve' illustrates that tax revenue is zero at both 0% and 100% tax rates, with a maximum somewhere in between. Economists disagree sharply about where that maximum is. Reagan's 1981 tax cuts from 70% to 28% top rate were predicted by supply-siders to increase revenue by stimulating growth. Revenue did not increase as predicted; the deficit tripled. Yet the idea that 'tax cuts pay for themselves' remains politically potent. The empirical evidence consistently shows the Laffer peak for income taxes in modern economies is well above current rates — meaning rate cuts reduce revenue. The curve is real; the political conclusions drawn from it frequently are not.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp5_desc: Financial planning is the process of setting financial goals and creating a strategy to achieve them. Budgeting tracks income and expenditure to ensure spending aligns with priorities. These foundational life skills are rarely taught in school yet determine financial wellbeing more than income level for most people.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp5_kp0: The 50/30/20 rule: 50% of take-home pay to needs (rent, food, utilities, transport); 30% to wants (entertainment, dining out, holidays); 20% to saving and debt repayment. A starting framework, not a rigid rule — adjust for high-cost cities.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp5_kp1: Emergency fund: 3–6 months of living expenses in an easily accessible savings account. Prevents the need to take on high-interest debt when unexpected expenses arise (car repair, redundancy, medical). Priority before other saving goals.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp5_kp2: Budgeting methods: zero-based budgeting (every pound of income assigned to a category until zero remains); envelope method (cash in labelled envelopes for each category); apps (Monzo, YNAB, Emma) automate categorisation of bank transactions.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp5_kp3: Opportunity cost of spending: every pound spent on X is a pound not available for Y. Viewed through compound interest: £100 spent today on a non-essential item costs not £100 but £100 × (1.07)^40 = £1,497 in retirement savings (at 7% return over 40 years).
learnSch_economics_f3_tp5_kp4: Credit vs debit: debit card spends money you have. Credit card spends borrowed money — essential to pay in full each month to avoid interest. Benefits of credit card: consumer protection (Section 75 protection in UK for purchases £100+), fraud protection, rewards. Risk: interest rates 20-30% APR if balance not cleared.
learnSch_economics_f3_tp5_insight: Morgan Housel’s 'The Psychology of Money' (2020) argues that the highest financial return available to most people is not finding better investments but changing their relationship with money. The greatest threat to long-term wealth is not poor investment choices but panic-selling during crashes, spending to impress people you don’t like, and lifestyle inflation (spending rises with income, eliminating any surplus). He identifies that the most powerful financial skill is 'saving without a specific reason' — building wealth simply by spending less than you earn, regardless of investment returns. Simple principles ignored by most finance education.
learnSch_health_f0_tp3_desc: Water is essential for every bodily process; even mild dehydration impairs concentration, mood, and physical performance. Understanding hydration needs and the effects of alcohol, caffeine, and other substances helps young people make informed choices about what they put into their bodies.
learnSch_health_f0_tp3_kp0: Hydration needs: adults need approximately 2 litres (8 glasses) of water per day, more during exercise or hot weather. Urine colour is a simple guide: pale yellow = well hydrated; dark yellow = dehydrated. Thirst is a late indicator of dehydration.
learnSch_health_f0_tp3_kp1: Dehydration effects: even 1-2% body water loss impairs cognitive performance, mood, and physical endurance. Headaches, poor concentration, and fatigue are early signs. Severe dehydration (>5%) causes confusion, rapid heartbeat, and can be life-threatening.
learnSch_health_f0_tp3_kp2: Caffeine: a stimulant found in coffee, tea, energy drinks, and chocolate. Moderate intake (up to 400mg/day for adults) is generally safe but causes sleep disruption, anxiety, and dependency. Energy drinks combine caffeine with sugar — linked to dental erosion, sleep problems, and cardiovascular stress in adolescents.
learnSch_health_f0_tp3_kp3: Alcohol: a depressant affecting brain function, coordination, and judgment. In the UK, the legal drinking age is 18. Alcohol is metabolised at approximately 1 unit/hour regardless of food or coffee. Long-term heavy use causes liver disease, cancer, and addiction. Short-term risks include accidents, blackouts, and alcohol poisoning.
learnSch_health_f0_tp3_kp4: Smoking and vaping: cigarettes contain 7,000 chemicals; smoking is the leading preventable cause of death in the UK (killing ~80,000/year). E-cigarettes (vapes) are marketed as safer alternatives but contain nicotine (highly addictive) and unknown long-term chemical effects. Youth vaping rates tripled 2019–2023.
learnSch_health_f0_tp3_insight: The human body is 60% water by weight. The brain is 73% water. NASA astronauts on the International Space Station drink recycled urine water — processed to be purer than most bottled water on Earth. Their water recycling system recovers 93% of all moisture (including sweat, urine, and humidity from breathing). The technology was partly inspired by studying how the human kidney achieves extraordinary water efficiency. Our bodies are essentially optimised water-recycling machines with legs.
learnSch_health_f0_tp4_desc: Smartphones, social media, and streaming have transformed how young people spend time, sleep, and socialize. Understanding the psychological mechanisms behind digital engagement — and developing intentional habits — is now a core health literacy skill.
learnSch_health_f0_tp4_kp0: Average screen time: UK teens spend 7+ hours daily on screens (not counting school work). Social media use peaks in early adolescence. Screen time itself is less important than what you're doing: passive scrolling is more harmful than creative or social use.
learnSch_health_f0_tp4_kp1: Sleep disruption: blue light from screens suppresses melatonin (the sleep hormone), delaying sleep onset. Using phones in bed is strongly correlated with shorter sleep, later sleep timing, and poorer sleep quality. Sleep deprivation impairs memory consolidation, emotional regulation, and immune function.
learnSch_health_f0_tp4_kp2: Social media and mental health: correlational studies show heavy social media use is associated with higher rates of anxiety and depression, especially in girls. Mechanisms include social comparison ('everyone else's life looks better'), cyberbullying, and fear of missing out (FOMO). Jonathan Haidt's 'The Anxious Generation' (2024) argues smartphones fundamentally changed adolescent mental health.
learnSch_health_f0_tp4_kp3: Dopamine and variable rewards: social media uses variable reward schedules (sometimes a post gets many likes, sometimes few) — the same mechanism that makes slot machines addictive. Infinite scroll was deliberately designed to prevent natural stopping points. The human brain’s reward circuitry was not designed for this environment.
learnSch_health_f0_tp4_kp4: Digital wellness strategies: phone-free bedrooms; scheduled 'digital sunset' 1 hour before sleep; app time limits; notification management (batch rather than constant); mindful use (asking 'am I choosing this or being pulled into it?'); regular phone-free social time.
learnSch_health_f0_tp4_insight: Former Google design ethicist Tristan Harris testified to the US Congress that Silicon Valley companies deliberately use 'persuasive technology' techniques — borrowed from gambling psychology — to maximise time spent on platforms. Instagram's infinite scroll was designed so users could not see the bottom of their feed. Facebook’s Like button was patented partly as an engagement mechanism. The 2020 documentary 'The Social Dilemma' features engineers from these companies describing their regret at what they built. The people who designed these systems often restrict their own children's use of them.
learnSch_health_f0_tp5_desc: Puberty is the biological process of sexual maturation driven by hormones. It involves physical changes, emotional shifts, and identity development. Body image — how a person perceives and feels about their body — is shaped by puberty, media, and social comparison. Positive body image is linked to better mental health and healthy behaviours.
learnSch_health_f0_tp5_kp0: Puberty in females (typically ages 8-13): oestrogen triggers breast development, pubic hair, hip widening, onset of menstruation (menarche). Menstruation is a monthly cycle of approximately 28 days (range 21-35) driven by oestrogen and progesterone fluctuations.
learnSch_health_f0_tp5_kp1: Puberty in males (typically ages 9-14): testosterone triggers testicular growth, pubic and body hair, voice deepening (larynx growth), and increased muscle mass. Growth spurts are typically 1-2 years later than in females. Sperm production begins.
learnSch_health_f0_tp5_kp2: Growth plates (epiphyseal plates): cartilage areas at bone ends where bone growth occurs. Fuse (close) at end of puberty (around 16-18 in girls, 18-21 in boys). After closure, bones cannot grow longer. Adequate nutrition — especially calcium and vitamin D — is critical during this window.
learnSch_health_f0_tp5_kp3: Body image: the subjective perception of one’s own body. Negative body image (dissatisfaction, distorted perception) is associated with eating disorders, low self-esteem, and depression. Social media exposure to idealised body images increases body dissatisfaction, particularly among young women.
learnSch_health_f0_tp5_kp4: Eating disorders: anorexia nervosa (restriction and intense fear of weight gain), bulimia nervosa (binge-purge cycles), binge eating disorder. Most common mental illnesses: anorexia has the highest mortality rate of any psychiatric disorder. Early intervention is critical. BEAT (beating eating disorders) is the UK national charity.
learnSch_health_f0_tp5_insight: Advertising standards now require disclaimers when images have been digitally altered to change body shape or size in ads targeting under-18s (UK ASA rules, 2021). Yet Instagram filters, TikTok beauty filters, and AI photo editors allow anyone to instantly reshape their body in selfies, creating what psychologists call 'Snapchat dysmorphia' — a phenomenon where people request plastic surgery to look like their filtered selfies. Surgeons report a major increase in requests from teens wanting to look like their own filtered images. The gap between filtered reality and physical reality has become a significant mental health issue.
learnSch_health_f1_tp3: Anxiety and depression: signs and coping strategies
learnSch_health_f1_tp3_desc: Anxiety and depression are the most common mental health conditions, affecting 1 in 4 people in the UK each year. They exist on spectrums from temporary emotional responses to clinical disorders requiring professional treatment. Understanding symptoms, self-help strategies, and when to seek help is essential mental health literacy.
learnSch_health_f1_tp3_kp0: Anxiety: characterised by excessive worry, fear, and physical symptoms (racing heart, shortness of breath, sweating). Generalised Anxiety Disorder (GAD), Social Anxiety, Panic Disorder, Phobias, and OCD are distinct diagnoses. Anxiety is adaptive (helps us avoid danger) but becomes problematic when disproportionate and persistent.
learnSch_health_f1_tp3_kp1: Depression: persistent low mood, loss of interest, fatigue, cognitive slowing, sleep disruption, and feelings of worthlessness. Distinct from temporary sadness. PHQ-9 questionnaire is used clinically to assess severity. Major Depressive Disorder affects 5% of UK adults at any one time.
learnSch_health_f1_tp3_kp2: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT): most evidence-based psychological treatment for both anxiety and depression. Identifies and challenges negative automatic thoughts (cognitive distortions). 'Catastrophising', 'all-or-nothing thinking', and 'mind-reading' are common patterns CBT addresses.
learnSch_health_f1_tp3_kp3: Physical exercise as treatment: meta-analyses show regular aerobic exercise is as effective as antidepressants for mild-to-moderate depression. Mechanism: increases BDNF (brain growth factor), serotonin, and dopamine; reduces cortisol. The UK NICE guidelines recommend exercise as a first-line treatment.
learnSch_health_f1_tp3_kp4: When to seek help: persistent symptoms for 2+ weeks; symptoms interfering with daily functioning; suicidal thoughts (always seek immediate help). Resources: GP referral, school counsellor, Samaritans (116 123, free 24/7), CALM (0800 58 58 58), Childline (0800 1111 for under-18s).
learnSch_health_f1_tp3_insight: The UK is experiencing a significant increase in young people receiving antidepressants: prescriptions for under-18s rose 28% between 2018 and 2023. However, antidepressants are generally not recommended as first-line treatment for adolescent depression (CBT is preferred). The surge in mental health conditions among young people — documented across multiple countries simultaneously from around 2012 — coincides almost exactly with the widespread adoption of smartphones and social media. The debate about causation vs correlation is one of the most important public health questions of our time.
learnSch_health_f1_tp4_desc: Resilience is the ability to adapt and recover from adversity, stress, and challenges. Emotional regulation is the capacity to manage and respond to emotional experiences in flexible, constructive ways. Both are skills that can be developed and are strongly predictive of mental health outcomes and life satisfaction.
learnSch_health_f1_tp4_kp0: Resilience factors: secure attachment relationships, problem-solving skills, sense of purpose and meaning, self-efficacy (belief in one’s ability to cope), optimism, and social support. Resilience is not the absence of difficulty but the ability to process it.
learnSch_health_f1_tp4_kp1: Growth mindset (Dweck): belief that abilities can be developed through effort, strategies, and learning from failure. Opposed to fixed mindset (abilities are innate and unchangeable). Growth mindset predicts academic resilience, persistence, and recovery from setback.
learnSch_health_f1_tp4_kp2: Emotional regulation strategies: deep breathing (activates parasympathetic nervous system, reduces cortisol); mindfulness (non-judgmental present-moment awareness); cognitive reappraisal (reinterpreting a situation to change its emotional impact); distraction for short-term regulation; social support.
learnSch_health_f1_tp4_kp3: Window of tolerance: the zone of nervous system activation in which we can function effectively. Below it: hypoarousal (numbness, dissociation). Above it: hyperarousal (panic, rage). Trauma narrows the window. Therapy and body-based practices (exercise, breath work) widen it.
learnSch_health_f1_tp4_kp4: Post-traumatic growth: the experience of positive psychological change following struggle with highly challenging life circumstances. Survivors of serious illness, bereavement, or trauma often report increased appreciation of life, closer relationships, and enhanced personal strength. Adversity and growth are not mutually exclusive.
learnSch_health_f1_tp4_insight: Victor Frankl’s 'Man’s Search for Meaning' (1946) — written after surviving four Nazi concentration camps — argues that the last human freedom is the ability to choose one’s attitude in any given situation. He observed that those who survived the camps longest were those who maintained a sense of purpose and meaning, even in extreme circumstances. His logotherapy (meaning-centred psychotherapy) influenced modern resilience research profoundly. The book has sold 16 million copies and remains one of the most influential texts in psychology — a testament to how lived experience of extremity can generate durable psychological insight.
learnSch_health_f1_tp5_desc: Healthy relationships — friendships, romantic relationships, and family bonds — are the single strongest predictor of life satisfaction and longevity. Learning to communicate honestly, set boundaries, recognise red flags, and navigate conflict are foundational life skills that formal education rarely teaches systematically.
learnSch_health_f1_tp5_kp0: Healthy relationship characteristics: mutual respect, trust, honesty, good communication, equality, and support for individual autonomy. Both people feel valued, heard, and safe to be themselves. No relationship is perfect; willingness to repair ruptures is more important than avoiding conflict entirely.
learnSch_health_f1_tp5_kp1: Unhealthy relationship signs: controlling behaviour (monitoring phone, isolating from friends), jealousy framed as love, pressure around physical boundaries, gaslighting (making someone doubt their own perception), verbal or emotional abuse. These patterns often escalate gradually.
learnSch_health_f1_tp5_kp2: Consent: freely given, reversible, informed, enthusiastic, and specific (FRIES model). Consent must be ongoing — previous consent does not imply future consent. In England and Wales, the age of consent is 16. Intoxication invalidates consent.
learnSch_health_f1_tp5_kp3: Communication skills: active listening (full attention, reflecting back), 'I statements' (I feel worried when... rather than You always...), non-violent communication (NVC: observe-feel-need-request), and knowing when a conversation needs a break rather than escalation.
learnSch_health_f1_tp5_kp4: Digital relationships: online relationships can be as meaningful as in-person ones but carry specific risks — catfishing (fake identity), grooming (building trust to exploit), sexting and image-based abuse (sharing images without consent is illegal in the UK under the Revenge Porn law). Online Safety Act 2023 creates new legal protections.
learnSch_health_f1_tp5_insight: The Harvard Study of Adult Development is the longest-running study of adult life — tracking 724 men (later expanded to 1,300 people including spouses and children) from 1938 to the present. Its principal conclusion, summarised by director Robert Waldinger in a 2015 TED Talk (watched 45 million times): 'The clearest message that we get from this 75-year study is this: Good relationships keep us happier and healthier. Period.' Not wealth, fame, or hard work. Relationship quality predicted physical health, cognitive decline, and longevity more reliably than any other single factor measured over 85 years.
learnSch_health_f2_tp3_desc: Basic first aid knowledge can save lives. Understanding how to respond to fractures, shock, and bleeding before professional help arrives is among the most practical skills taught in health education. The UK Resuscitation Council and St John Ambulance provide evidence-based guidelines.
learnSch_health_f2_tp3_kp0: Fractures: break or crack in a bone. Signs: pain (especially on movement), swelling, deformity, crepitus (grating sound), loss of function. First aid: do not straighten. Immobilise with a sling or improvised splint. Keep the casualty still and calm. Call 999 for suspected fractures involving spine, pelvis, or long bones.
learnSch_health_f2_tp3_kp1: Hypovolemic shock: dangerous drop in blood pressure due to major blood loss. Signs: pale/cold/clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, confusion, rapid shallow breathing. Action: lay flat, elevate legs (unless head/spinal injury suspected), keep warm, call 999. Do not give food or drink.
learnSch_health_f2_tp3_kp2: Wound care: minor cuts: clean under running water for 5-10 minutes; remove debris; apply antiseptic; cover with sterile dressing. Severe bleeding: apply direct pressure with clean cloth/bandage; do not remove the first dressing if soaked (add more on top); elevate injured limb above heart level; call 999.
learnSch_health_f2_tp3_kp3: Tourniquet: last resort for life-threatening limb bleeding that cannot be controlled with direct pressure. Apply 5-7cm above wound, tighten until bleeding stops, note time applied. The 'stop the bleed' campaign teaches tourniquet use to civilians following mass casualty events.
learnSch_health_f2_tp3_kp4: Head injuries: concussion signs: confusion, amnesia of the event, headache, nausea, balance problems. 'Red flags' requiring immediate 999 call: unconsciousness, repeated vomiting, seizure, clear fluid from ears/nose (possible skull fracture), unequal pupils, increasing drowsiness. Rest is first-line treatment; return to sport requires staged protocol.
learnSch_health_f2_tp3_insight: In 2017, Manchester Arena bombing survivor Steve Burrows survived severe shrapnel injuries partly because bystanders applied improvised tourniquets using scarves and belts within minutes of the blast. Emergency services credited civilian first aid action with preventing additional fatalities. Following this and other mass casualty events, the UK Government funded the 'HeartSafe' and 'Stop the Bleed' public training programmes. In high-mortality emergencies, the most valuable first responder is often the nearest untrained bystander — which is why first aid education in schools has life-or-death public health value.
learnSch_health_f2_tp4_desc: Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction requiring immediate intervention. Alongside anaphylaxis, recognising strokes, heart attacks, and diabetic emergencies and responding correctly in the first minutes can mean the difference between survival with full recovery and permanent disability or death.
learnSch_health_f2_tp4_kp0: Anaphylaxis: severe allergic reaction causing airway swelling, circulatory collapse. Triggers: peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, bee stings, latex, penicillin. Signs: throat swelling, difficulty breathing, stridor (high-pitched wheeze), hives, pale/blue skin, collapse. Action: use adrenaline auto-injector (EpiPen) immediately, call 999, lay flat (elevate legs), second EpiPen after 5-15 minutes if no improvement.
learnSch_health_f2_tp4_kp1: Stroke (FAST): Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 999. Stroke occurs when blood supply to part of brain is cut off (ischaemic — clot, 80%) or a blood vessel ruptures (haemorrhagic, 20%). Every minute of delay = 1.9 million neurons lost. Time-critical treatment (clot-busting drugs within 4.5 hours) depends on speed of recognition.
learnSch_health_f2_tp4_kp2: Heart attack (myocardial infarction): blockage in coronary artery causing heart muscle death. Signs: central crushing chest pain (may radiate to left arm/jaw), breathlessness, sweating, nausea, pale. Action: sit up and rest; 300mg aspirin (chew, not swallow) if available; call 999. Defibrillator (AED) needed if heart stops.
learnSch_health_f2_tp4_kp3: Diabetic emergencies: hypoglycaemia (low blood sugar) — signs: shaking, sweating, confusion, pale, aggressive. Action: give fast-acting sugar (glucose gel, fruit juice, sugary sweets) if conscious. Do not give anything by mouth if unconscious — call 999. Hyperglycaemia (high blood sugar): gradual onset, extreme thirst, frequent urination, fruity breath — needs insulin adjustment, usually not immediately life-threatening.
learnSch_health_f2_tp4_kp4: CPR and AED: if someone is unresponsive and not breathing normally, begin CPR: 30 chest compressions (hard and fast, 5-6cm depth, 100-120/min) then 2 rescue breaths. Repeat. An AED (automated external defibrillator) can be used by untrained bystanders — it gives voice instructions and will only shock if needed. Hands-only CPR (without rescue breaths) is now the recommended approach for untrained bystanders.
learnSch_health_f2_tp4_insight: Fourteen-year-old Mark Bhatt saved his father’s life in 2019 after learning CPR in a school lesson three weeks earlier. His father collapsed at home with a cardiac arrest; Mark called 999 and performed CPR for 8 minutes until paramedics arrived. His father made a full recovery. The UK Resuscitation Council estimates that bystander CPR doubles or trebles survival rates from out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, yet only 40% of UK cardiac arrest victims receive it (compared to 80% in Norway, which has mandatory CPR training). The evidence for school CPR training is so strong that it is now statutory in England.
learnSch_health_f2_tp5_desc: Drowning is the third leading cause of unintentional injury death worldwide, killing 320,000 people annually. In the UK, approximately 600 people drown each year, mostly young men in open water. Understanding cold water shock, water entry techniques, and prevention principles could save lives.
learnSch_health_f2_tp5_kp0: Cold water shock: immersion in cold water (<15°C) triggers gasping, hyperventilation, and panic within the first 3 minutes. This is the primary drowning mechanism in the UK, not exhaustion. The initial gasp reflex can cause water inhalation even in strong swimmers. The Royal National Lifeboat Institution (RNLI) mantra: 'float to live' — lie on your back, conserve energy, wait for help.
learnSch_health_f2_tp5_kp1: RNLI 'Float to Live' technique: if you enter cold water unexpectedly, suppress the urge to swim frantically. Roll onto your back, spread arms and legs, tilt head back. Float until the cold shock response passes (1-3 minutes), then call for help or make controlled movements to safety. Fighting the cold shock response by thrashing is counterproductive.
learnSch_health_f2_tp5_kp2: Open water risks: rivers and lakes have hidden currents, underwater obstacles, and cold layers at depth. 'Blue Flag' beaches have lifeguards; open water does not. Alcohol significantly increases drowning risk (40% of adult drowning victims have alcohol in their bloodstream). Never swim alone, at night, or in unfamiliar water.
learnSch_health_f2_tp5_kp3: Road safety: in the UK, 1,633 people died on roads in 2022. Young drivers (17-24) are disproportionately represented. Key risk factors: speeding, mobile phone use while driving, drink/drug driving, not wearing seatbelts. Cycle helmets reduce serious head injury risk by 85%. High-visibility clothing reduces pedestrian collision risk at night.
learnSch_health_f2_tp5_kp4: Falls prevention: falls are the leading cause of accident deaths in people over 65 — 55% of all accidental deaths. Prevention: exercise (balance and strength), home hazard removal, vision correction, appropriate footwear, vitamin D supplementation (bone strength). In younger people, falls from heights and alcohol-related falls are primary risks.
learnSch_health_f2_tp5_insight: In 1988, UK drowning deaths in open water numbered approximately 2,400 per year. By 2023, this had fallen to around 600 — a 75% reduction over 35 years despite increasing participation in water activities. The primary causes of this reduction: increased lifeguard provision, swimming education, RNLI safety campaigns, and better rescue technology. Yet 600 annual deaths remains preventable. The 'Float to Live' campaign alone — launched in 2013 with a single YouTube video showing the technique — is credited with measurably increasing survival rates in cold water incidents. A 90-second technique learned from a video has likely saved hundreds of lives.
learnSch_arts_f0_tp3_desc: Perspective is the technique for representing three-dimensional space on a two-dimensional surface. It creates the illusion of depth and distance, transforming flat marks into spaces you could imaginatively walk through. Mastery of perspective underpins architectural drawing, animation, video game design, and fine art.
learnSch_arts_f0_tp3_kp0: Linear perspective: parallel lines converge at a vanishing point on the horizon line (eye level). One-point perspective (one vanishing point) is used for rooms and streets. Two-point perspective (two vanishing points) is used for exterior building corners. Three-point perspective adds a third for extreme high/low viewpoints.
learnSch_arts_f0_tp3_kp1: Aerial (atmospheric) perspective: distant objects appear lighter, cooler in colour, and less detailed due to atmospheric haze. Used by Leonardo da Vinci and J.M.W. Turner. Blue-tinting distant mountains and lightening far backgrounds creates convincing landscape depth.
learnSch_arts_f0_tp3_kp2: Foreshortening: objects pointing towards the viewer appear compressed in length. Drawing an arm pointing directly at you requires extreme shortening of the limb. Michelangelo’s ceiling of the Sistine Chapel uses foreshortening extensively to create the illusion of figures in motion viewed from below.
learnSch_arts_f0_tp3_kp3: Optical illusions in art: artists exploit the brain’s assumptions about perspective. The Ponzo illusion, M.C.
