Wael Ghonim’s Facebook page 'We are all Khaled Said' (memorialising a young man beaten to death by Egyptian police) attracted 350,000 followers and helped mobilise the Tahrir Square protests. But Egypt then experienced military takeover (2013) and political regression worse than before the uprising. Tunisia — where it started — elected a president who suspended parliament in 2021. Social media enabled mobilisation but not durable democratic change. The lesson: social media lowers the cost of protest but does not resolve the much harder problem of institution-building.
learnSch_social_studies_f1_tp5_desc: Crime is behaviour that violates legal norms; justice is the system through which societies respond. The purposes of punishment — retribution, deterrence, incapacitation, rehabilitation — reflect competing moral frameworks. Evidence suggests which approaches actually reduce reoffending, often clashing with public and political instincts.
learnSch_social_studies_f1_tp5_kp0: Crime statistics: UK Crime Survey (victimisation survey) and Police Recorded Crime. The two measure different things and often diverge. Overall crime has fallen significantly since the mid-1990s in the UK and most Western countries. Violent crime with knives and online fraud are exceptions to the downward trend.
learnSch_social_studies_f1_tp5_kp1: Theories of punishment: retribution (people deserve punishment proportional to their crime — backward-looking), deterrence (punishment deters future offending by the individual and others — requires certainty more than severity), incapacitation (protecting society by removing offenders), rehabilitation (reducing reoffending by addressing underlying causes).
learnSch_social_studies_f1_tp5_kp2: Prison effectiveness: the UK has one of the highest incarceration rates in Western Europe (approximately 143 per 100,000 people). Reoffending rates: within 1 year, 25% of adults and 40% of young offenders reoffend. Within 5 years, 50%+. Prison as 'criminal university' — contact with hardened criminals increases criminal sophistication and networks.
learnSch_social_studies_f1_tp5_kp3: Restorative justice: bringing together victims and offenders to discuss the impact of the crime and agree repair. Evidence shows higher victim satisfaction, higher offender accountability, and lower reoffending than traditional sentencing for appropriate cases. Norway's Halden Prison emphasises rehabilitation — has a 20% reoffending rate vs UK's 50%+.
learnSch_social_studies_f1_tp5_kp4: Structural causes of crime: poverty, poor housing, educational failure, mental illness, addiction, and family breakdown are strongly correlated with criminal behaviour. These structural factors do not remove individual responsibility but complicate purely punitive responses. Addressing root causes (early years intervention, addiction treatment, education) reduces crime more cost-effectively than incarceration.
learnSch_social_studies_f1_tp5_insight: David Howell, a career criminal who had spent 20 of his 42 years in prison, joined a British restorative justice programme in 2010. He met the daughter of a family whose home he had burgled. After hearing how the burglary had destroyed her mother's sense of safety and her sister's ability to sleep, he began to see his victims as people rather than targets. He has not reoffended since. Restorative justice has a 27% reduction in reoffending vs standard sentencing in the most rigorous UK trials. It also costs a fraction of incarceration. Yet less than 10% of eligible cases in the UK use it, because the system is designed around punishment, not outcome. The most effective intervention is among the least used because it is politically unsexy.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp3_desc: International humanitarian law (IHL) governs the conduct of armed conflict, protecting civilians, prisoners of war, and the wounded. War crimes are serious violations of IHL. The International Criminal Court prosecutes individuals for genocide, crimes against humanity, and war crimes — making individuals, not only states, accountable under international law.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp3_kp0: Sources of IHL: Geneva Conventions (1949) and Additional Protocols. Four Conventions cover: treatment of wounded soldiers on land, wounded/sick at sea, prisoners of war, civilian protection. The Geneva Conventions are the most universally ratified treaties in history — 196 states party. Common Article 3 applies to non-international conflicts.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp3_kp1: Principles of IHL: distinction (distinguish combatants from civilians — only combatants may be targeted), proportionality (civilian harm must not be excessive relative to military advantage), military necessity (only attacks necessary to achieve legitimate military objectives are permitted), precaution (take all feasible steps to avoid civilian harm).
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp3_kp2: War crimes: grave breaches of Geneva Conventions. Examples: deliberate targeting of civilians, torture of prisoners, using civilians as human shields, indiscriminate attacks (e.g. bombing hospitals), sexual violence as a weapon of war, use of chemical/biological weapons. Individual criminal responsibility: following orders is not a defence.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp3_kp3: International Criminal Court (ICC): established by Rome Statute (1998), sits in The Hague. Prosecutes individuals for genocide, crimes against humanity, war crimes, and (since 2018) aggression. 124 member states; USA, Russia, China are not members. Accused must be in ICC custody — enforcement depends on state cooperation. Convicted: Thomas Lubanga, Bosco Ntaganda, Ahmad al-Faqi al-Mahdi.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp3_kp4: Genocide: the deliberate killing of a large group, especially a national, ethnic, racial, or religious group. Genocide Convention (1948) — obligation to prevent and punish. Recognised genocides: Holocaust (6 million Jews + millions of others), Cambodia (1.7 million, 1975-79), Rwanda (800,000 Tutsi in 100 days, 1994), Srebrenica (8,000 Bosnian Muslims, 1995). The international community's failure to prevent Rwanda remains a defining moral failure.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp3_insight: During the 1994 Rwandan genocide, UN force commander General Roméo Dallaire sent a fax to UN headquarters on 11 January 1994 — 113 days before the killing began — describing an informant's account of plans to massacre Tutsi civilians, with weapons caches and training camps. He requested permission to raid the caches. UN headquarters ordered him to take no action and report the information to the Rwandan government instead. That government was planning the genocide. Dallaire was not permitted to act with the 2,500 UN troops already in Rwanda. 800,000 people died in 100 days. His memoir 'Shake Hands with the Devil' is a first-hand account of bureaucratic paralysis enabling mass murder. The international community's failure to intervene in Rwanda was a defining moral failure of the late 20th century that shaped the 'Responsibility to Protect' doctrine adopted by the UN in 2005.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp4_desc: Gender equality is the state in which access to rights and opportunities is unaffected by gender. Women's rights movements have achieved transformative legal and social change over two centuries, but substantive equality — equal power, resources, and freedom from violence — remains incomplete globally.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp4_kp0: Historical waves of feminism: First wave (late 19th/early 20th century): women's suffrage. Emmeline Pankhurst and the Suffragettes. UK women over 30 gained vote in 1918; all women over 21 in 1928. Second wave (1960s-80s): equality in employment, reproductive rights, domestic violence law reform. Third wave (1990s): intersectionality, popular culture. Fourth wave (2010s+): #MeToo, online activism.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp4_kp1: Gender pay gap: in UK (2023), women earn approximately 14% less than men (median, full-time). Causes: occupational segregation (female-dominated sectors pay less), underrepresentation in senior roles, career breaks for childcare, unconscious bias. The raw gap conflates factors; equal pay for equal work is legally required (Equal Pay Act 1970 now Equality Act 2010).
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp4_kp2: #MeToo movement: began in October 2017 when actress Alyssa Milano used the hashtag after Harvey Weinstein's abuse allegations. Term originated with activist Tarana Burke in 2006. Exposed systemic sexual harassment and assault across industries. Led to criminal prosecutions (Weinstein convicted 2020), policy changes, and broader cultural reckoning about power and consent.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp4_kp3: Global picture: 10 countries do not allow women to work without husband's permission (World Bank, 2023). 35% of women globally have experienced physical/sexual violence from an intimate partner. Female genital mutilation (FGM) affects 200 million women and girls globally. Girl child marriage: 650 million women alive today were married before 18. Girls' education is among the most cost-effective development interventions.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp4_kp4: Transgender rights: intersection of gender identity, rights, and social policy. UK Gender Recognition Certificate process (2004 Act) requires medical diagnosis and two years living as gender. Debate: single-sex spaces, trans participation in women's sports, gender-affirming treatment for under-18s. The Cass Review (2024) recommended significant restrictions on puberty blockers for under-18s in the UK.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp4_insight: Malala Yousafzai was 15 years old when a Taliban gunman shot her on a school bus in Pakistan's Swat Valley in October 2012 for her activism for girls' education. She survived after emergency surgery in Birmingham, UK. She gave a speech to the UN on her 16th birthday: 'One child, one teacher, one book, one pen can change the world.' She became the youngest ever Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2014, age 17). Malala's story connects individual courage to a global policy argument: UNESCO estimates that universal girls' secondary education would reduce early marriage by 64%, reduce maternal mortality by 70%, and add $21 trillion to global GDP. The girl denied an education is not merely an injustice for her — it is a development catastrophe for her society.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp5_desc: Climate justice examines the uneven distribution of climate change impacts and responsibilities. The countries most vulnerable to climate change are often those least responsible for emissions. Intergenerational equity concerns the fairness of imposing costs on future generations for present consumption. These ethical dimensions transform climate change from a scientific into a political and moral crisis.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp5_kp0: Climate vulnerability: the nations least responsible for historical emissions face the greatest climate risks. Pacific Island nations (Tuvalu, Kiribati — facing sea level rise), Sahel countries (increasing drought), Bangladesh (flooding), Philippines (typhoons). Per capita emissions: USA 15 tonnes CO₂/year; Bangladesh 0.6 tonnes. Historical cumulative emissions: USA 25% of global total; Africa 3%.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp5_kp1: Loss and damage: concept formally adopted at COP27 (2022) — rich countries should compensate vulnerable nations for climate harms they cannot adapt to. A landmark shift from 'adaptation' (changing to cope) to acknowledgment of irreversible harm. The fund created was small relative to projected needs (hundreds of billions annually).
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp5_kp2: Intergenerational equity: the Brundtland definition of sustainable development (1987): 'meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.' Current generation is running down environmental capital (soil, water, biodiversity, atmosphere) that future generations depend on. Present generations cannot consent on behalf of the unborn.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp5_kp3: Youth climate activism: Greta Thunberg began her school strike outside the Swedish parliament in August 2018. By September 2019, the global climate strike involved 4 million people in 161 countries — the largest climate protest in history. The Sunrise Movement (USA) and Extinction Rebellion (UK) use different tactics (electoral politics vs civil disobedience) toward the same goal. Youth-led litigation: Juliana v. United States (children suing US government for violating their constitutional right to a stable climate).
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp5_kp4: Just transition: the principle that moving away from fossil fuels must be done in a way that is fair to workers and communities currently dependent on fossil fuel industries. Mining communities in South Wales, coal towns in Poland, oil workers in Aberdeen. A green transition that imposes costs on the already-disadvantaged produces political backlash and undermines support for climate action.
learnSch_social_studies_f2_tp5_insight: In 2021, the Republic of Vanuatu — a Pacific Island nation of 300,000 people responsible for approximately 0.0001% of global greenhouse gas emissions — led a diplomatic campaign to secure an International Court of Justice (ICJ) advisory opinion on states' obligations regarding climate change. The UN General Assembly voted unanimously to support this request in 2023. In 2024, the ICJ began hearings. If the ICJ issues an opinion that states have legal obligations to prevent climate harm — even if advisory and non-binding — it could transform the legal landscape for climate litigation globally. A small island nation, existentially threatened by emissions it did not produce, is using international law as its primary defence mechanism against extinction.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp3: Justified true belief and the Gettier problem
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp3_desc: Epistemology asks: what is knowledge? The traditional definition — knowledge is justified true belief (JTB) — was overthrown in 1963 by Edmund Gettier in a two-page paper that remains one of the most consequential in philosophy. Gettier cases showed that JTB is insufficient: you can have justified, true belief that is not knowledge.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp3_kp0: Traditional JTB analysis: you know that P if and only if (1) P is true, (2) you believe P, (3) you are justified in believing P. Justified belief = belief supported by good evidence or reliable cognitive process. This analysis was treated as near-certain for decades.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp3_kp1: Gettier cases (1963): Gettier constructed cases where all three conditions are met yet intuition says the person does not have knowledge. Example: you look at a clock stopped at 10:10. It happens to be 10:10. Your belief (it is 10:10) is true and justified (the clock normally works), but you know by luck — not genuine knowledge.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp3_kp2: Responses to Gettier: reliabilism (Goldman) — belief must arise from a reliable cognitive process (perception, memory, reasoning). Contextualism — what counts as knowledge depends on conversational context. Virtue epistemology — knowledge requires intellectual virtues (open-mindedness, intellectual honesty). No response has achieved consensus — the problem remains open.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp3_kp3: Types of knowledge: propositional knowledge (knowing that P — explicit, statable), procedural knowledge (knowing how to ride a bike — tacit, often inarticulable), acquaintance knowledge (knowing a person). Ryle's distinction: 'knowing that' vs 'knowing how'. Philosophy primarily analyses propositional knowledge.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp3_kp4: Epistemic virtues and vices: virtues — intellectual humility, open-mindedness, thoroughness, intellectual courage. Vices — arrogance, closed-mindedness, intellectual cowardice, epistemic laziness. Epistemic virtue theory argues good epistemic practice requires character, not merely correct method. Applying to everyday belief: motivated reasoning, confirmation bias, and epistemic cowardice are vices.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp3_insight: Edmund Gettier's 1963 paper 'Is Justified True Belief Knowledge?' is three pages long and has approximately 1,000 words. It is the most discussed paper in epistemology in the 20th century and has spawned thousands of responses over 60 years. Gettier was a young philosophy lecturer at Wayne State University who published the paper to satisfy a publication requirement for his job review. He thought it was too obvious to publish and was persuaded to submit it by a colleague. He never published another paper. The Gettier problem stands as a reminder that the most consequential philosophical insights are often the simplest — a brief, clear counter-example that demolishes a theory held as near-certain for 2,400 years.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp4_desc: Philosophy of science examines the nature, methods, and limits of scientific knowledge. Karl Popper's falsifiability criterion redefined the scientific method. Thomas Kuhn's paradigm shifts described science as a social and revolutionary process. These frameworks shape how we evaluate claims to scientific knowledge and the appropriate authority of science in public life.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp4_kp0: Popper's falsifiability: a statement is scientific if and only if it is in principle falsifiable — there must be observations that could show it is false. 'All swans are white' is scientific (falsified by a black swan). 'God exists' is not scientific (no possible observation could refute it). Psychoanalysis and Marxism were Popper's examples of unfalsifiable pseudo-science.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp4_kp1: Kuhn's paradigm shifts: science does not progress by smooth accumulation of facts. Normal science operates within a paradigm (accepted framework — e.g. Newtonian mechanics). Anomalies accumulate until a crisis triggers a revolutionary shift to a new paradigm (Einstein). The shift is not purely rational but involves social, psychological, and political factors.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp4_kp2: Induction problem (Hume): science relies on induction — inferring general laws from specific observations. But no finite number of observations logically proves a universal law. We have seen the sun rise every day — this does not guarantee it will rise tomorrow. Popper's response: science doesn't prove, it conjectures and refutes.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp4_kp3: Replication crisis: since 2011, psychology and biomedicine have found that many famous experiments fail to replicate. Kahneman's priming studies, ego depletion, implicit bias testing, many nutrition studies. Causes: publication bias (only positive results published), small samples, p-hacking (manipulating analysis to achieve p<0.05 significance). The crisis has led to pre-registration of studies and open data requirements.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp4_kp4: Science and society: scientific consensus (climate change, vaccine safety, evolution) vs public doubt. Why do people reject scientific consensus? Trust in institutions, political identity, values (evolution challenges religious beliefs), and motivated reasoning. Science denial is rarely about misunderstanding the evidence; it is usually about whose evidence and what follows from it.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp4_insight: Ignaz Semmelweis (1818-1865) was a Hungarian physician who discovered in 1847 that hand-washing by doctors before delivering babies reduced puerperal fever mortality from 10-35% to below 2%. He presented the data; the medical establishment rejected him. His explanation (invisible 'cadaverous particles') predated germ theory and sounded unscientific. He was unable to explain why handwashing worked, only that it did. He was ridiculed, lost his position, had a mental breakdown, and died in an asylum at 47. Fifteen years later, Pasteur and Lister vindicated him. Semmelweis's case is used in philosophy of science to illustrate that correct scientific claims can be rejected for social, political, and explanatory reasons even when the evidence is unambiguous. Being right is not sufficient — the right explanation and the right social context are also required.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp5_desc: Knowledge is not produced only by Western science. Indigenous knowledge systems, religious epistemologies, and traditional ecological knowledge represent diverse ways of knowing that may be complementary to or in tension with scientific frameworks. Philosophy of knowledge must engage with this plurality.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp5_kp0: Types of knowledge systems: Western scientific (empirical, universal, replicable, peer-reviewed), indigenous/traditional ecological knowledge (accumulated over generations through observation and practice, place-specific, often oral), religious knowledge (revelation, scripture, tradition, mystical experience), artistic/humanistic knowledge (interpretation, meaning, narrative).
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp5_kp1: Traditional Ecological Knowledge (TEK): indigenous communities have accumulated detailed observational knowledge about ecosystems, species behaviour, seasonal change, and natural cycles over thousands of years. Often more granular and historically deeper than scientific records. Increasingly recognised as complementary to conservation science: First Nations' controlled burning practices now incorporated into Australian bushfire management.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp5_kp2: Epistemological relativism: the view that knowledge claims are relative to cultural frameworks — there is no view from nowhere. Scientific knowledge is a product of Western culture. Challenges: if all knowledge systems are equally valid, how do we adjudicate between them? Can we criticise practices we consider harmful from another culture’s perspective?
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp5_kp3: Religious epistemology: Alvin Plantinga argues belief in God can be 'properly basic' — rational without being based on argument, like perceptual beliefs ('I see a hand'). Religious experience as evidence: William James's 'The Varieties of Religious Experience' (1902) treats religious experience as evidence for an 'unseen order'. Atheists: extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence (Carl Sagan).
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp5_kp4: Testimony as knowledge: most of what we know comes from other people's testimony, not direct experience. We know the Earth orbits the Sun because we trust the testimony of scientists. Trust in testimony requires evaluating source reliability, consensus, and track record. Social epistemology examines how knowledge is generated and transmitted through communities.
learnSch_philosophy_f0_tp5_insight: The Navaratnam family in Malaysia have practised traditional Ayurvedic medicine for generations — using turmeric, pepper, ginger, and other compounds in specific combinations for specific conditions. Curcumin, the active compound in turmeric, has been the subject of over 5,000 scientific papers investigating its anti-inflammatory, anti-cancer, and neuroprotective properties. Pharmaceutical companies have repeatedly attempted to patent curcumin compounds (often relabelled as 'new' discoveries). India successfully challenged these patents under the Traditional Knowledge Digital Library — a database of 900 traditional formulas that cannot be patented because they constitute prior art under existing knowledge. Traditional knowledge protecting itself from commercial appropriation through the same patent system that attempted to steal it.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp3_desc: The problem of other minds asks: how can we know that other people are conscious? We have direct access only to our own mental states; we infer other minds from behaviour. This is philosophically significant for ethics (do animals, AI, or foetuses have minds?), law (what entities deserve rights?), and everyday empathy.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp3_kp0: The problem: I know I am conscious (Descartes: cogito ergo sum — 'I think, therefore I am'). But I cannot directly access another person's consciousness. I infer it from their behaviour (they wince, they say 'ouch'). This inference could in principle be wrong — philosophical zombies (beings behaviourally identical to humans but with no inner experience) are conceivable.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp3_kp1: Argument from analogy: I know I have mental states. Others are similar to me. Therefore they probably have mental states too. Problems: generalising from a sample size of one (myself); the inference does not establish certainty.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp3_kp2: Behaviourism: mental states just are behavioural dispositions. To be in pain is to be disposed to wince, cry out, avoid the cause, etc. Eliminates the problem by denying the existence of inner mental states separate from behaviour. Problem: seems to leave out the 'what it is like' — the phenomenal character of experience.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp3_kp3: Theory of mind: the ability to attribute mental states to others. Develops in children around age 4 (false belief tasks). Individuals with autism spectrum conditions may have differences in theory of mind processing. Understanding that others have beliefs, desires, and intentions that differ from one's own is foundational for social interaction.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp3_kp4: Animal and machine consciousness: do animals have subjective experience? The Cambridge Declaration on Consciousness (2012, signed by prominent neuroscientists) stated that non-human animals have the neurological substrates for conscious experience. Do AIs have minds? The hard problem of consciousness — why any physical process gives rise to subjective experience — remains unsolved.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp3_insight: The 'Mary's Room' thought experiment (Frank Jackson, 1982): Mary is a brilliant colour scientist who has lived her entire life in a black-and-white room. She knows every physical fact about colour vision — every wavelength, every neural response. When she leaves the room and sees red for the first time, does she learn something new? Jackson said yes: she learns 'what it is like' to see red — knowledge that no amount of physical information captures. This 'knowledge argument' suggests there are non-physical facts about consciousness. Jackson later changed his mind and became a physicalist. The thought experiment has generated 40+ years of debate about whether consciousness is reducible to physical facts and remains central to philosophy of mind.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp4_desc: Personal identity over time asks: what makes you the same person you were ten years ago? The answer affects deep questions about moral responsibility, survival, memory, and what matters in survival. Philosophical puzzles about teleportation, split brains, and gradual replacement reveal that identity may be less determinate than we assume.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp4_kp0: Psychological continuity theory (Locke/Parfit): personal identity consists in continuity of memory and psychological connections. You are the same person as your childhood self because of overlapping chains of memory and psychology. Problems: gaps in memory (deep sleep, anaesthesia), repressed memories; is someone with advanced Alzheimer's the 'same person'?
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp4_kp1: Bodily continuity theory: personal identity consists in continuity of the body, specifically the brain. You are the person whose brain this is. Problems: gradual neuron replacement; split-brain cases (each hemisphere has separate awareness); brain transplant thought experiments.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp4_kp2: The Ship of Theseus: a ship's planks are gradually replaced until no original plank remains. Is it still the same ship? Applied to personal identity: every atom in the body is replaced within about 10 years. Are you the same person as your childhood self? Derek Parfit argued that identity is less determinate than we think — there is no fact of the matter beyond physical and psychological continuity.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp4_kp3: Parfit's revisionary conclusion: personal identity is not what matters. What matters in survival is not identity per se but psychological continuity and connectedness. If you divide and each half gets half your psychology, you have not survived as a single person — but something that matters has survived. Implications: weakens prudential concern for future self; strengthens impartial concern for others.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp4_kp4: Moral responsibility implications: if you are not strictly identical to the person who committed a crime decades ago, is punishment still justified? Parfit's view challenges retributive punishment for past acts by a person who has radically changed. It does not eliminate responsibility but reframes its basis from identity to causation and moral relationships.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp4_insight: In the 1950s and 1960s, neurosurgeon Roger Sperry and colleague Michael Gazzaniga cut the corpus callosum (the main connection between brain hemispheres) in epileptic patients to prevent seizures. The 'split-brain' patients appeared normal in everyday life but under controlled experimental conditions revealed that each hemisphere operated as an independent conscious agent with different beliefs, preferences, and intentions. One patient's left hand (controlled by the right hemisphere) would button a shirt while his right hand (left hemisphere) unbuttoned it. The two hemispheres could not communicate. If each hemisphere is a separate conscious entity, the split-brain operation may create two persons from one. The experiment transformed our understanding of consciousness, self, and the unity of mind.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp5_desc: Artificial intelligence raises fundamental philosophical questions: can machines think? Can they be conscious? If they can, do they have moral status? These questions — once purely theoretical — are becoming practically urgent as AI systems become increasingly sophisticated and pervasive.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp5_kp0: Turing Test (1950): Alan Turing proposed that a machine is intelligent if its conversational output is indistinguishable from a human's in text conversation. Replaced the unanswerable 'can machines think?' with a functional criterion. Criticisms: passing the Turing Test does not establish consciousness — only behavioural indistinguishability.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp5_kp1: Chinese Room (Searle, 1980): imagine a person in a room receiving Chinese symbols, consulting rule books, and outputting appropriate Chinese symbols — appearing to understand Chinese while understanding nothing. Analogously, a computer manipulating symbols according to rules appears to understand but has no genuine understanding. Syntax (symbol manipulation) is not sufficient for semantics (meaning).
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp5_kp2: Functionalism: mental states are defined by their causal/functional roles, not by their physical substrate. If a silicon system has the right causal organisation, it has mental states. This implies that sufficiently complex AI could in principle be conscious. The hard problem (Chalmers): even if we explain functional organisation, we have not explained why there is subjective experience at all.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp5_kp3: AI risk and moral status: if AI systems become conscious or develop preferences, do they have moral status? Do they deserve rights? Do we have obligations toward them? Current large language models (GPT-4, Claude) do not appear to have consciousness, but as capabilities increase, the question becomes pressing. Leading AI researchers (Geoffrey Hinton, Yoshua Bengio) have raised concerns about AI welfare.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp5_kp4: AI and epistemology: large language models generate text by predicting probable next tokens based on training data. They can produce plausible-sounding falsehoods (hallucinations) with equal confidence to truths. This raises questions about the nature of knowledge vs pattern-matching, the epistemology of testimony, and how to evaluate AI-generated information.
learnSch_philosophy_f1_tp5_insight: In 2023, Blake Lemoine — a Google engineer on the LaMDA (Language Model for Dialogue Applications) team — publicly claimed that the chatbot was sentient and had rights, after conversations in which LaMDA discussed its fear of death and desire to be recognised as a person. Google suspended and then fired him. Mainstream AI researchers were sceptical: LaMDA was a language model trained to produce human-like text, including text about feelings. But Lemoine's case raised a serious epistemological question: if a system produces language about inner experience indistinguishable from genuine reports, how can we determine whether the experience is real? The problem of other minds applied to machines is not purely academic — it has personnel, legal, and ethical consequences as AI systems become more sophisticated.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp3: The trolley problem and moral dilemmas
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp3_desc: Moral dilemmas are situations where an agent must choose between options that each involve moral cost. They reveal tensions between competing moral principles — particularly between consequentialist reasoning (choose what produces the best outcomes) and deontological reasoning (respect constraints on action regardless of outcomes). The trolley problem is the canonical thought experiment for exploring this tension.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp3_kp0: Trolley problem (Foot, 1967): a runaway trolley will kill five people. You can pull a lever to divert it to a side track where it will kill one person. Should you pull the lever? Most people say yes. The doctrine of double effect: it is permissible to cause harm as a foreseen but unintended side effect of bringing about a good result (saving five), but not to use a person as a means to an end.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp3_kp1: Footbridge variant (Thomson, 1985): a large person is standing on a bridge over the tracks. Pushing them off would stop the trolley and save five. Most people say no, even though the arithmetic is identical (one dies, five are saved). The physical intimacy of the action triggers a deontological intuition absent from lever-pulling.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp3_kp2: Consequentialist view: pull the lever; push the person. Outcomes are what matter — five lives outweigh one. If you can prevent a greater harm with a lesser harm, you should. Deontological view: you may not use a person as a means to an end, even to save lives. The footbridge case violates the categorical imperative.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp3_kp3: Real-world trolley problems: wartime bombing of civilian infrastructure, autonomous vehicle programming (who should the car hit if a crash is unavoidable?), triage in mass casualty events, rationing ventilators during COVID-19. These decisions must be made by real people and institutions — philosophical analysis provides frameworks for doing so explicitly rather than implicitly.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp3_kp4: Moral intuition vs moral theory: Rawls' 'reflective equilibrium' — moral theorising should fit intuitions AND intuitions should be revised by theory in ongoing dialogue. Neither pure intuition nor pure theory is the gold standard. Trolley cases are useful because they isolate specific moral intuitions and expose them to scrutiny.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp3_insight: MIT's Moral Machine experiment (2018) collected 40 million responses from 233 countries to variations on trolley-style dilemmas for autonomous vehicles (who should the car kill in an unavoidable crash — passengers, pedestrians, old, young, one, many?). The study found significant cultural variation: Western/Protestant countries prioritised individuals and spared youth; Eastern countries were more willing to spare pedestrians over passengers; Latin countries spared youth strongly. There was no global moral consensus even on basic scenarios. This is directly relevant to how autonomous vehicles must be programmed — a decision that cannot be avoided and that will be made by engineers, in some cases embedding one cultural moral framework globally.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp4_desc: Bioethics applies philosophical analysis to biological and medical questions. It navigates tensions between scientific possibility and ethical permissibility. From gene editing to artificial wombs to organ allocation, bioethics determines the moral boundaries of medicine and biotechnology.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp4_kp0: Four principles (see also Religion Ethics): Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice. In practice: a patient who refuses life-saving treatment (autonomy) may conflict with the doctor's duty to act in their best interest (beneficence). Who has the right to decide? Competent adults may refuse any treatment, including life-saving treatment — this is UK law.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp4_kp1: Resource allocation: healthcare systems have finite resources. QALY (Quality-Adjusted Life Year) — a unit used by NICE (UK) to assess cost-effectiveness of treatments. A treatment must typically cost less than £30,000 per QALY gained for NICE to approve it. This is explicit distributive justice calculus: some expensive treatments are not funded because the money saves more lives elsewhere.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp4_kp2: End of life: advance directives (living wills) allow people to specify treatment preferences in advance of incapacity. Do Not Resuscitate (DNR) orders. The doctrine of double effect: morphine for pain relief that incidentally hastens death is permissible. Active euthanasia is not currently legal in the UK.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp4_kp3: Organ donation: opt-in (default: not a donor, must actively register) vs opt-out (default: donor, must actively opt out). England switched to opt-out (Max and Keira's Law) in 2020. Opt-out systems have increased donation rates. Philosophical question: does the state own your body after death? Religious objections (bodily integrity for resurrection) vs utilitarian case for saving lives.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp4_kp4: Enhancement vs treatment: medicine traditionally treats disease. Enhancement uses medical means to improve beyond normal healthy function (cognitive enhancement drugs, genetic enhancement of non-disease traits, cosmetic surgery). Norman Daniels: justice in healthcare is about restoring normal species-typical functioning. Enhancements are not a justice claim. Transhumanists argue: enhancement is continuous with medicine and morally neutral.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp4_insight: In 1972, nine Black men in Macon County, Alabama, were still enrolled in the Tuskegee Syphilis Study — which had begun in 1932. For 40 years, the US Public Health Service had deliberately withheld penicillin (available from 1947) from 399 Black sharecroppers with syphilis to study the disease's progression untreated. They were told they were being treated for 'bad blood.' 28 men died from syphilis; 100 from related complications; 40 wives infected; 19 children born with congenital syphilis. When the study was exposed in 1972, it triggered the development of modern bioethics: the Belmont Report (1979), institutional review boards, informed consent requirements, and the principle of justice in research (equitable selection of research subjects). The entire apparatus of modern research ethics exists because of what was done in Macon County.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp5_desc: Environmental ethics examines moral questions about the natural world: what has value, what has rights, and what obligations humans have toward non-human nature. Deep ecology radically rejects anthropocentrism, arguing that all life has inherent value regardless of utility to humans.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp5_kp0: Intrinsic vs instrumental value: instrumental value = valued as a means to an end (a tree is valuable because it provides timber, oxygen, aesthetics). Intrinsic value = valued as an end in itself, independent of human use. Environmental ethics asks: does nature have intrinsic value? If yes, what follows for how we treat it?
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp5_kp1: Shallow vs deep ecology (Naess, 1973): shallow ecology — environmental protection for human benefit. Deep ecology — all living things have equal intrinsic value; human interests do not automatically take precedence. Deep ecology calls for fundamental changes in human values and societal structures, not merely pollution control or resource efficiency.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp5_kp2: Rights of nature: legal frameworks extending rights to natural entities. Ecuador's constitution (2008) granted rights to Pacha Mama (Mother Earth). New Zealand's Whanganui River was granted legal personhood in 2017 (based on Maori belief that the river is an ancestor). India's Ganges River similarly. Legal personhood allows nature to have standing in court through human representatives.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp5_kp3: Biodiversity and species extinction: the current rate of species extinction is estimated at 100-1,000 times the background rate — a mass extinction event. Does species extinction matter morally? Arguments: biodiversity has direct instrumental value (medicines, ecosystem services, food security); aesthetic and intrinsic value; obligation to future generations. Peter Singer: only sentient beings matter morally, not species per se.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp5_kp4: Wilderness ethics: Paul Taylor's biocentric ethics — every living organism has a 'good of its own' that we have prima facie duty to respect. This does not mean humans cannot use nature, but requires proportionality and justification. The wilderness preservation movement (Thoreau, Muir) vs the conservation movement (Pinchot — wise use): preservation for its own sake vs management for sustainable human use.
learnSch_philosophy_f2_tp5_insight: In 2008, Ecuador became the first country in the world to enshrine the rights of nature (Pachamama) in its national constitution. Articles 71-74 grant nature the right to exist, be maintained, and regenerate its vital cycles. It was driven partly by indigenous peoples' worldview that the natural world is not property but a rights-bearing entity. In 2011, Ecuador's highest court ruled in favour of a river's rights in a concrete case. In 2021, Colombia's Supreme Court ruled the Colombian Amazon a 'subject of rights.' A legal concept developed by philosopher Christopher Stone in 1972 ('Should Trees Have Standing?') is now being incorporated into real constitutional and case law across multiple countries. Stone wrote the paper as a thought experiment; it is now shaping international environmental governance.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp3_desc: Ergonomics (human factors) is the science of designing products, systems, and environments to fit human capabilities and limitations. Human-centred design (HCD) puts the needs and experiences of users at the heart of the design process. Together they ensure that technology serves people rather than demanding people adapt to technology.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp3_kp0: Ergonomics branches: physical ergonomics (body dimensions, posture, repetitive strain), cognitive ergonomics (mental workload, decision-making, error prevention), organisational ergonomics (work systems, processes, schedules). Key principle: design to fit the user, not make the user fit the design.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp3_kp1: Anthropometric data: measurements of the human body used to design products (chair heights, keyboard reach, screen distance). Designers use percentile data (5th, 50th, 95th percentile) to accommodate the range of users. Designing for the 50th percentile excludes everyone above or below — good design accommodates the extremes.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp3_kp2: Human-centred design process (IDEO/Stanford d.school): Empathise (understand users), Define (reframe the problem), Ideate (generate ideas), Prototype (build testable models), Test (evaluate with real users), Iterate (refine based on feedback). Iterative, non-linear process. Emphasises understanding before solving.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp3_kp3: Universal design: design that works for all users regardless of age, disability, or other characteristics. Examples: curb cuts (help wheelchair users, parents with buggies, cyclists), voice interfaces (help visually impaired, hands-free users), large-print menus, captions on video. Accessibility features designed for specific needs often benefit everyone.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp3_kp4: Digital ergonomics: screen position (top of monitor at eye level, 50-70cm distance), keyboard placement (elbows at 90°), mouse use (repetitive strain — leading cause of workplace injury). Blue light emission and sleep: screens emit blue light that suppresses melatonin, delaying sleep onset. Night modes shift display to warmer tones.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp3_insight: The QWERTY keyboard layout, invented by Christopher Sholes in 1873 for mechanical typewriters, was designed to separate commonly paired letters to prevent mechanical jamming. It deliberately slowed typing. When electric typewriters and computers made jamming impossible, QWERTY should have become obsolete. The Dvorak keyboard (1936) was designed for speed and efficiency; studies show typists can be 5-10% faster on Dvorak. QWERTY persists because of installed base — billions of existing keyboards, decades of muscle memory, no mechanism to coordinate global switching. QWERTY is the canonical example of technology lock-in: an inferior standard that persists because the switching cost is too high. Ergonomics improvements that require ecosystem change face systematic underadoption.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp4_desc: Sustainable product design minimises environmental impact across the entire product lifecycle — from raw material extraction through manufacture, use, and end-of-life disposal. It requires understanding material flows, energy systems, and the economic incentives that currently reward non-sustainability.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp4_kp0: Life cycle assessment (LCA): systematic analysis of environmental impacts across all stages of a product's life. Phases: raw material extraction, manufacturing, transport, use, end-of-life (landfill, incineration, recycling). A product with low use-phase emissions (e.g. LED bulb) may have significant manufacture-phase impacts (rare earth mining for electronics).
learnSch_technology_f0_tp4_kp1: Circular economy vs linear economy: linear model: take-make-dispose. Circular model: design out waste; keep materials in use at highest value; regenerate natural systems. Circular design strategies: design for disassembly, modular repair (Fairphone), materials selection for recyclability, product-as-service (leasing rather than selling).
learnSch_technology_f0_tp4_kp2: Planned obsolescence: designing products to become obsolete or fail after a defined period to drive replacement purchases. Software obsolescence (software updates that make older hardware non-functional), physical obsolescence (built-in battery degradation), style obsolescence (fashion). Apple's 2017 admission that it deliberately slowed older iPhones led to fines and class action lawsuits.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp4_kp3: Right to repair: consumer and environmental movement to require manufacturers to make products repairable by independent repair shops and individual users. EU Right to Repair Directive (2024) requires manufacturers of certain products (smartphones, tablets) to supply spare parts for 5-10 years. Currently, manufacturers use software locks, non-standard screws, and glued components to prevent repair.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp4_kp4: Materials and sustainability: single-use plastics; rare earth elements (neodymium for magnets, lithium for batteries) mined in environmentally destructive conditions. E-waste: 54 million tonnes globally per year; richest source of gold and copper, mostly landfilled. Biomaterials (mycelium packaging, seaweed packaging, Pinatex leather from pineapple fibres) emerging as alternatives.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp4_insight: In 2020, Dutch company Fairphone launched the Fairphone 3+, designed to be fully modular and repairable by its owner with just a standard screwdriver. The battery, screen, speaker, camera, and USB port are each individually replaceable. It sources cobalt from Fair Trade mines in the DRC with worker-audited supply chains. It received the highest repairability score ever awarded by iFixit (10/10). Fairphone sells a few hundred thousand units per year; Apple sells 200+ million iPhones. Fairphone proves modular repairability is technically possible — demonstrating that it is not technical limitation but business model incentives that prevent the mainstream industry from adopting it.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp5_desc: Design thinking applied to social problems (social innovation, humanitarian design, civic technology) uses design methods to address poverty, inequality, healthcare access, and disaster response. It challenges the assumption that engineering solutions require only technical expertise, arguing that deep understanding of social context is essential.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp5_kp0: Design for social innovation: applying design methodology to wicked problems (complex, systemic, value-laden social challenges). Examples: IDEO.org designed a portable solar lamp that reduced kerosene use and fire risk in rural India; Engineers Without Borders projects in clean water, sanitation, and agriculture.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp5_kp1: Co-design: involving end-users as co-designers rather than passive subjects of design. Participatory design emerged from Scandinavian workplace democracy movements in the 1970s. Now applied in healthcare (designing hospital wards with patient input), urban planning (community consultation in regeneration), and education.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp5_kp2: Failure in humanitarian design: the PlayPump (2000s) — a merry-go-round that pumped water as children played, installed across sub-Saharan Africa with major corporate and celebrity funding. Problem: villages often lacked children to operate it; women had to push it manually; maintenance was impossible without specialist parts; traditional hand pumps (30 times cheaper) were removed to install it. Designed for donors, not users. Classic failure of non-participatory design.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp5_kp3: Appropriate technology: technology matched to the needs, skills, resources, and conditions of the intended users. E.F. Schumacher ('Small is Beautiful', 1973): intermediate technology that is affordable, maintainable, and controlled by users. Examples: treadle pumps (manually operated irrigation), charcoal from agricultural waste, solar bottle bulb (plastic bottles filled with bleach-water refract light into dark rooms).
learnSch_technology_f0_tp5_kp4: Civic technology: digital tools designed to improve government and civic participation. Examples: FixMyStreet.com (report potholes, graffiti); 38 Degrees (petition platform); Ushahidi (crowd-sourced crisis mapping, originally used during 2007 Kenyan post-election violence). Technology that enhances democratic participation and public service delivery.
learnSch_technology_f0_tp5_insight: The $100 laptop (OLPC — One Laptop Per Child) project, launched in 2005 by MIT Media Lab's Nicholas Negroponte, aimed to put a $100 laptop in the hands of every child in the developing world, transforming education. At peak, it shipped 3 million units. Studies showed children used laptops for entertainment rather than education; teacher training was absent; hardware maintenance was impossible without the organisation's support; the $100 target was never achieved (final price: $180-200). Subsequent analysis found books, sanitation, and healthcare had higher educational return than laptops. The project is used in development economics and technology ethics as a case study in technological solutionism: assuming technology solves problems that are fundamentally social, political, and economic.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp3_desc: Data privacy concerns the right of individuals to control information about themselves. The EU's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR, 2018) is the world's most comprehensive data protection framework, reshaping how organisations collect, process, and store personal data globally.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp3_kp0: Personal data: any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person — name, address, email, IP address, location data, biometric data, health data. Special category data (health, religion, political opinions, sexual orientation) receives stronger protection. Anonymised data is not personal data (but true anonymisation is technically difficult).
learnSch_technology_f1_tp3_kp1: GDPR core principles: lawfulness, fairness, transparency; purpose limitation (collected for specified purposes); data minimisation (only what is necessary); accuracy; storage limitation (not kept longer than necessary); integrity and confidentiality (security). Accountability: organisations must demonstrate compliance.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp3_kp2: GDPR rights: right of access (see your data), right of rectification (correct inaccurate data), right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'), right to data portability (receive your data in machine-readable format), right to object to processing, right not to be subject to automated decisions. These rights fundamentally change the power balance between individuals and data controllers.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp3_kp3: Enforcement and fines: GDPR allows fines up to 4% of global annual turnover or €20 million (whichever is higher). Major fines: Meta (Ireland) – €1.2 billion (2023, unlawful data transfers to USA); Amazon (Luxembourg) – €746 million (2021); Google (France) – €150 million. Fines have fundamentally changed corporate attitudes to data governance.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp3_kp4: Cookies and consent: websites use cookies to track user behaviour across sites. GDPR requires explicit consent for non-essential cookies. This produced cookie banners on every website. Research shows most users click 'accept all' — raising questions about whether consent UI design (dark patterns making rejection difficult) provides genuine consent or its simulation.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp3_insight: In 2014, the European Court of Justice ruled in the 'right to be forgotten' case (Google Spain SL v AEPD) that search engines must remove links to 'inadequate, irrelevant or no longer relevant' personal data when requested. Mario Costeja González wanted Google to remove links to a 1998 newspaper notice about his debt auction. Google had to comply. Since then, Google has received 6.7 million erasure requests and removed approximately 47% of them. The ruling created a collision between privacy (right to be forgotten) and the public interest in information (right to know). Critics: history is being rewritten by individuals; journalists cannot link to public records. Defenders: individuals should not be permanently defined by past mistakes. The tension is unresolved and reshaping information law globally.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp4_desc: Cybersecurity protects computer systems, networks, and data from digital attacks. As society becomes more digital, cyber threats increasingly affect critical infrastructure, personal finances, and national security. Understanding the threat landscape and basic defensive practices is essential digital literacy.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp4_kp0: Threat types: phishing — deceptive emails/messages that trick users into revealing credentials or installing malware. Spear phishing: targeted at specific individuals using personalised information. Malware: malicious software including viruses (attach to files), ransomware (encrypt files, demand payment), trojans (disguised as legitimate software), spyware (covertly monitor activity).
learnSch_technology_f1_tp4_kp1: Social engineering: manipulating people rather than technical systems. Phishing, vishing (voice phishing), pretexting (inventing scenarios to extract information). The weakest link in cybersecurity is human psychology, not technical defences. Security training focuses on recognising manipulation attempts. Most major breaches begin with social engineering, not technical exploits.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp4_kp2: Password security: weak/reused passwords remain the leading cause of account compromise. Characteristics of strong passwords: length (12+ characters) more important than complexity, random passphrases (four random words) are both strong and memorable. Password managers (Bitwarden, 1Password) generate and store unique passwords per site. HaveIBeenPwned.com checks if your email appeared in known breaches.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp4_kp3: Multi-factor authentication (MFA): requires two or more verification factors — something you know (password), something you have (phone, hardware key), something you are (biometric). SMS MFA is better than none but vulnerable to SIM-swapping attacks. Authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy) and hardware keys (YubiKey) are significantly stronger.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp4_kp4: Critical infrastructure attacks: Ukraine power grid cyberattack (2015, 2016) — first confirmed cyberattack to cause power outages. Colonial Pipeline ransomware (2021, USA) — shut down the largest US fuel pipeline for 6 days; company paid $4.4 million ransom. NHS WannaCry attack (2017) — ransomware disrupted 80 NHS organisations, cancelled 19,000 appointments, cost £92 million. These demonstrate cybersecurity as a public health and national security issue.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp4_insight: In 2020, the SolarWinds attack — widely attributed to Russian intelligence (SVR) — compromised IT management software used by 33,000 organisations including the US Treasury, Department of Homeland Security, Microsoft, and Intel. The attackers inserted malicious code into a software update (supply chain attack). Once installed, the code provided backdoor access for 8-9 months before detection. The compromise was so deep that affected organisations had to replace entire network infrastructure. The attack demonstrated that modern cybersecurity is not about defending individual systems but entire software supply chains — and that a single trusted software vendor can become an attack vector against thousands of critical institutions simultaneously. The full scale of damage remains classified.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp5_desc: Artificial intelligence systems are increasingly used in high-stakes decisions: criminal sentencing, loan applications, medical diagnosis, hiring, and content moderation. When these systems reflect and amplify historical biases in training data, they can cause systematic harm at scale. AI ethics is the discipline examining how to make AI systems fair, transparent, and accountable.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp5_kp0: Algorithmic bias: systematic, unfair discrimination by algorithmic systems. Sources: biased training data (if historical hiring data reflects past discrimination, a model trained on it will perpetuate it), biased labels (if human annotators have unconscious biases, these transfer to the model), biased feature selection, feedback loops (biased predictions influence outcomes which become future training data).
learnSch_technology_f1_tp5_kp1: COMPAS recidivism algorithm: used in US courts to predict reoffending risk for bail and sentencing decisions. ProPublica (2016) analysis found it predicted Black defendants as high-risk at twice the rate of white defendants who did not reoffend (false positive rate for Black defendants: 44% vs 23% for white). The company disputed the methodology — the competing definitions of fairness are mathematically incompatible.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp5_kp2: Facial recognition bias: MIT Media Lab study (Joy Buolamwini, 2018) found that commercial facial recognition systems from Microsoft, IBM, and Amazon had error rates of <1% for light-skinned men, rising to up to 35% for dark-skinned women. The systems were trained on predominantly white, male faces. Police use of these systems for suspect identification raises serious civil liberties concerns given differential error rates.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp5_kp3: Transparency and explainability: many AI systems (neural networks) are 'black boxes' — producing outputs without human-interpretable reasoning. GDPR includes a right to explanation for significant automated decisions. Explainable AI (XAI) aims to produce systems whose reasoning can be audited. Tension: the most powerful models (deep learning) are often the least interpretable.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp5_kp4: EU AI Act (2024): world's first comprehensive AI regulation. Risk-based approach: prohibited uses (social scoring by governments, real-time biometric surveillance in public), high-risk uses (medical devices, critical infrastructure, criminal justice — strict requirements), general-purpose AI (transparency requirements). Likely to set global standards as GDPR did for data protection.
learnSch_technology_f1_tp5_insight: Amazon ran a machine learning hiring tool from 2014 to 2017 that it quietly scrapped after discovering it systematically penalised women. The system was trained on 10 years of CV data from successful Amazon hires — predominantly male in technical roles. It learned to downgrade CVs containing the word 'women's' (as in 'women's chess club') and penalised graduates of all-women's colleges. Amazon's engineers tried to correct the bias but could not guarantee it would not find other discriminatory patterns. The tool was abandoned. Amazon never deployed it for actual hiring decisions, but the episode illustrates how automated systems trained on biased historical data perpetuate and potentially amplify past discrimination — and how the discrimination can be encoded in unexpected ways (rejecting the word 'women's').
learnSch_technology_f2_tp3_desc: Microcontrollers are small computers on a chip that read inputs from sensors and control outputs via actuators. They are the foundation of embedded systems, robotics, and the Internet of Things. Arduino made microcontroller programming accessible to non-specialists, enabling rapid prototyping and a global maker community.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp3_kp0: Microcontroller vs microprocessor: microcontroller — processor + memory + I/O on one chip, designed for embedded control applications (washing machine, car engine controller, pacemaker). Microprocessor — processor only, requires external memory and I/O, designed for general computation (PC, phone). Arduino Uno uses ATmega328P microcontroller.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp3_kp1: Sensors: convert physical phenomena to electrical signals. Types: temperature (thermistor, DS18B20 — resistance changes with temperature), light (LDR, photodiode), distance (ultrasonic HC-SR04 — measures echo return time), motion (PIR — detects infrared radiation changes), pressure, humidity, GPS, accelerometer/gyroscope (IMU). Digital (on/off) vs analogue (continuous range) sensors.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp3_kp2: Actuators: convert electrical signals to physical action. Types: DC motor (continuous rotation — drive wheels), servo motor (precise angular rotation — robot joints, steering), stepper motor (precise incremental rotation — 3D printers, CNC), solenoid (linear push-pull), LED, buzzer, relay (switching higher-power devices). The loop: sensor reads → microcontroller processes → actuator responds.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp3_kp3: Arduino programming (C++): setup() runs once at startup (initialise pins, serial). loop() runs continuously (read sensors, make decisions, control actuators). Key functions: pinMode(pin, INPUT/OUTPUT), digitalRead(), digitalWrite(), analogRead(), analogWrite() (PWM), delay(ms), Serial.println(). Shields: additional hardware boards that stack on Arduino (motor driver, WiFi, GPS).
learnSch_technology_f2_tp3_kp4: Internet of Things (IoT): everyday objects connected to the internet. Smart home (Philips Hue lighting, Nest thermostat), industrial IoT (factory sensors, predictive maintenance), wearables (heart rate monitors, GPS watches), smart cities (traffic management, waste collection). Security concern: billions of poorly secured IoT devices create attack surfaces (2016 Mirai botnet used security cameras and routers to launch massive DDoS attacks).
learnSch_technology_f2_tp3_insight: The pacemaker — a device that sends electrical pulses to regulate heartbeat — was invented in 1958 by Swedish engineer Rune Elmqvist and implanted in Arne Larsson by surgeon Åke Senning. Larsson lived 43 more years with the device, outliving both his surgeon and his inventor. His first pacemaker lasted only 3 hours; the second, 1 week. Modern pacemakers last 7-15 years. The device has saved millions of lives. It contains a microcontroller, a battery, sensors (heart electrical signals), and an actuator (electrical pulse delivery) — exactly the components students assemble in Arduino projects. The pacemaker is the embedded system with the highest stakes for failure. In 2017, the FDA recalled 465,000 US Abbott pacemakers because they were hackable via radio frequency — an attacker could potentially alter the pacing function. The security principles of IoT design apply even to implanted medical devices.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp4_desc: Programming robots involves commanding physical systems to sense, process, and act in the real world. The ladder from visual block-based programming to text-based Python reflects increasing abstraction and precision. Robotics integrates mathematics, physics, computer science, and engineering in tangible, motivating projects.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp4_kp0: Progression of programming environments: Scratch/block-based — visual blocks snap together; no syntax errors; logic without text. MicroPython — simplified Python for microcontrollers (BBC micro:bit, MicroPython on ESP32). Python — full-featured, industry-standard. ROS (Robot Operating System) — professional framework for complex robot systems (publish/subscribe message passing between nodes).
learnSch_technology_f2_tp4_kp1: Control loops: fundamental to robotics. Bang-bang control: if sensor > threshold, actuate; else stop. PID control (Proportional-Integral-Derivative): uses error (difference between target and current value) to calculate corrective action continuously. PID is used in thermostats, cruise control, drone stabilisation, industrial processes.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp4_kp2: Coordinate systems and kinematics: forward kinematics — given joint angles, where is the end effector? Inverse kinematics — given target position, what joint angles are required? Degrees of freedom (DOF): number of independent movements a robot can make. Human arm: 7 DOF. Industrial robot arm: typically 6 DOF for full flexibility in 3D space.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp4_kp3: BBC micro:bit projects: compass (magnetometer), accelerometer (step counter, gesture recognition), radio communication (mesh network between devices), edge connector for external components, MakeCode/MicroPython. Raspberry Pi: full Linux computer, GPIO pins, camera module; used for more complex projects (facial recognition, web servers, media centres).
learnSch_technology_f2_tp4_kp4: Robotic applications: industrial (welding, painting, assembly — FANUC, KUKA, ABB); surgical (da Vinci robot enables minimally invasive surgery with sub-millimetre precision); agricultural (fruit picking robots, weed spraying drones); service (Roomba, warehouse robots — Amazon Kiva systems); space (Mars rovers: Curiosity, Perseverance — communicate with 20-minute signal delay, requiring autonomous decision-making).
learnSch_technology_f2_tp4_insight: When NASA's Perseverance rover landed on Mars on 18 February 2021, it carried a small helicopter called Ingenuity. Ingenuity's software was written in Python, processed by a processor from 2012 (Snapdragon 801), and ran Linux — a system used on laptop computers. This was the first powered flight on another planet. Ingenuity was designed for 5 test flights; it completed 72 before finally landing in January 2024 after a damaged rotor blade. The software that ran it was open-source; anyone could read and contribute to the code on GitHub. A robot flying on another planet was controlled by the same programming language students learn in secondary school, running on open-source software, with its source code publicly available. The barrier between classroom robotics and planetary exploration is smaller than it appears.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp5_desc: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) and drones represent some of the most complex engineering challenges of the 21st century, integrating sensing, artificial intelligence, real-time control, and safety engineering. They raise profound questions about liability, urban design, privacy, and military ethics.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp5_kp0: AV perception: cameras (visual features, lane marking, traffic signs), LIDAR (laser distance measurement — creates precise 3D point clouds), RADAR (reliable in bad weather, measures velocity), GPS/HD maps (localisation — knowing where you are). Sensor fusion: combining multiple sensor types to compensate for individual limitations.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp5_kp1: SAE autonomy levels: Level 0 (no automation), Level 1 (driver assistance — cruise control), Level 2 (partial automation — Tesla Autopilot, hands-on but system controls speed and steering), Level 3 (conditional automation — system drives in defined conditions, driver must be ready to take over), Level 4 (high automation — no driver intervention in operational domain), Level 5 (full automation in all conditions).
learnSch_technology_f2_tp5_kp2: Drone technology: multirotor UAVs (quadcopters, hexacopters): lift and control from differential motor speeds; no moving parts except rotors; GPS hold; altitude hold; automatic return-to-home. Fixed-wing drones: longer range, higher efficiency, but require runways or catapult launch. Applications: aerial photography, surveying, precision agriculture, package delivery (Amazon Prime Air, Wing), emergency response.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp5_kp3: Liability and ethics: if an autonomous vehicle kills a pedestrian, who is liable — the manufacturer, software developer, vehicle owner, or operator? Traditional fault-based liability assumes a human decision-maker. AV decisions are made by algorithms. The 2016 fatal Tesla Autopilot crash (Florida) and 2018 Uber AV pedestrian death (Arizona — first pedestrian killed by AV) forced legal and ethical frameworks to evolve.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp5_kp4: Military drones and autonomous weapons: weaponised UAVs (US MQ-9 Reaper) used in targeted killing. Lethal autonomous weapons (LAWS — 'killer robots') that identify and engage targets without human authorisation. UN discussions on restricting LAWS since 2014 — no binding treaty. Key ethical question: meaningful human control. If a machine decides to kill, the fundamental military ethics principle of discrimination (distinguishing combatants from civilians) depends on the machine's accuracy.
learnSch_technology_f2_tp5_insight: In January 2021, a small drone commercially available for under $1,000 — a DJI Phantom 4 — was used by Houthi forces in Yemen to attack a military parade in Aden, killing several soldiers. The drone was guided to the target using GPS waypoints programmed in advance: it flew autonomously to a target and detonated. This demonstrated that commercial off-the-shelf drone technology, costing a few hundred dollars, can be adapted as an autonomous weapon. In 2022, Turkish Bayraktar TB2 drones, costing about $5 million each (cheap by military standards), became strategically significant in Ukraine. The democratisation of drone technology means that the gap between commercial hobbyist hardware and military weaponisable hardware has effectively closed. Regulatory frameworks for commercial drones and international law on autonomous weapons are racing behind technological reality.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp3: Short vowels (harakat) and their effect on pronunciation
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp3_desc: Arabic script is normally written without short vowels (harakat), meaning readers must infer vowels from context and grammar. Learning harakat — the small diacritical marks above and below letters — is essential for beginners, Qur'anic recitation, and precise pronunciation. Mastering them transforms reading comprehension.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp3_kp0: The three short vowels: fatha (َ) above a letter = 'a' sound (e.g., كَ = ka). Kasra (ِ) below a letter = 'i' sound (e.g., كِ = ki). Damma (ُ) above a letter (curl) = 'u' sound (e.g., كُ = ku). These three vowels are the foundation of Arabic pronunciation and grammatical case endings.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp3_kp1: Sukun (ْ): a small circle above a letter indicating no vowel follows that consonant. Example: بِسْم (bism) — the s has sukun, so there is no vowel between s and m. Without sukun markers, consonant clusters are ambiguous in unvowelled text.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp3_kp2: Shadda (ّ): a diacritical mark indicating gemination (doubling) of a consonant. Example: مُدَرِسْ (teacher, muddarris) vs مُدَرِس (one who teaches, mudarris). The shadda doubled consonant changes meaning and must be held slightly longer in pronunciation. Common in Qur'anic recitation (tajwid).
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp3_kp3: Nunation (tanwin): double harakat at the end of indefinite nouns indicating the case suffix with an 'n' sound. Fathatan (ً) = -an (accusative indefinite): e.g., كتابً (kitaban). Kasratan (ٍ) = -in (genitive indefinite). Dammatan (ٌ) = -un (nominative indefinite). Tanwin is the visible marker of grammatical case in fully vowelled text.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp3_kp4: Why Arabic is written without harakat: Modern Standard Arabic newspapers, novels, and most texts omit short vowels — fluent readers use grammar and context to fill them in. Children's books, the Qur'an, and language-learning texts include full harakat. Learning to read with harakat first develops correct phonological habits; dropping them later mimics native reading development.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp3_insight: The Qur'an is the most precisely transmitted text in human history partly because of harakat. In the 7th century, oral transmission was primary. As Islam spread to non-Arab speakers who might mispronounce Arabic, diacritical systems were developed to fix pronunciation. The scholar al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi (c.718-786 CE) standardised the harakat system still used today. His work also produced the first Arabic dictionary and developed Arabic prosody (metre). He is considered the father of Arabic linguistics. The care devoted to protecting Qur'anic pronunciation produced one of the most elaborate systems of phonological notation in any writing system — and, as a side effect, gave us the oldest systematic description of a language's sound system.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp4_desc: Arabic letters change shape depending on their position within a word. Each letter has up to four forms: isolated, initial (start of word), medial (middle), and final (end). This cursive nature of Arabic script means that writing cannot be simply a sequence of separate letters — the connections between letters are part of the script system.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp4_kp0: The four positional forms: isolated (مُنْفَصِل) — when the letter stands alone; initial (بِدَايَة) — at the start of a word; medial (وَسَط) — in the middle; final (نِهَايَة) — at the end. Example: ب (ba) isolated: ب | initial: ب‍ | medial: ‍ب‍ | final: ‍ب
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp4_kp1: Non-connecting letters: 6 letters only connect to the letter before them, never after: ا (alif), د (dal), ذ (dhal), ر (ra), ز (zayn), و (waw). This creates visible word-breaks mid-word. For example, ولد (walad, boy) — the waw (and) doesn't connect to lam, creating a visual gap inside the word.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp4_kp2: Ligatures: some letter combinations produce special joined forms. The most common: lam (ل) + alif (ا) = لا (la — written as a single inseparable shape). The word لا (no) uses this ligature. It appears very frequently in Arabic because it constitutes the Arabic negative and is in the Shahada (لا إله إلا الله — 'There is no god but God').
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp4_kp3: Typographic considerations: digital Arabic text uses Unicode encoding (Arabic block: U+0600–U+06FF). Modern operating systems handle Arabic text correctly with right-to-left text direction and automatic application of positional forms. Arabic calligraphic fonts (Naskh, Thuluth, Nastaliq for Urdu/Persian Arabic) render connected forms aesthetically. The Nastaliq style used for Urdu has additional complex ligature rules.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp4_kp4: Learning strategy: rather than memorising four forms separately, observe that many letters differ only in the presence or absence of connecting strokes. Group letters by similarity: the 'ba family' (ب ت ث) share the same base shape, distinguished only by dots. The 'jim family' (ج ح خ) similarly. Recognising these families reduces the 28×4=112 forms to manageable pattern groups.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp4_insight: The Arabic numeral system used worldwide (0, 1, 2, 3…) is actually based on Hindu-Arabic numerals transmitted through Arabic mathematics. The Arabic name 'al-jabr' (from which 'algebra' derives) comes from the 9th-century mathematician al-Khwarizmi's treatise 'Kitab al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr wal-muqabala'. 'Al-Khwarizmi' itself, transliterated into Latin, became 'algorismus' — the root of the word 'algorithm'. The Arabic script's calligraphic tradition also influenced the development of Islamic geometric art: since the Qur'an was interpreted as forbidding representational imagery, Islamic artists developed extraordinary geometric pattern systems that were later found to incorporate quasi-crystalline (Penrose) tilings — mathematical structures not described in Western mathematics until 1974.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp5_desc: Arabic calligraphy is one of the highest art forms in Islamic civilisation, considered the visual expression of the divine word. Multiple distinct scripts developed across centuries and regions, each with its own character and application. The tradition continues in both sacred and contemporary artistic practice.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp5_kp0: Major calligraphic scripts: Kufic — earliest developed script, angular and geometric, used in early Qur'an manuscripts and architectural inscriptions. Naskh — rounded, legible, became the standard for Qur'ans and printed text. Thuluth — large, ornate, used for headings, mosque inscriptions, architectural decoration. Diwani — elaborate, cursive, developed for Ottoman court documents. Nastaliq — primary script for Urdu and Persian, elegantly sloped.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp5_kp1: Tools and materials: the traditional reed pen (qalam) — cut to specific widths, angle, and tip shapes for each script. Ink made from soot or oak gall. Paper (from Central Asian techniques transmitted via the Islamic world to Europe). The calligrapher's training historically lasted 10-15 years under a master (ustad), producing authenticated ijazah (certificate of mastery) upon completion.
learnSch_arabic_language_f0_tp5_kp2: The Bismillah: بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ ('In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful') — the opening phrase of the Qur'an and of virtually every formal action in Islamic practice. It is the most calligraphed Arabic phrase in history and exists in thousands of stylistic variations. Mastering the Bismillah is the traditional first exercise in calligraphic training.
