learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp4_kp0: Addition: y (and), además (moreover, in addition), asimismo (likewise, furthermore — formal), por añadidura (in addition — very formal), incluso (even, including), también (also), ni siquiera (not even), tanto... como (both... and). 'Además' is the most useful everyday addition connector; 'asimismo' and 'por añadidura' are formal writing markers.
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp4_kp1: Contrast and concession: pero (but), sin embargo (however), no obstante (nevertheless — formal), aunque (although — + indicative for fact, + subjunctive for hypothesis), a pesar de (in spite of + noun), a pesar de que (in spite of the fact that), por el contrario (on the contrary), en cambio (on the other hand), si bien (while, even though — formal).
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp4_kp2: Cause and result: porque (because), ya que (since, given that), puesto que (given that — formal), dado que (given that), por ello/por eso (for this reason, therefore), así pues (so then), por consiguiente (consequently — formal), de ahí que + subjunctive (hence, which is why). 'De ahí que' is elegant and distinctive: 'El clima cambia rápidamente, de ahí que sea necesario actuar.'
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp4_kp3: Sequence and ordering: en primer lugar (in the first place), en segundo lugar (secondly), por último (finally/lastly), a continuación (next, below), luego (then), a su vez (in turn), finalmente (finally), por un lado... por otro lado (on one hand... on the other hand), por una parte... por otra (on the one hand... on the other).
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp4_kp4: Exemplification and explanation: por ejemplo (for example), como (such as, like), es decir (that is to say, i.e.), o sea (that is — colloquial), en otras palabras (in other words), a saber (namely), concretamente (specifically), en particular (in particular), en lo que respecta a (regarding, with respect to), en cuanto a (as for, regarding).
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp4_insight: The word 'ojalá' stands as a monument to the linguistic legacy of Al-Andalus. Borrowed from Arabic inshallah in the 13th-15th centuries, it became so thoroughly integrated into Spanish that it now triggers the subjunctive — the most distinctly Spanish grammatical feature — in a way that no native Spanish word does. It appears in every dialect from Mexico City to Buenos Aires to Madrid. Similarly, the connector 'aunque' (although) comes from 'a' + 'un' + 'que' — a grammaticalised Latin construction. The Spanish connector system reflects the full layered history of the language: Latin conjunctions (pero < per hoc, through this), Arabic borrowings (ojalá), and innovated Romance constructions (sin embargo, no obstante). Tracking the etymology of connectors in any language is a route into the history of its logical and philosophical development.
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp5_desc: Literary analysis in Spanish follows conventions that are both universal (literary devices, characterisation, theme) and distinctly shaped by the Spanish-language literary tradition. From the Golden Age (Siglo de Oro) through Modernismo and Magical Realism, Spanish literature has produced canonical works that form the reference points for literary analysis in Spanish-language education.
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp5_kp0: Literary devices in Spanish (recursos literarios): símil (simile: 'como'), metáfora (metaphor), personificación (personification), hipérbole (hyperbole), antitésis (antithesis), anáfora (anaphora: repetition at start of consecutive lines/sentences), encabalgamiento (enjambement: poetic line running over to next), aliter ación (alliteration), onomatopeya (onomatopoeia), sinestesia (synaesthesia: mixing senses).
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp5_kp1: Key literary periods: Siglo de Oro (16th-17th century): Cervantes (Don Quijote, 1605 — first modern novel), Lope de Vega, Calderón de la Barca, Tirso de Molina, Góngora, Quevedo. Romanticismo (early 19th century): Espronceda, Bécquer (Rimas y Leyendas). Realismo/Naturalismo: Galdós, La Regenta (Clarín). Generación del 98: Unamuno, Machado, Baroja — Spain's crisis of identity after losing Cuba (1898). Vanguardismo/Modernismo: Dario (Latin America).
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp5_kp2: Latin American literary boom (1960s-70s): One Hundred Years of Solitude (García Márquez, 1967), Rayuela (Cortázar, 1963), La ciudad y los perros (Vargas Llosa, 1963), Pedro Páramo (Rulfo, 1955 — precursor to magical realism). Realismo mágico: the presentation of magical events as if ordinary, reflecting Latin American cultural, mythological, and colonial experience. Nobel Prizes: García Márquez (1982), Vargas Llosa (2010), Neruda (1971, poetry).
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp5_kp3: Analytical vocabulary for Spanish literary essays: el narrador (narrator), el punto de vista (point of view), el protagonista/antagonista, el conflicto, el desenlace (dénouement), la trama (plot), los personajes (characters), el ambiente (setting/atmosphere), el tema (theme), el leitmotiv, el climáx, el símbolo, la ironia (irony), el tono, la voz narrativa (narrative voice).
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp5_kp4: Essay structure for literary analysis in Spanish: introducción: present the work, author, context, thesis (tesis). Desarrollo: three or more body paragraphs each making an analytical point with textual evidence (cita textual) and analysis. Conclusión: restate thesis in light of analysis, broader significance. Each paragraph: topic sentence (oración temÃ¡tica), evidence ('Como se puede observar en...', 'En el siguiente fragmento...'), analysis ('Esto refleja...', 'El autor emplea este recurso para...').
learnSch_spanish_language_f2_tp5_insight: Don Quijote de la Mancha by Miguel de Cervantes (Part I: 1605, Part II: 1615) is consistently ranked by literary scholars as the greatest novel ever written — it topped a 2002 Nobel Institute survey of 100 leading authors worldwide. It is also the best-selling novel in history (an estimated 500 million copies across all editions). The novel is about a man who reads so many chivalric romances that he goes mad and believes he is a knight errant — it is therefore literally about the relationship between fiction and reality, between literary imagination and lived experience. Its self-referential qualities (characters in Part II have read Part I and comment on it; a false Part II by another author is referenced and rejected) make it the first metafictional novel. It invented the modern novel — the interior psychology of characters, the unreliable narrator, the complex relationship between storyteller and story — four centuries before those ideas were 'invented' by modernism. Every literary analysis tradition that reads Cervantes encounters the same paradox: the father of the novel is also its most sophisticated critic.
learnSch_english_as_foreign_language_f0_: Conditionals: zero, first, second, and third

English conditional sentences express relationships between conditions and results. The four main types (zero, first, second, third) cover factual relationships, real future possibilities, hypothetical present/future situations, and impossible past hypotheticals. Mixed conditionals add further complexity. Mastery of conditionals is a hallmark of advanced English proficiency.

Zero conditional: expresses universal truths, scientific facts, habitual situations. Structure: If + present simple, present simple. 'If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.' 'If I miss breakfast, I get headaches.' No uncertainty — the result always follows the condition. 'When' can often replace 'if' without change of meaning in zero conditionals.

First conditional: expresses real and possible future conditions. Structure: If + present simple, will + infinitive. 'If it rains tomorrow, we will cancel the picnic.' 'If you study hard, you'll pass the exam.' Both parts refer to future, but the if-clause uses present tense (not 'will'). Common error: 'If it will rain...' — INCORRECT in standard English.

Second conditional: expresses hypothetical or unlikely present/future situations; polite requests; giving advice. Structure: If + past simple, would + infinitive. 'If I had a million pounds, I would travel the world.' (I don't have a million pounds.) 'If you were the president, what would you do?' 'If I were you, I wouldn't do that.' (Note: 'were' for all persons in formal/written English.)

Third conditional: expresses impossible past hypotheticals — imagining how the past could have been different. Structure: If + past perfect, would have + past participle. 'If I had studied harder, I would have passed.' (I didn't study hard; I didn't pass.) 'If she had arrived on time, she would have caught the train.' Used for regret, criticism, speculation about past.

Mixed conditionals: blend second and third conditional structures to express different time relationships. Present result of past condition: If + past perfect, would + infinitive: 'If I had taken that job, I would be rich now.' (past decision, present result). Past result of present condition: If + past simple, would have + past participle: 'If she weren't so stubborn, she would have apologised.' (present characteristic, past result).

The use of 'were' (subjunctive) instead of 'was' in second conditionals ('If I were you...' 'If she were taller...') is a grammatical marker of formal and careful English. In informal speech, 'was' has largely replaced 'were' ('If I was you...'). Linguistically, this represents the decline of the English subjunctive — a grammatical mood that once matched Latin and French in complexity but has nearly disappeared from English over 500 years. The conditional 'were' is one of its last strongholds. The distinction between 'If I was' (perhaps implying it really happened — indicative) and 'If I were' (clearly hypothetical — subjunctive) preserves a meaningful grammatical distinction. Style guides and examination boards in English-speaking countries still mark 'If I was you' as incorrect in formal writing — a prescriptive rearguard action against a change that is essentially complete in spoken English.
Reported speech and indirect questions

Reported speech (indirect speech) converts direct quotation into third-person narration, requiring changes to pronouns, tenses (backshift), and time expressions. Indirect questions follow different word order from direct questions. Mastering these transformations is essential for academic writing, news reporting, and narrative in English.

Tense backshift in reported speech: when the reporting verb is past tense ('said', 'told', 'asked'), tenses in the reported clause shift back one step. Present simple → past simple: 'I work here' → She said she worked there. Present continuous → past continuous. Past simple → past perfect. Will → would. Can → could. Must → had to (or 'must' if still relevant). 'I am happy' → He said he was happy.

Pronoun and time expression changes: pronouns change to reflect the new speaker's perspective: 'I' → he/she/they; 'you' → I/he/she/they (context-dependent); 'we' → they. Time expressions: now → then; today → that day; yesterday → the day before / the previous day; tomorrow → the next day; here → there; this → that; these → those. Place references may also change.

Reporting verbs: 'said' and 'told' are basic. More specific verbs add meaning: admitted (confession), denied (negation), claimed (assertion, possibly doubtful), suggested (proposal), insisted (strong assertion), warned (danger), promised (commitment), threatened (negative consequence), asked (request or question), urged (strong recommendation), explained (information). Using varied reporting verbs is a mark of advanced writing.

Indirect questions: word order in indirect questions is STATEMENT order (subject + verb), NOT question order (verb + subject). Direct: 'Where does she live?' Indirect: 'He asked where she lived.' (NOT 'where did she live' — the question word order disappears.) Yes/no questions use 'if' or 'whether': 'Are you coming?' → 'She asked if/whether I was coming.' No question mark in indirect questions.

No backshift exceptions: backshift is NOT required (though possible) when the reported situation is still true at time of reporting. 'She said the Earth is round' (still true — no backshift needed: 'is' or 'was' both acceptable). When reporting very recently: 'He just told me he is/was coming tomorrow.' The rule is that backshift is obligatory when the fact is past or uncertain; optional when still currently true.

The grammatical phenomenon of tense backshift in reported speech reflects how English encodes epistemic distance — the psychological and temporal distance between the reporter and the original statement. Languages handle reported speech very differently: in Japanese, quotation markers encode whether the speaker witnessed the event or is reporting second-hand. In Turkish, evidential suffixes mark whether information is directly witnessed or reported — grammatically mandatory to specify one's source. English reported speech backshift encodes temporal distance but not evidential source. Linguists studying evidentiality — how languages mark information sources — have found that languages with grammatical evidential systems (Turkish, Tibetan, many Native American languages) force speakers to constantly signal how they know what they know. English relies on lexical markers ('apparently', 'it seems', 'reportedly') rather than grammatical ones, which may affect how speakers think about epistemic responsibility.
Relative clauses: defining and non-defining

Relative clauses are subordinate clauses that modify nouns, providing additional information. The distinction between defining (restrictive) and non-defining (non-restrictive) relative clauses is one of the most important in English grammar — it affects meaning, punctuation, and which relative pronoun to use. Mastering this distinction is essential for advanced writing.

Defining relative clauses: restrict the meaning of the noun — they identify which specific person, thing, or group is being referred to. WITHOUT this clause, the sentence would be ambiguous or incomplete. No commas. 'The man who called yesterday left a message.' (The clause 'who called yesterday' identifies which man.) 'The book that I recommended is available online.' Relative pronouns: who/that (people), which/that (things), whose (possession), where (place), when (time).

Non-defining relative clauses: add extra information about a noun that is already fully identified. REMOVING this clause does not change the core meaning. Always use commas (or dashes). 'My sister, who lives in Paris, is visiting next week.' ('My sister' is already specific — the clause adds extra info.) 'The Eiffel Tower, which was built in 1889, attracts millions of visitors.' Pronoun: NEVER 'that' in non-defining clauses — always who/which/whose.

'That' vs 'which': in defining clauses, 'that' and 'which' are both acceptable for things, though 'that' is more common in informal English. American English strongly prefers 'that' in defining clauses. In non-defining clauses, ONLY 'which' (not 'that'): 'My car, which is ten years old, needs replacing.' 'Which' can also introduce a clause referring to the entire previous clause: 'She passed her exam, which surprised everyone.'

Omitting relative pronouns: in defining clauses, the relative pronoun can be omitted when it is the OBJECT of the relative clause. 'The book (that) I recommended...' — 'that' is the object (I recommended it) — can be omitted. 'The man who called...' — 'who' is the subject (he called) — CANNOT be omitted. 'The woman (whom) I met...' — 'whom' is object — can be omitted. In non-defining clauses, the pronoun can never be omitted.

Whom vs who: 'whom' is formally required when the relative pronoun is the object of the clause. 'The person whom I contacted was helpful.' (I contacted him.) 'The lawyer whom she hired was excellent.' In spoken English, 'who' is increasingly used in both subject and object positions ('The person who I contacted'). 'Whom' remains expected in formal writing and after prepositions: 'The colleague with whom I work...' (not 'who I work with...' in formal register).

The distinction between defining and non-defining relative clauses encodes a fundamental logical principle: the difference between essential and accidental properties. In philosophy, Aristotle distinguished essential properties (without which a thing would not be what it is) from accidental properties (additional features that could be otherwise). 'The man who called is here' (defining — essential to identify which man) mirrors the essential property distinction: without the relative clause, we don't know which man. 'John, who called yesterday, is here' (non-defining — accidental information) mirrors the accidental property: we already know which man; the clause adds contingent detail. The fact that English marks this logical distinction through punctuation and pronoun choice means that correct comma use in relative clauses is not merely a stylistic nicety — it is a semantic choice that changes what the sentence asserts. 'My brother who lives in London is a doctor' implies I have multiple brothers; 'My brother, who lives in London, is a doctor' implies I have one brother.
learnSch_english_as_foreign_language_f1_: Vocabulary in context and guessing meaning

Advanced vocabulary acquisition in English involves more than memorising word lists. Understanding words in context — inferring meaning from surrounding text, recognising roots and affixes, and identifying register — is more effective and lasting than rote learning. These skills are essential for reading academic and literary texts.

Context clues for meaning inference: definition clue: the author defines the word directly: 'The term osmosis, which refers to the movement of water across a membrane, is key to...' Synonym clue: 'The treaty was nullified, or invalidated, by the court.' Antonym clue: 'Unlike her gregarious sister, Maria was shy and withdrawn.' Example clue: 'Nocturnal animals, such as owls, bats, and foxes, are active at night.' General context: the overall topic and tone suggest meaning.

Word roots and families: English draws heavily on Latin and Greek roots. Knowing roots unlocks multiple words. Latin: scrib/script (write) → describe, inscription, manuscript, prescribe. Latin: port (carry) → transport, export, portable, import. Greek: bio (life) → biology, biosphere, antibiotic. Greek: graph (write) → biography, photograph, graphology. Latin: aud (hear) → audience, audio, audition, auditorium.

Prefixes and suffixes: prefixes: un- (not), re- (again), pre- (before), mis- (wrongly), over- (too much), dis- (not/reverse), sub- (under), inter- (between). Suffixes for word class changes: -tion/-sion (noun from verb: educate → education), -ity (noun from adjective: complex → complexity), -ous/-ious (adjective from noun: fame → famous), -ify (verb from adjective: simple → simplify), -ment (noun from verb: achieve → achievement).

Register and connotation: words can have the same denotation (dictionary meaning) but different connotations (emotional/social associations). 'Slim' vs 'skinny' vs 'thin' (all = not fat, but different connotations: slim = positive, skinny = slightly negative, thin = neutral). Formal vs informal vocabulary: 'obtain' (formal) vs 'get' (informal); 'sufficient' (formal) vs 'enough' (neutral); 'commence' (formal) vs 'begin/start'. Academic vocabulary lists (AWL — Coxhead, 2000) identify the most important academic English words.

Vocabulary learning strategies: spaced repetition systems (Anki, Quizlet) — review words at increasing intervals. Learning words in context (full sentences) rather than isolated translations. Keeping a vocabulary journal with example sentences. Extensive reading (reading large quantities of appropriate-level text) for incidental acquisition. Word families: learn noun, verb, adjective, and adverb forms together (analyse, analysis, analytical, analytically).

English has the largest vocabulary of any language — approximately 170,000 words currently in use, with another 47,000 obsolete words, according to the Oxford English Dictionary (which has 600,000 entries including all historical and current words). Why so many? English is a contact language — after the Norman Conquest (1066), Old English (Germanic) and Norman French (Latin-derived) coexisted, creating parallel vocabularies for the same concepts: ask/question, want/desire, help/assist, buy/purchase, begin/commence. This Germanic-Latinate pairing persists today: English uniquely has formal Latinate vocabulary (used in law, medicine, science) and informal Germanic vocabulary (everyday speech) for essentially the same concepts. A doctor 'perspires' (Latin); a person 'sweats' (Old English). A king 'reigns' (Old French); a farmer 'rules his land' (Old English). This vocabulary depth is simultaneously English's greatest asset (precision) and its greatest challenge for learners.
Reading academic texts: abstracts and reports

Academic English reading requires specific strategies for navigating complex texts: understanding genre conventions, identifying argument structure, evaluating evidence, and reading critically rather than passively. Abstracts and reports follow predictable structures that, once recognised, greatly speed comprehension.

Abstract structure (IMRAD): most scientific and academic abstracts follow Introduction (background/context, research question), Methods (how the study was conducted), Results (key findings), Discussion/Conclusion (interpretation, implications). Even reading only the abstract of a paper gives a reliable summary of the whole. Abstracts are typically 150-300 words. Practice: read the abstract, predict the paper's content, then verify.

Report structure conventions: executive summary (key findings and recommendations, written for busy decision-makers), introduction (context, scope, objectives), methodology, findings/results (data, organised by theme or chronology), analysis/discussion, conclusions and recommendations, references/bibliography, appendices. Understanding this structure allows reading reports non-linearly — going straight to relevant sections.

Hedging language in academic texts: academics hedge claims to show appropriate uncertainty. 'It appears that', 'The data suggest', 'This may indicate', 'It is possible that', 'The results seem to support'. Strong claims without hedging: 'This proves' (rare and risky). The degree of hedging signals confidence level and is a feature of academic integrity. Reading hedges correctly prevents over-interpreting findings.

Critical reading: identifying the argument. Thesis/claim: what is the main point? Evidence: what data, examples, or sources support it? Assumptions: what does the author take for granted? Counterarguments: does the author engage with opposing views? Implications: what follows if the argument is correct? Critical reading is not finding fault — it is evaluating the strength and validity of reasoning.

Skimming and scanning: skimming = reading quickly to get the main idea (read headings, first and last paragraphs, topic sentences of each paragraph). Scanning = looking for specific information (a date, a name, a figure). These are complementary to close reading. Academic reading typically involves: 1. Skim for overview; 2. Identify sections relevant to your purpose; 3. Read those sections closely; 4. Scan for specific details as needed.

The academic paper format (IMRAD — Introduction, Methods, Results, And Discussion) was not formalised until the 20th century. Before that, scientific writing followed the style of letters, essays, or books (Darwin's 'On the Origin of Species' is a continuous argumentative essay, not an IMRAD paper). The shift to IMRAD reflects the increasing specialisation, institutionalisation, and peer review of science — the format allows rapid assessment by specialists who can go directly to Methods (to evaluate validity) or Results (to assess findings). The format is now so standardised globally that a Chinese, German, Brazilian, and Japanese biologist working in their native languages on separate continents all write papers following essentially identical structural conventions. This standardisation has democratised scientific communication — and simultaneously created a globally homogenised academic English that has become the lingua franca of science.
Literary texts and cultural context

Reading literature in English as a foreign language requires attention to both linguistic and cultural dimensions. Canonical English-language literary texts are embedded in specific historical, cultural, and literary contexts that affect meaning. Understanding allusion, irony, and cultural reference is essential for deeper comprehension.

Cultural allusion in English literature: allusions to the Bible, Shakespeare, Greek mythology, and British/American history pervade English literary texts. Recognising allusions unlocks layers of meaning. 'An Achilles heel' (fatal weakness from Greek mythology). 'A Pyrrhic victory' (costly win — King Pyrrhus). Biblical: 'turn the other cheek', 'the writing on the wall', 'a prodigal son'. Shakespearean: 'the lady doth protest too much', 'to be or not to be', 'all the world's a stage'.

Irony and understatement: English literature is particularly rich in irony (saying the opposite of what is meant) and understatement (expressing something as less than it is). Jane Austen's 'It is a truth universally acknowledged that a single man in possession of a good fortune must be in want of a wife' — ironic: not actually a universal truth, but presented as one. British understatement: 'not bad' (= very good); 'a bit of a problem' (= serious problem). Detecting irony requires cultural knowledge.

Post-colonial English literature: major English-language literature comes from writers whose first language or cultural background is not English: Chinua Achebe (Nigeria — Things Fall Apart), Salman Rushdie (India/UK), Kazuo Ishiguro (Japan/UK, Nobel 2017), Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie (Nigeria), V.S. Naipaul (Trinidad/UK, Nobel 2001), Derek Walcott (St Lucia, Nobel 1992). These writers use English while engaging with non-English cultural traditions — expanding the cultural range of 'English literature'.

Reading strategies for literary texts: read for gist first (main characters, basic plot, setting). Identify the narrator and point of view (first person, third limited, third omniscient). Note the tone and style. Then re-read key passages closely, considering: word choice (diction), sentence structure (syntax), sound effects (rhythm, alliteration), imagery (metaphor, simile), and how these choices contribute to meaning.

Language change in literary texts: older English texts (Shakespeare, Chaucer, even 19th century) use vocabulary and grammar significantly different from modern English. Shakespearean 'thee/thou/thy' (informal singular you). 'Art' (are), 'dost' (do/does), 'hath' (has). Victorian literature uses sentences that would be considered over-elaborate by modern standards. Understanding that language is not static — and that the 'standard' we learn is a historical moment in an ongoing evolution — prevents confusion when encountering archaic forms.

Chinua Achebe's novel 'Things Fall Apart' (1958) — the title from W.B. Yeats's poem 'The Second Coming' — is the most widely read African novel, with over 20 million copies sold and translations into 57 languages. Achebe wrote in English deliberately: he argued that English was needed to reach across the 250 languages of Nigeria, and that he could 'fashion it out into a new English... to carry the weight of my African experience.' But he also argued forcefully (in his essay 'An Image of Africa: Racism in Conrad's Heart of Darkness') that Joseph Conrad's novella — a canonical text of English literature — was racist in its dehumanisation of Africans. Achebe's critique sparked one of the most sustained debates in literary studies about the relationship between canonical texts and colonial ideology. It demonstrates that reading literature as an EFL student means engaging with power, perspective, and cultural politics — not just comprehension.
learnSch_english_as_foreign_language_f2_: Cohesion: connectives, pronouns, and lexical chains

Cohesion refers to the grammatical and lexical mechanisms that create texture and flow in written and spoken English. Cohesive devices — connectives, reference words, lexical chains — allow a text to be more than a list of unrelated sentences. Mastering cohesion transforms disjointed writing into flowing, coherent prose.

Connectives (cohesive conjunction): additive: furthermore, moreover, in addition, besides, also, what is more. Adversative: however, nevertheless, on the other hand, yet, although, despite this. Causal: therefore, consequently, as a result, hence, for this reason, thus. Temporal: subsequently, previously, meanwhile, thereafter, initially, eventually. Each type signals a different logical relationship between ideas.

Reference and substitution: pronouns (he, she, it, they) and demonstratives (this, that, these, those) create cohesion by referring back (anaphora) or forward (cataphora) to other parts of the text. 'Climate change is accelerating. It threatens ecosystems worldwide.' (it = climate change). 'This process...' or 'The above findings...' are formal cohesive references. Overuse of 'this' without a clear referent is a common error in EFL writing.

Lexical chains: repetition and semantic links across a text create lexical cohesion. Same word repetition: 'The river...the river's...the river'. Synonymy: 'The economy has contracted. The downturn affects millions. This recession...' (economy, downturn, recession = same concept). Collocation chains: words that typically co-occur: 'economy...growth...investment...market...profit'. Superordinate/hyponym: 'I bought a rose. The flower smells wonderful.' (rose = flower's hyponym).

Parallel structure (parallelism): grammatical balance across a sentence or series of clauses creates cohesion and rhetorical force. 'She likes reading, writing, and swimming' (all gerunds — parallel). NOT: 'She likes reading, to write, and swimming' (mixed forms). In professional and academic writing: 'The report aims to identify the problem, to assess the risks, and to recommend solutions' (parallel infinitives). Political rhetoric makes heavy use of parallelism: 'Government of the people, by the people, for the people.'

Cohesion in academic vs creative writing: academic writing: explicit connectives guide the reader through logical progression; formal reference (the former, the latter, the aforementioned); consistent subject matter throughout each paragraph. Creative writing: may use more subtle, implicit cohesion; lexical patterns; imagery that returns across a text (leitmotif); intentional ellipsis. Understanding which type of cohesion is appropriate for each genre is a mark of advanced EFL proficiency.

Halliday and Hasan's 'Cohesion in English' (1976) is one of the most influential books in applied linguistics. They identified the systematic mechanisms (reference, substitution, ellipsis, conjunction, lexical cohesion) by which texts hold together. Before their analysis, 'good writing' was considered intuitive or merely stylistic. After it, the mechanisms of textual coherence became teachable. Their framework is now embedded in EFL curricula worldwide, in IELTS and TOEFL assessment criteria (which explicitly assess 'cohesion and coherence'), and in English teaching at every level. The fact that academic writing quality can be partially described in terms of explicit, learnable cohesive mechanisms makes it teachable to EFL learners as a system — rather than as mysterious 'native speaker intuition'. This democratised access to academic English norms for millions of non-native speakers.
Academic style: avoiding informal language

Academic English writing requires a formal, impersonal, and precise style that differs significantly from spoken English or informal writing. Identifying and replacing informal features with their academic equivalents is essential for producing writing appropriate to university and examination contexts.

Contractions: contractions (can't, won't, it's, they're) are not appropriate in academic writing. Use full forms: cannot, will not, it is, they are. Similarly, shortened forms: 'info' (information), 'vet' (veterinarian), 'exam' (examination), 'photo' (photograph) — use the full form in academic contexts. Abbreviations like 'etc.' should be replaced with 'and so forth' or the items listed in full.

Phrasal verbs → Latinate equivalents: academic writing prefers Latinate or formal vocabulary to informal phrasal verbs. 'Look into' → investigate. 'Find out' → discover/ascertain. 'Carry out' → conduct/perform/implement. 'Go up' → increase/rise. 'Put off' → postpone. 'Come up with' → develop/devise/generate. 'Look at' → examine/consider/analyse. This pattern is consistent: the formal Latinate word always exists alongside the Germanic phrasal verb.

First person avoidance (impersonal style): traditional academic English avoids 'I' and 'we' in favour of impersonal constructions. 'I think...' → 'It appears that...' / 'It could be argued that...' / 'The evidence suggests...'. 'We can see...' → 'It is evident that...' / 'As the data indicates...'. Passive voice: 'The experiment was conducted...' (passive) rather than 'We conducted...' (active). Note: many disciplines now accept or even prefer first person — conventions vary.

Vague language to avoid: 'a lot of' → many/numerous/a significant number of. 'Lots of things' → numerous factors/a range of elements. 'Big' → substantial/considerable/significant. 'Good' → effective/beneficial/advantageous. 'Bad' → detrimental/harmful/adverse. 'Stuff' → material/substance/content/element. 'Kind of'/'sort of' → somewhat/relatively/to some extent. Precision in word choice is central to academic credibility.

Sentence structure in academic writing: academic sentences are typically longer and more complex than conversational sentences, but should never sacrifice clarity for complexity. Avoid very long sentences with multiple subordinate clauses. Use complex noun phrases rather than simple nouns: 'The rapid increase in global temperature' (rather than 'It's getting hotter fast'). Use nominalisation (turning verbs into nouns): 'The system failed' → 'The failure of the system had consequences for...'

The dominance of academic English is simultaneously a democratising and an exclusionary force in global education. On one hand, the standardisation of academic English means that a researcher in Vietnam, Nigeria, or Brazil can communicate their findings to a global audience — without needing to master multiple European languages as scientists once did. On the other hand, publishing in English systematically advantages native speakers and those from well-resourced educational systems. Studies show that non-native English speaking scientists' papers are rejected more often from high-impact journals, partially due to language issues. The EFL student learning academic style is thus navigating a deeply unequal system: they must master a foreign register of a foreign language in order to access global knowledge networks. This is why academic English skills — including IELTS and TOEFL — have become among the most economically valuable language competencies in the world.
Referencing and avoiding plagiarism

Academic integrity requires proper acknowledgment of sources through referencing. Plagiarism — presenting others' ideas or words as your own — is a serious academic offence. Understanding the principles of referencing and the different citation systems used in English-language academia is essential for any student at secondary or university level.

What is plagiarism? Copying text directly without quotation marks and citation. Paraphrasing someone's ideas without attribution. Using another's structure or argument without acknowledgment. Self-plagiarism: submitting previously assessed work as new. Contract cheating: having someone else write your work. Note: plagiarism is not primarily a language problem — it is an honesty problem. Many EFL students inadvertently plagiarise due to unfamiliarity with citation conventions, which is why these must be explicitly taught.

Quotation vs paraphrase vs summary: quotation: exact words, in quotation marks, with citation: According to Smith (2020), 'renewable energy is now cheaper than fossil fuels in most markets' (p. 45). Paraphrase: restatement in your own words with citation: Smith (2020) argues that clean energy has become more economically competitive than conventional sources. Summary: condensed overview of a source's main argument, with citation. All three require citation — only quotation requires quotation marks.

Common referencing systems: APA (American Psychological Association): widely used in social sciences, education, psychology. In-text: (Author, Year) or (Author, Year, p. X) for quotes. Reference list at end. MLA (Modern Language Association): used in humanities, literature. In-text: (Author page). Works Cited list. Harvard referencing: widely used in UK universities. Similar to APA. Chicago/Turabian: used in history, arts. Can use footnotes or author-date. The choice depends on discipline and institution.

In-text citation in APA: single author: (Smith, 2020). Two authors: (Smith & Jones, 2020). Three or more: (Smith et al., 2020). Direct quote: (Smith, 2020, p. 45). No author: ('Article Title', 2020). Reference list entry for book: Smith, J. (2020). Title of book. Publisher. For journal article: Smith, J. (2020). Title of article. Journal Name, 15(2), 45-67.

Effective paraphrasing technique: read the original → close it → write in your own words from memory → compare with original to check accuracy → cite. This forces genuine engagement with the ideas rather than word-substitution. Changing a few words from the original while keeping the same structure is NOT paraphrasing — it is mosaic plagiarism. True paraphrasing requires understanding the idea well enough to explain it independently.

Plagiarism detection software (TurnItIn, iThenticate, Copyleaks) is now used by virtually every English-language university worldwide and by major academic journals. TurnItIn alone processes 3 million papers per day and has a database of over 1.5 billion student papers. This has fundamentally changed academic writing instruction — students cannot rely on copying even partially, even from obscure sources. The rise of AI-generated text (ChatGPT, Claude) has created a new challenge: AI-detection tools are less reliable than plagiarism detectors, and the same AI systems used to generate text can be used to paraphrase it to evade detection. Universities are responding by increasing emphasis on handwritten exams, oral assessments, and tasks that require demonstrable personal engagement. The history of academic integrity tools mirrors the history of any security system: a continual escalation between detection and evasion. The most durable solution remains unchanged: genuinely engaging with ideas and expressing them in one's own voice — the skill that EFL writing instruction ultimately aims to develop.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp3_desc: Referendums allow citizens to vote directly on specific policy questions rather than electing representatives to decide. They are a form of direct democracy that supplements (or sometimes challenges) representative democracy. Major recent referendums — Brexit, Scottish independence, Brexit — have shown both the power and the limitations of direct democracy as a mechanism.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp3_kp0: Types of referendum: binding referendum: the result is legally required to be implemented (UK Brexit referendum 2016; Scottish independence referendum 2014). Advisory/consultative referendum: the result is not legally binding but carries political weight (Denmark's 1992 Maastricht referendum). Initiative referendum: citizens collect signatures to force a referendum vote on a specific question (Switzerland — the most extensive direct democracy system; California ballot initiatives). Compulsory referendum: required by constitution for certain changes (Australia — constitutional amendments require referendum).
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp3_kp1: Arguments for referendums: democratic legitimacy — decisions made directly by the people carry greater legitimacy than parliamentary votes. Checks on elites — allows citizens to override political class decisions. Civic engagement — increases public awareness of issues. Bypassing political deadlock — when Parliament cannot decide (as with Brexit), a referendum can force resolution. Historical precedent: Switzerland has held over 600 federal referendums since 1848 with generally stable outcomes.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp3_kp2: Arguments against referendums: majority tyranny — simple majority can override minority rights (50%+1 is sufficient for major constitutional change). Complexity — binary yes/no choices cannot capture policy complexity. Manipulation — campaigns can use misinformation, emotional appeals, and foreign interference. Inconsistency with representative democracy — elected representatives are accountable; voters in referendums are not. 'Tyranny of the moment' — vote reflects transient mood, not considered deliberation.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp3_kp3: Brexit referendum (UK, 2016): 51.9% Leave, 48.1% Remain. The most consequential UK political decision in decades. Controversies: Â£350 million/week NHS claim on Leave bus (widely disputed); Russian interference allegations; Cambridge Analytica data targeting; whether a 52% majority is sufficient for such a major constitutional change. Result: legally advisory but treated as politically binding; led to UK leaving the EU in 2020.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp3_kp4: Switzerland's direct democracy model: Swiss citizens vote on roughly 4-12 federal referendums per year. Popular initiative: 100,000 signatures within 18 months can force a constitutional referendum. Optional referendum: 50,000 signatures within 100 days of a parliamentary law being passed can trigger a vote on that law. This system means Swiss voters directly shape policy on issues from nuclear power to immigration to cow horn cutting rights (a 2018 initiative that narrowly failed). The Swiss model is studied as the world's most developed direct democracy.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp3_insight: The Brexit referendum exposed a core tension in democratic theory: what happens when the outcome of direct democracy conflicts with the deliberative outcome of representative democracy? The UK Parliament voted Remain in 2016 (by a large majority, if MPs had voted freely); the electorate voted Leave. The subsequent years of parliamentary impasse — Parliament unable to implement, modify, or reverse the referendum result for three years — illustrated what political scientists call the 'referendum problem': referendums can express a will but cannot negotiate, deliberate, or adapt. Jean-Jacques Rousseau (the philosopher of direct democracy) believed the 'general will' of the people was always right; Edmund Burke (the philosopher of representative democracy) believed elected representatives must exercise independent judgment. Brexit placed these two democratic philosophies in direct conflict. The outcome was resolved only when a new general election (2019) produced a parliamentary majority aligned with the referendum result.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp4_desc: Most democratic states are either constitutional monarchies (where a hereditary monarch serves as head of state within constitutional limits) or republics (where the head of state is elected or appointed through non-hereditary means). Both systems can be fully democratic; the distinction reflects historical development and constitutional tradition rather than democratic quality.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp4_kp0: Constitutional monarchy: a monarch serves as head of state with powers defined and limited by a constitution. The monarch is usually ceremonial/symbolic, with actual government authority vested in elected politicians. Examples: UK (King Charles III), Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Netherlands, Belgium, Japan, Spain, Canada, Australia, New Zealand. The UK has no written constitution — the monarch's powers are defined by convention, statute, and common law.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp4_kp1: The UK constitutional monarchy: the monarch: formally appoints the Prime Minister (in practice, the leader who commands a House of Commons majority), gives Royal Assent to legislation, opens Parliament, receives foreign ambassadors. The King/Queen can theoretically refuse Royal Assent or refuse to appoint a Prime Minister — powers never used in modern times (last refused 1707). 'The Queen reigns but does not rule' (Walter Bagehot, 1867): she is the dignified part; the Cabinet is the efficient part of the constitution.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp4_kp2: Republic types: presidential republic (USA, France, Brazil, Mexico): directly elected president who is both head of state and head of government with significant executive powers. Parliamentary republic (Germany, Ireland, Italy): president has largely ceremonial role; actual executive power with Prime Minister/Chancellor. Semi-presidential republic (France): both a president with real powers and a Prime Minister accountable to Parliament.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp4_kp3: Arguments for constitutional monarchy: continuity and stability — the monarch provides a non-partisan head of state above party politics. Soft power — royal visits, Commonwealth connections. Historical legitimacy — the institution embeds the nation's historical narrative. Constitutional safety valve — in extreme crisis, the monarch can act (as in Australia 1975: Governor-General dismissed the Whitlam government — the 'Australian constitutional crisis'). Arguments against: hereditary principle conflicts with meritocracy and democratic equality; expensive to maintain; unaccountable.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp4_kp4: The Commonwealth realm: 15 countries share Charles III as head of state (including Australia, Canada, New Zealand, Jamaica, Papua New Guinea). Each is fully independent with its own governor-general representing the King. Several Commonwealth realms are debating becoming republics (Jamaica, Barbados became republic 2021, Australia has had multiple republic referendum discussions). The UK monarchy's role as head of state of multiple independent nations is a unique constitutional arrangement globally.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp4_insight: The British Crown is legally peculiar in ways that few people understand. 'The Crown' is a legal concept distinct from the monarch as a person — it is the institutional embodiment of the state. Government actions are taken 'in the name of the Crown'. The Crown cannot sue itself in its own courts (the doctrine of crown immunity — largely abolished in 1947). The monarch has two 'bodies': the body natural (mortal, subject to illness and death) and the body politic (the immortal Crown). 'The King is dead, long live the King' reflects the legal fiction that the Crown never dies even though the monarch does. When Queen Elizabeth II died in September 2022, Charles III became King instantaneously — there was no interregnum, no gap in sovereignty. This doctrine, developed in Tudor constitutional law, is why the proclamation says 'long live the King' at the moment of announcing the King's death. It is one of the most ancient pieces of live constitutional law in existence.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp5_desc: Democratic backsliding refers to the gradual erosion of democratic norms and institutions by elected governments — typically through legal rather than coup-based means. It has accelerated globally since 2010. Understanding the mechanisms of backsliding and the difference between competitive authoritarianism and full authoritarianism is essential for political literacy.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp5_kp0: What is democratic backsliding? The incremental erosion of democratic norms by elected governments. Unlike military coups, backsliding typically involves: manipulating electoral rules to advantage the ruling party, weakening independent courts and media, using state power against opposition, and expanding executive power at the expense of legislative and judicial oversight. Freedom House annual reports track democracy scores globally — for 17 consecutive years (2006-2022) more countries declined than improved in democratic freedoms.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp5_kp1: Case studies: Hungary (Viktor Orbán, 2010-present): changed electoral laws, captured courts and media, rewrote constitution. EU still debates whether Hungary is a democracy. Turkey (Recep Erdogan): following failed 2016 coup attempt, massive purges of military, judiciary, academia; 2017 constitutional referendum expanded presidential powers significantly. India: Freedom House downgraded from 'Free' to 'Partly Free' in 2021 due to BJP government's restrictions on press and civil society. Poland (2015-2023): PiS government captured courts, public broadcaster — partially reversed after 2023 election.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp5_kp2: Mechanisms of backsliding: court packing/capture (appointing loyalists to constitutional courts). Electoral manipulation (gerrymandering, restricting voting, changing electoral rules). Media capture (state advertising withdrawn from critical outlets; friendly businessmen buy independent media). Anti-NGO laws (Hungary's 'Lex Soros' required NGOs receiving foreign funding to register as 'foreign agents'). Emergency powers (COVID-19 used in Hungary and elsewhere to grant executive rule by decree).
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp5_kp3: Competitive authoritarianism: a regime type (identified by Steven Levitsky and Lucan Way) that is neither fully democratic nor fully authoritarian. Elections are held but are not fair; opposition exists but is harassed; media operates but faces pressure. Examples: Russia under Putin (at least until 2022), Venezuela under Chavez/Maduro. The distinction from full authoritarianism: competitive authoritarian regimes occasionally lose elections (as Maduro nearly did in 2024). Full authoritarian regimes: China, North Korea, Saudi Arabia — no meaningful opposition.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp5_kp4: Defending democracy: institutional checks are more resilient when multiple actors (courts, media, civil society, opposition parties, federalism) resist simultaneously. When each institution retreats separately, backsliding succeeds. The US Capitol attack (January 6, 2021) tested democratic resilience — state and federal courts refused to overturn the election (60+ cases dismissed), state officials certified despite pressure, and the transfer of power eventually occurred. This demonstrated both the fragility and resilience of democratic norms simultaneously.
learnSch_politics_civics_f0_tp5_insight: Political scientists Steven Levitsky and Daniel Ziblatt wrote 'How Democracies Die' (2018) — published just after Trump's 2016 election — identifying two norms essential for democratic survival that are not written into any constitution: mutual toleration (the acceptance that political opponents are legitimate, not enemies or traitors) and institutional forbearance (restraint in using legal but destructive powers — not always threatening to impeach, filibuster everything, or investigate opponents). They showed that democracies since World War II rarely die through military coups — they die through the gradual abandonment of these informal norms by elected leaders. The book was cited by politicians, judges, and constitutional scholars across the political spectrum during the Trump presidency and is now standard reading in political science. Their central argument: the written constitution is not self-enforcing — it depends on unwritten norms that themselves depend on a political culture that values them. If that culture erodes, the constitution follows.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp3: The European Convention on Human Rights
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp3_desc: The European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) is the primary human rights treaty in Europe, enforced by the European Court of Human Rights in Strasbourg. It is separate from the EU — membership in the Council of Europe is distinct from EU membership. Understanding the ECHR, its articles, and its relationship with UK law is essential for politics and law students.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp3_kp0: The ECHR: signed in Rome, 1950; came into force 1953. Created by the Council of Europe (distinct from the EU) in response to the Holocaust and WWII. Currently 46 member states. The European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR) in Strasbourg: hears applications from individuals, groups, or states alleging violations of the ECHR. Court judgments are binding on member states; failure to comply can result in suspension from the Council of Europe.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp3_kp1: Key articles: Article 2 (Right to Life), Article 3 (Prohibition of Torture — absolute, cannot be derogated even in emergency), Article 5 (Right to Liberty), Article 6 (Right to a Fair Trial), Article 8 (Right to Private and Family Life — qualified right, can be limited), Article 9 (Freedom of Thought, Conscience and Religion), Article 10 (Freedom of Expression — qualified), Article 11 (Freedom of Assembly and Association), Article 14 (Prohibition of Discrimination — only applies to other Convention rights).
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp3_kp2: Absolute vs qualified rights: absolute rights cannot be limited under any circumstances: Article 3 (torture prohibition), Article 4 (prohibition of slavery), Article 7 (no punishment without law). Qualified rights can be interfered with by the state if: the interference is in law, necessary in a democratic society, and pursuing a legitimate aim (national security, public safety, prevention of crime, etc.). Article 8 is the most frequently litigated qualified right.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp3_kp3: Human Rights Act 1998 (HRA): incorporated the ECHR into UK domestic law. Before the HRA, UK citizens had to go to Strasbourg (which could take 5+ years). After the HRA, UK courts can hear ECHR claims directly. Section 3: courts must interpret UK legislation 'so far as possible' compatibly with ECHR rights. Section 4: courts can issue a 'declaration of incompatibility' if a law cannot be read compatibly — Parliament must then decide whether to amend the law. The HRA did not make ECHR rights superior to Acts of Parliament (unlike EU law).
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp3_kp4: UK and the ECHR: significant UK defeats at Strasbourg: Golder v UK (1975, right to legal advice); McCann v UK (1995, killing of IRA suspects in Gibraltar: Art. 2 violation). Prisoner voting rights: ECtHR ruled (Hirst v UK, 2005) that UK blanket ban on prisoner voting violated Art. 3 of Protocol 1 (free elections). UK did not comply for 15+ years. Conservative governments have repeatedly discussed withdrawing from the ECHR (particularly in context of Rwanda deportation policy), though UK is the treaty's original sponsor.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp3_insight: The European Convention on Human Rights was drafted substantially by British lawyers, specifically David Maxwell Fyfe (later Lord Kilmuir) and others from the Council of Europe's legal committee. Winston Churchill championed the Council of Europe and the ECHR as part of his vision for a united democratic Europe — a vision specifically prompted by wanting to prevent another Holocaust. The profound irony is that 70 years later, UK Conservative governments are the most vocal critics of the court they helped create, specifically because ECtHR judgments (on prisoner voting, deportation of alleged terrorists, privacy rights) limit parliamentary sovereignty. The UK's relationship with the ECHR thus encapsulates the central tension in human rights law: are rights defined by democratic majorities (parliamentary sovereignty) or do they constrain democratic majorities (constitutional rights)? Churchill's answer was clearly the latter; some of his successors disagree.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp4_desc: Environmental rights — the right to a clean environment, healthy air and water, and a stable climate — are increasingly being recognised in national constitutions and international law. Climate litigation — using courts to force governments and corporations to take stronger climate action — has grown rapidly since 2015. These developments represent a major expansion of rights-based approaches to environmental policy.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp4_kp0: Environmental rights in constitutions: approximately 100 national constitutions now include the right to a healthy environment. Portugal (1976) was among the first. Ecuador (2008): the most radical — the Pacha Mama rights clause gives legal rights to nature itself. New Zealand (2017): Whanganui River granted legal personhood. Spain, France, Chile have debated or enacted constitutional environmental protections. The EU Charter of Fundamental Rights does not explicitly include environmental rights, though Article 37 mentions environmental protection.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp4_kp1: Climate litigation — governments: Urgenda Foundation v. Netherlands (2019, Dutch Supreme Court): landmark ruling that the Netherlands government must cut greenhouse gas emissions by at least 25% from 1990 levels by 2020 — found the Dutch state violated its duty of care under ECHR Article 8 (right to private life) and Article 2 (right to life). The first case to use human rights law to force a government to take specific climate action. Neubauer et al. v Germany (2021, German Constitutional Court): 2019 Climate Protection Act insufficient — required more ambitious near-term targets.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp4_kp2: Climate litigation — corporations: Milieudefensie v. Shell (Dutch District Court, 2021): Royal Dutch Shell ordered to reduce its global CO₂ emissions by 45% by 2030 relative to 2019 (later reduced on appeal). The first time a court ordered a private company to align with the Paris Agreement. Held that Shell had a duty of care under Dutch tort law and that its emissions policy (covering its own activities and suppliers and customers) violated this duty. Shell is appealing.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp4_kp3: Climate litigation — future generations: many climate cases argue that current emissions violate the rights of future generations who will suffer the worst effects. Legal challenges: future people do not yet exist and cannot be legal claimants. Guardianship models: some legal systems (Wales: Future Generations Commissioner) appoint representatives for future interests. Youth climate litigation: Juliana v USA (filed 2015) — 21 young Americans argued the government violated their constitutional rights through fossil fuel promotion; litigation continues.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp4_kp4: ECHR and climate: Verein Klimaseniorinnen Schweiz v. Switzerland (European Court of Human Rights, April 2024): the ECtHR ruled that Switzerland violated Article 8 (right to private and family life) by failing to take adequate steps to meet its climate targets. The first time the Strasbourg court has ruled on climate change. It held that states have a positive obligation under the ECHR to adopt and implement effective climate policy. Landmark: applies to all 46 ECHR member states.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp4_insight: The Urgenda case (2019) was remarkable not only for its legal holding but for its methodology: the Dutch court used the IPCC's own scientific reports to establish what climate action was required, and then held the government to that scientific standard. The court didn't have to decide what 'good enough' climate policy looked like — it simply applied the government's own commitments and the scientific consensus it had publicly endorsed. This approach — holding governments to scientific standards they have already accepted — is being replicated worldwide. By 2024, over 2,500 climate litigation cases had been filed in 65 countries. The growth of climate law is perhaps the most significant development in human rights law since the post-WWII period, and it raises a question that will define the 21st century: can courts compel governments and corporations to take action that democratic processes have failed to deliver?
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp5: Rights of the child and education as a human right
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp5_desc: Children's rights are a distinct area of international human rights law, codified in the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (1989). Education is recognised as a fundamental human right and the gateway to other rights. Understanding the legal framework for children's rights and the barriers to education globally is essential for global citizenship.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp5_kp0: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC, 1989): the most widely ratified human rights treaty in history (196 states; only the USA has not ratified). Four core principles: non-discrimination (Article 2); best interests of the child (Article 3); right to life, survival and development (Article 6); respect for children's views (Article 12). Article 28: right to education, compulsory primary education free to all. Article 29: education must develop child's personality, talents, and mental and physical abilities.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp5_kp1: Education as a human right: the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) Article 26: everyone has the right to education; elementary education shall be free and compulsory. UNESCO's Education for All (EFA) initiative; the Sustainable Development Goal 4 (SDG4): quality inclusive education for all by 2030. Despite progress, 244 million children are still out of school globally (2023). Girls are disproportionately excluded in sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp5_kp2: Malala Yousafzai: Pakistani activist shot by the Taliban in 2012 for advocating girls' education; survived and became the youngest Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2014, age 17). Her case brought global attention to the Taliban's ban on girls' education in Afghanistan (reinstated after Taliban return to power in 2021). As of 2024, Afghanistan is the only country in the world that prohibits girls from attending secondary school and university — a unique violation of universal rights.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp5_kp3: Child labour and the right to education: an estimated 160 million children (5-17) are in child labour globally (ILO, 2022), of whom 79 million are in hazardous work. Child labour and lack of education are mutually reinforcing: children working cannot attend school; uneducated children earn less as adults and send their own children to work. The worst forms of child labour (ILO Convention 182): slavery, trafficking, debt bondage, forced recruitment for armed conflict, prostitution, drug production.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp5_kp4: Children's rights in UK law: the UNCRC is not directly incorporated into UK domestic law (though it is in Scotland since 2024 — Scotland incorporated the UNCRC by statute, the first part of the UK to do so). The Children Act 1989: 'the welfare of the child is paramount' — the foundational principle of family law. The UN Committee on the Rights of the Child periodically reviews UK compliance and has criticised: use of seclusion in schools, treatment of child asylum seekers, and child poverty levels.
learnSch_politics_civics_f1_tp5_insight: The phrase 'best interests of the child' (Article 3 UNCRC) is the central standard in children's rights law, yet it is deliberately vague. What are a child's best interests? Legal judgments on custody, education, immigration status, medical treatment, and identity all turn on this standard. Courts, social workers, and governments must interpret it in each context. This vagueness is often criticised — different interpreters reach different conclusions. But it may also be the standard's strength: it resists ideological capture and forces a case-by-case consideration of the actual child's situation rather than abstract rules. The UK Supreme Court has said that 'best interests' must include giving weight to the child's own expressed views in accordance with their age and maturity (following Article 12 UNCRC). A 16-year-old's expressed wish carries more weight than a 4-year-old's — introducing a developmental relativism unusual in rights law, where rights are typically held equally regardless of capacity.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp3_desc: Voter turnout and voting behaviour are central concerns of electoral studies. Who votes, who doesn't, and what determines vote choice reveal how democracy functions in practice. Long-term trends of declining turnout among young voters, and the rise of social identity as a voting driver, have reshaped electoral politics globally.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp3_kp0: Voter turnout: the percentage of eligible voters who cast a valid vote. UK general election turnout: 1950: 83.9%; 1992: 77.7%; 2001: 59.4% (lowest since 1918); 2017: 68.7%; 2019: 67.3%; 2024: 59.7% (second lowest in modern era). US presidential turnout: typically 50-60%; 2020 Biden-Trump: 66.9% (highest in 120 years). Compulsory voting (Australia, Belgium) produces 90%+ turnout. Non-compulsory high-turnout countries: Sweden (~80%), Germany (~75%).
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp3_kp1: Why people don't vote: rational choice theory: voting is instrumentally irrational (cost of voting > expected influence of one vote). Disillusionment: distrust of politicians, perception that parties are all the same. Structural barriers: voter registration requirements, polling station accessibility, working hours, postal voting complexity. Age factor: consistently across democracies, younger voters have lower turnout than older voters. UK 2019: 47% of 18-24 year olds voted vs 74% of 65+.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp3_kp2: Models of vote choice: party identification model (Butler & Stokes): voters develop psychological attachment to a party in childhood that predicts lifetime voting behaviour. Issue voting: voters choose the party closest to their position on issues they care about. Valence voting: voters judge parties on competence on shared goals (economic management, NHS) rather than ideological differences. Economic voting: 'It's the economy, stupid' — incumbent parties punished or rewarded based on economic performance.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp3_kp3: Social cleavages and voting: traditional cleavages (class, religion) have weakened. Class voting: in the 1960s, manual workers voted Labour by ~2:1; by 2019 the 'red wall' of working-class northern constituencies voted Conservative for the first time. New cleavages: education (degree-holders now more likely to vote Labour/Lib Dem; non-degree holders more likely Conservative), age, urban-rural divide, cultural values (authoritarian-libertarian axis). The 2016 Brexit vote was strongly correlated with education level, not income.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp3_kp4: Turnout and democratic legitimacy: low turnout raises questions about the legitimacy of electoral outcomes. The 2024 UK general election produced a Labour 'landslide' (412 seats out of 650) on 33.7% of the vote, the lowest vote share for a majority government since universal suffrage. This result, combined with 59.7% turnout, means Labour won on the votes of approximately 20% of the eligible electorate. This is the mechanical effect of first-past-the-post systems combined with low turnout — a challenge to claims of democratic mandate.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp3_insight: The 2014 Scottish independence referendum had a 84.6% turnout — the highest for any UK election or referendum since universal suffrage was extended to women in 1928. This extraordinary turnout, in a country that had been achieving 50-60% in general elections, demonstrated that disengaged voters can and do participate when they feel the vote matters to their lives, when there is a clear binary choice, and when there is sustained civic mobilisation over a multi-year campaign. Political scientists studying the Scottish referendum found that the campaign itself — through community meetings, canvassing, social media, and debate — permanently increased civic engagement: turnout in subsequent Scottish elections remained higher than pre-2014 levels. This finding challenges the idea that low turnout is inevitable or structural — it is partly a function of whether voters believe their vote matters.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp4_desc: Money and media shape electoral outcomes in ways that raise fundamental questions about democratic equality. Campaign finance rules, the role of social media, and the concentration of media ownership are central issues in contemporary democratic politics. Reforms in these areas are contested because they involve balancing free speech with electoral fairness.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp4_kp0: Campaign finance in the UK: Electoral Commission regulates spending. UK spending limits: general election — each constituency candidate limited to ~£13,000-18,000 (depending on size); national party limits vary. Donations: from UK-registered companies, UK citizens, or unions affiliated to parties. Foreign donations prohibited. 2019 election: Conservatives spent £16.4M nationally; Labour £11M. US comparison: 2020 presidential election: $14.4 billion total — the most expensive election in history.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp4_kp1: US campaign finance: Citizens United v FEC (US Supreme Court, 2010): ruled that corporations and unions can spend unlimited money on independent political advertising (First Amendment protection of speech). Result: rise of 'Super PACs' (Political Action Committees) that can raise unlimited funds. 2024 election: Elon Musk contributed $277 million to Trump-aligned political organisations. Critics: elections become plutocratic. Supporters: spending is protected speech and does not corrupt when 'independent'.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp4_kp2: Media ownership and concentration: in the UK, Rupert Murdoch's News Corp owns The Sun, The Times, The Sunday Times, TalkTV. Daily Mail Group: Daily Mail, Mail on Sunday, Metro, MailOnline. IPSO (Independent Press Standards Organisation): the press self-regulator. Ofcom: regulates broadcast media (TV, radio) under statutory obligations for impartiality. The BBC: licence fee funded, statutory obligation of impartiality. Bias debate: Leveson Inquiry (2012) found systematic phone hacking and excessive press power.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp4_kp3: Social media and elections: Facebook's algorithm amplifies emotionally engaging content — which tends to be more outraged and polarising. Cambridge Analytica scandal (2018): harvested data of 87 million Facebook users without consent; used to build psychographic profiles for targeted political advertising in the 2016 US election and Brexit referendum. The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined Facebook £500,000 (the maximum pre-GDPR) and Cambridge Analytica filed for bankruptcy.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp4_kp4: Disinformation and democratic integrity: online disinformation ('fake news') spreads 6 times faster than accurate news on Twitter (MIT study, 2018). Foreign interference: US intelligence services identified Russian Internet Research Agency operations targeting the 2016 election (systematic fake social media accounts and advertising). UK Intelligence and Security Committee 2020 report: Russia attempted to interfere in Scottish independence referendum and Brexit — UK government did not commission an inquiry into the scale of Russian influence.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp4_insight: In the 1992 UK general election, The Sun newspaper on election day ran the headline 'It's The Sun Wot Won It' after the Conservatives unexpectedly defeated Labour. The paper had vigorously campaigned against Neil Kinnock (Labour leader), including publishing a front page on polling day: 'If Kinnock wins today will the last person to leave Britain please turn out the lights.' Whether media coverage actually changed the election outcome is disputed by political scientists — the evidence for media effects on vote choice (as opposed to media reflecting existing preferences) is mixed. What is clear is that politicians believe media coverage matters enormously, which is why they spend enormous effort cultivating press relationships. Tony Blair's Labour party spent years building a relationship with Rupert Murdoch before the 1997 election (The Sun switched support to Blair from Major). The belief in media power, regardless of its actual size, is itself a political force that shapes how governments and parties behave.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp5: Youth political participation and civic education
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp5_desc: Young people participate in politics at lower rates than older generations in most democracies, but through different channels: protest, social media activism, and issue-based campaigning. Civic education — how schools teach about democracy and participation — is increasingly recognised as a driver of political engagement. Debates about lowering the voting age to 16 are live in many democracies.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp5_kp0: Youth political participation trends: low electoral turnout among young people across most democracies is long-established but not universal. In elections where young people feel the stakes are high (Scottish independence referendum 2014: 75% of 16-17 year olds voted; Brexit 2016: high youth turnout), participation rises sharply. School strikes for climate (Fridays for Future): millions of young people participated globally in 2018-2019. Black Lives Matter (2020): disproportionately young participants.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp5_kp1: Votes at 16: Scotland and Wales allow 16-year-olds to vote in devolved elections (Scottish Parliament, Senedd, local elections). England and Northern Ireland: 18. Austria, Malta, Estonia: 16 for national elections. Germany (some Länder): 16 for local elections. Arguments for: 16-year-olds pay taxes, can marry, join the military — why not vote? Evidence from Scotland shows 16-17 year olds who voted in 2014 were more likely to vote in subsequent elections (habit formation). Arguments against: brain development; susceptibility to manipulation; lack of life experience.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp5_kp2: Civic education: the teaching of how government, law, and democracy work. UK: Citizenship became a statutory subject in English secondary schools in 2002 (following the Crick Report, 1998). Quality is highly variable — many schools underprovide it due to curriculum pressures. PISA civic education assessments: countries that prioritise civic education (Finland, Sweden) show higher political knowledge and reported willingness to participate. The Hansard Society annual Audit of Political Engagement measures UK civic knowledge and engagement.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp5_kp3: Digital citizenship: young people are political in online spaces even when they don't vote. Petitions (38 Degrees, Change.org): millions of signatures; occasionally influence policy. Social media campaigns: #MeToo, #BlackLivesMatter, #SchoolStrike4Climate — all driven substantially by young, digitally-native participants. Risks: echo chambers and filter bubbles may increase polarisation without increasing substantive understanding; online engagement can substitute for more effective forms of participation.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp5_kp4: Greta Thunberg and youth political mobilisation: Greta Thunberg (born 2003) began striking outside the Swedish parliament in August 2018 (age 15), demanding action on climate change. The Fridays for Future movement grew to 4 million participants in 161 countries during the 20 September 2019 global climate strike — likely the largest climate protest in history. Thunberg addressed the UN General Assembly and the Davos World Economic Forum. She demonstrates both the power and the limitations of youth protest: enormous awareness-raising; uncertain policy impact.
learnSch_politics_civics_f2_tp5_insight: The Crick Report (1998), commissioned by New Labour, recommended making citizenship education statutory in UK schools. The chair, philosopher and political theorist Bernard Crick, wrote: 'We aim at no less than a change in the political culture of this country both nationally and locally.' Twenty-five years on, the evidence that compulsory citizenship education has changed political culture is mixed. The Hansard Society's 2023 Audit of Political Engagement found that political knowledge has improved among young people since 2002; but trust in politicians and satisfaction with democracy has declined dramatically across all ages. The paradox is that better-informed citizens may be more, not less, dissatisfied — they know enough to see how far reality falls short of democratic ideals. Civic education may produce engaged critics rather than engaged participants — which may ultimately be healthier for democracy than passive deference, even if it is less comfortable for governments.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp3_desc: Behaviourism, one of psychology's dominant schools through the mid-20th century, explains learning as the acquisition of associations between stimuli and responses. Classical conditioning (Pavlov) and operant conditioning (Skinner) are its two foundational mechanisms. Both have extensive real-world applications in education, therapy, advertising, and animal training.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp3_kp0: Classical conditioning (Pavlov): Ivan Pavlov (1849-1936) discovered classical conditioning while studying dog digestion. Unconditioned stimulus (UCS) — food → unconditioned response (UCR) — salivation. Conditioned stimulus (CS) — bell, repeatedly paired with food → conditioned response (CR) — salivation to bell alone. Key concepts: acquisition (learning the association); extinction (CR diminishes when CS presented without UCS); spontaneous recovery (CR reappears after rest); generalisation (similar stimuli produce CR); discrimination (CR only to specific CS).
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp3_kp1: Operant conditioning (Skinner): B.F. Skinner (1904-1990) studied voluntary behaviour. Reinforcement increases behaviour: positive reinforcement (add pleasant stimulus — praise, food); negative reinforcement (remove unpleasant stimulus — remove pain by taking painkiller). Punishment decreases behaviour: positive punishment (add unpleasant stimulus — detention, pain); negative punishment (remove pleasant stimulus — take away phone). Key: reinforcement is always about increasing behaviour; punishment always about decreasing it.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp3_kp2: Schedules of reinforcement: Skinner found that HOW OFTEN reinforcement is given (the schedule) dramatically affects the pattern of behaviour. Fixed ratio (FR): reinforce after every Nth response — produces high, steady rate; pause after reward. Variable ratio (VR): reinforce after unpredictable number of responses — produces highest, most persistent rates (slot machines, social media likes). Fixed interval (FI): reinforce after fixed time — low rate until time approaches. Variable interval (VI): reinforce after unpredictable time — steady, persistent rate.
