learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp3_kp3: Applications of classical conditioning: phobia development (Little Albert: Watson & Rayner 1920 — conditioned 9-month-old to fear white rat by pairing rat with loud noise). Systematic desensitisation: treats phobias by gradually pairing feared stimulus with relaxation (counter-conditioning). Advertising: product paired with attractive person or emotions (CS paired with emotional UCR). Taste aversion: one trial conditioning — nausea after eating specific food creates lasting aversion (adaptive: helps avoid poison).
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp3_kp4: Evaluation of behaviourism: strengths: scientific, objective, measurable, has practical applications (behaviour modification, token economy systems). Weaknesses: ignores cognitive processes (thoughts, beliefs), ignores biology and genetics, reduces complex human behaviour to simple stimulus-response, cannot explain language acquisition (Chomsky's critique of Skinner), ethical concerns (Little Albert, animal experiments). Cognitive revolution (1950s-60s) displaced pure behaviourism as the dominant paradigm, though behavioural techniques remain clinically valuable.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp3_insigh: B.F. Skinner designed the 'Skinner Box' (operant conditioning chamber) — a soundproof box with a lever, light, and food dispenser — to precisely control and measure rat behaviour. His most ambitious (and notorious) proposal was the 'Air Crib' — a temperature-controlled, glass-fronted box for babies, intended to replace the crib. His daughter Deborah spent her first two years in one. Rumours spread that she had gone mad, sued her father, or committed suicide — all false. She became an artist and co-authored a book defending her father. Skinner's behaviourist utopian novel 'Walden Two' (1948) describes a society run on operant conditioning principles where people are shaped by positive reinforcement into cooperative, happy citizens. It was simultaneously praised as a blueprint for a better society and condemned as a totalitarian nightmare. The debate it provoked — can and should humans shape human behaviour through conditioning? — remains alive in discussions of nudge theory, social media design, and behavioural economics.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp4_desc: Sigmund Freud (1856-1939) founded psychoanalysis — a theory of mind, a theory of psychological development, and a clinical treatment method simultaneously. Despite being widely criticised as unscientific, Freudian concepts (the unconscious, repression, defence mechanisms, Oedipus complex) have profoundly shaped Western culture, literature, and clinical practice.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp4_kp0: The structure of the mind: Freud's topographic model (early): conscious (currently aware), preconscious (accessible by effort), unconscious (inaccessible, repressed content). Structural model (later, 1923): id (entirely unconscious; operates on pleasure principle; instinctive drives — eros/libido and thanatos/death drive); ego (largely conscious; operates on reality principle; mediates between id and external world); superego (largely unconscious; moral conscience, internalised parental rules; source of guilt).
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp4_kp1: Defence mechanisms: the ego protects itself from anxiety produced by conflicts between id impulses and superego demands. Repression: pushing threatening thoughts into the unconscious (the fundamental mechanism). Denial: refusing to acknowledge reality. Projection: attributing your own unacceptable impulses to others. Displacement: redirecting emotion from original target to safer one. Sublimation: channelling unacceptable impulses into socially acceptable activity. Rationalisation: creating logical explanations for irrational behaviour.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp4_kp2: Psychosexual stages: Freud believed personality developed through five stages in which the libido focuses on different erogenous zones. Oral (0-18 months): fixation → dependency, smoking, over-eating. Anal (18 months-3 years): fixation → orderliness or messiness. Phallic (3-6 years): Oedipus/Electra complex — attraction to opposite-sex parent, rivalry with same-sex parent; resolved by identifying with same-sex parent. Latency (6-puberty): sexual impulses dormant. Genital (puberty-adult): mature sexuality.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp4_kp3: Psychoanalytic therapy: free association (patient says whatever comes to mind without censoring — Freud believed this bypasses ego defences to access unconscious). Dream analysis (dreams as 'the royal road to the unconscious' — manifest content = what is dreamed; latent content = hidden symbolic meaning). Transference (patient displaces emotions from past relationships onto the therapist). Insight: making the unconscious conscious. Freud's therapy took years; modern derivatives (psychodynamic therapy) are shorter-term.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp4_kp4: Evaluation of Freud: criticisms: concepts are unfalsifiable (cannot be proved wrong — Karl Popper's criterion of science). Evidence is case studies (unreliable, unrepresentative, retrospective). Sexual theory is culturally specific and sexist. The unconscious as a repository of repressed memories is not supported by cognitive neuroscience. Contributions: emphasised importance of childhood; recognised unconscious information processing; defence mechanisms have empirical support; opened up sexuality as a subject of study; inspired vast cultural production.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp4_insigh: Freud published 'The Interpretation of Dreams' in November 1899 (dated 1900 by his publisher, who wanted it associated with the new century). He initially printed 600 copies; it took eight years to sell out. By the end of the 20th century it had been translated into over 20 languages and was considered one of the most influential books of the century. Freud's influence on the 20th century is extraordinary and paradoxical: his scientific credibility has largely collapsed (meta-analyses of repression find little support; his case studies are now considered methodologically unsound). Yet Freudian vocabulary has permeated everyday language: ego, unconscious, repression, denial, projection, fixation, Freudian slip — all Freud's coinages or popularisations. The poet W.H. Auden wrote on Freud's death: 'To us he is no more a person / Now but a whole climate of opinion.' The concepts live on in culture long after the scientific edifice has crumbled.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp5_desc: Humanistic psychology emerged in the 1950s-60s as a 'third force' against both behaviourism and psychoanalysis. It emphasises human potential, free will, subjective experience, and growth. Maslow's hierarchy of needs and Rogers' person-centred therapy are its central contributions.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp5_kp0: Abraham Maslow (1908-1970) and the hierarchy of needs: human needs are organised hierarchically. Physiological needs (food, water, shelter, sleep) → Safety needs (security, stability) → Love and belonging needs (friendship, intimacy, family) → Esteem needs (self-esteem, achievement, recognition) → Self-actualisation (realising one's full potential, creativity, peak experiences). Lower needs must generally be met before higher needs motivate behaviour. Maslow studied 'exemplary people' (Einstein, Lincoln, Beethoven) to understand self-actualisation.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp5_kp1: Self-actualisation: the highest level of Maslow's hierarchy. Characteristics of self-actualised people: perceive reality accurately; accept themselves and others; are spontaneous and creative; have deep relationships with a few people rather than many superficial ones; are autonomous; have frequent 'peak experiences' (moments of profound transcendence and joy). Maslow estimated fewer than 1% of people achieve full self-actualisation. Critics: very ethnocentric sample; concepts vague and unfalsifiable.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp5_kp2: Carl Rogers (1902-1987) and person-centred therapy: Rogers believed people have an innate drive toward self-actualisation (the 'actualising tendency') but this is blocked by conditions of worth — conditions placed by significant others on when one is loveable. The therapeutic relationship is the key: therapists must provide unconditional positive regard (acceptance without conditions), empathy (accurately understanding the client's experience), and congruence (being genuine). This creates conditions for natural growth.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp5_kp3: The self-concept: Rogers' theory. The self-concept = how a person views themselves. The ideal self = how a person wants to be. Psychological health = congruence between self-concept and ideal self. Incongruence = significant gap between self-concept and ideal self → anxiety, depression, defensive behaviour. Therapy helps clients align self-concept and ideal self through acceptance and honest self-exploration. Rogers argued it is the client, not the therapist, who directs the therapeutic process.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp5_kp4: Evaluation of humanistic psychology: strengths: emphasises subjective experience and free will; more optimistic view of human nature than behaviourism or psychoanalysis; Rogers' therapy has empirical support (meta-analyses show it is effective for depression, anxiety); influenced counselling, education, management, and social work. Weaknesses: concepts like 'self-actualisation' are difficult to define and measure; hierarchy of needs lacks empirical support for the hierarchy itself; may be individualistic and culturally biased.
learnSch_psychology_school_f0_tp5_insigh: Maslow's hierarchy of needs pyramid is one of the most reproduced diagrams in the history of psychology — and also one of the most misrepresented. Maslow never actually drew a pyramid. The pyramid diagram was created by others summarising his work. Cross-cultural research (Tay & Diener, 2011, using data from 123 countries) tested the hierarchy and found that the strict hierarchical order does not hold: people in poverty report feeling love and meaning; people in safety can lack belonging. The hierarchy is a useful heuristic but not an empirically supported sequence. Maslow himself, in his later writings, added a level above self-actualisation: 'self-transcendence' (transcending ego, serving others, spiritual experiences) — a stage that rarely appears in textbook summaries of his work.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp3_desc: Attention is the cognitive process of selectively concentrating on one aspect of the environment while ignoring others. Perception is the process of interpreting sensory information. Both are active, constructive processes — we do not simply receive the world; we actively organise and interpret it. Research in these areas has revolutionised understanding of consciousness.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp3_kp0: Selective attention: the ability to attend to a specific stimulus while ignoring others. Cocktail party effect: the ability to attend to one conversation in a noisy room while filtering others — but if your name is mentioned in another conversation, you notice it (Cherry, 1953). Dichotic listening studies (Broadbent, 1958): different messages played simultaneously to each ear — participants could shadow (repeat) one message while filtering the other. Early vs late selection models debate when irrelevant information is filtered out.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp3_kp1: Inattentional blindness: failure to notice an unexpected stimulus in plain sight due to attention being focused elsewhere. Simons & Chabris (1999) 'invisible gorilla' study: participants watching a video counting basketball passes failed to notice a person in a gorilla suit walking through the scene. ~50% missed the gorilla. Applications: aviation (pilots missing warnings), driving (drivers missing cyclists), surgery. Demonstrates that conscious perception depends on attention, not just sensory input.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp3_kp2: Bottom-up vs top-down perception: bottom-up processing (data-driven): starts with raw sensory data and builds up to perception. Useful for unfamiliar stimuli. Top-down processing (concept-driven): uses prior knowledge, expectations, and context to interpret sensory data. Explains why we can read jumbled text where letters within words are scrambled. Both processes operate simultaneously in normal perception.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp3_kp3: Perceptual set: the tendency to perceive stimuli in a particular way based on expectations, context, motivation, or emotion (Allport, 1955). Context: the same shape is read as B or 13 depending on whether it is in a letter or number sequence. Motivation: hungry people see more food-related images in ambiguous pictures. Emotion: anxious people interpret ambiguous faces as threatening. Culture: Muller-Lyer illusion is stronger for Westernised people than those from non-carpentered environments.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp3_kp4: Divided attention and multitasking: divided attention attempts to attend to two tasks simultaneously. People often believe they can multitask effectively; research consistently shows a dual-task cost — performance declines on both tasks. Multitasking typically involves rapid switching between tasks, not true simultaneous processing. Automatic vs controlled processing: some tasks become automatic with practice (driving, reading) and can run in parallel with controlled tasks, but two controlled-processing tasks cannot truly be done simultaneously.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp3_insigh: The invisible gorilla study by Daniel Simons and Christopher Chabris won the Ig Nobel Prize in 2004. The study revealed something profound: we experience far less of the visual world than we believe we do. Our sense of seeing everything in front of us is an illusion constructed by the brain. Follow-up studies showed that people who had been told about the gorilla study still missed other unexpected stimuli. This is 'change blindness': we often fail to notice even large changes to visual scenes if our attention is directed elsewhere. The implication for everyday life is significant: eyewitness testimony is less reliable than intuition suggests; pilots, surgeons, and drivers may miss critical changes; and our subjective experience of being fully present and attentive is, in many situations, a compelling fiction.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp4_desc: Problem solving and decision making are core cognitive abilities studied extensively in psychology. Research reveals that human reasoning is neither fully rational nor fully irrational, but characterised by systematic biases and heuristics that are often adaptive but sometimes lead to predictable errors. This field, pioneered by Kahneman and Tversky, has transformed economics, policy, and medicine.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp4_kp0: Problem-solving strategies: algorithms: step-by-step procedures that guarantee a solution if followed correctly. Slow but reliable. Heuristics: mental shortcuts that are quick but do not guarantee correct solutions. The availability heuristic: judging likelihood by how easily examples come to mind. The representativeness heuristic: judging likelihood by how similar something is to a prototype. Trial and error: unsystematic, useful for simple problems. Insight: sudden solution ('aha moment') — associated with gamma-band EEG activity.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp4_kp1: Cognitive biases in decision making: confirmation bias: tendency to search for, interpret, and remember information that confirms pre-existing beliefs. Anchoring bias: over-relying on the first piece of information encountered. Availability heuristic: over-weighting examples that come easily to mind (people overestimate frequency of dramatic causes of death — plane crashes, murders — because they are memorable). Sunk cost fallacy: continuing a failing course of action because of past investment. Dunning-Kruger effect: poor performers overestimate their ability; experts underestimate theirs.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp4_kp2: Kahneman's System 1 and System 2 thinking: Daniel Kahneman (Nobel Economics 2002; 'Thinking, Fast and Slow', 2011): System 1 (fast, automatic, intuitive, heuristic, unconscious — most everyday thinking, associative, emotional); System 2 (slow, deliberate, analytical, effortful, conscious — used for maths, logical reasoning, careful decisions). System 1 errors: cognitive biases. System 2 can override System 1 but is cognitively expensive. The bat and ball problem: a bat and ball together cost $1.10; the bat costs $1 more than the ball; how much does the ball cost? System 1 says 10 cents; System 2 says 5 cents.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp4_kp3: Kahneman and Tversky — prospect theory: standard economic theory assumes people are rational maximisers. Kahneman & Tversky (1979) showed people are not. Prospect theory: people evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point; losses loom larger than equivalent gains (loss aversion — losing 100 dollars feels worse than gaining 100 dollars feels good, roughly 2:1 ratio); people are risk-averse for gains but risk-seeking for losses. This explains irrational economic behaviour: selling winning stocks early, holding losing ones too long.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp4_kp4: Barriers to problem solving: mental set: tendency to use a previously successful approach even when not optimal. Functional fixedness: inability to see objects as having uses other than their usual function (Duncker's candle problem). Insight and incubation: some problems are best solved after a break — unconscious processing continues and may produce insights. The Einstellung effect: a dominant solution approach blocks finding a better one.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp4_insigh: The work of Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky on cognitive biases is arguably the most impactful research programme in 20th-century psychology, not just within psychology but across economics, medicine, law, and public policy. Kahneman received the Nobel Prize in Economics in 2002 (Tversky died in 1996; the Nobel is not awarded posthumously). Their insight — that human judgement is characterised by predictable, systematic biases — directly challenged the dominant economic model of the perfectly rational decision-maker. The resulting field of behavioural economics has transformed how governments design policies. The UK Behavioural Insights Team ('Nudge Unit'), set up in 2010, applied these insights to pension enrolment: changing the default option (making enrolment automatic rather than opt-in) increased enrolment from 61% to 83% without changing any incentives. The power of cognitive defaults is one of the most practically important discoveries of 20th-century psychology.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp5: Language and thought: the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp5_desc: The relationship between language and thought is one of psychology's most enduring and contested questions. Does language shape thought? Does thought shape language? Or are they independent? The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis — that language determines or influences thought — has had a controversial history but has been revived by recent empirical evidence.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp5_kp0: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis (linguistic relativity): Edward Sapir and Benjamin Lee Whorf proposed that the language a person speaks influences or determines how they think about reality. Strong form (linguistic determinism): language determines thought — you cannot think what your language cannot express. Now largely rejected. Weak form (linguistic relativity): language influences thought — different languages make different ways of thinking more or less habitual. There is some empirical support for the weak form.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp5_kp1: Evidence for linguistic relativity — colour: Russian has two words for blue (goluboy = light blue; siniy = dark blue) where English has one. Russian speakers are faster at discriminating between light and dark blues at the category boundary than English speakers (Winawer et al., 2007) — but only when the colours span the linguistic boundary. The Dani of Papua New Guinea have only two colour terms (dark/cool and light/warm) but can still distinguish colours — evidence against strong determinism.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp5_kp2: Evidence for linguistic relativity — space: different languages use different reference frames. English: relative/egocentric: left, right, in front, behind (relative to speaker's body). Guugu Yimithirr (Australian Aboriginal): cardinal directions (north, south, east, west) — always, regardless of context. Speakers always know which direction is north and think about space absolutely. This shapes cognition: Guugu Yimithirr speakers navigate differently and maintain a persistent internal compass that English speakers do not.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp5_kp3: Evidence for linguistic relativity — time: English speakers conceptualise time as horizontal (future ahead, past behind). Mandarin speakers often conceptualise time vertically (future below, past above). Aymara speakers (Bolivia/Peru) place the past in front (because it is known/seen) and future behind (because unseen). Lera Boroditsky's research shows these linguistic conventions influence how speakers reason about temporal sequences in non-verbal tasks.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp5_kp4: Language and number: the Pirahã language (studied by Dan Everett) has no number words beyond approximate terms. In tasks requiring exact number matching, Pirahã speakers perform much worse than speakers of languages with precise number words. The Mundurukú (Amazonian) have number words only to 5; performance on exact arithmetic tasks drops beyond this. This suggests that precise numerical thought may be facilitated by precise number words — a possible case of linguistic determinism for a specific cognitive domain.
learnSch_psychology_school_f1_tp5_insigh: Dan Everett's research on the Pirahã language is one of the most controversial in linguistics and cognitive science. Everett, a former Christian missionary who went to the Amazon to convert the Pirahã, instead converted himself into a linguist and eventually an atheist after learning their language and culture. He claimed that Pirahã violates fundamental properties that Noam Chomsky argued are universal to all human languages: it has no recursion (no embedded clauses), no colour terms, no numbers, no creation myths, no fiction. If true, this would fundamentally challenge the 'Universal Grammar' hypothesis — the idea that all human languages share a common deep structure hardwired into the human brain. Chomsky and his followers disputed Everett's analysis vigorously. The debate remains unresolved and continues to animate linguistics and cognitive science.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp3_desc: Social psychology studies how individuals' thoughts, feelings, and behaviours are influenced by others. Majority influence (conformity) was studied extensively by Asch; minority influence — how small groups can change majority opinion — was studied by Moscovici. Understanding both directions of social influence is essential for understanding social change.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp3_kp0: Asch conformity studies (1951-56): Solomon Asch showed participants a line and asked which of three comparison lines matched it. The answer was obvious — but confederates (actors) unanimously gave the wrong answer. Results: approximately 75% of participants conformed at least once; 25% never conformed; the overall conformity rate across trials was 37%. Why conform? Normative social influence (wanting to be liked/accepted — social pressure); informational social influence (believing others know better). Variables: group size, unanimity (dissenter reduces conformity dramatically), task difficulty, culture.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp3_kp1: Types of conformity: compliance: outwardly going along while privately disagreeing (most common in Asch). Identification: conforming to a group one wishes to be part of (real change while in the group, less stable). Internalisation: genuine acceptance of group norms (deepest, most permanent; occurs with informational influence). Asch's study was largely about compliance — participants privately maintained correct answers but didn't want to be different.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp3_kp2: Moscovici and minority influence: Serge Moscovici (1925-2014) showed that minorities CAN influence majorities if they behave consistently. Blue-green colour naming study (1969): 6 participants, 4 naive, 2 confederates. Slides shown (blue) — confederates consistently said 'green'. In the consistent condition, 8.42% of naive participants agreed (compared to 1.25% in inconsistent condition). More importantly, in a subsequent test, participants exposed to the consistent minority showed a private shift towards seeing blue-green colours differently.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp3_kp3: Conditions for minority influence effectiveness: consistency: the minority must maintain its position consistently over time and members must agree with each other. Commitment: willingness to suffer for the position (increases credibility). Flexibility: being open to dialogue without changing core position. Conversion effect: minority influence tends to produce private attitude change (internalisation) rather than public compliance — the opposite of majority influence.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp3_kp4: Social influence and social change: minority influence is the mechanism through which social change occurs. Historical examples: suffragettes (consistent, committed minority — eventually changed majority views on women's voting rights), civil rights movement, LGBTQ+ rights movement, environmental movement. In each case, a consistent, committed minority maintained a position over time, produced a snowball effect (converting individuals who then influenced others), and eventually shifted majority opinion and law.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp3_insigh: Solomon Asch's conformity experiments were conducted in 1951 and published in a paper that has been cited over 7,000 times. Asch himself was disturbed by his results — not because 37% of responses were conforming but because participants experienced genuine distress. They knew the majority was wrong, yet they yielded. Asch's research was partly motivated by his personal experience of the Holocaust — he wanted to understand how ordinary people could participate in atrocities under group pressure. The same question motivated Stanley Milgram, Asch's student, whose obedience experiments (1960s) showed that 65% of participants would administer what they believed to be potentially lethal electric shocks when instructed by an authority figure. Together, Asch and Milgram documented the extraordinary power of social forces to override individual conscience and judgement — findings that remain as unsettling today as when first published.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp4_desc: Prejudice (attitudes) and discrimination (behaviour) based on group membership — race, gender, age, religion, sexuality, disability — are among the most socially consequential psychological phenomena. Modern psychology distinguishes explicit (conscious, deliberately held) biases from implicit (unconscious, automatic) biases, with different implications for how they can be changed.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp4_kp0: Definitions: prejudice = a preconceived negative attitude toward a group or its members, not based on direct experience or evidence. Discrimination = differential treatment of a person on the basis of group membership. Stereotypes = oversimplified, generalised beliefs about groups (cognitive component). All three are related but distinct: one can hold a prejudice without discriminating (attitude-behaviour gap); one can discriminate without conscious prejudice (institutional discrimination); one can hold a stereotype without prejudice.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp4_kp1: Explanations for prejudice: realistic conflict theory (Sherif, Robbers Cave 1954): prejudice arises from real competition for scarce resources. Superordinate goals (shared goals requiring cooperation) reduced prejudice between groups. Social identity theory (Tajfel & Turner, 1979): people categorise themselves into ingroups and outgroups and enhance self-esteem by viewing the ingroup favourably and the outgroup negatively. Even trivial, arbitrary group divisions (minimal group paradigm) produce ingroup favouritism. Authoritarian personality (Adorno et al., 1950): prejudice associated with a rigid, conventional, submissive-to-authority personality.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp4_kp2: Implicit bias: the Implicit Association Test (IAT) (Greenwald, McGhee & Schwartz, 1998) measures the strength of automatic associations between concepts (e.g., Black/White, male/female) and evaluations (good/bad) or stereotypes (intelligent/athletic). People respond faster when the pairings match their implicit associations. Most US White participants show a faster association of Black faces with negative words. Similar results across many countries. Project Implicit (Harvard) has collected over 30 million tests showing widespread implicit biases.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp4_kp3: Reducing prejudice: contact hypothesis (Allport, 1954): contact between groups reduces prejudice WHEN groups have equal status in the contact situation; common goals; intergroup cooperation; support of authorities. Jigsaw classroom (Aronson, 1978): students in cooperative mixed-race learning groups where each member has unique information needed by others — reduced prejudice and improved performance among minority students. Reading fiction has been shown to reduce implicit bias by increasing empathy.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp4_kp4: Institutional discrimination: discrimination can operate through institutional structures and practices even without individual prejudice. Macpherson Report (1999, following the Stephen Lawrence murder investigation): defined institutional racism as 'the collective failure of an organisation to provide an appropriate and professional service to people because of their colour, culture or ethnic origin.' CV studies show identical CVs with White-sounding names receive more callbacks than Black-sounding names. Algorithm bias: facial recognition software has higher error rates for darker-skinned faces.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp4_insigh: The Robbers Cave experiment (Sherif et al., 1954) is one of the most ethically controversial experiments in psychology. Twenty-two 11-year-old boys were taken to a summer camp in Oklahoma, unaware they were part of an experiment. Groups formed separately, then put in competition — hostility, name-calling, raids, and physical conflict emerged rapidly. Superordinate goals (broken water supply, bus stuck in mud requiring cooperation) reduced conflict. The experiment seemed to confirm realistic conflict theory perfectly. Later analyses (based on Sherif's unpublished notes) revealed that the experimenters intervened repeatedly to provoke conflict when it failed to emerge naturally — the boys were less naturally aggressive than the published results suggested. A powerful lesson in how the published record of an experiment can differ from what actually happened — and why replication and transparency are essential in science.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp5_desc: Pro-social behaviour refers to voluntary actions intended to benefit others: helping, sharing, donating, cooperating, volunteering. Altruism is helping motivated by genuine concern for others' welfare rather than self-interest. Why humans help strangers, and why they sometimes fail to help, are central questions of social psychology with important real-world implications.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp5_kp0: The bystander effect: Darley & Latane (1968), prompted by the murder of Kitty Genovese in New York (1964). Experimental simulation: participants hearing another person apparently having an epileptic seizure. When alone: 85% helped within 6 minutes. With 4 others present: 31% helped. Why? Diffusion of responsibility (each assumes another will help); pluralistic ignorance (others' calm reaction convinces each that it is not an emergency); evaluation apprehension (fear of embarrassing oneself if response seems an overreaction).
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp5_kp1: Arousal-cost-reward model (Piliavin et al., 1981): witnessing an emergency produces arousal (distress). People assess costs and benefits of helping and not helping. Costs of helping: personal danger, time, disgust. Costs of NOT helping: guilt, social disapproval, distress at seeing victim suffer. Benefits of helping: satisfaction, gratitude, social approval. When costs of helping are low and costs of not helping are high, helping is most likely. Explains why people are less likely to help someone who appears drunk (high disgust cost) than someone who appears ill.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp5_kp2: Altruism vs self-interest: negative state relief model (Cialdini): helping relieves the helper's own distress — therefore ultimately selfish. Empathy-altruism hypothesis (Batson): high empathy produces genuinely altruistic motivation (helping for the other's sake, not to relieve own distress). Batson's experiments: when empathy was high, people helped even when they could easily escape from the situation (reducing self-interested motivation). Debate continues between evolutionary, psychological, and philosophical perspectives.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp5_kp3: Evolutionary explanations for pro-social behaviour: kin selection (Hamilton, 1964): helping genetic relatives increases inclusive fitness (shared genes). 'Altruism' toward relatives is ultimately genetic self-interest. Reciprocal altruism (Trivers, 1971): helping non-relatives when there is likelihood of future reciprocation. Costly signalling: conspicuous altruism (public donations, visible charity work) signals quality as a mate or ally. These evolutionary explanations reduce altruism to disguised self-interest at the genetic level.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp5_kp4: Situational factors in helping: Good Samaritan study (Darley & Batson, 1973): seminary students asked to give a talk. En route, they encountered a man slumped in a doorway. The only significant predictor of helping: whether the student was in a hurry (60% of unhurried vs 10% of hurried helped). The topic of the talk (including the Good Samaritan parable itself) made no difference. Situational pressures (time, anonymity, crowd size) are stronger predictors of helping than personality or moral reasoning.
learnSch_psychology_school_f2_tp5_insigh: The Kitty Genovese case — which directly inspired Darley and Latane's bystander research — is based on a partly false account. The original New York Times story (1964) claimed 38 witnesses watched without calling police. Subsequent investigation found that only a few witnesses saw parts of the attack; at least one witness did call police; the attack occurred in multiple stages and was not continuously witnessed. The 38 passive witnesses were, to a significant extent, a journalistic invention. Yet the social psychological research it inspired — the bystander effect — is robust and replicates across hundreds of studies worldwide. This is one of psychology's most interesting methodological ironies: a major research programme, producing valid and important findings, was inspired by a media account that was substantially inaccurate. The bystander effect is real even though the specific case that inspired it was misreported.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp3: Formation of the solar system: nebular hypothesis
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp3_desc: The nebular hypothesis explains how the solar system formed from a rotating cloud of gas and dust approximately 4.6 billion years ago. Understanding this process reveals why planets orbit in the same plane, why terrestrial planets are rocky and gas giants are huge, and what conditions are required for planetary system formation.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp3_kp0: Nebular hypothesis (Kant-Laplace): a rotating cloud of gas and dust (solar nebula) contracted under gravity. Conservation of angular momentum caused it to spin faster and flatten into a disc (protoplanetary disc). The centre heated to become the proto-Sun. Material in the disc collided and accreted into planetesimals, then protoplanets, then planets.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp3_kp1: Differentiation: as the proto-Earth accreted, collisions released heat. The interior melted; denser materials (iron, nickel) sank to form the core; lighter silicates floated to form the mantle and crust. This explains why Earth has a layered structure. Similar differentiation occurred in all rocky planets.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp3_kp2: Frost line: the boundary (approximately 2.7 AU from the Sun) beyond which water, methane, and ammonia could freeze. Interior: only high-temperature minerals could condense — rocky/metallic planets. Exterior: ice added to solid building blocks, enabling gas giants to grow large enough to gravitationally capture hydrogen and helium from the nebula. This explains the rocky/gas giant divide.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp3_kp3: Late Heavy Bombardment (c. 4.1-3.8 billion years ago): a period of intense asteroid/comet bombardment, evidenced by lunar crater counts. Thought to have been triggered by gravitational instabilities as Jupiter and Saturn migrated outward. Water delivery to early Earth: much of Earth's water may have arrived via icy asteroids and comets during this period.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp3_kp4: Moon formation (giant impact hypothesis): the Moon formed approximately 4.5 billion years ago when a Mars-sized body (Theia) collided with early Earth. Ejected material coalesced to form the Moon. Evidence: lunar rocks have the same isotopic composition as Earth's mantle (distinguishing it from captured asteroid); the Moon's low density suggests iron-poor composition (Theia's core merged with Earth's).
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp3_insight: In 2022, the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) directly imaged protoplanetary discs around young stars in the Orion Nebula — solar system formation in progress, 1,344 light-years away. JWST can detect the infrared signature of molecules in these discs, including water and carbon-containing compounds. It identified a disc around a young star with a gap (possibly carved by a forming planet) at the distance where Earth-like planets could form with liquid water. We can now observe solar system formation in real time and test the nebular hypothesis against direct observation rather than inference from our own solar system. The Webb telescope has transformed astronomy's ability to study planet formation within a single year of operation.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp4: The Moon: tides, phases, and lunar geology
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp4_desc: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite, 384,400 km away — a distance so precise that a laser bounced off retroreflectors left by Apollo astronauts returns in exactly 2.5 seconds. It governs Earth's tides, stabilises Earth's axial tilt, and has driven human timekeeping since the earliest civilisations.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp4_kp0: Tides: the Moon's gravitational field is slightly stronger on Earth's near side than its far side. This differential (tidal force) pulls the oceans into two bulges — one facing the Moon, one on the opposite side. As Earth rotates, these bulges sweep around — producing two high tides and two low tides per day (approximately). Spring tides (Moon-Earth-Sun aligned): larger; neap tides (Moon-Earth-Sun at right angle): smaller.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp4_kp1: Lunar phases: the Moon does not emit light; it reflects sunlight. As the Moon orbits Earth (27.3 days sidereal period; 29.5 days synodic — new moon to new moon), we see different proportions of the illuminated hemisphere. New moon → waxing crescent → first quarter → waxing gibbous → full moon → waning gibbous → last quarter → waning crescent → new moon.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp4_kp2: Synchronous rotation: the Moon's rotation period equals its orbital period (both 27.3 days). This means the same hemisphere always faces Earth. The far side is not the 'dark side' — it receives as much sunlight as the near side — but it was never seen by humans until the Soviet Luna 3 probe photographed it in 1959. Synchronous rotation results from tidal locking — tidal forces have slowed the Moon's rotation over billions of years.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp4_kp3: Lunar geology: the Moon has highlands (pale, heavily cratered ancient crust) and maria (dark, smooth plains of solidified lava, 3-4 billion years old). No plate tectonics; no significant atmosphere; extreme temperature range (−73°C at night, +127°C in daytime). Permanently shadowed craters near poles contain water ice (confirmed by LCROSS impactor, 2009).
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp4_kp4: Lunar exploration: Apollo programme (1969-1972) landed 12 astronauts on the Moon; returned 382 kg of lunar samples. Apollo 11 (Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, July 1969) — first landing. Modern missions: Chandrayaan-3 (India, 2023) — first landing near lunar south pole; Artemis programme (NASA, target late 2020s) aims to return humans to the Moon, including the first woman and first non-white astronaut.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp4_insight: On the night of 20 July 1969, an estimated 600 million people (one fifth of humanity) watched Neil Armstrong step onto the Moon — the largest simultaneous global television audience in history to that point. The journey required a Saturn V rocket generating 34 million newtons of thrust, 400,000 engineers and scientists, and a guidance computer with less processing power than a modern digital watch. The onboard Apollo Guidance Computer (AGC) had 4 KB of RAM and 72 KB of read-only memory. At the critical final 30 seconds of descent, the computer was showing overload alarms ('1202 error' — executive overflow) that the 26-year-old programmer Margaret Hamilton had anticipated and programmed to handle correctly: the computer prioritised the landing task and kept working. Hamilton's decision to include error recovery in the flight software saved the mission. The software concept she developed — priority scheduling and error recovery — became foundational to modern software engineering.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp5: Space exploration: missions and future travel
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp5_desc: Space exploration has transformed human understanding of the cosmos, Earth, and our place in the universe. From Sputnik (1957) to the James Webb Space Telescope (2021), each mission has pushed the boundaries of technology and knowledge. Future exploration — Mars, Europa, the outer solar system — will define 21st-century science.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp5_kp0: Milestones: 1957 Sputnik (first satellite), 1961 Gagarin (first human in space), 1969 Apollo 11 (first Moon landing), 1977 Voyager 1 and 2 launched (now in interstellar space), 1990 Hubble Space Telescope, 1998 International Space Station, 2004 Mars rovers Spirit and Opportunity, 2015 New Horizons (Pluto flyby), 2021 JWST launch.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp5_kp1: Types of missions: flyby (no orbit, brief close encounter — New Horizons at Pluto), orbiter (sustained orbit — Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter), lander (surface landing without mobility — Viking, InSight), rover (mobile surface vehicle — Curiosity, Perseverance), sample return (bring material back to Earth — Apollo, Hayabusa2, OSIRIS-REx).
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp5_kp2: Mars exploration: Mars is the most explored planet after Earth. Current: Perseverance rover (2021–) collecting samples for future return; Ingenuity helicopter; MAVEN orbiter; ESA’s Trace Gas Orbiter. Evidence of past liquid water: dried riverbeds, delta deposits, ancient lake beds. Search for biosignatures — chemical signatures of past or present life.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp5_kp3: Human spaceflight challenges: radiation exposure (cosmic rays, solar particle events — Mars transit would expose astronauts to equivalent of several years of Earth surface radiation), muscle and bone loss in microgravity (without countermeasures, 1-2% bone density per month), psychological isolation (communication delay to Mars: 4-24 minutes one way), food and water recycling over 2+ year missions.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp5_kp4: Commercial spaceflight: SpaceX Falcon 9 (reusable first stage, $2,700/kg to orbit vs $54,500/kg on Space Shuttle), Starship (designed for Mars with 100-person capacity), Starlink (satellite internet). Blue Origin (Jeff Bezos), Virgin Galactic (Richard Branson), Rocket Lab. Commercial sector now accounts for majority of orbital launches. Launch costs have fallen by factor of 20 in 15 years.
learnSch_astronomy_f0_tp5_insight: Voyager 1, launched on 5 September 1977, crossed into interstellar space in August 2012 — the first human-made object to do so. It is currently approximately 24 billion km from Earth (about 160 AU). Its radio signals, travelling at the speed of light, take over 22 hours to reach Earth. It operates on 70 watts of power (less than a light bulb). NASA engineers still communicate with it using the Deep Space Network's 70-metre dish antennas. It carries a gold record with sounds and images of Earth, curated by a committee chaired by Carl Sagan, in case it is ever intercepted by an extraterrestrial intelligence. Mathematically, given the distances involved and the age of the galaxy, the probability of interception is vanishingly small. But we sent it anyway. It represents the longest continuous operation of any spacecraft in history — and the farthest any human-made object has ever travelled.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp3: Nuclear fusion in stars: the proton-proton chain
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp3_desc: Stars are nuclear fusion reactors. In the Sun's core, hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium, releasing energy that has powered Earth for 4.6 billion years. Understanding stellar fusion is fundamental to astrophysics, and the quest to replicate it on Earth — as an essentially unlimited clean energy source — is one of the great scientific challenges.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp3_kp0: Why fusion occurs: in the Sun's core, temperature is 15 million K and density is 150 times water. At these conditions, hydrogen nuclei (protons) move fast enough to overcome electrostatic repulsion and interact via the strong nuclear force. Below the core, there is insufficient temperature and pressure. Fusion energy released: E = mc² — the mass of the helium product is slightly less than the input hydrogen; the missing mass is converted to energy.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp3_kp1: Proton-proton chain (main sequence stars like the Sun): Step 1: p + p → deuterium (²H) + positron + neutrino (this step takes ~9 billion years on average for any given proton). Step 2: ²H + p → helium-3 (³He) + gamma ray (seconds). Step 3: ³He + ³He → helium-4 (⁴He) + 2 protons (occurs after both step 2 have happened). Net result: 4 protons → 1 helium-4 + energy.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp3_kp2: Stellar luminosity and hydrostatic equilibrium: the outward pressure from fusion energy exactly balances the inward gravitational pressure — hydrostatic equilibrium. This is what makes a star stable rather than collapsing or exploding. When hydrogen is depleted in the core, equilibrium fails: the star evolves. Mass determines the fusion rate: more massive stars burn hotter and faster and live shorter lives.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp3_kp3: Solar neutrinos: the proton-proton chain produces vast numbers of neutrinos. The Sun emits 2×10³⁶ neutrinos per second. 65 billion pass through every square centimetre of your body every second. They barely interact with matter — detecting them required the Sudbury Neutrino Observatory built 2km underground in Canadian nickel mines (lined with ultra-pure water). The 'solar neutrino problem' (detected fewer than predicted) was solved by discovering neutrino oscillations.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp3_kp4: Fusion energy on Earth: tokamak reactors (ITER, under construction in France — the largest scientific collaboration in history) aim to achieve net energy gain from deuterium-tritium fusion. Ignition was achieved in December 2022 by the National Ignition Facility (NIF, USA) — more energy out than laser energy in — but not yet more than total electrical energy input. Fusion produces no CO₂, minimal radioactive waste, and uses hydrogen isotopes from seawater. It has been '30 years away' for 70 years.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp3_insight: Every second, the Sun converts 600 million tonnes of hydrogen into 596 million tonnes of helium. The 4 million tonne difference is converted to energy via E = mc² — about 3.8 × 10²⁶ watts. Earth receives approximately 1/2.2 billion of this output. Of that, human civilisation currently uses approximately 18 terawatts of primary energy. This means the Sun provides in about 90 minutes as much energy as humanity uses in a year. The Sun has been fusing at this rate for 4.6 billion years and has enough hydrogen remaining for another 5 billion years. When we measure the energy available for civilisation, we are not close to any fundamental limit imposed by physics — we are limited only by our ability to collect and convert what is already arriving.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp4_desc: Neutron stars are the collapsed cores of massive stars after supernova explosions, containing the mass of 1-2 Suns compressed into a sphere 20 km across. They are the densest objects in the universe short of black holes, and they exhibit some of the most extreme physics known: gravity a billion times Earth's, magnetic fields a trillion times stronger, and rotation rates up to 716 times per second.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp4_kp0: Formation: a massive star (>8 solar masses) exhausts its nuclear fuel. The core collapses in fractions of a second; the outer layers are ejected as a supernova. In the collapse, electrons and protons are forced together to form neutrons (inverse beta decay: p + e⁻ → n + νₑ). Neutron degeneracy pressure — quantum mechanical resistance to further compression — halts collapse. Result: neutron star.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp4_kp1: Properties: mass 1-2 solar masses; radius ~10-12 km; density ~10¹⁷ kg/m³ (one teaspoon ~one billion tonnes); surface gravity 2×10¹¹ m/s² (200 billion g); escape velocity ~half the speed of light; magnetic field 10⁸-10¹µ Tesla (Earth: 5×10⁻⁵ Tesla); temperature newly formed: 10¹¹ K.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp4_kp2: Pulsars: rapidly rotating neutron stars that emit narrow beams of electromagnetic radiation from their magnetic poles. If the beam sweeps past Earth, we detect regular radio pulses (like a lighthouse). Pulsar PSR J1748-2446ad rotates 716 times per second — its equator moves at 24% of the speed of light. Pulsars are the most accurate natural clocks in the universe.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp4_kp3: Magnetars: a subset of neutron stars with ultra-strong magnetic fields (10¹⁴-10¹⁵ Tesla). The strongest magnetic forces in the known universe. Their magnetic energy can cause starquakes — sudden crust displacements releasing energy as gamma-ray flares. On 27 December 2004, the SGR 1806-20 magnetar released in 0.2 seconds more energy than the Sun emits in 250,000 years. The burst was so intense it partially ionised Earth's upper atmosphere from 50,000 light-years away.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp4_kp4: Gravitational wave detection (LIGO/Virgo): neutron star mergers produce gravitational waves (ripples in spacetime) detectable on Earth. GW170817 (2017): first multi-messenger event — gravitational waves + gamma-ray burst + optical transient detected simultaneously. This merger confirmed neutron stars are the origin of heavy elements (gold, platinum, uranium) — created in the collision's r-process nucleosynthesis.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp4_insight: Jocelyn Bell Burnell was a 24-year-old PhD student at Cambridge when she discovered the first pulsar in 1967, using a radio telescope she had helped build. She noticed a regular pulse every 1.337 seconds — so regular that she and her supervisor Antony Hewish initially designated it 'LGM-1' (Little Green Men 1) as a half-joke about extraterrestrial origin. It was the first pulsar — and evidence of neutron stars, which had been purely theoretical. The discovery is considered one of the most important in 20th-century astrophysics. In 1974, the Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded for the discovery — to Antony Hewish and Martin Ryle. Jocelyn Bell Burnell was not included. The omission was widely criticised as one of the most egregious Nobel oversights in history. She has since won virtually every other major prize in science, including the $3 million Breakthrough Prize in 2018, which she donated entirely to fund scholarships for underrepresented groups in physics.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp5: Exoplanets and the search for life
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp5_desc: Exoplanets are planets orbiting stars other than the Sun. Since the first confirmed detection in 1992, over 5,500 have been catalogued. The search for potentially habitable exoplanets — and for biosignatures — is one of the most compelling frontiers of modern astronomy and astrobiology.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp5_kp0: Detection methods: transit photometry — planet crosses in front of star, causing tiny dip in brightness (Kepler Space Telescope; TESS). Radial velocity — planet's gravity causes star to 'wobble' toward and away from us; detected as Doppler shift in starlight. Direct imaging — only possible for large planets far from bright stars (rare). Gravitational microlensing — planet's gravity bends light from background star.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp5_kp1: Habitable zone: the range of orbital distances from a star where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface — given suitable atmospheric pressure. Also called the 'Goldilocks zone'. Not a guarantee of habitability: Mars is in the Sun's habitable zone but has a thin atmosphere and no liquid surface water. Venus is near the inner edge but has a runaway greenhouse effect.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp5_kp2: TRAPPIST-1 system: a cool red dwarf 39 light-years away with 7 Earth-sized planets, three in the habitable zone. Discovered 2017. JWST has begun atmospheric characterisation of TRAPPIST-1 planets. Results so far suggest TRAPPIST-1c (rocky, Venus-like) has no substantial atmosphere. TRAPPIST-1e and TRAPPIST-1f remain prime candidates for further study.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp5_kp3: Biosignatures: chemical signatures that would indicate biological activity if detected in a planetary atmosphere. Oxygen/ozone — produced by photosynthesis (oxygen is highly reactive and must be continuously replenished). Methane + oxygen together — they react, so co-presence requires continuous production from biology. Phosphine in Venus's atmosphere (2020 claim — disputed): no known geological source; could be biological.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp5_kp4: Fermi Paradox: if intelligent life is common, where is everybody? The universe is 13.8 billion years old; there are 200-400 billion stars in the Milky Way; many with habitable planets. Yet no confirmed extraterrestrial intelligence has been detected. Explanations: Great Filter (life/intelligence is very rare or civilisations self-destruct); we are early (not enough time); they are present but we are not looking correctly; interstellar travel is impossible.
learnSch_astronomy_f1_tp5_insight: On 15 August 1977, astronomer Jerry Ehman, scanning data from Ohio State University's Big Ear radio telescope, circled a sequence of numbers and wrote 'Wow!' in the margin. The signal — now known as the Wow! signal — was a strong, narrowband radio burst at 1420 MHz (the hydrogen frequency) lasting 72 seconds, consistent with what a signal of extraterrestrial intelligent origin might look like. It has never been detected again despite searches on the same frequency. 45 years later, in 2022, an amateur astronomer proposed it originated from a hydrogen cloud near two sun-like stars. The signal remains unexplained. SETI (Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence) has been scanning radio frequencies for 60+ years. The fact that the universe's 13.8-billion-year history has had time to produce intelligence on Earth suggests it has had time to produce intelligence elsewhere. The absence of confirmed detection in 60 years of listening is itself a profound scientific result.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp3_desc: The cosmic microwave background (CMB) is thermal radiation left over from the early universe, approximately 380,000 years after the Big Bang. It is the oldest light in the universe, reaching us from across 46 billion light-years. Its precise measurement has made modern cosmology an empirical science with extraordinary precision.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp3_kp0: Origin: for the first 380,000 years after the Big Bang, the universe was too hot for neutral atoms to form. Free electrons scattered photons in all directions — the universe was opaque, like a dense fog. When the universe cooled to ~3,000 K, electrons and protons combined to form neutral hydrogen (recombination). The universe became transparent, and photons streamed freely for the first time.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp3_kp1: Discovery (Penzias and Wilson, 1965): working on a radio telescope at Bell Labs, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson detected persistent microwave noise in all directions regardless of where they pointed. After ruling out all other sources (including pigeon droppings in the antenna), they concluded it was a genuine cosmic signal. It matched the theoretically predicted thermal afterglow of the Big Bang. Nobel Prize 1978.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp3_kp2: Temperature and isotropy: the CMB has a temperature of 2.725 K — almost uniform in all directions. But tiny temperature fluctuations (about 1 part in 100,000) map the density variations in the early universe that gravity later amplified into galaxies and galaxy clusters. The Planck satellite (2009-2013) mapped the CMB with extraordinary precision.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp3_kp3: What the CMB tells us: age of the universe (13.8 billion years), geometry of the universe (flat), composition (ordinary matter 5%, dark matter 27%, dark energy 68%), baryon acoustic oscillations (sound waves in early universe imprinted on CMB and galaxy distribution). The CMB is the most information-rich cosmological dataset available.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp3_kp4: Inflation: tiny quantum fluctuations in the inflaton field, amplified by cosmic inflation (10⁻³⁶ to 10⁻³² seconds), seeded the density variations visible in the CMB. The pattern of fluctuations is consistent with inflation predictions. Detecting primordial gravitational waves in CMB polarisation (B-modes) would be the 'smoking gun' of inflation — the quest for which drove the disputed BICEP2 announcement of 2014.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp3_insight: The Planck satellite (2009-2013) measured the CMB with such precision that cosmologists could determine the age of the universe to 13.80 ± 0.02 billion years — an uncertainty of 0.1%. From the pattern of temperature fluctuations, they could determine that the universe contains 4.9% ordinary matter, 26.8% dark matter, and 68.3% dark energy — meaning that everything ever observed by any telescope, every atom, every star, every galaxy, constitutes less than 5% of the total content of the universe. This is the most precisely measured cosmic composition in history. We are, as Carl Sagan said, made of star stuff — but star stuff constitutes a tiny minority of what the universe actually is. The nature of the other 95% (dark matter and dark energy) remains completely unknown.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp4_desc: The ultimate fate of the universe depends on its geometry, composition, and the nature of dark energy. Current observations point to an accelerating expansion driven by dark energy, suggesting the universe will continue expanding forever. The eventual heat death of the universe — maximum entropy, no usable energy — is among the most philosophically challenging scientific conclusions.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp4_kp0: Expansion of the universe: Hubble (1929) discovered that galaxies are receding from us at velocities proportional to their distance (Hubble's Law: v = H₀d). The Hubble constant H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc. The universe is expanding in all directions simultaneously — like raisins in rising bread — not from a central point. Space itself is expanding, not galaxies moving through space.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp4_kp1: Accelerating expansion: in 1998, two independent teams measuring Type Ia supernova brightness (standard candles) found distant supernovae were dimmer than expected — the expansion is accelerating. The cause: dark energy (cosmological constant Λ — a property of space itself). Nobel Prize 2011 (Saul Perlmutter, Brian Schmidt, Adam Riess).
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp4_kp2: Possible fates: Big Freeze (heat death, most likely given current data) — expansion continues, stars exhaust fuel, black holes evaporate (Hawking radiation, ~10¹⁰⁰ years), universe reaches maximum entropy. Big Rip — if dark energy strengthens over time, expansion tears apart galaxies, stars, atoms. Big Crunch — if gravity eventually overcomes expansion (ruled out by current data). Bounce — Big Crunch followed by new Big Bang.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp4_kp3: Stellar evolution endpoints: Sun will become a red giant (in ~5 billion years), expand to engulf Mercury and Venus, possibly Earth, then collapse to a white dwarf (Earth-sized but Sun-mass). More massive stars end as neutron stars or black holes. All stars will have exhausted their fuel within ~100 trillion years. The universe's age in terms of stellar activity is cosmologically very young.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp4_kp4: Hawking radiation: Stephen Hawking (1974) theoretically demonstrated that black holes are not truly black — quantum effects near the event horizon cause them to emit thermal radiation and slowly evaporate. A stellar-mass black hole would take 2×10⁶⁶ years to evaporate. When all black holes have evaporated, the universe will consist only of photons, neutrinos, and an ever-expanding, ever-cooling void.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp4_insight: In 1854, physicist Hermann von Helmholtz and later Lord Kelvin estimated the Sun's energy source as gravitational contraction and concluded the Sun could be at most 20-40 million years old. This directly contradicted Darwin's geological estimate (hundreds of millions of years required for evolution) and geologists' estimates from rock strata. For 50 years, this was the most serious scientific argument against evolution. The problem was not resolved until the discovery of nuclear physics in the 20th century — fusion had not yet been conceived as a possibility. The Sun's true age (4.6 billion years) required physics beyond anything available to the 19th century. The episode illustrates that scientific contradictions between well-established fields are often resolved by a third discovery that transforms the terms of the debate, rather than one field being simply wrong.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp5: Multiverse theories and the fine-tuned universe
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp5_desc: The fundamental constants of physics appear to be fine-tuned for the existence of complex structures, chemistry, and life. If they were slightly different, no atoms, stars, or life would exist. Multiverse theories propose that our universe is one of many, with different constants in each — explaining fine-tuning without invoking design. These ideas sit at the intersection of cosmology, philosophy, and the limits of scientific testability.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp5_kp0: Fine-tuning: the laws of physics contain ~30 fundamental constants (speed of light, Planck's constant, gravitational constant, electron mass, etc.) whose values appear precisely calibrated for the universe to contain stars, planets, and life. Example: if the strong nuclear force were 2% stronger, no hydrogen would exist; if 2% weaker, no elements heavier than hydrogen would form — no chemistry, no life.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp5_kp1: Three responses to fine-tuning: 1. Design (anthropic theism): the universe was created with these constants by a creator. 2. Multiverse (anthropic selection): there are vast numbers of universes with different constants; we necessarily find ourselves in one compatible with our existence. 3. It is not actually fine-tuned: different constants might support different forms of complexity we cannot imagine.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp5_kp2: Eternal inflation multiverse (Linde): cosmic inflation, once started, may produce 'bubble universes' continuously. Each bubble has different physical constants determined by quantum fluctuations. We inhabit one bubble. String theory landscape: string theory permits ~10µ⁰⁰ possible sets of physical laws. If all are realised in different universes, fine-tuning requires no explanation.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp5_kp3: Anthropic principle: weak anthropic principle — we can only observe a universe compatible with our existence. This is a logical tautology (we are here, so we are here) but has explanatory content in a multiverse: of all regions, we inhabit one where observers can exist. Strong anthropic principle (Barrow and Tipler) — the universe must have properties allowing life to develop — is more controversial and often rejected as unfalsifiable.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp5_kp4: Scientific controversy: multiverse theories are criticised as unscientific because other universes are in principle unobservable and unfalsifiable (violating Popper's criterion). Defenders: testable predictions exist (inflation predicts the CMB power spectrum, confirmed); the string landscape may be constrained by consistency conditions. The debate concerns the appropriate boundary of scientific explanation.
learnSch_astronomy_f2_tp5_insight: Fred Hoyle, the British astronomer who coined the term 'Big Bang' (ironically, as a term of mockery — he favoured steady state cosmology), in 1953 predicted the existence of an excited state of carbon-12 (the Hoyle state) at 7.65 MeV — not from direct experiment but from the logical argument that carbon had to exist in abundance (because we exist), and without this energy level, stellar nucleosynthesis could not produce it in sufficient quantity. When experimentalists tested this, the energy level was exactly where Hoyle predicted. He used anthropic reasoning — starting from the existence of observers to deduce a physical constant — to make a precise, testable, and confirmed prediction. Hoyle was so astonished by this that he later wrote: 'A common sense interpretation of the facts suggests that a superintellect has monkeyed with physics.' He remained agnostic, but the statement illustrates how fine-tuning encounters science at its limits.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp3_desc: Participles and gerunds are non-finite verb forms — they derive from verbs but function as adjectives (participles) or nouns/adjectives (gerunds and gerundives). They are among the most versatile and frequently used constructions in Latin, appearing in virtually every classical text, and mastering them dramatically improves both translation accuracy and appreciation of Latin's stylistic range.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp3_kp0: Participles: verbal adjectives. Three active and one passive participle in Latin. Present active participle (PAP): ongoing action simultaneous with the main verb. Formed: present stem + -ans/-antis (1st/2nd conj.) or -ens/-entis (3rd/4th). 'Puella cantans' (the singing girl / the girl who is singing). Perfect passive participle (PPP): completed passive action; the 4th principal part. 'Milites victi' (the conquered soldiers / the soldiers who had been conquered). Future active participle (FAP): action about to be done; shows intention. 4th principal part stem + -urus/-ura/-urum. 'Dux pugnaturus' (the general about to fight). Future passive participle (gerundive): see below.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp3_kp1: Participial phrases: participles agree with the noun they modify in gender, number, and case. Often form a phrase with the noun: 'Hostes terram vastantes fugerunt.' (The enemies, ravaging the land, fled.) The participial phrase 'terram vastantes' modifies 'hostes'. Latin uses participial phrases where English uses relative clauses or temporal/causal clauses: 'Hostes victi discesserunt.' (The defeated enemies departed. / The enemies, having been defeated, departed. / After the enemies were defeated, they departed.) Context determines the best English rendering.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp3_kp2: The gerund: a verbal noun — the action of the verb used as a noun. Appears only in accusative, genitive, dative, and ablative (not nominative — use infinitive instead). Formed: present stem + -ndum (accusative/ablative). 'Amor legendi' (the love of reading). 'Ad pugnandum' (for the purpose of fighting). 'In scribendo' (in writing). Translate as English '-ing' noun: reading, fighting, writing. The gerund is rare in the nominative; instead, Latin uses the infinitive ('Errare humanum est' — To err is human).
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp3_kp3: The gerundive (future passive participle): a verbal adjective expressing necessity or obligation. Agrees with its noun in gender, number, and case. 'Liber legendus est.' (The book must be read / The book is to be read.) 'Urbs delenda est.' (The city must be destroyed — Cato the Elder's famous phrase urging Rome to destroy Carthage.) Gerundive of obligation: passive periphrastic construction: gerundive + esse + dative of agent. 'Mihi faciendum est.' (It must be done by me. / I must do it.) The dative of agent (mihi, tibi, ei...) expresses who bears the obligation.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp3_kp4: Gerundive replacing gerund + object: when a gerund would take an accusative object, Latin prefers to replace the gerund+object with a gerundive agreeing with the object. Gerund version (less preferred): 'Ars legendum libros' (the art of reading books). Preferred: 'Ars librorum legendorum' (the art of books-to-be-read — gerundive 'legendorum' agrees with 'librorum'). This substitution means gerundives often appear in genitive or ablative where gerunds theoretically could appear, and recognising this substitution is essential for accurate translation.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp3_insight: The gerundive's expression of obligation — 'X must be done' — gave Latin one of its most potent political phrases. Marcus Porcius Cato (Cato the Elder, 234–149 BCE), Roman senator and notorious moralist, is said to have ended every speech in the Senate with 'Ceterum censeo Carthaginem esse delendam' (Furthermore, I am of the opinion that Carthage must be destroyed), regardless of the topic of debate. 'Delendam' is a gerundive of 'delere' (to destroy), and the phrase became an idiom for obsessive, repeated advocacy of a single cause. After Third Punic War (149–146 BCE), Rome did destroy Carthage: the city was razed, its population killed or enslaved, and the site cursed. The Roman historian Appian (2nd century CE) records that Scipio Aemilianus, who commanded the final destruction, wept as Carthage burned, quoting Homer's lines about the fall of Troy and reflecting that Rome's own fate might one day be similar. The gerundive — a grammatical form expressing moral necessity — encapsulates a political obsession that destroyed a civilisation.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp4_desc: Indirect speech (oratio obliqua) is the Latin method for reporting what was said, thought, felt, or believed. Unlike English which uses 'that' + clause (He said that he was tired), Latin uses the accusative and infinitive construction for indirect statement, and ut/ne + subjunctive for indirect command. This construction is ubiquitous in Latin prose, particularly in Caesar, Cicero, and Livy, and is essential for reading narrative and rhetorical texts.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp4_kp0: Indirect statement (accusative + infinitive): used after verbs of saying, thinking, knowing, perceiving, and believing: dico (say), puto (think), credo (believe), scio (know), video (see), sentio (feel), audio (hear). Structure: subject of the infinitive goes into the ACCUSATIVE; the verb of the indirect statement becomes an INFINITIVE. 'Puellam currere dicit.' (He says that the girl is running. Literally: He says the girl to be running.) The accusative 'puellam' is the subject of the infinitive 'currere'.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp4_kp1: Tense of the infinitive: the infinitive tense is relative to the main verb's time. Present infinitive: action simultaneous with main verb. 'Dicit puerum currere.' (He says the boy IS running.) Perfect infinitive (from 3rd principal part + -isse): action BEFORE main verb. 'Dicit puerum cucurrisse.' (He says the boy RAN / has run.) Future infinitive (future active participle + esse): action AFTER main verb. 'Dicit puerum cursurum esse.' (He says the boy WILL run.) These distinctions allow Latin to precisely specify the temporal relationship between reported action and main verb.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp4_kp2: Reflexive pronouns in indirect statement: when the subject of the infinitive is the same as the subject of the main verb, Latin uses se (reflexive) and suus/sua/suum (reflexive possessive). 'Dixit se venturum esse.' (He said that he [himself] would come.) This use of se shows that 'he' refers back to the speaker. If non-reflexive 'eum' were used: 'Dixit eum venturum esse' would mean he said that a different person would come. This distinction is critical for accurate interpretation of Caesar's battle narratives.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp4_kp3: Indirect command: verbs of ordering, asking, urging, begging, forbidding take ut/ne + subjunctive (NOT accusative + infinitive). 'Caesar milites hortatus est ut fortiter pugnarent.' (Caesar urged the soldiers to fight bravely.) 'Imperavit ne discederen't.' (He ordered them not to depart.) Distinction from iubeo (to order) which unusually takes accusative + infinitive: 'Caesar milites discedere iussit.' (Caesar ordered the soldiers to leave.) These constructions appear constantly in Latin historical narrative.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp4_kp4: Indirect question: introduced by indirect question words (quis? who?, quid? what?, cur? why?, quando? when?, ubi? where?, quomodo? how?, num? whether?) + subjunctive. The subjunctive is required because the question is embedded in reported thought. 'Nescio cur discesserit.' (I don't know why he departed.) 'Rogavit quid fecisset.' (He asked what he had done.) Distinguish from direct question (indicative): 'Cur discessit?' (Why did he depart?) vs indirect 'Rogavit cur discessisset.' (He asked why he had departed.) The tense follows sequence of tenses rules.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp4_insight: The accusative and infinitive construction for indirect statement — Latin's method of reporting speech — was so deeply embedded in classical Latin that it resisted replacement by the 'quod' + finite verb construction that gradually took over in Vulgar Latin and eventually produced the modern Romance languages' 'that'-clauses (French 'que', Italian 'che', Spanish 'que'). Classical authors like Cicero strongly preferred the accusative + infinitive as the mark of educated, formal Latin. The shift to 'quod' clauses is traceable in Late Latin texts from the 3rd century CE onwards, accelerating in Christian writers like Jerome (translator of the Bible into Latin, the Vulgate, ~382–405 CE) who wrote in a more vernacular style. This grammatical shift is one of the clearest markers distinguishing Classical Latin from Vulgar Latin and tracking the historical transition from Roman to medieval culture. Jerome's Vulgate: 'Dixit quod resurget' (He said that he would rise again) — the 'quod' construction that Cicero would have corrected is now the ancestor of every Western European 'that'-clause.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp5: Conditionals and the subjunctive in Latin
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp5_desc: The Latin subjunctive mood is used in an extraordinarily wide range of constructions: purpose clauses, result clauses, indirect commands, indirect questions, cum clauses, conditional sentences, and others. Unlike the indicative (which states facts), the subjunctive signals that an action is possible, desirable, feared, commanded, or hypothetical. Mastering the subjunctive is essential for reading any advanced Latin text.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp5_kp0: The four subjunctive tenses: Present subjunctive: used in primary sequence (main verb is present, future, or perfect with present relevance). amem, ames, amet, amemus, ametis, ament (1st conj.). Imperfect subjunctive: used in secondary sequence (main verb is imperfect, perfect in narrative, or pluperfect). amarem, amares, amaret, amaremus, amaretis, amarent. Formed: present infinitive + personal endings. Perfect subjunctive: primary sequence, action completed before subordinate action. amaverim, amaveris, amaverit. Pluperfect subjunctive: secondary sequence, action completed before. amavissem, amavisses, amavisset.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp5_kp1: Independent uses of the subjunctive: Jussive (command or exhortation): 'Vivamus atque ameamus' (Let us live and let us love — Catullus). Deliberative (wondering what to do): 'Quid faciam?' (What am I to do?). Potential (what might happen): 'Quis hoc diceret?' (Who would say this?). Optative (wish): 'Utinam adesset!' (Would that he were here! / If only he were here!). These subjunctive uses appear without a conjunction, distinguishing them from the subjunctive of subordinate clauses.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp5_kp2: Conditional sentences — the three types: 1. Simple (open) condition — the condition is stated as a real possibility: si + indicative (any tense) in both clauses. 'Si venis, laetus sum.' (If you come, I am happy.) 'Si venies, laetus ero.' (If you will come/come, I will be happy.) 2. Contrary-to-fact present: si + present/imperfect subjunctive in both clauses. 'Si adesset, gauderem.' (If he were here [he is not], I would rejoice.) 3. Contrary-to-fact past: si + pluperfect subjunctive in both clauses. 'Si advenisset, gausissem.' (If he had arrived [he did not], I would have rejoiced.)
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp5_kp3: Sequence of tenses: in subordinate subjunctive clauses, the tense of the subjunctive is determined by the tense of the main verb. Primary sequence (main verb: present, future, perfect-as-present): present subjunctive (simultaneous/after), perfect subjunctive (before). Secondary sequence (main verb: imperfect, perfect-as-narrative, pluperfect): imperfect subjunctive (simultaneous/after), pluperfect subjunctive (before). Example: 'Rogat cur venias.' (He asks why you are coming — primary, present subj.) vs 'Rogavit cur venires.' (He asked why you were coming — secondary, imperfect subj.) Sequence of tenses is one of the most systematically tested skills in Latin examinations.
learnSch_latin_language_f0_tp5_kp4: The subjunctive in purpose and result: Purpose (final): ut + subjunctive — what the action aims to achieve. 'Venit ut te videret.' (He came in order to see you.) Imperfect subjunctive because main verb (venit) is secondary. Negative purpose: ne + subjunctive. 'Cucurrit ne captus esset.' (He ran so that he would not be captured.) Result (consecutive): ut + subjunctive — what actually resulted. Often signalled by 'tam', 'tantus', 'tot', 'ita', 'sic' in the main clause. 'Tam celeriter currebat ut nemo eum vincere posset.' (He was running so quickly that no one was able to beat him.) Distinguish: purpose = intended; result = actual.
